PagerDuty P1: clock-in-api error rate >2% during active event window (defined by events table start_time ±2h), schedule-api p99 >2s. Alert: clock_in_invalid_location_rate >10% during event (may indicate venue polygon misconfiguration).
target_output.cost_and_scaling: MVP cost ~$700/month: 3 GKE Autopilot pods baseline, Cloud SQL db-standard-2 ~$150, Redis Memorystore Basic ~$80, Cloud Logging and Monitoring ~$50. At 10-venue scale: ~$3500/month with per-venue Cloud SQL instances and GKE node pool scaling for multi-venue simultaneous events. Cost controls: clock-in-api scales to zero outside event windows, Cloud SQL dev instance paused overnight, Autopilot billing only for requested pod CPU/memory.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (10 weeks): Keycloak setup with RBAC, schedule builder UI with drag-and-drop, shift publishing and Twilio SMS notification, QR code clock-in with HMAC token validation and PostGIS geolocation check, basic attendance report, ADP payroll CSV export, GitLab CI/CD pipeline, Prometheus and Grafana setup. Phase 2 (6 weeks): Shift swap request and approval workflow, attendance analytics dashboard, payroll export audit trail, GDPR pseudonymization job, multi-department supervisor view. Phase 3 (8 weeks): Multi-venue network scheduling, labor cost forecasting with scheduled vs actual hours delta, mobile-optimized PWA for staff clock-in, ADP direct API integration replacing CSV.
target_output.development_guidelines: Go 1.22 strict: golangci-lint with errcheck and govet. 80% test coverage on Gin handlers and business logic. Clock-in endpoint load tested with k6 at 400 concurrent requests before each event-day release. TypeScript strict mode in Next.js. dnd-kit interactions accessibility-tested with keyboard navigation. ADP CSV format validated against test import file before each payroll export release. ADR for clock-in Redis hot-path design and QR HMAC token validity window choice.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Redis Memorystore outage during event-day clock-in. Mitigation: clock-in-api falls back to direct Cloud SQL query with 2-second timeout; Grafana alert if fallback rate exceeds 5%. Risk: PostGIS venue polygon inaccurate for outdoor venues causing high invalid_location rate. Mitigation: 30-meter buffer added to polygon boundary; invalid_location alert triggers operations review. Risk: dnd-kit schedule builder performance degradation with 400-staff roster. Mitigation: virtualized staff roster list using react-window; lazy-load staff cards on scroll. Tradeoff: Go Gin over Node.js for clock-in service favored for concurrency under burst load at cost of separate runtime from frontend.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Selected Twilio over GCP Cloud SMS for international touring staff compatibility. Chose PostGIS polygon approach over IP-geolocation for geofencing accuracy in stadium environments. Confirmed ADP CSV export over direct ADP API for MVP to reduce integration risk; direct API in Phase 3. Selected 10-minute QR token window as balance between usability (late arrivals) and replay attack risk.

metadata.notes: First-pass plan for arena venue workforce scheduling modernization; 400-staff concurrent clock-in and ADP payroll integration are key technical challenges.

project_goal.value: Decompose a monolithic fitness club management system into independent microservices covering member enrollment, class booking, personal trainer scheduling, and equipment maintenance tracking, with a unified web portal for gym staff and members
project_goal.rationale: Current monolith cannot scale class booking independently during peak morning enrollment windows; maintenance tracking is tightly coupled causing cascading failures

target_users.value: Gym members (class booking and trainer scheduling), gym staff (equipment management, admin), personal trainers (appointment management)
target_users.rationale: Three distinct user types with different usage patterns and permission needs

project_class.rationale: Unified portal with multiple microservice backends; not a pure API service as it includes member-facing UI

capabilities.value: frontend, backend, data, auth, integrations, analytics, realtime, admin_panel
capabilities.rationale: Real-time class waitlist promotion, member enrollment event bus, analytics for class utilization, and admin panel for gym operations all required

complexity_level.rationale: Microservices architecture with event bus coordination, real-time waitlist, and 10,000+ concurrent member sessions requires high-scale design

risk_level.rationale: Member personal and payment data require protection; system downtime during peak booking windows has direct revenue impact

data_sensitivity.rationale: Member names, contact details, fitness goals, and training history constitute personal data under GDPR for EU gym chains

external_exposure.rationale: All portal features require authentication; no public browsing of member data

access_model.value: Auth0 with RBAC: member (own bookings and trainer sessions), trainer (own appointment calendar and client list), staff (equipment and class management), admin (full gym configuration)
access_model.rationale: Trainers must not see other trainers' client lists; members must not access other members' data

feature_scope.value: Member enrollment with multi-service event fan-out, class booking with real-time capacity and waitlist promotion, personal trainer appointment scheduling, equipment maintenance work order creation and tracking, usage analytics, admin configuration panel
feature_scope.rationale: All four service domains required for feature parity with current monolith plus analytics capability

mvp_scope.value: Member enrollment, class booking with waitlist, and equipment maintenance — trainer scheduling in Phase 2
mvp_scope.rationale: Three services sufficient to replace highest-traffic monolith functions; trainer scheduling monolith module can run in parallel during transition

security_baseline.value: Auth0 JWT for all services, HTTPS everywhere, member PII encrypted at rest, service-to-service communication via internal VPC only, no direct DB access between services
security_baseline.rationale: Microservice isolation requires service-to-service API boundaries; no cross-service DB joins

frontend_stack.value: Vue 3 with TypeScript
frontend_stack.rationale: Existing frontend team Vue expertise; Pinia state management aligns with event-driven updates

backend_stack.value: Java 21 with Spring Boot
backend_stack.rationale: Spring Boot ecosystem (Spring Cloud, Spring Data JPA) provides mature microservice infrastructure; Java 21 virtual threads for high-concurrency booking endpoint

data_platform.value: PostgreSQL 16 per service (database-per-service pattern), Redis 7 for class capacity cache and session tokens
data_platform.rationale: Database-per-service enforces service independence; shared PostgreSQL instance would reintroduce monolith coupling

hosting_target.value: AWS ECS Fargate
hosting_target.rationale: Managed containers; team already operates ECS; avoids Kubernetes operational overhead for current team size

privacy_retention_policy.value: Member personal data retained for membership duration plus 3 years for GDPR; EU member deletion requests fulfilled within 30 days; equipment maintenance records retained 5 years
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: GDPR Article 17 right to erasure and Article 5 storage limitation apply to EU member gym chains

future_scope.value: Mobile app for member booking, cross-gym chain analytics, AI-powered class recommendation engine, integration with wearable fitness devices
future_scope.rationale: Mobile app requires stable microservice API contracts; recommendations engine requires analytics data foundation

constraints.value: Each microservice must be independently deployable without coordinated downtime; class booking API must handle 500 concurrent booking requests during peak morning window; budget under $4000/month
constraints.rationale: Independent deployment is the core microservices value proposition; 500 concurrent bookings during 6am-8am peak is measured from current monolith traffic

observability_baseline.value: New Relic APM with distributed tracing across services, structured JSON logs, alerting on booking error rate and waitlist queue depth
observability_baseline.rationale: New Relic already deployed on monolith; distributed tracing critical for microservice debugging

execution_preference.value: Strangler fig migration pattern — new microservices deployed alongside monolith with traffic routed incrementally per service
execution_preference.rationale: Zero big-bang cutover; monolith remains fallback until each service reaches production confidence

compliance_context.value: GDPR for EU member personal data; SOC 2 Type II recommended for enterprise gym chain contracts; GDPR deletion within 30 days of member request
compliance_context.rationale: Enterprise gym chain clients require vendor security attestation and GDPR data processing agreements

project.goal: Decompose fitness club monolith into microservices with unified Vue 3 portal
project.target_users: Gym members, personal trainers, gym staff, admins
project.timeline: 3-service MVP in 14 weeks; full 4-service decomposition in 6 months

frontend.state: Pinia for global session and booking state
frontend.realtime: WebSocket for class capacity updates and waitlist promotion notifications

backend.api: RESTful per service; events over Amazon SQS/EventBridge

security.auth: Auth0 OIDC with RBAC

data.cache: Redis 7 on ElastiCache for booking capacity and sessions

reasoner_reviews.product: Class booking with real-time capacity and instant waitlist promotion is the member-facing feature with highest business impact. The critical member experience moment is: class fills — member joins waitlist — cancellation occurs — member gets instant notification and slot is reserved. If this takes more than 5 seconds, members will refresh and book competing classes. The real-time channel must be designed for this latency target.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Database-per-service is correctly scoped. The inter-service coupling risk is the member enrollment fan-out: creating a member must trigger billing provisioning, access control setup, and welcome email across three services. Saga pattern with compensating transactions is appropriate here — outbox pattern on the enrollment service publishing to EventBridge, with downstream services consuming asynchronously. The booking service must use optimistic locking on class capacity to handle 500 concurrent booking requests without distributed locking overhead.
reasoner_reviews.security: Auth0 RBAC with per-service JWT validation is sound. Risk: service-to-service calls within the ECS VPC — if any service calls another service's API, it must use a machine-to-machine Auth0 client credential token, not a user JWT. Ensure inter-service routes are not accessible via the ALB — only internal VPC traffic allowed between services.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: 500 concurrent class bookings is achievable with Java 21 virtual threads eliminating thread-per-request blocking. The booking service Redis cache for class capacity must use atomic DECR operations to prevent overselling when multiple bookings arrive simultaneously. Optimistic locking in Spring Data JPA as a fallback for cache misses.

frontend.app_structure: Vue 3 single SPA with route-based modules: /classes (browse and book), /my-bookings (upcoming and history), /trainers (find and schedule), /equipment (staff view), /admin (configuration). Vue Router with lazy-loaded route components. Composables for each service domain (useClassBooking, useWaitlist, useEquipment).
frontend.pages_and_flows: ClassBrowser: grid of class schedules with real-time capacity badges (WebSocket-updated). ClassDetail: booking CTA with waitlist join if full. MyBookings: upcoming reservations with cancel option, waitlist position badges. TrainerDirectory: trainer profiles with availability calendar and booking form. EquipmentDashboard (staff): asset list with maintenance status indicators and work order creation modal.
frontend.state_management: Pinia: authStore (Auth0 session), bookingStore (active and pending bookings), classCapacityStore (WebSocket-updated capacities). Real-time: WebSocket connection to capacity-feed service updating classCapacityStore on class_capacity_updated and waitlist_promoted events.
frontend.performance_notes: Vue virtual scrolling for class schedule grid with 200+ classes. WebSocket reconnection with exponential backoff. Class capacity badge debounced 200ms to avoid excessive re-renders on burst capacity updates.

backend.service_design: Four Spring Boot microservices: enrollment-service (member and membership_plan management), booking-service (class schedules, bookings, waitlist), trainer-service (trainer profiles, appointments, client lists), equipment-service (assets, maintenance_tickets, work_orders). Shared Spring Cloud dependencies: Auth0 JWT validation library, outbox publisher. API Gateway (AWS API Gateway or ALB routing) routes /api/v1/enrollments/* to enrollment-service, /api/v1/bookings/* to booking-service, etc.
backend.api_patterns: RESTful per service. booking-service uses optimistic locking (JPA @Version) on class_schedules.capacity_remaining. Booking endpoint returns 409 Conflict with waitlist_position if capacity exhausted. enrollment-service uses transactional outbox pattern: member creation and MemberEnrolled event written in same PostgreSQL transaction.
backend.background_jobs: EventBridge rule triggers daily job in equipment-service: scan equipment_assets WHERE (current_usage_hours - last_maintenance_hours) >= maintenance_interval_hours; create maintenance_tickets for due assets; notify assigned technician via SES. Booking-service: waitlist promotion job triggered by booking cancellation event — promotes first waitlist entry, sends SNS notification, and updates booking status to confirmed.
backend.scaling_notes: Booking-service: ECS auto-scaling on CPU 70%, min 3 tasks, max 12 tasks. Redis DECR atomic operation for class_capacity to prevent overselling. Java 21 virtual threads eliminate thread exhaustion under 500 concurrent booking requests.

security.auth_design: Auth0 tenant with OIDC. Vue 3 uses @auth0/auth0-vue SDK. Spring Boot services validate Auth0 JWT using spring-security-oauth2-resource-server with JWKS endpoint cached. Machine-to-machine: enrollment-service uses Auth0 client credentials grant to call access-control-service internally.
security.authorization_model: Per-service Spring Security method-level security: @PreAuthorize('hasRole(MEMBER)') on booking creation; @PreAuthorize('hasRole(STAFF) or hasRole(ADMIN)') on equipment write endpoints. Trainers restricted to bookings WHERE trainer_id = jwt.sub.
security.privacy_controls: Member PII (email, phone, address) stored encrypted in enrollment-service PostgreSQL with AWS RDS KMS. GDPR deletion: MemberDeletionRequested event triggers compensating actions in all services to pseudonymize or delete member references within 30-day SLA.

data.entities: enrollment-service: members (id, auth0_sub, name_encrypted, email_encrypted, phone, membership_plan_id, joined_at, gdpr_delete_requested_at), membership_plans (id, name, monthly_fee, class_credits, active). booking-service: class_schedules (id, class_name, instructor, start_time, duration_mins, capacity, capacity_remaining, location), bookings (id, member_id, class_schedule_id, status ENUM confirmed/cancelled/waitlisted, booked_at), waitlist_entries (id, class_schedule_id, member_id, position, notified_at). equipment-service: equipment_assets (id, name, model, location, purchase_date, usage_hours, last_maintenance_hours, maintenance_interval_hours, status ENUM active/maintenance/retired), maintenance_tickets (id, asset_id, trigger_type ENUM scheduled/reported, created_at, assigned_to, status ENUM open/in_progress/closed, completed_at). trainer-service: personal_trainers (id, auth0_sub, name, specializations, bio), trainer_appointments (id, trainer_id, member_id, appointment_time, duration_mins, status ENUM scheduled/completed/cancelled).
data.storage_design: Database-per-service: separate RDS PostgreSQL 16 instance per service (db.t3.medium for booking and enrollment; db.t3.small for equipment and trainer). Shared ElastiCache Redis 7 cluster for class capacity cache and session tokens. Cross-service data: services hold only foreign IDs; no cross-service JOINs; enrichment via API calls at read time.
data.retention_and_deletion: GDPR: member records pseudonymized after delete request + 30 days; all four services consume MemberDeletionCompleted event. equipment maintenance records retained 5 years. booking history retained 3 years.

devops.deployment_topology: Four ECS Fargate services, each with independent deployment pipeline and ALB target group. API Gateway routes by path prefix. Shared: ElastiCache Redis, EventBridge event bus. Per-service: RDS PostgreSQL instance, ECR repository, ECS Fargate service.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions per service: Maven build, Spring Boot test with 80% coverage, Docker build, ECR push, ECS rolling deploy. Strangler fig: AWS API Gateway routing rules updated per-service migration milestone. Feature flag via Spring Cloud Config for gradual traffic routing.
devops.observability_stack: New Relic APM Java agent on each Fargate task for distributed tracing. Custom metrics: booking_success_rate, waitlist_depth_per_class, equipment_overdue_maintenance_count, enrollment_fan_out_duration_ms. Alert: booking error rate >1%, waitlist_depth >20 (signals capacity planning issue).

target_output.thinking_summary: Designing a microservices decomposition of a fitness club monolith using strangler fig migration pattern. The key architectural decisions are: database-per-service with EventBridge event bus for cross-service coordination; transactional outbox on enrollment-service for reliable fan-out to billing, access control, and welcome email on member creation; optimistic locking plus Redis DECR for class booking under 500-concurrent peak load; Java 21 virtual threads for booking-service concurrency. GDPR deletion must propagate across all four services via a compensating event.
target_output.title: Fitness Club Microservices Management Platform
target_output.executive_summary: Microservices decomposition of a fitness club management monolith into four independently deployable Spring Boot 3.2 services — enrollment, booking, trainer, and equipment — with a unified Vue 3 portal. Member enrollment triggers an event-bus fan-out via Amazon EventBridge using the transactional outbox pattern. Class booking handles 500 concurrent morning-peak requests using Java 21 virtual threads and atomic Redis DECR on capacity counters with real-time WebSocket waitlist promotion. Auth0 RBAC enforces per-role data scoping across all services. GDPR member deletion propagates via compensating events to all four service databases within 30 days.
target_output.architecture_overview: Vue 3 SPA communicates via HTTPS to AWS API Gateway which routes requests by path prefix to four ECS Fargate services: enrollment-service (/api/v1/enrollments/*), booking-service (/api/v1/bookings/*), trainer-service (/api/v1/trainers/*), equipment-service (/api/v1/equipment/*). Each service has its own PostgreSQL 16 RDS instance (database-per-service isolation). Cross-service events flow via Amazon EventBridge with SQS subscriptions per consumer service. Enrollment-service uses a transactional outbox pattern to guarantee MemberEnrolled event delivery. Booking-service uses atomic Redis DECR for class capacity with JPA optimistic locking as fallback, and publishes capacity updates via WebSocket for real-time class browser. Auth0 validates JWTs at each service independently. GDPR deletion propagates via MemberDeletionRequested EventBridge event consumed by all services. Strangler fig migration keeps monolith running until each service milestone is production-validated.
target_output.technology_stack: Frontend: Vue 3.4, TypeScript, Pinia, Vue Router 4, @auth0/auth0-vue, native WebSocket for capacity feed. Backend: Java 21 virtual threads, Spring Boot 3.2, Spring Security OAuth2 Resource Server (Auth0 JWKS), Spring Data JPA, Spring Cloud OpenFeign for inter-service calls. Database: PostgreSQL 16 on RDS per service, Flyway migrations. Cache: ElastiCache Redis 7 with Lettuce client. Event Bus: Amazon EventBridge + SQS with dead-letter queues. Auth: Auth0 OIDC. Infra: ECS Fargate, API Gateway, ECR, GitHub Actions, New Relic APM.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Member Enrollment: registration with membership plan selection, enrollment fan-out to locker provisioning, billing activation, and welcome email via event bus. Class Booking: real-time class capacity browser with WebSocket-updated counters, booking with instant confirmation, waitlist join with position display, automatic waitlist promotion on cancellation with SNS notification. Trainer Scheduling: trainer profile directory with availability calendar, appointment booking by member, trainer-side appointment management and client history view. Equipment Maintenance: asset registry with usage-hour tracking, scheduled work order generation when maintenance interval reached, technician assignment and work order status tracking. Admin Panel: gym configuration, membership plan management, staff and trainer user provisioning, class schedule administration. Analytics: class utilization rates, peak booking hour heatmap, equipment maintenance compliance rate, member retention cohorts.
target_output.system_components: API Gateway (path-based routing to 4 ECS services). ECS Fargate: enrollment-service (2-5 tasks), booking-service (3-12 tasks, HPA on CPU 70%), trainer-service (2-4 tasks), equipment-service (2-3 tasks). PostgreSQL 16 RDS per service (db.t3.medium for enrollment and booking; db.t3.small for trainer and equipment). ElastiCache Redis 7 cluster (class capacity counters, session tokens). Amazon EventBridge custom event bus (FitnessClubEvents). SQS queues per service with DLQ. Outbox publisher in enrollment-service. WebSocket server in booking-service for capacity feed. New Relic APM agents. EventBridge cron for equipment maintenance scan.
target_output.workflows: Member Enrollment Fan-Out: Member submits registration form. enrollment-service validates, inserts members row and outbox_events row (MemberEnrolled payload) in single PostgreSQL transaction. Outbox publisher reads outbox_events, publishes to EventBridge FitnessClubEvents bus. Downstream consumers: access-control-service provisions locker code and gym entry token; billing-service creates first monthly charge; notification-service sends SES welcome email. Each consumer acknowledges via SQS message deletion. Compensating rollback if billing fails: MemberEnrollmentFailed event triggers enrollment-service to set member status=enrollment_failed. Class Booking Under Peak Load: Member sends POST /api/v1/bookings/classes/:id/book. booking-service checks Redis capacity counter for class_schedule_id via SETNX-style DECR — if counter reaches 0, returns 409 with waitlist offer. If capacity available, inserts bookings row, publishes ClassBooked event to EventBridge, emits capacity_updated WebSocket message to all connected clients viewing that class. On cancellation: CancellationReceived event triggers waitlist promotion job — promotes first waitlist_entries entry to confirmed booking, sends SNS push notification to promoted member, emits WebSocket update. Equipment Preventive Maintenance: EventBridge cron fires daily at 03:00 UTC. equipment-service queries equipment_assets WHERE (usage_hours - last_maintenance_hours) >= maintenance_interval_hours AND status='active'. Creates maintenance_tickets with trigger_type=scheduled for each due asset. SES notification sent to assigned technician with asset details and due date. Ticket closure updates last_maintenance_hours.
target_output.data_model: enrollment-service: members (id UUID PK, auth0_sub VARCHAR UNIQUE, name_encrypted BYTEA, email_encrypted BYTEA, phone VARCHAR, membership_plan_id FK, status ENUM active/suspended/enrollment_failed, joined_at TIMESTAMPTZ, gdpr_delete_requested_at TIMESTAMPTZ NULLABLE), membership_plans (id UUID PK, name VARCHAR, monthly_fee_cents INT, class_credits_per_month INT, active BOOL), outbox_events (id UUID PK, event_type VARCHAR, payload JSONB, published BOOL DEFAULT false, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ). booking-service: class_schedules (id UUID PK, class_name VARCHAR, instructor VARCHAR, start_time TIMESTAMPTZ, duration_mins INT, capacity SMALLINT, capacity_remaining SMALLINT, location VARCHAR, VERSION INT for JPA optimistic locking), bookings (id UUID PK, member_id UUID, class_schedule_id FK, status ENUM confirmed/cancelled/waitlisted, booked_at TIMESTAMPTZ), waitlist_entries (id UUID PK, class_schedule_id FK, member_id UUID, position SMALLINT, notified_at TIMESTAMPTZ NULLABLE). equipment-service: equipment_assets (id UUID PK, name VARCHAR, model VARCHAR, location VARCHAR, purchase_date DATE, usage_hours FLOAT, last_maintenance_hours FLOAT, maintenance_interval_hours FLOAT, status ENUM active/maintenance/retired), maintenance_tickets (id UUID PK, asset_id FK, trigger_type ENUM scheduled/reported, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ, assigned_to VARCHAR, status ENUM open/in_progress/closed, completed_at TIMESTAMPTZ NULLABLE). trainer-service: personal_trainers (id UUID PK, auth0_sub VARCHAR, name VARCHAR, specializations TEXT[], bio TEXT), trainer_appointments (id UUID PK, trainer_id FK, member_id UUID, appointment_time TIMESTAMPTZ, duration_mins INT, status ENUM scheduled/completed/cancelled).
target_output.api_design: API Gateway routes: /api/v1/enrollments POST (register member), /api/v1/enrollments/:id GET (profile). /api/v1/bookings/classes GET (class schedule list with capacity), /api/v1/bookings/classes/:id/book POST (book or join waitlist), /api/v1/bookings/my GET (member booking list), /api/v1/bookings/:id DELETE (cancel booking). /api/v1/trainers GET (trainer directory), /api/v1/trainers/:id/appointments POST (book appointment), /api/v1/trainers/:id/appointments GET (trainer-scoped: own appointment list). /api/v1/equipment GET (asset list with maintenance status), /api/v1/equipment/:id/tickets POST (create maintenance report), /api/v1/equipment/tickets GET (staff: all open tickets). WebSocket: wss://api/capacity-feed — emits {class_schedule_id, capacity_remaining} events on each booking/cancellation. All endpoints: Auth0 JWT Bearer validation per-service.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Auth0 RBAC: Spring Security @PreAuthorize annotations per endpoint; member role: POST /bookings, GET /my-bookings, POST /trainer-appointments; trainer role: GET own appointments and client list; staff role: write /equipment; admin: all. Machine-to-machine: enrollment-service uses Auth0 client credentials grant for inter-service calls to access-control-service — user JWTs never used for service-to-service calls. ALB security group: only API Gateway can reach ECS services (internal VPC traffic only). Member PII encrypted at column level with RDS KMS in enrollment-service. GDPR: MemberDeletionRequested event published on deletion request; all services have SQS consumer that pseudonymizes (replaces name/email/phone with NULL + hashed identifier) within 30-day window; MemberDeletionCompleted event published per service on completion. Audit log in CloudTrail for all IAM actions; application-level access log in CloudWatch for member data access.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: GitHub Actions per service: Maven clean test (80% coverage gate via JaCoCo), mvn package, Docker build, ECR push tagged with git SHA, ECS rolling deploy with minimum healthy percent 100%. Services deployed independently with no shared deploy coordination. Strangler fig routing: AWS API Gateway initially routes all traffic to monolith; per-milestone, path rules updated to route specific prefixes to new microservices. Monolith database read replica consulted during migration window. Flyway migrations in each service executed as ECS one-off task pre-deploy. Environments: dev (shared RDS cluster, LocalStack for EventBridge/SQS), staging (per-service RDS, real EventBridge), production (multi-AZ RDS for booking and enrollment services, single-AZ for trainer and equipment MVP).
target_output.observability: New Relic APM Java agents on all ECS tasks with distributed tracing context propagated via W3C TraceContext headers across service calls and EventBridge events. Custom New Relic metrics: booking_requests_per_second, booking_error_rate, class_capacity_remaining_min (alert if any class at 0 and waitlist > 20), waitlist_promotion_latency_ms (SLA 5s), enrollment_fan_out_duration_ms, equipment_overdue_maintenance_count (alert if > 0 for > 24h), outbox_unpublished_events_count (alert if > 5 for > 2 min). New Relic alerts: booking error rate > 1% triggers PagerDuty; capacity feed WebSocket connection drop > 50% triggers immediate alert.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: MVP cost ~$2800/month: 4 ECS services baseline (10 tasks total), 4 RDS db.t3.medium instances ~$1200, ElastiCache cache.t3.medium ~$120, API Gateway ~$50, EventBridge/SQS minimal. At enterprise gym chain scale (50 locations, 100K members): ~$12,000/month with RDS multi-AZ for booking/enrollment, ElastiCache cluster mode, and 30-task peak for booking service. Cost optimization: RDS reserved instances after 3-month baseline, booking-service scales to 3 minimum after 9pm, ElastiCache reserved nodes.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (14 weeks): Enrollment-service with outbox fan-out, booking-service with Redis capacity and waitlist, equipment-service with maintenance tracking, Auth0 RBAC, Vue 3 portal with class browser and equipment dashboard, EventBridge event bus, GitHub Actions CI per service, New Relic setup, strangler fig API Gateway routing for three services. Phase 2 (6 weeks): Trainer-service, WebSocket real-time capacity feed, GDPR deletion event propagation, class booking analytics, trainer scheduling Vue module. Phase 3 (8 weeks): Admin analytics dashboard, cross-service member profile view, mobile-optimized Vue PWA, membership plan management, SOC 2 evidence collection and audit preparation.
target_output.development_guidelines: Java 21 strict: SpotBugs + PMD in Maven build. JUnit 5 with 80% JaCoCo coverage. Each service tested in isolation with WireMock for inter-service API calls and TestContainers for PostgreSQL and Redis. Spring Boot Testcontainers integration tests mandatory for EventBridge outbox publishing. Vue 3 TypeScript strict. Pinia stores typed with full TypeScript interfaces. WebSocket reconnection logic tested with simulated disconnect. ADR for database-per-service isolation, outbox pattern choice over saga orchestrator, and Java 21 virtual threads for booking service concurrency.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Eventual consistency lag in enrollment fan-out — member created but locker not yet provisioned when member first logs in. Mitigation: enrollment-service returns member_status=provisioning until all fan-out consumers publish completion events; Vue portal shows provisioning banner. Risk: Redis DECR capacity counter diverging from PostgreSQL capacity_remaining under network partition. Mitigation: nightly reconciliation job compares Redis counter vs booking count per class; discrepancies trigger counter reset and alert. Risk: Strangler fig migration window where monolith and new services accept concurrent writes to overlapping data. Mitigation: API Gateway routing is all-or-nothing per service path — no traffic splitting for write endpoints. Tradeoff: database-per-service over shared PostgreSQL schema increases operational overhead (4 RDS instances) but enforces service independence required for independent deployment.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Selected EventBridge + SQS over Apache Kafka — team has no Kafka operational experience; EventBridge managed service reduces ops burden. Chose transactional outbox over Saga orchestrator for enrollment fan-out — simpler to implement and reason about for 3-service fan-out. Selected Java 21 virtual threads over reactive WebFlux — easier migration from existing Spring MVC monolith. Confirmed database-per-service with separate RDS instances over schema-per-service — stronger isolation and independent backup/restore.

metadata.notes: First-pass plan for fitness club microservices decomposition; strangler fig migration, real-time class booking, and GDPR fan-out deletion are key architectural themes.

project_goal.value: Static reference website for recreational sports league administrators displaying aggregated season statistics — standings tables, top scorers, win/loss records, and attendance figures — generated at build time from a league management database and refreshed nightly via CI
project_goal.rationale: League administrators need a public-facing stats page without the cost and complexity of a dynamic server; data changes only once per day

target_users.value: Recreational sports league administrators (content owners) and the general public (readers)
target_users.rationale: Admins trigger redeploys; public users browse standings and stats with no login required

project_class.rationale: All content generated at build time from league API; no server-side runtime needed

capabilities.rationale: Static pages with Vercel Analytics page-view tracking; no backend runtime or auth required

complexity_level.rationale: SSG with nightly data refresh; no user sessions, no dynamic queries, no auth

risk_level.rationale: No user data collected, no auth, no PII; only public aggregated sports statistics

data_sensitivity.rationale: Displays only publicly available aggregated league stats; no personal data stored or processed

external_exposure.rationale: Fully public website; no authentication required for any page

access_model.value: No authentication — fully public read access to all pages; only GitHub Actions workflow_dispatch authenticated via GitHub token for manual redeploy
access_model.rationale: Public league stats require no access control; redeploy trigger protected by GitHub repository permissions

feature_scope.value: Season standings tables per league and division, top scorers leaderboard, per-team win/loss/draw records, match results by matchday, attendance figures, nightly automated rebuild and deploy, manual redeploy via GitHub Actions workflow_dispatch
feature_scope.rationale: All display features confirmed by league administrators; manual redeploy required for mid-week corrections

mvp_scope.value: Season standings, top scorers, and match results pages for current season; nightly CI build and Vercel deploy
mvp_scope.rationale: Three page types cover 90% of administrator and public use cases for the current season

security_baseline.value: HTTPS enforced via Vercel Edge Network; no cookies set; no user input accepted; no server-side code executed at runtime; Content-Security-Policy header disabling inline scripts
security_baseline.rationale: Static site threat surface is minimal; CSP prevents XSS if any third-party embed is added later

frontend_stack.value: Astro with TypeScript
frontend_stack.rationale: Astro generates zero-JavaScript static HTML for data-display pages; partial hydration for interactive sort tables

backend_stack.value: none — all data fetched at Astro build time from league management API
backend_stack.rationale: Static site requires no runtime server; Astro content collections and getStaticPaths fetch data during CI build

data_platform.value: none — no database; Astro fetches JSON from league management API at build time and writes static pages
data_platform.rationale: No runtime data storage needed; build-time data fetch is sufficient for nightly refresh cadence

hosting_target.value: Vercel Edge Network with GitHub integration
hosting_target.rationale: Vercel provides automated deploy on push, global CDN edge delivery, preview deployments per PR, and built-in Vercel Analytics

privacy_retention_policy.value: No personal data collected or retained; Vercel Analytics collects anonymized aggregate page-view counts only — no cookies, no PII
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Static site displays only public stats; Vercel Edge Network privacy-safe analytics configured

future_scope.value: Historical season archive pages, embedded interactive match timeline widget, RSS feed for match results, multi-language support via Astro i18n
future_scope.rationale: Season archive and RSS are natural Phase 2 additions once nightly pipeline is stable

constraints.value: No server-side runtime at hosting layer; nightly build must complete within 10 minutes; league management API must be publicly readable without auth for build-time fetch
constraints.rationale: Vercel static hosting constraint; CI window and API access requirements from league IT team

observability_baseline.value: Vercel Analytics for page views and Core Web Vitals; GitHub Actions build status badge on README; email notification on build failure via GitHub Actions
observability_baseline.rationale: Lightweight observability appropriate for a public static site; build failure notification ensures stale data is caught

execution_preference.value: Single developer maintains Astro codebase; deploy-on-push via Vercel GitHub integration
execution_preference.rationale: Small league admin team; minimal operational overhead required

project.goal: Nightly-refreshed static standings and stats site for recreational sports league
project.timeline: Launch before current season begins; 6 weeks to production

frontend.data: Astro content collections fetching JSON from league management API at build time

devops.hosting: Vercel Edge Network with GitHub integration
devops.preview: Vercel preview deployments per pull request

constraints.api_access: league management API public read endpoint

reasoner_reviews.product: This site has one job: display current season stats with nightly freshness. The two most important quality attributes are build reliability and page load speed. If the nightly build fails, administrators will be calling support about stale standings tables. Build failure notification and a fallback to last successful build are more important for this project than any feature addition.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Astro is the right choice — zero JS for data-display pages, partial hydration for the sortable standings table. The data fetch strategy must handle league API unavailability gracefully: the fetch-league-data script should write successful API responses to committed cache files in src/data/cache/, and on API failure exit 0 so the Astro build continues reading from those cached files. This was missing in Round 1 and Round 2 plans — Round 2 incorrectly suggested Vercel Build Cache which is for build tool dependencies, not API data.
reasoner_reviews.security: Threat surface is minimal: no user input, no server-side execution at runtime, no cookies. The one security addition worth making is a strict Content-Security-Policy header via vercel.json headers configuration to prevent any accidental inline script execution if a third-party widget is embedded later. Vercel Analytics uses no cookies and collects no PII — confirm this is configured in Vercel project settings.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Build under 10 minutes is easily achievable for a static site with a handful of leagues. The main risk is API latency from the league management system during the nightly build window. Astro parallelizes page generation; the bottleneck will be the fetch script, not rendering.

devops.deployment_topology: Vercel project connected to GitHub repository. Main branch deploys to production URL. Pull requests generate Vercel preview deployments at unique URLs. No server infrastructure — Vercel Edge Network serves all static assets globally.
devops.environments: production (main branch, custom domain), preview (per-PR Vercel preview URL). No staging environment needed for static site.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions workflow: triggers on schedule (nightly 03:00 UTC) and workflow_dispatch. Steps: checkout, bun install, run fetch-league-data.ts script, astro build, vercel deploy --prod. On failure: GitHub Actions sends email notification to league admin via actions/email-action. Cache files committed back to data-cache branch by bot commit on successful run.
devops.rollback_strategy: Vercel preserves all deployment history. Rollback by promoting previous successful deployment via Vercel dashboard or vercel rollback CLI. Instant rollback — no database migration needed.
devops.observability_stack: Vercel Analytics enabled in project settings (privacy-safe, no cookies). Build status badge in repository README. GitHub Actions build duration metric in workflow summary. Email alert on workflow failure.

security.auth_design: No runtime authentication. GitHub Actions workflow_dispatch protected by GitHub repository write permission — only repository collaborators can trigger manual redeploys.
security.abuse_prevention: No user input forms — no attack surface for injection. Rate limiting not applicable to static file serving. Vercel Edge Network provides DDoS protection for static assets natively.
security.audit_and_logging_controls: Vercel deployment log for each build. GitHub Actions run history for build audit trail. No application-level audit log needed — no user actions or data mutations occur at runtime.

data.entities: leagues (slug, name, season), teams (id, league_slug, name, wins, losses, draws, goals_for, goals_against, points), players (id, team_id, name, position, goals, assists, matches_played), match_results (id, league_slug, matchday, home_team_id, away_team_id, home_score, away_score, played_at, attendance).
data.storage_design: No database. Data fetched as JSON from league management API during Astro build by fetch-league-data.ts script. JSON responses written to src/data/cache/ directory. Cache files committed to repository as authoritative fallback for API unavailability.
data.retention_and_deletion: No personal data retained. Build-time JSON cache files contain only aggregated public sports statistics. Cache files overwritten on each successful build run.

frontend.app_structure: Astro static site with TypeScript; standings and leaderboard pages generated at build time from JSON season data; no client-side routing
frontend.pages_and_flows: Home, Season Standings (per league/division), Top Scorers Leaderboard, Per-Team Stats, Schedule and Results Archive
frontend.performance_notes: All tables rendered as static HTML for maximum performance; season data embedded as inline JSON at build time; no client-side data fetching needed
frontend.accessibility_notes: Standings and leaderboard tables need proper thead, th with scope; sort controls must be keyboard-accessible with aria-sort attributes

ISS-001.title: No fallback when league management API is unavailable during nightly build
ISS-001.detail: Rounds 1 and 2 plans describe fetching league API data during build but provide no fallback if the API returns non-200. A failed fetch causes the Astro build to fail and Vercel skips the deploy, leaving the site on stale data indefinitely. Round 2 incorrectly proposed Vercel Build Cache which is for build dependencies, not API response data.

history.note: First identified by architect_reasoner
Round 2 proposed Vercel Build Cache — incorrect tool for API data persistence

ISS-002.title: Content-Security-Policy header missing from vercel.json configuration
ISS-002.detail: Rounds 1 and 2 reference CSP as a security baseline requirement but neither plan included the actual vercel.json headers block with the CSP directive value.

history.note: First identified in security review
Plan text mentioned CSP but omitted vercel.json configuration detail

previous_plan.executive_summary: Astro-based static website on Vercel Edge Network displaying nightly-refreshed recreational sports league standings, scorers, and match results. GitHub Actions nightly build fetches league API data and deploys to Vercel.
previous_plan.architecture_overview: Astro SSG fetches league JSON at build time. GitHub Actions runs nightly at 03:00 UTC. Vercel deploys on push. Vercel Build Cache mentioned for API data caching (incorrectly).
previous_plan.data_model: leagues, teams, players, match_results as build-time JSON from league management API. No database.

target_output.thinking_summary: Round 3 must close ISS-001 with a concrete correct solution: a fetch-league-data.ts TypeScript script that writes API responses to committed src/data/cache/ JSON files on success and exits 0 on failure so the Astro build continues with cached data. Round 2 incorrectly proposed Vercel Build Cache which persists only build tool artifacts. ISS-002 requires the actual vercel.json headers block with Content-Security-Policy using 'self' directives. Both fixes are small but the plan must be precise enough that an auditor can verify implementation.

fix_report.action_taken: Added scripts/fetch-league-data.ts: iterates over configured league slugs, calls league management API endpoints, writes successful JSON responses to src/data/cache/{slug}-standings.json, src/data/cache/{slug}-scorers.json, src/data/cache/{slug}-results.json. On any API error (non-200 or network timeout), logs a GitHub Actions warning annotation for that league slug and exits with code 0 so the Astro build continues reading from previously committed cache files. GitHub Actions workflow runs this script before astro build. On full success, a bot commit updates the cache files on a data-cache branch to keep the fallback current.
fix_report.expected_outcome: ISS-001 resolved: API unavailability no longer causes build failure; site serves last-successful cached data with warning annotation visible in GitHub Actions run log.

fix_report.action_taken: Added vercel.json with explicit headers block applying to all routes: Content-Security-Policy directive with default-src 'self', script-src 'self', style-src 'self' 'unsafe-inline' (Astro inlines critical CSS), img-src 'self' data:, frame-ancestors 'none'. Also added X-Frame-Options DENY and X-Content-Type-Options nosniff. Development guidelines updated: any new third-party asset domain must be added to img-src or script-src in vercel.json before merging.
fix_report.expected_outcome: ISS-002 resolved: CSP header present in vercel.json with specific directives; prevents accidental inline script injection from future third-party embeds.

target_output.title: Sports League Season Statistics Website
target_output.executive_summary: Zero-runtime static website for recreational sports league administrators built with Astro 4 on Vercel Edge Network, displaying nightly-refreshed season standings, top scorers, match results, and attendance figures for public audiences. A TypeScript fetch-league-data script runs before each Astro build, writing league API responses to committed JSON cache files in src/data/cache/; on API failure the script exits 0 and the build continues with cached data, emitting a GitHub Actions warning annotation. vercel.json configures Content-Security-Policy and X-Frame-Options headers. No server-side runtime, no database, no user data collected.
target_output.architecture_overview: GitHub Actions nightly workflow runs fetch-league-data.ts which calls the league management API for each configured league slug and writes standings, scorers, and match results JSON to src/data/cache/. On API failure (non-200 or timeout), the script logs a warning annotation and exits 0, leaving existing cache files intact so the Astro build continues with last-known data. Astro 4 content collections read from src/data/cache/ JSON files at build time, generating static HTML pages for each league, team, and matchday. Vercel deploys the built output to its Edge Network on push to main. Pull requests generate preview deployments at unique Vercel URLs. vercel.json applies security headers including Content-Security-Policy to all routes. Vercel Analytics provides privacy-safe page-view tracking with no cookies.
target_output.technology_stack: Framework: Astro 4 with TypeScript, Astro content collections for type-safe JSON data loading, Astro Islands for sortable standings table hydration. Build script: fetch-league-data.ts using Node.js built-in fetch and fs/promises for cache file writes, Zod for API response schema validation. Hosting: Vercel Edge Network. CI: GitHub Actions (nightly cron + workflow_dispatch). Analytics: Vercel Analytics (no cookies, no PII). Configuration: vercel.json headers and cleanUrls. No database, no server runtime, no backend framework.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Season Standings Pages: per-league sortable table with team rank, wins, draws, losses, goals-for, goals-against, points, and last-5-match form badges. Top Scorers Leaderboard: ranked player cards with goals, assists, and matches-played, filterable by league via Astro Island. Match Results Browser: matchday-accordion layout with home/away scores, venue, and attendance per fixture. Team Profile Pages: per-team season record summary and five most recent results. Nightly Data Refresh: automated CI build with API fetch, cache write, and Vercel deploy. Manual Redeploy: workflow_dispatch trigger for league administrators to apply mid-week corrections.
target_output.system_components: GitHub Actions nightly-build.yml workflow (cron 03:00 UTC, workflow_dispatch). fetch-league-data.ts script (Node.js fetch, Zod validation, writes to src/data/cache/, exits 0 on failure). src/data/cache/ directory of committed JSON fallback files. Astro 4 static site generator (content collections, Island for standings sort). vercel.json (security headers, cleanUrls: true). Vercel project (GitHub integration, Edge Network, preview deployments). Vercel Analytics dashboard. GitHub Actions secrets store (LEAGUE_API_KEY, VERCEL_TOKEN).
target_output.workflows: Nightly Automated Rebuild: GitHub Actions cron fires at 03:00 UTC. Workflow checks out repository, runs bun install. fetch-league-data.ts iterates league slugs from config: calls GET /api/v1/leagues/{slug}/standings, /scorers, /results. On 200 response and Zod validation pass: writes JSON to src/data/cache/{slug}-standings.json etc. On non-200 or Zod error: logs 'WARNING: using cached data for {slug}' as GitHub Actions warning annotation, skips file write. astro build runs reading from src/data/cache/ files. vercel deploy --prod publishes build output. On full success: bot commit updates cache files on data-cache branch for fallback freshness. On Astro build or Vercel deploy failure: GitHub Actions email notification to league-admin@example.com. Manual Redeploy: League administrator navigates to GitHub Actions tab, clicks Run workflow on nightly-build, optionally adds reason comment. Same fetch-and-build steps run on demand.
target_output.data_model: Build-time JSON structures (no runtime database). leagues: [{slug, name, season_year, divisions[]}]. teams: [{id, league_slug, division, name, wins, draws, losses, goals_for, goals_against, points, played, form_last5}]. players: [{id, team_id, name, position, goals, assists, yellow_cards, matches_played}]. match_results: [{id, league_slug, matchday, home_team_id, away_team_id, home_score, away_score, played_at, venue, attendance}]. All entities sourced from league management API at build time. JSON files in src/data/cache/ are the authoritative fallback. Astro content collections define TypeScript interfaces matching these shapes for build-time type checking.
target_output.api_design: No runtime API endpoints — static site serves pre-generated HTML and assets only. Build-time API consumption by fetch-league-data.ts: GET /api/v1/leagues (list of league slugs), GET /api/v1/leagues/{slug}/standings (team table JSON), GET /api/v1/leagues/{slug}/scorers (player leaderboard JSON), GET /api/v1/leagues/{slug}/results?matchday={n} (match result JSON per matchday). LEAGUE_API_KEY Bearer token sent in Authorization header from GitHub Actions secret. Zod schema validates required fields in each response. Vercel serves static files; no application routing at request time.
target_output.security_and_compliance: vercel.json headers block applied to all routes: Content-Security-Policy: default-src 'self'; script-src 'self'; style-src 'self' 'unsafe-inline'; img-src 'self' data:; frame-ancestors 'none'. X-Frame-Options: DENY. X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff. Referrer-Policy: strict-origin-when-cross-origin. No cookies set by the application; no user input fields anywhere on the site. LEAGUE_API_KEY stored only in GitHub Actions secrets — never committed to repository or included in build output. Vercel Analytics configured in privacy-safe mode: aggregated page views, no PII, no cookies. Zero runtime attack surface: Vercel Edge Network serves pre-generated static files; no code executes on incoming requests.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: GitHub Actions nightly-build.yml: triggers on schedule (cron 03:00 UTC) and workflow_dispatch. Steps: actions/checkout, bun install, node scripts/fetch-league-data.ts, bun run build (astro build), vercel deploy --prod --token $VERCEL_TOKEN. On any step failure: send email via dawidd6/action-send-mail to league admin. On full success: bot commit via stefanzweifel/git-auto-commit-action updates src/data/cache/ files on data-cache branch. vercel.json: headers block (CSP, X-Frame-Options, X-Content-Type-Options, Referrer-Policy), cleanUrls: true, trailingSlash: false. Rollback: vercel rollback or Vercel dashboard deployment promotion — instant, no migration. No staging environment: Vercel PR preview deployments used for content review.
target_output.observability: Vercel Analytics dashboard: daily page views per route (/standings/{slug}, /scorers/{slug}, /results/{slug}/{matchday}), Core Web Vitals (LCP, CLS, FID) per page type. GitHub Actions workflow summary: fetch duration per league slug, cache-hit vs cache-miss count per run, Vercel deployment URL. GitHub Actions warning annotations visible inline in run log when cache fallback is used. Email notification on build or deploy failure. No application-level metrics needed — build health and Core Web Vitals are the relevant quality signals for a static site.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Vercel Hobby tier sufficient for a single-league public site (free). Vercel Pro ($20/month) required for custom domain, team access, and advanced Vercel Analytics. GitHub Actions free tier covers nightly builds comfortably — a 3-minute build uses under 100 minutes of the 2000 free minutes per month. Total estimated cost: $0-$20/month. No autoscaling configuration needed; Vercel Edge Network handles arbitrary public traffic for static files.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (6 weeks): Astro project setup with content collections, fetch-league-data.ts with API fetch and Zod validation and JSON cache write, standings and scorers pages, GitHub Actions nightly workflow with email failure alert, Vercel deployment with vercel.json CSP headers, Vercel Analytics enabled. Phase 2 (3 weeks): Match results browser with matchday accordion, team profile pages, bot commit for cache file updates on successful builds, workflow_dispatch manual redeploy documentation. Phase 3 (future): Season archive pages for prior seasons, RSS feed for match results via @astrojs/rss, multi-language variants via Astro i18n, embedded match timeline widget.
target_output.development_guidelines: TypeScript strict mode in Astro config. fetch-league-data.ts uses Zod schemas for API response validation — schema changes in the league API surface immediately as build warnings rather than runtime errors. Committed cache files in src/data/cache/ must contain only aggregated stats with no player personal data beyond display names. Any new third-party asset domain added to the site must be added to vercel.json img-src or script-src before the PR merges. Astro Islands used sparingly — only the standings sort table requires client-side JavaScript. PR preview deployment URL shared with league administrators for content review before merge to main.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: league management API changes response schema without notice, causing Zod validation failures and cache fallback on every build. Mitigation: Zod schema uses .passthrough() for unknown fields to tolerate additive schema changes; only required fields are strictly validated. Risk: committed cache files become stale if API is unavailable for more than 7 consecutive days. Mitigation: GitHub Actions warning annotation appears on every run when cache is used; league admin email sent if any cache file modification timestamp is more than 48 hours behind current run date. Tradeoff: committing cache files to git increases repository size over time; accepted because league JSON files are small (under 100KB per league) and git history provides an audit trail of data changes across the season.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Confirmed Vercel Build Cache is for build tool artifacts such as node_modules and the Astro cache directory, not for application API response data — resolved the Round 2 confusion. Chose committed JSON files in src/data/cache/ as the API fallback mechanism over a separate key-value store. Confirmed Vercel Analytics over Google Analytics — no cookies required, aligned with no-PII data sensitivity constraint. Selected Zod for API response validation at build time over runtime TypeScript casting for early schema mismatch detection.

metadata.notes: Round 3 revision of static sports league stats site; closes API build fallback mechanism and CSP vercel.json configuration after two rounds of partial fixes.

project_goal.value: Python SDK for sports analytics platforms providing cryptographically signed data access tokens, per-consumer rate limits, and authenticated request proxying to Sportradar and Stats Perform
project_goal.rationale: Core SDK purpose replacing ad-hoc API client code across analytics teams

target_users.value: Enterprise sports analytics developers integrating Sportradar and Stats Perform data feeds into downstream applications
target_users.rationale: Primary SDK consumers identified in partnership agreements

project_class.rationale: Distributed as a PyPI package with no hosted runtime

capabilities.rationale: SDK handles request signing, rate limiting, and provider proxying without a frontend

complexity_level.rationale: Cryptographic token handling and rate-limit state management add meaningful complexity

risk_level.rationale: Compromised signing keys would allow unauthorized access to partner data feeds

data_sensitivity.rationale: API credentials and signed tokens provide access to commercially licensed sports data

external_exposure.rationale: SDK is distributed to enterprise analytics customers who integrate directly with partner data providers

access_model.value: Per-consumer credential files with environment-scoped signing keys; no shared credentials
access_model.rationale: Isolates blast radius of any single compromised credential

feature_scope.value: Signed token generation, per-consumer rate limit enforcement, Sportradar and Stats Perform request proxying, secret rotation utilities, structured audit logging
feature_scope.rationale: All features required for SOC 2 compliance attestation

mvp_scope.value: Signed token issuance, rate limit middleware, Sportradar proxy client with retry logic
mvp_scope.rationale: Minimum feature set enabling first enterprise customer onboarding

security_baseline.value: HMAC-SHA256 token signing, credential encryption at rest via OS keyring, TLS 1.3 for all upstream requests, no credentials in plaintext config files
security_baseline.rationale: SOC 2 CC6 controls require strong cryptographic protections

frontend_stack.value: N/A — library package, no frontend
frontend_stack.rationale: SDK has no browser or UI component

backend_stack.value: Python 3.12 with httpx for async HTTP, cryptography library for HMAC signing
backend_stack.rationale: Team expertise and async performance for high-throughput proxying

data_platform.value: No persistent database; rate-limit state in process memory with optional Redis sidecar for distributed deployments
data_platform.rationale: SDK must remain stateless by default for simple installation

hosting_target.value: PyPI package, self-hosted by consumer
hosting_target.rationale: SDK is a library, not a hosted service

privacy_retention_policy.value: Audit log entries retained by consumer per their own policy; SDK emits structured audit events to consumer-configured sinks
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: SDK is not the data controller; consumer is responsible for log retention

future_scope.value: OAuth 2.0 PKCE flow support, Stats Perform real-time WebSocket proxy, plugin interface for custom provider adapters
future_scope.rationale: Roadmap items beyond initial SOC 2 scope

constraints.value: Must be installable with pip install, zero C extension dependencies, Python 3.10+ compatibility
constraints.rationale: Target analytics environments often cannot compile native extensions

observability_baseline.value: Structured audit events emitted via Python logging with configurable handlers; Prometheus metrics export via optional grafana-prometheus extra
observability_baseline.rationale: Consumers integrate SDK telemetry into their own monitoring stacks

execution_preference.value: Trunk-based development with semantic versioning and changelog-driven releases
execution_preference.rationale: Partner SDK consumers require stable release channels

compliance_context.value: SOC 2 Type II — SDK distributed to enterprise sports analytics customers requiring vendor security attestation; CC6 and CC7 controls apply to signing key management
compliance_context.rationale: Enterprise customer contracts require SOC 2 report

project.goal: Provide enterprise sports analytics teams a secure, rate-limited Python SDK for Sportradar and Stats Perform access

backend.api: Public SDK API surface with typed dataclasses

security.auth: HMAC-SHA256 signed tokens, OS keyring for credential storage
security.transport: TLS 1.3 for all upstream provider requests

data.db: None (stateless); optional Redis for distributed rate-limit counters

devops.ci: GitHub Actions with tox multi-version matrix

reasoner_reviews.product: SDK feature scope is well-bounded for SOC 2 attestation timeline. Secret rotation utilities are essential for enterprise adoption — key rotation without downtime is a hard requirement from the two largest prospective customers.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Pure Python with httpx is the correct choice for broad environment compatibility. The optional Redis sidecar for distributed rate-limit state is a clean design that keeps the default installation simple. Recommend the audit log emission follow the Python logging handler interface so consumers can route to their existing SIEM.
reasoner_reviews.security: HMAC-SHA256 signing is appropriate. The OS keyring integration must use the keyring library abstracting platform-specific backends. Plaintext credential files in the user home directory are a previous audit finding that must be explicitly closed in this revision.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Zero C extension dependency constraint is achievable with the cryptography pure-Python wheel. tox matrix should cover Python 3.10, 3.11, and 3.12 as the supported range.
reasoner_reviews.critic: Round 2 plan failed to specify key derivation for token signing — raw HMAC with the master key is insufficient; derive per-consumer subkeys using HKDF. This must be corrected.

backend.service_design: SDK structured as three public modules: SportsDataClient (main entry point), TokenManager (signing and validation), RateLimiter (per-consumer budget enforcement). Internal ProviderAdapter protocol allows Sportradar and Stats Perform adapters to share retry and timeout logic.
backend.api_patterns: Public API surface: SportsDataClient(credential_path).get_fixtures(sport, date), .get_live_scores(event_id), .rotate_secret(new_key). TokenManager.issue(consumer_id, scopes, ttl) returns signed JWT. All public functions are async-native with sync wrappers.
backend.failure_handling: httpx transport retries with exponential backoff (max 3 attempts, base 0.5s). RateLimitExceededError raised with retry_after field. ProviderUnavailableError wraps upstream 5xx responses.
backend.backend_risks: HKDF subkey derivation adds latency on first token issue per consumer session — mitigated by in-process subkey cache with TTL matching token lifetime.

security.auth_design: Master credential loaded from OS keyring via keyring library. HKDF-SHA256 derives per-consumer subkeys from master key and consumer_id. HMAC-SHA256 signs token payload. Tokens expire after configurable TTL (default 3600s).
security.secrets_management: Secret rotation: generate new master key, re-derive all active consumer subkeys, write new credential to keyring, invalidate old tokens after grace_period_seconds (default 300s). Grace period prevents service interruption during rolling deploys.
security.audit_and_logging_controls: AuditEvent dataclass emitted to sdk.audit Python logger: fields include timestamp, consumer_id, action (token_issued/request_proxied/rate_limited/secret_rotated), provider, endpoint, status_code, latency_ms. Consumer configures handlers.
security.security_risks: Keyring unavailable on headless CI environments — SDK falls back to encrypted .credentials file with consumer-supplied passphrase; this fallback must be documented as lower security posture.

data.entities: api_credentials (consumer_id, hashed_key_id, scopes[], created_at, expires_at), signed_tokens (token_id, consumer_id, issued_at, expires_at, scopes[], revoked), rate_limit_configs (consumer_id, endpoint_pattern, requests_per_minute, burst_allowance), audit_log_entries (event_id, consumer_id, action, provider, endpoint, status_code, latency_ms, timestamp), secret_versions (version_id, algorithm, created_at, retired_at, grace_period_seconds)
data.schema_notes: All entities are in-process dataclasses or optional Redis hashes — no relational database required for standard deployment.
data.data_risks: Rate-limit state loss on process restart resets consumer budgets — document this behavior; recommend Redis sidecar for production deployments with burst-sensitive SLAs.

ISS-001.title: Raw HMAC signing without key derivation
ISS-001.detail: Round 2 plan used the master credential key directly as the HMAC signing key. This means a token forgery against one consumer could be used to derive the master key material. HKDF subkey derivation per consumer_id is required.

history.note: First identified in round 1 audit
Plan acknowledged risk but did not implement HKDF

ISS-002.title: Plaintext credential fallback not documented as reduced security posture
ISS-002.detail: The fallback to encrypted .credentials file for headless environments was introduced in round 2 but lacks explicit documentation that this mode does not meet SOC 2 CC6 requirements without additional controls.

history.note: Introduced with headless CI fallback

ISS-003.title: Grace period not enforced during concurrent token issuance
ISS-003.detail: Round 2 plan added grace_period_seconds but did not address concurrent token issuance during rotation window. Resolved in round 2 by adding a rotation_lock mutex in TokenManager.

focus_issues.title: Raw HMAC signing without key derivation
focus_issues.detail: Master key used directly for HMAC; HKDF per-consumer subkey derivation must be implemented to prevent key material inference.

focus_issues.title: Plaintext credential fallback undocumented security posture
focus_issues.detail: Headless CI fallback to encrypted file must be flagged in SDK documentation and README as not SOC-2-CC6-compliant without supplemental controls.

latest_recommendations: Implement HKDF-SHA256 subkey derivation per consumer_id before signing tokens
Add explicit security-posture warnings for headless credential fallback in package README and docs
Add mypy strict mode to CI tox matrix

convergence_state.reason: Critical signing key derivation issue unresolved across two rounds

previous_audits.summary: SDK architecture is sound but the critical HMAC key derivation flaw renders the security model non-compliant with SOC 2 CC6. The credential fallback gap is a secondary blocker for enterprise distribution.
strengths: Clean three-module SDK structure with well-defined public API surface
Grace period rotation mutex correctly handles concurrent issuance
concerns: HMAC signing uses raw master key — token forgery could expose key material
Headless credential fallback has no documented security posture warning
blocking_issues: ISS-001: HKDF subkey derivation not implemented — SOC 2 CC6 non-compliant
ISS-002: Fallback mode lacks security posture documentation
recommendations: Use HKDF(master_key, salt=consumer_id, info=b'token-signing') to derive per-consumer subkeys
Add SECURITY.md and README warning block for headless fallback mode
Add mypy strict to tox CI matrix for type safety across Python 3.10-3.12

issue_updates.title: Raw HMAC signing

issue_updates.title: Fallback documentation gap

strengths: Provider adapter protocol enables Sportradar and Stats Perform to share retry logic
In-process rate-limit budget design is appropriate for default deployment
concerns: No key derivation strategy defined
Concurrent rotation race condition unaddressed

ISS-003: Concurrent token issuance during rotation not protected
recommendations: Adopt HKDF-SHA256 for per-consumer subkey derivation
Add rotation_lock mutex to TokenManager

issue_updates.detail: No subkey derivation in token issuance path
