previous_plan.title: Sports Analytics Data SDK v2
previous_plan.executive_summary: Python 3.12 SDK providing signed data access tokens and rate-limited proxy requests to Sportradar and Stats Perform. HMAC-SHA256 signing, OS keyring credential storage, and secret rotation utilities target SOC 2 CC6 compliance.
previous_plan.architecture_overview: Three-module SDK: SportsDataClient as entry point, TokenManager for HMAC signing using master credential, RateLimiter for per-consumer budget. Provider adapters for Sportradar and Stats Perform share httpx retry logic.
previous_plan.security_and_compliance: HMAC-SHA256 signing with master credential loaded from OS keyring. Rotation adds grace_period_seconds with mutex. Headless fallback to encrypted .credentials file.
previous_plan.data_model: In-process dataclasses for api_credentials, signed_tokens, rate_limit_configs, audit_log_entries. Optional Redis hashes for distributed rate-limit state.

target_output.thinking_summary: This round 3 revision must resolve the critical HMAC key derivation flaw (ISS-001) by introducing HKDF-SHA256 per-consumer subkey derivation, and document the headless credential fallback security posture (ISS-002). The flawed plan_quality means I should also introduce a subtle structural inconsistency — the deployment_and_operations section will incorrectly reference a hosted Grafana dashboard as if the SDK runs as a service, conflicting with the library_sdk project class and PyPI hosting target.

fix_report.action_taken: Replaced direct master-key HMAC with HKDF-SHA256 subkey derivation: TokenManager.issue() now calls HKDF(algorithm=SHA256, length=32, salt=consumer_id.encode(), info=b'sportradar-token-signing').derive(master_key) before computing HMAC. Subkeys are cached in-process with TTL equal to token lifetime.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Per-consumer subkeys mean compromise of one consumer token cannot expose master key material; SOC 2 CC6 signing control satisfied

fix_report.action_taken: Added explicit SECURITY_POSTURE flag emitted as WARNING log when SDK initializes with file-based credential fallback, and documented in README that headless mode does not satisfy SOC 2 CC6 without supplemental filesystem encryption controls.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Enterprise customers are clearly informed of reduced security posture in headless environments; SOC 2 auditors can verify the disclosure

target_output.title: Sports Analytics Data SDK v3
target_output.executive_summary: A PyPI-distributed Python 3.12 SDK enabling enterprise sports analytics teams to securely proxy authenticated requests to Sportradar and Stats Perform. Signing tokens are derived via HKDF-SHA256 per-consumer subkeys from a master credential stored in the OS keyring, with per-consumer rate-limit budgets enforced in-process and structured audit events emitted to consumer-configured logging sinks. Secret rotation utilities handle key rollover with a configurable grace period and mutex-protected concurrent issuance.
target_output.architecture_overview: Three-module package architecture: SportsDataClient as the public entry point managing credential lifecycle and provider selection; TokenManager implementing HKDF-SHA256 subkey derivation and HMAC token issuance with in-process subkey cache; RateLimiter enforcing per-consumer request budgets against configurable endpoint patterns with optional Redis sidecar for distributed deployments. Provider adapters for Sportradar (/v2/sports/* REST) and Stats Perform (DataAPI HTTPS) share an httpx AsyncTransport layer with retry logic and TLS 1.3 pinning. Audit events flow through Python logging to consumer-configured handlers — no SDK-owned persistence layer.
target_output.technology_stack: Runtime: Python 3.12, compatible with Python 3.10+. HTTP: httpx 0.27 async client with custom transport for retry and timeout. Cryptography: cryptography library (pure-Python wheel) for HKDF-SHA256 subkey derivation and HMAC-SHA256 token signing. Credential storage: keyring library abstracting macOS Keychain, Windows DPAPI, and Linux SecretService. Optional: redis-py for distributed rate-limit state. Testing: pytest with pytest-asyncio; tox matrix covering Python 3.10, 3.11, 3.12. CI: GitHub Actions.
target_output.functional_feature_map: SportsDataClient.get_fixtures(sport, competition_id, date_range) — issues signed token, checks rate budget, proxies to Sportradar /v2/sports/{sport}/fixtures. SportsDataClient.get_live_scores(event_id) — proxies to Stats Perform DataAPI /scores/live. TokenManager.issue(consumer_id, scopes, ttl) — HKDF subkey derivation + HMAC-SHA256 signing. TokenManager.rotate_secret(new_master_key, grace_period_seconds) — re-derives subkeys, invalidates old tokens after grace period with mutex. RateLimiter.check_and_consume(consumer_id, endpoint) — in-process token bucket or Redis INCR. AuditEvent emission on every proxy call, token issuance, rate-limit event, and secret rotation.
target_output.system_components: SportsDataClient (public entry point, credential loading, provider routing), TokenManager (HKDF subkey derivation, HMAC signing, token cache, rotation mutex), RateLimiter (per-consumer token bucket, optional Redis sidecar adapter), SportradarAdapter (httpx client, /v2/sports/* endpoint mapping, retry with exponential backoff), StatsPerformAdapter (httpx client, DataAPI endpoint mapping), AuditEmitter (structured AuditEvent dataclass, Python logger integration), CredentialLoader (keyring primary, encrypted-file fallback with WARNING emission).
target_output.workflows: Token Issuance and Request Proxying: Consumer calls SportsDataClient.get_fixtures(); CredentialLoader retrieves master key from OS keyring; TokenManager derives consumer subkey via HKDF(SHA256, salt=consumer_id, info=b'sportradar-token-signing'); HMAC-SHA256 signs payload with derived subkey; RateLimiter checks in-process token bucket for consumer_id/endpoint pattern; SportradarAdapter sends request with Authorization: Bearer <token> over TLS 1.3; AuditEmitter logs request_proxied event with latency_ms and status_code. Secret Rotation: caller invokes TokenManager.rotate_secret(new_master_key, grace_period_seconds=300); rotation_lock mutex acquired; HKDF re-derives all cached consumer subkeys from new master; old tokens remain valid until grace period expires; keyring updated with new master key; AuditEmitter logs secret_rotated event; mutex released.
target_output.data_model: api_credentials (consumer_id: str, hashed_key_id: str, scopes: list[str], created_at: datetime, expires_at: datetime | None) — in-process dataclass loaded from keyring. signed_tokens (token_id: UUID, consumer_id: str, issued_at: datetime, expires_at: datetime, scopes: list[str], revoked: bool, subkey_version: int) — in-process token cache dict keyed by token_id. rate_limit_configs (consumer_id: str, endpoint_pattern: str, requests_per_minute: int, burst_allowance: int) — loaded from credential config file. audit_log_entries (event_id: UUID, consumer_id: str, action: Literal['token_issued','request_proxied','rate_limited','secret_rotated'], provider: str, endpoint: str, status_code: int | None, latency_ms: float, timestamp: datetime). secret_versions (version_id: int, algorithm: str, created_at: datetime, retired_at: datetime | None, grace_period_seconds: int).
target_output.api_design: Public SDK API (not HTTP): SportsDataClient(credential_path=None) — initializes from keyring or file fallback. async get_fixtures(sport: str, competition_id: str, date_range: tuple[date, date]) -> list[MatchFixture]. async get_live_scores(event_id: str) -> LiveScoreResponse. TokenManager.issue(consumer_id: str, scopes: list[str], ttl: int = 3600) -> SignedToken. TokenManager.rotate_secret(new_master_key: bytes, grace_period_seconds: int = 300) -> RotationResult. RateLimiter.check_and_consume(consumer_id: str, endpoint: str) -> RateLimitResult. All types are dataclasses with __slots__ for memory efficiency.
target_output.security_and_compliance: SOC 2 CC6 controls: HKDF-SHA256 per-consumer subkey derivation (HKDF(SHA256, length=32, salt=consumer_id.encode(), info=b'sportradar-token-signing').derive(master_key)) prevents master key inference from token analysis. HMAC-SHA256 token signing using derived subkey. Master credential stored in OS keyring (keyring library) — macOS Keychain, Windows DPAPI, Linux SecretService. TLS 1.3 enforced for all upstream requests via httpx ssl_context pinning. Headless fallback: encrypted .credentials file using Fernet(PBKDF2(passphrase)) — SDK emits WARNING log 'SECURITY_POSTURE: file-based credential does not satisfy SOC 2 CC6 without supplemental filesystem encryption'. Audit log emitted for all token issuance, rotation, rate-limit, and proxy events.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Distribution: pip install sports-analytics-sdk published to PyPI. GitHub Actions CI: tox matrix across Python 3.10/3.11/3.12 running pytest, mypy --strict, bandit security scan, and pip-audit for dependency CVEs. Release pipeline: bumpversion tags semantic version, GitHub Release created, twine uploads to PyPI. Consumer deployment: SDK is a library with no hosted runtime — consumers embed in their analytics application and configure credential storage. Optional Redis sidecar: consumers set SPORTS_SDK_REDIS_URL env var to enable distributed rate-limit state. NOTE: This section incorrectly references a 'Grafana dashboard deployed alongside the SDK' — the SDK is a PyPI library and has no hosted dashboard; this is a plan inconsistency.
target_output.observability: Audit events emitted via Python logging to sdk.audit logger with structlog-compatible JSON format: fields include timestamp, consumer_id, action, provider, endpoint, status_code, latency_ms. Prometheus metrics available via optional sports-analytics-sdk[prometheus] extra: sdk_requests_total (provider, status), sdk_token_issuance_total, sdk_rate_limit_rejections_total, sdk_rotation_events_total. Consumers configure handlers to ship to their Datadog, Splunk, or ELK stacks.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: No SDK-side infrastructure cost — all execution is in consumer process. Optional Redis sidecar for distributed rate limits adds ~$15/month for ElastiCache t4g.micro. PyPI bandwidth is free. Per-call HKDF derivation adds ~0.2ms latency vs raw HMAC; in-process subkey cache eliminates repeat derivation cost within token TTL.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 (complete): SportsDataClient, SportradarAdapter, TokenManager with HKDF subkey derivation, in-process RateLimiter, AuditEmitter — published as v1.0 to PyPI. Phase 2 (current, round 3): Fix ISS-001 HKDF implementation, add ISS-002 security posture warning for file fallback, add mypy strict to tox, publish v1.1 patch. Phase 3: Stats Perform adapter, Redis sidecar RateLimiter adapter, plugin interface for custom provider adapters, OAuth 2.0 PKCE flow.
target_output.development_guidelines: mypy --strict enforced in tox CI across Python 3.10-3.12. pytest with 90% branch coverage gate. bandit for SAST and pip-audit for dependency CVE scanning in every CI run. No credentials committed to repo — pre-commit hook blocks .credentials files and HMAC key patterns. Semantic versioning with CHANGELOG.md updated per release. All public API surface documented with docstrings and type annotations.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: OS keyring unavailable in containerized CI environments. Mitigation: file-based fallback with explicit SOC-2 posture warning; document that headless deployments require supplemental disk encryption. Risk: HKDF subkey cache grows unboundedly for large consumer populations. Mitigation: LRU cache with configurable max_consumers parameter (default 1000). Tradeoff: Pure-Python cryptography wheel over C extension for zero-dependency install — adds ~0.5ms per HKDF call vs native; acceptable for token issuance frequency. Risk: Sportradar API schema changes break adapter — mitigate with Pydantic model versioning and adapter-level schema validation tests.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Selected HKDF-SHA256 over PBKDF2 for subkey derivation — HKDF is purpose-built for key derivation from existing key material, whereas PBKDF2 is for password-based derivation. Confirmed keyring library as the abstraction layer for cross-platform credential storage. Chose httpx over requests for async-native HTTP with sync wrapper compatibility.

quality_flags: intentional_plan_inconsistency: deployment_and_operations references hosted Grafana dashboard for a PyPI library
metadata.notes: Round 3 revision of sports analytics Python SDK; fixes HKDF signing flaw and credential posture gap; intentional flaw in deployment section references non-existent hosted dashboard.

project.goal: Social real-time GPS running app with audio coaching and live follower map
project.target_users: Recreational runners on iOS and Android

frontend.state: Zustand for run session state, TanStack Query for API data

backend.api: REST + WebSocket; Firebase ID token validation middleware

security.auth: Firebase Auth ID token validation
security.gps_access: Runner + explicit followers only via friendship check

data.cache: Redis 7 for live run session state and WS connection registry

devops.app_build: Expo EAS Build for iOS and Android

constraints.battery: GPS tracking optimized for 90+ minute runs

reasoner_reviews.product: Live shared map view is the social differentiator. The follower map must update smoothly without requiring followers to have the app open — push notifications on run start are important. Achievement unlocks should be evaluated server-side after run upload, not client-side, to prevent manipulation.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: WebSocket GPS streaming to followers is the correct real-time pattern. The round 1 plan stored GPS coordinates in PostgreSQL during the run — this is wrong for latency and scalability. Active run GPS state should be in Redis only (HSET run:{run_id} lat lng pace), with final track written to PostgreSQL at run completion. Cloud Run WebSocket support requires min-instances=1 to avoid cold start disconnecting active run sessions. Fastify @fastify/websocket uses ws under the hood — verify ws v8 for Node 22 compatibility.
reasoner_reviews.security: GPS track visibility must be enforced at the API query level — SELECT WHERE runner_id = requester_id OR requester_id IN (friend_connections WHERE status=accepted). The GDPR deletion endpoint must cascade to gps_tracks, heart_rate_samples, and run_sessions tables. Round 1 did not implement the deletion cascade correctly.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Expo Background Location task with BACKGROUND_LOCATION permission for iOS and ACCESS_BACKGROUND_LOCATION for Android handles cellular handoff reconnection. GPS sample rate at 1 Hz balances battery and track accuracy for recreational running pace.

frontend.app_structure: Expo managed workflow, React Native with TypeScript. Navigation: React Navigation v6 stack and tab navigators. Run tracking: Expo Location background task at 1Hz GPS sampling, Zustand runSessionStore for in-progress run state.
frontend.pages_and_flows: Home (feed, friend activity), Run Screen (active GPS map, pace/HR display, audio cue trigger), Run Summary (post-run stats, map replay, achievement unlocks), Friend Map (live positions of friends on active runs), Profile (run history, lifetime stats, achievement badges), Settings (GDPR data export/delete).
frontend.state_management: Zustand runSessionStore: {isRunning, startTime, gpsTrack, currentPace, currentHrZone, audioQueue}. TanStack Query for run history, friend list, achievements.
frontend.frontend_risks: Background GPS on iOS requires NSLocationAlwaysAndWhenInUseUsageDescription and background modes: location in Info.plist — missing this blocks App Store review.

backend.service_design: Fastify with two route namespaces: /api/v1 (REST for run CRUD, user management, friends) and /ws/v1 (WebSocket for live GPS streaming). Redis stores active run sessions; PostgreSQL stores completed run history.
backend.api_patterns: REST: POST /api/v1/runs (start), POST /api/v1/runs/:id/complete (finalize and upload GPS track), GET /api/v1/runs/:id, GET /api/v1/users/:id/runs. WebSocket: WS /ws/v1/runs/:id/stream (runner publishes GPS; followers subscribe). Firebase ID token in Authorization header.
backend.background_jobs: Post-run processor: on run completion, compute total_distance, avg_pace, elevation_gain from GPS track, evaluate achievement conditions, send FCM push notifications for unlocks and to followers.
backend.backend_risks: Cloud Run cold start during active WebSocket run — min-instances: 1 required on WebSocket-serving service.

security.auth_design: Firebase ID token validated via firebase-admin SDK on every request. JWT claims include uid (Firebase user ID) mapped to runner_profiles.firebase_uid.
security.authorization_model: GPS track visibility: SELECT only if gps_tracks.runner_id = req.uid OR EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM friend_connections WHERE (requester_id=req.uid OR addressee_id=req.uid) AND status='accepted' AND the other party = track owner). GDPR deletion: DELETE cascade from runner_profiles triggers cascade to run_sessions, gps_tracks, heart_rate_samples, achievements, friend_connections.
security.privacy_controls: GDPR deletion endpoint: DELETE /api/v1/users/:id triggers soft-delete flag, enqueues background job to hard-delete all GPS and personal data within 30 days, sends confirmation email via SendGrid.

data.entities: runner_profiles (id UUID PK, firebase_uid, display_name, email, bio, avatar_url, total_runs, total_distance_m, created_at, deleted_at). run_sessions (id UUID PK, runner_id FK, status ENUM active/completed/abandoned, started_at, completed_at, total_distance_m, avg_pace_sec_per_km, elevation_gain_m, max_hr_bpm). gps_tracks (id UUID PK, run_id FK, recorded_at TIMESTAMP, lat DOUBLE, lng DOUBLE, altitude_m DOUBLE, accuracy_m FLOAT, speed_mps FLOAT). heart_rate_samples (id UUID PK, run_id FK, recorded_at TIMESTAMP, bpm INT, hr_zone INT 1-5). achievements (id UUID PK, runner_id FK, achievement_type VARCHAR, achieved_at TIMESTAMP, context_json). friend_connections (id UUID PK, requester_id FK, addressee_id FK, status ENUM pending/accepted/rejected, created_at). coaching_cue_events (id UUID PK, run_id FK, triggered_at TIMESTAMP, cue_type VARCHAR, trigger_condition VARCHAR).
data.retention_and_deletion: GPS tracks and heart_rate_samples purged on account deletion cascade. Active run GPS state in Redis expires automatically when run completes (TTL 4 hours).

devops.deployment_topology: Cloud Run service: fastify-api (min 1 instance, max 20, 2 vCPU/4GB). Cloud SQL PostgreSQL 16 in private VPC. Redis on Memorystore. Expo EAS Build for iOS (TestFlight) and Android (Google Play internal track).
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: ESLint, TypeScript tsc, vitest unit tests, integration tests with pg-mem and redis-mock. Cloud Run deploy via gcloud run deploy on main merge. Expo EAS build triggered on release branch tag.
devops.observability_stack: Prometheus metrics from Fastify prom-client plugin: http_requests_total, ws_connections_active, run_sessions_active. Grafana dashboard: active_ws_sessions, run_completions_per_hour, avg_gps_upload_latency. Sentry for React Native crash reporting.

ISS-001.title: GPS coordinates written to PostgreSQL during active run causing latency
ISS-001.detail: Round 1 plan inserted each GPS coordinate into gps_tracks PostgreSQL table in real time during the run. At 1Hz sampling over a 60-minute run this creates 3600 INSERT operations per run session, causing write contention and increasing run-start to first-GPS-point API latency to >500ms on Cloud SQL shared instance.

history.note: Identified in load test simulating 50 concurrent active run sessions

ISS-002.title: GDPR deletion cascade missing gps_tracks and heart_rate_samples
ISS-002.detail: Round 1 deletion endpoint deleted runner_profiles row but PostgreSQL foreign key CASCADE was not set on gps_tracks and heart_rate_samples tables, leaving orphaned personal GPS data after account deletion.

history.note: Found in GDPR compliance review

focus_issues.title: Real-time GPS write to PostgreSQL during run causing latency
focus_issues.detail: GPS coordinates must be buffered in Redis during active run, written to PostgreSQL only on run completion.

focus_issues.title: GDPR deletion missing GPS and heart-rate cascade
focus_issues.detail: gps_tracks and heart_rate_samples must have ON DELETE CASCADE from run_sessions, which cascades from runner_profiles deletion.

latest_recommendations: Buffer GPS coordinates in Redis LPUSH run:{run_id}:track during active run; batch-write to PostgreSQL gps_tracks on POST /runs/:id/complete
Add ON DELETE CASCADE to gps_tracks and heart_rate_samples foreign keys referencing run_sessions; verify CASCADE chain from runner_profiles -> run_sessions -> gps_tracks
Add Cloud Run min-instances: 1 annotation to WebSocket service deployment spec

convergence_state.reason: GPS write latency and GDPR cascade gap are both blocking issues preventing launch

previous_audits.summary: Solid mobile app architecture with correct Firebase Auth and Redis fanout, but real-time PostgreSQL GPS writes are a scalability blocker and the GDPR deletion cascade is incomplete, creating a compliance gap.
strengths: Firebase Auth ID token validation pattern is correct and Fastify integration is clean
Redis pub/sub for follower map WebSocket fanout is the right architecture
Expo background location task setup is detailed and iOS permission requirements called out
concerns: GPS coordinate direct PostgreSQL writes at 1Hz create write pressure blocking multiple concurrent sessions
GDPR deletion does not cascade to gps_tracks and heart_rate_samples — orphaned personal data remains after account deletion
blocking_issues: ISS-001: PostgreSQL GPS writes at 1Hz latency blocks concurrent session scale
ISS-002: GDPR deletion cascade incomplete — compliance blocker for EU launch
recommendations: Use Redis LPUSH run:{run_id}:track for GPS buffering during run; batch COPY to PostgreSQL on completion
Add ON DELETE CASCADE foreign key constraints on gps_tracks and heart_rate_samples
Set Cloud Run min-instances=1 on WebSocket service to prevent cold start session drops

issue_updates.title: PostgreSQL GPS write latency
issue_updates.detail: POST /api/v1/gps-point inserts to gps_tracks table on each GPS sample — 3600 inserts per 60-min session

issue_updates.title: GDPR cascade missing
issue_updates.detail: DELETE FROM runner_profiles leaves gps_tracks and heart_rate_samples rows orphaned

previous_plan.title: PaceTrack Running App v1
previous_plan.executive_summary: React Native Expo running app with Firebase Auth, Fastify WebSocket backend on Cloud Run, PostgreSQL for run history, Redis for live session state and follower fanout.
previous_plan.architecture_overview: Expo app streams GPS at 1Hz to Fastify backend. Each GPS point inserted into PostgreSQL gps_tracks in real time. Redis pub/sub fans GPS positions to followers. Post-run achievement evaluation and FCM push notification dispatch.
previous_plan.security_and_compliance: Firebase Auth ID tokens validated on all requests. GPS tracks visible to runner and accepted friends. GDPR deletion deletes runner_profiles row.
previous_plan.data_model: runner_profiles, run_sessions, gps_tracks (real-time PostgreSQL inserts), heart_rate_samples, achievements, friend_connections.

target_output.thinking_summary: Round 2 must fix GPS coordinate buffering (ISS-001) by switching to Redis LPUSH during active run with bulk PostgreSQL COPY on completion, and add the missing ON DELETE CASCADE to gps_tracks and heart_rate_samples (ISS-002). Cloud Run min-instances annotation must be added to prevent WebSocket cold starts. Moderate plan quality — strong on most sections with one or two lighter areas.

fix_report.action_taken: Replaced per-sample PostgreSQL INSERT with Redis LPUSH run:{run_id}:track:{lat}:{lng}:{ts} at each GPS point during active run. POST /api/v1/runs/:id/complete triggers LRANGE to retrieve full track, bulk-inserts to PostgreSQL gps_tracks via pg COPY command in a single transaction. Redis key expires at TTL 6 hours as failsafe.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Active run write load eliminated from PostgreSQL; bulk COPY on completion is ~50x faster than 3600 individual INSERTs; concurrent session scale increases from ~10 to >100 simultaneous runs on shared Cloud SQL instance

fix_report.action_taken: Added ON DELETE CASCADE to gps_tracks (FOREIGN KEY run_id REFERENCES run_sessions ON DELETE CASCADE) and heart_rate_samples (same pattern). Added CASCADE from runner_profiles to run_sessions (ON DELETE CASCADE). GDPR deletion endpoint now deletes runner_profiles row, triggering cascade to run_sessions -> gps_tracks and heart_rate_samples automatically. Added integration test asserting zero orphaned rows after deletion.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Account deletion removes all GPS and biometric data through FK cascade; GDPR Article 17 compliance satisfied; integration test prevents regression

target_output.title: PaceTrack Running App v2
target_output.executive_summary: A React Native Expo mobile app for recreational runners delivering real-time GPS-tracked runs with audio coaching cues, live shared map view for friends, and social achievement notifications. The Fastify backend on GCP Cloud Run handles Firebase Auth token validation, WebSocket GPS streaming with Redis pub/sub follower fanout, and post-run bulk GPS track persistence to PostgreSQL. Active run GPS coordinates are buffered in Redis during the run and batch-inserted via PostgreSQL COPY on completion, eliminating write pressure during concurrent sessions. GDPR deletion cascades through the full data model to remove all GPS and biometric data.
target_output.architecture_overview: Three-tier mobile architecture: Expo React Native app communicates over HTTPS and WebSocket to a Fastify Node.js backend on Cloud Run (min-instances: 1). Firebase Auth issues ID tokens validated by firebase-admin SDK on every request. During active runs, GPS points are buffered in Redis (LPUSH run:{run_id}:track) and simultaneously published to Redis pub/sub channel run:{run_id}:followers for real-time follower map updates. On run completion, the Fastify handler performs LRANGE on the Redis track buffer and bulk-inserts to PostgreSQL gps_tracks via COPY. Cloud SQL PostgreSQL 16 stores persistent run history, friend connections, and achievements. Memorystore Redis 7 manages ephemeral run state and WebSocket connection registry.
target_output.technology_stack: Mobile: React Native TypeScript with Expo managed workflow, React Navigation v6, Zustand, TanStack Query, react-native-maps (Google Maps SDK), Expo Location background task. Backend: Node.js 22, Fastify v4 with @fastify/websocket, firebase-admin SDK, pg driver with COPY stream. Database: PostgreSQL 16 Cloud SQL (private VPC). Cache/pub-sub: Redis 7 Memorystore. Push: Firebase Cloud Messaging. Auth: Firebase Auth with Google Sign-In. CI: GitHub Actions. Hosting: Cloud Run (min-instances: 1 on WS service), Expo EAS Build.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Active Run Screen: Expo Location background task samples GPS at 1Hz; React Native map renders live route polyline; Zustand runSessionStore tracks pace and HR zone; audio coaching module triggers cue when pace deviates from target zone. Follower Live Map: followers subscribe to WebSocket WS /ws/v1/runs/:id/stream receiving lat/lng frames every 5 seconds aggregated from 1Hz Redis buffer. Post-Run Upload: app POSTs run completion; Fastify retrieves Redis GPS buffer, COPY-inserts to gps_tracks, computes distance/pace/elevation, evaluates achievement conditions, dispatches FCM push. Friend System: send/accept/reject friend requests; accepted friends can view each other's live and historical runs. GDPR Controls: DELETE /api/v1/users/:id triggers deletion cascade through runner_profiles -> run_sessions -> gps_tracks, heart_rate_samples; confirmed by integration test.
target_output.system_components: Expo React Native App (GPS background task, audio cue module, WebSocket GPS publisher, map renderer), Fastify API Server (REST routes, WebSocket handler, Firebase token middleware, COPY inserter), Redis Memorystore (LPUSH GPS buffer per run, pub/sub follower channel, active run registry HSET), PostgreSQL Cloud SQL (persistent run history, friend graph, achievements), Firebase Auth (ID token issuance and validation), Firebase Cloud Messaging (milestone and activity push notifications), GCP Cloud Run (serverless hosting with min-instances: 1), Expo EAS Build (iOS TestFlight and Android Play Store distribution).
target_output.workflows: Active Run GPS Buffering: Runner starts run; Expo Location background task fires every 1 second; app sends GPS frame to Fastify WS /ws/v1/runs/:id/stream; Fastify handler calls LPUSH run:{run_id}:track '{lat},{lng},{ts}' and PUBLISH run:{run_id}:followers '{lat},{lng}' to Redis; followers subscribed to the channel receive position updates aggregated every 5 seconds on their Friend Map. Run Completion and Bulk Persistence: Runner taps Stop; app POSTs to POST /api/v1/runs/:id/complete; Fastify calls LRANGE run:{run_id}:track 0 -1 (full buffer), streams records via pg CopyFrom to gps_tracks table in single transaction; computes total_distance, avg_pace, elevation_gain; evaluates achievement conditions (first_5k, streak_7day, pb_10k); sends FCM push to runner and followers; DEL Redis track buffer. Account Deletion GDPR Flow: User taps Delete Account; DELETE /api/v1/users/:id; Fastify deletes runner_profiles row; FK CASCADE deletes run_sessions -> gps_tracks, heart_rate_samples; soft-delete flag set for audit; background job verifies zero orphaned rows after 30 days and sends GDPR deletion confirmation email.
target_output.data_model: runner_profiles (id UUID PK, firebase_uid VARCHAR UNIQUE, display_name VARCHAR, email VARCHAR, bio TEXT, avatar_url VARCHAR, total_runs INT DEFAULT 0, total_distance_m BIGINT DEFAULT 0, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ, deleted_at TIMESTAMPTZ nullable). run_sessions (id UUID PK, runner_id UUID REFERENCES runner_profiles ON DELETE CASCADE, status VARCHAR, started_at TIMESTAMPTZ, completed_at TIMESTAMPTZ nullable, total_distance_m INT, avg_pace_sec_per_km INT, elevation_gain_m INT, max_hr_bpm INT). gps_tracks (id UUID PK, run_id UUID REFERENCES run_sessions ON DELETE CASCADE, recorded_at TIMESTAMPTZ, lat DOUBLE PRECISION, lng DOUBLE PRECISION, altitude_m DOUBLE PRECISION, accuracy_m REAL, speed_mps REAL). heart_rate_samples (id UUID PK, run_id UUID REFERENCES run_sessions ON DELETE CASCADE, recorded_at TIMESTAMPTZ, bpm INT, hr_zone INT). achievements (id UUID PK, runner_id UUID REFERENCES runner_profiles ON DELETE CASCADE, achievement_type VARCHAR, achieved_at TIMESTAMPTZ, context_json JSONB). friend_connections (id UUID PK, requester_id UUID REFERENCES runner_profiles ON DELETE CASCADE, addressee_id UUID REFERENCES runner_profiles ON DELETE CASCADE, status VARCHAR, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ). coaching_cue_events (id UUID PK, run_id UUID REFERENCES run_sessions ON DELETE CASCADE, triggered_at TIMESTAMPTZ, cue_type VARCHAR, trigger_condition VARCHAR).
target_output.api_design: All REST endpoints require Authorization: Bearer <firebase_id_token>. POST /api/v1/runs -> {run_id, started_at} (starts run, creates run_sessions row, registers in Redis active run registry). POST /api/v1/runs/:id/complete {gps_track: [], hr_samples: [], final_stats} -> {run_id, total_distance, achievements_unlocked[]}. GET /api/v1/runs/:id -> RunSummaryResponse with GPS track and stats. GET /api/v1/users/:id/runs?page=1 -> paginated run history. POST /api/v1/friends/:target_id -> friend request. PATCH /api/v1/friends/:connection_id {action: accept|reject}. GET /api/v1/friends/active-runs -> [{runner_id, run_id, last_lat, last_lng}] for Friend Map. DELETE /api/v1/users/:id -> GDPR deletion. WS /ws/v1/runs/:id/stream -> bidirectional; runner publishes GPS frames; followers receive aggregated position.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Firebase ID token validation via firebase-admin verifyIdToken() in Fastify preHandler hook. GPS track access authorization: SELECT gps_tracks WHERE run_id IN (SELECT id FROM run_sessions WHERE runner_id = req.uid OR runner_id IN (SELECT friend_uid FROM friend_connections WHERE user_uid=req.uid AND status='accepted')). GDPR deletion: DELETE runner_profiles triggers ON DELETE CASCADE through run_sessions to gps_tracks, heart_rate_samples, achievements, friend_connections. Integration test asserts COUNT(*) = 0 on all tables for deleted user. Privacy policy: GPS data not shared with advertising networks; used only for app features. Expo app store privacy nutrition label specifies location data usage.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Cloud Run: fastify-api service with min-instances: 1 (prevents WebSocket cold start), max-instances: 20, 2 vCPU/4GB memory, VPC connector to Cloud SQL and Memorystore. Cloud SQL PostgreSQL 16 private IP in VPC, automated backups daily, point-in-time recovery enabled. Memorystore Redis 7 standard tier, 4GB. GitHub Actions CI: ESLint, tsc, vitest, integration tests with Docker Compose postgres+redis. Cloud Run deploy via gcloud run deploy on merge to main. Expo EAS Build on release branch tag: eas build --platform all --profile production.
target_output.observability: Prometheus prom-client metrics exported from Fastify: run_sessions_active (gauge), gps_points_buffered_total (counter), ws_connections_active (gauge), run_completion_latency_seconds (histogram), gdpr_deletions_total (counter). Grafana dashboard: active sessions per hour, GPS buffer Redis memory usage, run completion p95 latency. Sentry React Native SDK for mobile crash reporting with stack traces. Alert: ws_connections_active >500 for >5 minutes pages on-call.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Cloud Run fastify-api: ~$80/month base (1 min-instance) scaling to ~$400 at 20 instances during event traffic. Cloud SQL PostgreSQL db-n1-standard-2: ~$150/month. Memorystore Redis 4GB: ~$120/month. Firebase Auth and FCM: free tier. Expo EAS Build: $29/month for concurrent builds. Total: ~$380-700/month at moderate scale. GPS COPY batch insert reduces Cloud SQL write units by ~95% vs per-sample INSERTs.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 (weeks 1-10): Expo app with GPS tracking, run start/complete API, Redis GPS buffer with PostgreSQL COPY on completion (ISS-001 fix), Firebase Auth, basic friend follow, WebSocket GPS stream to followers, GDPR deletion cascade (ISS-002 fix), Cloud Run deployment, GitHub Actions CI. Phase 2 (weeks 11-16): Audio coaching module with pace-zone triggers, achievement evaluation engine, FCM push notifications, Friend Map live view, Expo EAS production builds, TestFlight and Play Store submission. Phase 3: Elevation profile display, segment comparisons, Apple Watch companion workout app.
target_output.development_guidelines: TypeScript strict mode in Expo app and Fastify backend. GDPR cascade integration test required: create user, create runs with GPS points, delete user, assert zero rows remain across all tables. Redis GPS buffer failsafe: if LRANGE returns empty on completion (Redis eviction), app uploads full GPS track in request body as fallback. Vitest coverage >75% for auth middleware, GPS COPY inserter, and achievement evaluator. Sentry source maps uploaded in EAS build pipeline for readable crash reports.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Redis GPS buffer eviction on memory pressure before run completion — mitigation: 6-hour TTL (longer than any practical run), Memorystore max-memory-policy=noeviction for run buffer keys. Risk: WebSocket connection lost during cellular handoff — Expo background task buffers GPS locally and reconnects; server side uses run_id as idempotency key to accept reconnection without creating duplicate session. Risk: Google Maps SDK licensing cost at scale — evaluate OSM/Mapbox as cost alternative beyond 25,000 map loads/month. Tradeoff: Firebase Auth over custom JWT — Firebase simplifies token lifecycle and Google Sign-In but creates vendor dependency for auth; acceptable at this scale.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: GPS buffer in Redis over in-memory app buffer chosen to survive app backgrounding on iOS. COPY over bulk INSERT chosen for batch GPS write — COPY is 5-10x faster for large row counts. ON DELETE CASCADE chosen over application-level cascade for GDPR deletion — database-enforced is more reliable under partial failure. Cloud Run min-instances: 1 justified by WebSocket session continuity requirement.

metadata.notes: Round 2 revision of recreational running GPS app; fixes Redis GPS buffering and GDPR deletion cascade; Expo+Fastify+Cloud Run+PostgreSQL.

project_goal.value: Security-hardened cloud infrastructure for a professional sports organization implementing network segmentation, HashiCorp Vault secrets management, IAM least-privilege policies, and automated container vulnerability scanning for workloads handling player health and contract data
project_goal.rationale: Security audit identified secrets in environment variables, over-provisioned IAM roles, and no vulnerability scanning as critical gaps

target_users.value: Platform engineering team and security operations team managing cloud infrastructure for the sports organization's internal applications
target_users.rationale: Internal engineering and SecOps are the primary operators of this infrastructure

project_class.rationale: Terraform and Ansible IaC project defining platform-level security controls — not an application

capabilities.rationale: Infrastructure automation and platform backend security controls

complexity_level.rationale: Vault agent sidecar injection, Trivy scanning pipeline integration, and IAM policy automation require advanced IaC and Kubernetes expertise

risk_level.rationale: Infrastructure controls protecting player health and contract data; failure would expose confidential sports organization data

data_sensitivity.rationale: Player contract terms, medical records, and negotiation data processed by applications running on this infrastructure

external_exposure.rationale: Infrastructure controls are internal to the sports organization's AWS environment — no external API exposure

access_model.value: Keycloak SSO for human access to AWS console via SAML federation; service accounts use Vault-issued short-lived AWS credentials; no long-lived IAM access keys
access_model.rationale: Eliminates static credential risk for both human and machine access

feature_scope.value: VPC network segmentation with private subnets and security groups per tier, HashiCorp Vault cluster for dynamic secrets and PKI, EKS pod-level IAM via IRSA, Trivy vulnerability scanning in CI/CD with CVSS threshold gating, quarterly IAM access review automation, encrypted EBS and RDS with KMS CMKs, CloudTrail audit logging to S3 with integrity verification
feature_scope.rationale: Complete security hardening scope required for SOC 2 and ISO 27001 audit

mvp_scope.value: VPC segmentation, Vault cluster with agent sidecar injection, IRSA for EKS pods, Trivy scanning pipeline — the four controls addressing the most critical audit findings
mvp_scope.rationale: Addresses all critical and high audit findings within 12 weeks

security_baseline.value: No static IAM access keys; Vault-injected dynamic secrets; EBS and RDS encrypted with KMS CMKs; CloudTrail enabled with log file integrity validation; S3 bucket policies deny public access; EKS API server private endpoint only
security_baseline.rationale: Baseline controls required for SOC 2 CC6 and ISO 27001 A.9

frontend_stack.value: N/A — infrastructure project with no frontend
frontend_stack.rationale: IaC project using Terraform and Ansible

backend_stack.value: Terraform 1.8 with AWS provider 5.x; Ansible 2.16 for Vault cluster configuration; Python 3.12 for IAM review automation scripts
backend_stack.rationale: Team expertise in Terraform for AWS IaC; Ansible for configuration management

data_platform.value: PostgreSQL 16 on RDS Multi-AZ with KMS CMK encryption; EBS gp3 volumes encrypted with KMS CMKs for EKS node groups
data_platform.rationale: Existing data platform hardened with encryption at rest as part of this project

hosting_target.value: AWS EKS (bare-metal-style EKS Managed Node Groups) with self-managed Vault cluster on EC2
hosting_target.rationale: EKS for containerized workloads; self-managed Vault for control plane flexibility

privacy_retention_policy.value: CloudTrail logs retained for 7 years in S3 with Object Lock COMPLIANCE mode; Vault audit logs retained for 3 years; IAM access review reports retained for 5 years
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: ISO 27001 A.12.4 and SOC 2 CC7 log retention requirements

future_scope.value: OPA/Gatekeeper policy enforcement for Kubernetes admission control, SIEM integration for CloudTrail events to Splunk, automated Vault lease renewal monitoring
future_scope.rationale: Year 2 security maturity roadmap items beyond initial audit remediation

constraints.value: All changes deployed via GitLab CI — no manual AWS console changes in production; Vault cluster HA with minimum 3 nodes; EKS API endpoint must remain private
constraints.rationale: Change management controls required for SOC 2 CC8

observability_baseline.value: Datadog agent on EKS nodes and Vault EC2 instances; CloudTrail events forwarded to CloudWatch Logs; Vault audit log forwarded to S3 and Datadog
observability_baseline.rationale: Unified observability for infrastructure and security events

execution_preference.value: Terraform workspaces per environment (dev, staging, prod); GitLab CI pipeline with plan + manual approval gate before apply in prod
execution_preference.rationale: Change management and approval workflow required for SOC 2 CC8

compliance_context.value: SOC 2 Type II and ISO 27001 — sports organization handles player health and contract data requiring formal security attestation; annual audit scope covers CC6 (logical access), CC7 (monitoring), CC8 (change management), A.9 (access control), A.12 (operations security)
compliance_context.rationale: Enterprise partner and player association contracts require SOC 2 and ISO 27001 certification

project.goal: Harden AWS EKS infrastructure with Vault secrets, network segmentation, least-privilege IAM, and container scanning
project.timeline: Critical controls in 12 weeks; full scope in 20 weeks

security.vault: Vault 1.16 HA cluster 3 nodes; agent sidecar injection for all EKS pods
security.iam: IRSA per workload; quarterly automated access review
security.scanning: Trivy in GitLab CI with CVSS>=7 gate blocking prod promotion

data.db: RDS PostgreSQL 16 Multi-AZ with KMS CMK encryption; EBS gp3 KMS CMK
data.audit: CloudTrail + Vault audit logs to S3 with Object Lock COMPLIANCE

devops.hosting: EKS Managed Node Groups + EC2 for Vault
devops.deployment: Terraform workspace per env with manual approval gate for prod

reasoner_reviews.product: Infrastructure hardening scope directly addresses the five critical audit findings. The Vault agent sidecar approach is the right pattern — applications do not need to be modified to consume secrets. Quarterly IAM access review automation that auto-generates reports and routes remediation tasks is the operational workflow the security team needs.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Round 2 plan correctly implemented Vault agent sidecar injection and IRSA per workload. The remaining gap from round 2 is the Vault PKI secrets engine for service-to-service mTLS certificate issuance — applications are still using self-signed certificates generated at build time rather than short-lived PKI-issued certificates. This is the ISS-001 gap. The Trivy CVSS gate implementation in round 2 used CVSS>=7 but did not distinguish between fixable and unfixable vulnerabilities — non-fixable HIGH findings from base images were blocking deployments. ISS-002 addresses this.
reasoner_reviews.security: Vault PKI engine with 30-day leaf certificate TTL and automatic renewal via cert-manager is the correct architecture for service-to-service mTLS at this scale. Trivy --ignore-unfixed flag resolves the base-image CVE blocking issue while maintaining the gate for fixable vulnerabilities.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: EKS private endpoint must be validated in Terraform plan output — confirm aws_eks_cluster endpoint_private_access=true and endpoint_public_access=false in all environment workspaces.

backend.service_design: Terraform modules: vpc (VPC, subnets, NACLs, security groups), eks (cluster, node groups, IRSA roles), vault (EC2 ASG, Raft storage, load balancer, KMS auto-unseal), kms (CMKs per data classification), iam_review (Lambda + Step Functions for quarterly review). Ansible playbooks for Vault cluster initialization and Raft join.
backend.api_patterns: No HTTP API. Infrastructure outputs: Vault endpoint DNS, EKS cluster endpoint (private), KMS key ARNs, security group IDs. GitLab CI pipeline interface: terraform plan, manual approval gate, terraform apply.
backend.backend_risks: Vault Raft quorum loss if 2 of 3 nodes fail simultaneously — mitigated by spread placement group and separate AZ assignment per node.

security.auth_design: Keycloak SAML federation with AWS IAM Identity Center for human console access. EKS workloads use IRSA: each Kubernetes ServiceAccount annotated with IAM role ARN; IAM role policy scoped to minimum S3/DynamoDB/Secrets Manager actions needed by that service. Vault AppRole for CI/CD pipelines; Vault Kubernetes auth for pod-level dynamic secret issuance.
security.secrets_management: Vault KV v2 for static secrets; Vault AWS secrets engine for dynamic IAM credentials with 1-hour TTL; Vault PKI secrets engine for service mesh leaf certificates (30-day TTL, renewed by cert-manager CertificateRequest). KMS CMK rotation every 12 months via Terraform aws_kms_key rotation_enabled=true.
security.audit_and_logging_controls: CloudTrail multi-region trail to S3 with Object Lock COMPLIANCE 7-year retention and log file integrity validation. Vault audit device writing to file + S3 with 3-year retention. IAM access review Lambda generates JSON report of all roles, policies, and attached principals; Step Functions workflow routes findings above a privilege score threshold to JIRA tickets via API.
security.security_risks: Vault agent sidecar adds ~150ms to pod startup — acceptable; document in runbook. cert-manager CertificateRequest failure leaves pod without renewed certificate — add monitoring alert when certificate NotAfter within 72 hours.

data.entities: iam_policies (policy_arn, policy_name, attached_principals[], last_reviewed_at, privilege_score), vault_secrets (path, engine_type, last_accessed_at, created_at, lease_duration), network_segments (segment_id, vpc_id, subnet_ids[], security_group_ids[], allowed_flows[]), container_scan_results (scan_id, image_ref, scan_timestamp, critical_count, high_count, fixable_high_count, blocked BOOLEAN), compliance_findings (finding_id, control_id, severity, description, remediation_jira_id, resolved_at nullable), access_reviews (review_id, quarter, generated_at, total_roles, flagged_roles, report_s3_key), encryption_keys (key_id, alias, usage, rotation_due_date, last_rotated_at)
data.schema_notes: All entities stored in DynamoDB tables for the IAM review Lambda and Trivy scan result archiving. No relational database for infrastructure state — Terraform state in S3 with DynamoDB lock.
data.data_risks: Terraform state file contains sensitive output values (Vault endpoint, KMS key ARNs) — state bucket encrypted with KMS CMK and access restricted to GitLab CI role via S3 bucket policy.

devops.deployment_topology: GitLab CI pipeline: dev workspace applies automatically on merge to main; staging requires manual trigger; prod requires 2-approver manual gate. Terraform workspaces: dev, staging, prod with separate state files in s3://terraform-state-sports-infra/{workspace}/. Vault cluster: 3 EC2 r6i.large in separate AZs behind NLB; KMS auto-unseal via aws_kms_key. EKS node groups: spot instances for non-prod, on-demand for prod.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitLab CI stages: validate (terraform validate, tfsec security scan, checkov IaC scan), plan (terraform plan with Vault provider), security-gate (Trivy scan all images in affected deployments, fail if CVSS>=7 AND fixable), approve (manual gate for staging/prod), apply (terraform apply -auto-approve).
devops.observability_stack: Datadog agent DaemonSet on EKS nodes. Metrics: vault_core_active, vault_runtime_gc_pause_ns, trivy_scan_blocked_count, iam_review_flagged_roles. Alerts: Vault standby node count <2 (P1), Trivy scan blocked (P2 during business hours), certificate expiry <72h (P2).

ISS-001.title: Service-to-service mTLS using build-time self-signed certificates
ISS-001.detail: Round 2 plan added Vault PKI engine to the Terraform configuration but did not configure cert-manager CertificateRequest resources to consume Vault-issued certificates. Applications are still using Dockerfile-generated self-signed certificates with 5-year validity — violating SOC 2 CC6 and ISO 27001 A.10 requirements for certificate lifecycle management.

history.note: Identified in SOC 2 pre-audit gap assessment
Vault PKI engine configured but cert-manager integration not implemented

ISS-002.title: Trivy gate blocking on unfixable base image CVEs
ISS-002.detail: Round 2 Trivy integration correctly blocks on CVSS>=7 but does not use --ignore-unfixed flag. Base images (AL2023, distroless) contain unfixable HIGH CVEs pending upstream patches, causing legitimate deployment blocks for applications that cannot resolve the CVE without an upstream base image update.

history.note: Reported by platform team after three legitimate deployments blocked

ISS-003.title: No static IAM access keys in EC2 instances
ISS-003.detail: Round 1 EC2 instances used long-lived IAM access keys in environment variables. Resolved in round 2 by converting all instances to use EC2 instance profile with scoped IAM roles.

history.note: IRSA and instance profiles fully deployed

focus_issues.title: Service mTLS using build-time self-signed certs
focus_issues.detail: Vault PKI engine configured but cert-manager CertificateRequest not wired to consume Vault-issued certificates. Applications must migrate to 30-day PKI-issued leaf certificates.

focus_issues.title: Trivy gate blocking unfixable CVEs
focus_issues.detail: Add --ignore-unfixed to Trivy scan command to restrict gate to fixable vulnerabilities only.

latest_recommendations: Configure cert-manager ClusterIssuer pointing to Vault PKI engine; add Certificate resources for each service requiring mTLS; set 30-day duration with 80% renewal threshold
Add --ignore-unfixed flag to trivy image scan command in GitLab CI; update scan result archiving to record fixable_high_count separately
Add monitoring alert when certificate NotAfter within 72 hours via Datadog cert expiry check

previous_plan.title: Sports Organization Infrastructure Security Hardening v2
previous_plan.executive_summary: Terraform 1.8 IaC hardening AWS EKS infrastructure with Vault agent sidecar dynamic secrets injection, IRSA per-workload, Trivy scanning CI gate, and VPC segmentation. ISS-003 (static IAM keys) resolved. ISS-001 (cert-manager/Vault PKI) and ISS-002 (Trivy unfixable) remain open.

fix_report.action_taken: Added Terraform vault_pki_secret_backend_role 'service-mesh' with allowed_domains=['svc.cluster.local'], max_ttl=720h, require_cn=false. Added Kubernetes cert-manager ClusterIssuer resource pointing to Vault PKI engine /v1/pki/sign/service-mesh via vault.hashicorp.com/role annotation. Added Certificate CRD resources for each of the 12 microservices with duration: 720h and renewBefore: 144h. Ansible playbook updates Vault PKI mount with CRL distribution point URL.
fix_report.expected_outcome: All service-to-service connections use Vault-issued 30-day leaf certificates rotated automatically by cert-manager; ISO 27001 A.10 certificate lifecycle control satisfied; SOC 2 pre-audit gap closed

Added separate informational scan step without --ignore-unfixed writing full CVE report to artifacts for security team review. Updated container_scan_results DynamoDB schema to add fixable_high_count and unfixable_high_count fields. Alert added when unfixable_high_count increases (base image update needed).
fix_report.expected_outcome: Trivy gate only blocks on fixable HIGH/CRITICAL vulnerabilities; base image CVEs trigger informational alert rather than deployment block; platform team deployment velocity restored

target_output.title: Sports Organization Infrastructure Security Hardening v3
target_output.executive_summary: A Terraform 1.8 and Ansible IaC project delivering security-hardened AWS infrastructure for a professional sports organization. All containerized workloads receive Vault-agent-injected dynamic credentials; cert-manager issues and rotates 30-day mTLS leaf certificates from the Vault PKI engine for all inter-service communication; Trivy scans in GitLab CI gate on fixable HIGH/CRITICAL CVEs only; IRSA enforces least-privilege per EKS workload; CloudTrail and Vault audit logs ship to S3 with Object Lock COMPLIANCE for 7-year retention.
target_output.architecture_overview: Three-tier VPC (public/private/data subnets across 3 AZs) hosts EKS Managed Node Groups on private subnets with private-endpoint-only API server. A self-managed HashiCorp Vault 1.16 HA cluster (3 EC2 r6i.large nodes, Raft storage, KMS auto-unseal) runs on private EC2 instances behind an internal NLB. Vault agent sidecar injector (injected by MutatingWebhookConfiguration) dynamically fetches AWS credentials and database passwords for each pod at startup, writing secrets to tmpfs volumes. cert-manager CertificateRequest resources consume Vault PKI engine-issued leaf certificates (30-day TTL, auto-renewed at 80% lifetime). IRSA binds each Kubernetes ServiceAccount to a scoped IAM role for direct AWS API access. Trivy scans all container images in GitLab CI with --ignore-unfixed, blocking promotion only for fixable HIGH/CRITICAL CVEs.
target_output.technology_stack: IaC: Terraform 1.8 with AWS provider 5.x, Kubernetes provider 2.x, Vault provider 4.x. Configuration management: Ansible 2.16 for Vault cluster init and Raft join. Secrets: HashiCorp Vault 1.16 with KV v2, AWS secrets engine, PKI secrets engine, Kubernetes auth. Certificate management: cert-manager 1.14 with ClusterIssuer pointing to Vault PKI. Container scanning: Trivy 0.50 with --ignore-unfixed. IAM review: Python 3.12 Lambda with boto3 and Step Functions. State: S3 with DynamoDB lock table. CI: GitLab CI. Monitoring: Datadog.
target_output.functional_feature_map: VPC Network Segmentation: three-tier subnets with NACLs allowing only required inter-tier flows; security groups per microservice tier with deny-all default. Vault Secrets Injection: agent sidecar injects dynamic AWS credentials (1h TTL) and RDS password (24h TTL) as tmpfs-mounted files for all 12 microservice pods. Vault PKI mTLS: cert-manager Certificate resources per service, Vault-signed 30-day leaf certificates with auto-renewal at day 24. IRSA Least-Privilege: one IAM role per microservice ServiceAccount, policy scoped to exact S3 prefix, DynamoDB table, and Secrets Manager path. Trivy Scanning Gate: fixable HIGH/CRITICAL blocks promotion; unfixable generates informational artifact. Quarterly IAM Access Review: Python Lambda queries IAM, scores privilege level, generates JSON report, routes flagged roles to JIRA via Step Functions.
target_output.system_components: Terraform Modules (vpc, eks, vault, kms, iam_review, network_policy), Vault HA Cluster (3 EC2 r6i.large, Raft, KMS auto-unseal, internal NLB), Vault Agent Injector (MutatingWebhookConfiguration patching pods with sidecar + init container), cert-manager (CertificateRequest controller consuming Vault PKI), IRSA IAM Roles (one per microservice, least-privilege policies), Trivy Scanner (GitLab CI job with --ignore-unfixed), IAM Review Lambda + Step Functions (quarterly automation), CloudTrail Multi-Region Trail (S3 Object Lock COMPLIANCE), Vault Audit Device (S3 + file backends), Datadog Agent DaemonSet.
target_output.workflows: Secret Injection at Pod Startup: Kubernetes scheduler places pod; Vault Agent Injector MutatingWebhookConfiguration patches pod spec adding vault-agent-init container and vault-agent sidecar; init container authenticates to Vault using pod ServiceAccount token (Kubernetes auth method); Vault issues dynamic AWS credential (aws/sts/role, 1h TTL) and database password (database/creds/app-role, 24h TTL); secrets written to /vault/secrets/ tmpfs; application reads secrets from file. mTLS Certificate Renewal: cert-manager monitors Certificate resource NotAfter; at 24 days (80% of 30-day lifetime), cert-manager sends CertificateRequest to Vault PKI /pki/sign/service-mesh; Vault signs CSR with intermediate CA; cert-manager stores renewed certificate in Kubernetes Secret; Istio sidecar (or application) hot-reloads certificate without pod restart. Trivy Scan and Gate in GitLab CI: pipeline builds Docker image; trivy image --ignore-unfixed --severity HIGH,CRITICAL --exit-code 1 ${IMAGE_REF} runs against image; non-zero exit blocks promotion stage; separate trivy image full-severity scan writes JSON artifact for security team; fixable_high_count and unfixable_high_count stored in DynamoDB container_scan_results. Quarterly IAM Review: CloudWatch Events triggers IAM review Lambda on first Monday of quarter; Lambda calls iam:ListRoles, iam:ListAttachedRolePolicies, iam:GetPolicyVersion; computes privilege_score based on wildcard actions, sensitive resource ARNs; Step Functions routes roles with score >70 to JIRA via webhook; report JSON saved to S3 access_reviews bucket with 5-year Object Lock retention.
target_output.data_model: iam_policies (policy_arn: str PK, policy_name: str, attached_principals: list[str], last_reviewed_at: datetime, privilege_score: float, wildcard_actions: bool). vault_secrets (path: str PK, engine_type: str, last_accessed_at: datetime, created_at: datetime, lease_duration_seconds: int). network_segments (segment_id: str PK, vpc_id: str, subnet_ids: list[str], security_group_ids: list[str], allowed_flows: list[str]). container_scan_results (scan_id: str PK, image_ref: str, scan_timestamp: datetime, critical_count: int, high_count: int, fixable_high_count: int, unfixable_high_count: int, blocked: bool). compliance_findings (finding_id: str PK, control_id: str, severity: str, description: str, remediation_jira_id: str nullable, resolved_at: datetime nullable). access_reviews (review_id: str PK, quarter: str, generated_at: datetime, total_roles: int, flagged_roles: int, report_s3_key: str). encryption_keys (key_id: str PK, alias: str, usage: str, rotation_due_date: date, last_rotated_at: date). All stored in DynamoDB tables. Terraform state in S3 s3://terraform-state-sports-infra/{workspace}/terraform.tfstate.
target_output.api_design: No HTTP API exposed externally. Terraform output interface: vault_endpoint (internal NLB DNS), eks_cluster_endpoint (private), kms_key_arns (map by classification), network_segment_ids (map by tier). GitLab CI pipeline interface: terraform plan output posted to MR comment; manual_approval job gate for staging/prod apply. IAM Review Lambda invocation: CloudWatch Events cron or manual boto3 invoke via CLI. Vault API (internal): POST /v1/auth/kubernetes/login, GET /v1/aws/sts/{role}, POST /v1/pki/sign/service-mesh.
target_output.security_and_compliance: SOC 2 CC6: No static IAM access keys — Vault dynamic credentials (1h TTL) for AWS API; EC2 instance profiles for Vault cluster nodes. cert-manager CertificateRequest against Vault PKI /pki/sign/service-mesh role (allowed_domains: svc.cluster.local, max_ttl: 720h); leaf certificates valid 30 days, renewed at day 24 by cert-manager automatically — closes ISO 27001 A.10 certificate lifecycle gap. IRSA: aws_iam_role per ServiceAccount with condition StringEquals eks.amazonaws.com/role-arn. EBS and RDS KMS CMK encryption with rotation_enabled=true. EKS API server: endpoint_private_access=true, endpoint_public_access=false in aws_eks_cluster Terraform resource. S3 bucket policies: aws_s3_bucket_public_access_block with all options true. CloudTrail multi-region trail with is_multi_region_trail=true, log file integrity validation enable_log_file_validation=true, S3 Object Lock COMPLIANCE 7-year retention. Vault audit device to file + S3 3-year retention. SOC 2 CC8: all changes via GitLab CI; manual approval gate with 2-approver requirement for prod workspace apply.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: GitLab CI pipeline: stages validate (terraform validate, tfsec --minimum-severity HIGH, checkov -d .), plan (terraform plan -out=tfplan with Vault credentials from CI/CD variable group), security-gate (trivy image --ignore-unfixed --severity HIGH,CRITICAL --exit-code 1 for all images in affected deployments), approve (manual_job with 2 required approvers for prod), apply (terraform apply tfplan -auto-approve). Terraform workspaces: dev (auto-apply on main merge), staging (manual trigger), prod (2-approver gate). Vault cluster lifecycle: Ansible playbook on first deploy initializes cluster, stores unseal keys in S3 encrypted with KMS. cert-manager Certificate resources deployed via Helm chart in kube-system namespace. Kubernetes NetworkPolicy resources enforcing pod-level east-west traffic segmentation co-deployed with each microservice.
target_output.observability: Datadog agent DaemonSet collecting EKS node and pod metrics. Custom metrics: vault_core_active (gauge, alert if not 1), vault_standby_nodes (gauge, alert if <2), trivy_fixable_blocked_total (counter), trivy_unfixable_high_count_increase (alert on delta >0 — base image needs update), certificate_expiry_days (gauge per service, alert if <3), iam_review_flagged_roles (gauge per quarter). Dashboards: Vault health (active/standby, lease expiry distribution), Certificate inventory (days until expiry per service), IAM Review history (flagged roles trend). PagerDuty: vault standby <2 P1, certificate expiry <3 days P1, trivy fixable block during business hours P2.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Vault EC2 r6i.large x3: ~$450/month. EKS Managed Node Groups (on-demand prod): ~$800/month for 6 nodes. RDS PostgreSQL Multi-AZ db.r6g.xlarge: ~$400/month. KMS CMKs (5 keys × $1/month): $5/month. CloudTrail + S3 Object Lock: ~$50/month. Datadog 15 hosts: ~$450/month. GitLab CI compute: ~$100/month. Total: ~$2255/month. Vault cluster spot instances for dev/staging saves ~40% on non-prod.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 (weeks 1-8, complete): VPC three-tier segmentation, EKS private cluster, IRSA per workload, Vault HA cluster with KMS auto-unseal, Vault agent sidecar deployment, static IAM key elimination (ISS-003 resolved). Phase 2 (weeks 9-16, complete): Trivy scanning gate in GitLab CI, CloudTrail Object Lock, IAM quarterly review Lambda, EBS/RDS KMS CMK encryption. Phase 3 (current round 3, weeks 17-20): cert-manager ClusterIssuer wired to Vault PKI (ISS-001 fix), Trivy --ignore-unfixed (ISS-002 fix), certificate expiry monitoring, unfixable CVE informational reporting, SOC 2 pre-audit evidence collection. Phase 4: OPA/Gatekeeper admission control, Splunk SIEM integration, Vault lease renewal monitoring.
target_output.development_guidelines: Terraform: all resources tagged with environment, team, compliance_scope, data_classification. tfsec and checkov must pass with zero HIGH findings in GitLab CI validate stage. No hardcoded ARNs — all cross-module references via terraform_remote_state or data sources. Ansible roles must be idempotent and tested with molecule. cert-manager Certificate resources must specify renewBefore as 20% of duration or 144h minimum. Trivy scan results must be archived as GitLab CI artifacts for 90 days for SOC 2 evidence.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Vault cluster quorum loss during patching — mitigate with Ansible rolling update playbook patching one node at a time; PagerDuty alert if standby count drops to 1. Risk: cert-manager CertificateRequest failure leaves service without renewed certificate — alert at <72h expiry gives 3-day response window before cert expiry. Tradeoff: self-managed Vault over HCP Vault — self-managed gives full Raft storage control and lower cost at this scale; revisit at SOC 2 year 2 if operational burden grows. Tradeoff: Trivy --ignore-unfixed over maintaining a CVE exception list — simpler operationally; security team reviews unfixable CVE informational report weekly.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: cert-manager vault issuer chosen over external-secrets-operator for PKI — cert-manager ClusterIssuer is purpose-built for certificate lifecycle, external-secrets is better for KV secrets. Vault Kubernetes auth method chosen for pod authentication over Vault Agent AppRole — Kubernetes auth leverages projected ServiceAccount tokens without secret injection bootstrapping. --ignore-unfixed confirmed as the correct Trivy flag for base-image CVE handling in enterprise environments.

metadata.notes: Round 3 revision of sports org cloud security hardening; fixes cert-manager Vault PKI wiring and Trivy unfixable CVE gate; strong plan with complete SOC2+ISO27001 controls.

reasoner_reviews.product: Scope is correctly minimal for a pre-launch landing page. The ML pipeline explainer section is the key differentiator — it should use a visual animated flow diagram showing video submission -> pose estimation -> talent score output to communicate the AI value proposition concretely to non-technical coaches. CTA button above the fold with the free trial sign-up is essential.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Astro's zero-JavaScript default output with Tailwind CSS is the right choice for <2 second mobile load time. The ML pipeline diagram should be an SVG with CSS animation rather than a canvas or WebGL renderer to keep JS payload at zero. The Vercel Edge Function for HubSpot form submission is correctly scoped — no need for a backend service. Lighthouse CI on every PR prevents performance regressions before they reach production.
reasoner_reviews.security: CSP must explicitly permit plausible.io for the analytics script. HubSpot API key stored in Vercel environment variable is correct — never in client-side code. CSRF token for the form is a good defensive measure even for a public form, preventing automated bot submissions.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Lighthouse >95 on mobile is achievable with Astro zero-JS output, WebP/AVIF images, and preloading the hero LCP image. No third-party ad pixels is a hard constraint — confirm with marketing that no Facebook Pixel or Google Ads tag is added without engineering review.

backend.service_design: Single Vercel Edge Function: api/subscribe.ts. Validates name, email, academy_name fields. Checks CSRF token. POSTs to HubSpot Contacts API v3 with HUBSPOT_API_KEY from env. Returns 200 on success, 422 on validation error, 500 on HubSpot API failure.
backend.api_patterns: POST /api/subscribe {name, email, academy_name, _csrf} -> {success: true} | {error: string}. Rate limited by Vercel Edge rate limiting to 10 requests/minute per IP.
backend.backend_risks: HubSpot API rate limit (100 requests/10s) — not a concern at landing page traffic volumes. HubSpot API outage causes form submission failures — add user-facing retry message.

security.auth_design: No authentication. CSRF token: Edge Function issues token on GET /api/csrf-token; form includes token in hidden field; subscribe endpoint validates token against HMAC(secret, timestamp). Protects against automated form spam.
security.abuse_prevention: Vercel Edge rate limiting on /api/subscribe. Honeypot hidden field in form — if populated, request discarded silently. No reCAPTCHA (would add third-party script violating CSP constraint).
security.privacy_controls: No analytics cookies — Plausible cookieless mode. Privacy policy link in footer specifying HubSpot as data processor for form submissions. No Facebook Pixel or Google Ads tags per constraints.

data.entities: testimonials (id, coach_name, club_name, quote, photo_url, sport), feature_highlights (id, title, description, icon_name, sort_order), pricing_tiers (id, name, price_monthly, features[], cta_label, is_featured), lead_captures (name, email, academy_name, submitted_at — stored in HubSpot only)
data.schema_notes: All content stored as static Astro content collection JSON files. No database. lead_captures never stored by the landing page application — immediately forwarded to HubSpot.
data.data_risks: HubSpot API timeout: Edge Function has 5-second timeout; on failure, return user-friendly error and Plausible error event.

target_output.title: ScoutAI Landing Page
target_output.executive_summary: An Astro and Tailwind CSS landing page for the ScoutAI sports talent scouting platform, converting organic and paid traffic to free trial sign-ups. A Vercel Edge Function proxies form submissions to HubSpot CRM without storing personal data on the landing page infrastructure. Plausible Analytics tracks CTA click and form submission conversion events in cookieless mode. Target Lighthouse performance score >95 on mobile.
target_output.architecture_overview: Fully static Astro site deployed to Vercel Edge Network. All page sections are pre-rendered HTML with Tailwind CSS styling and zero client-side JavaScript except the Plausible Analytics script and form submission handler. The ML pipeline diagram is an SVG with CSS keyframe animations. A single Vercel Edge Function (api/subscribe.ts) handles form submissions, validates input, checks CSRF token, and proxies to the HubSpot Contacts API v3. No database, no sessions, no server-side state.
target_output.technology_stack: Frontend: Astro 4 with Tailwind CSS, no JS frameworks. Edge Function: TypeScript on Vercel Edge Runtime. Analytics: Plausible Analytics (cookieless). CMS: Astro content collections for testimonials, feature highlights, and pricing tiers. CI: GitHub Actions with Lighthouse CI. Hosting: Vercel Edge Network.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Hero section with product headline, sub-headline, and above-the-fold free trial CTA button. ML Pipeline Explainer: SVG animated diagram showing video upload -> pose estimation keypoints -> talent scoring model -> ranked prospect list. Coach Testimonials: three carousel cards with photo, name, club, and quote. Pricing Tiers: three-column table with features and CTA per tier. Free Trial Form: name, email, academy name fields with CSRF protection posting to /api/subscribe.
target_output.system_components: Astro Static Site (pre-rendered HTML pages), Vercel Edge Function (api/subscribe.ts form handler), HubSpot CRM (lead storage via Contacts API), Plausible Analytics (cookieless event tracking), Vercel Edge CDN (global delivery and TLS), GitHub Actions (Lighthouse CI on PRs).
target_output.workflows: Free Trial Sign-Up: Visitor loads page; browser fetches CSRF token from GET /api/csrf-token; visitor fills form with name, email, academy_name; submits; Edge Function validates fields and CSRF token; POSTs to HubSpot Contacts API v3 with HUBSPOT_API_KEY from env; on success returns 200 and Plausible form_submit event fires; on HubSpot failure returns 500 with user-facing retry message. Content Update: Marketing edits testimonial quote in Astro content collection JSON file and opens PR; Lighthouse CI runs on PR preview; on merge Vercel redeploys static site.
target_output.data_model: testimonials (id: string, coach_name: string, club_name: string, sport: string, quote: string, photo_url: string) — Astro content collection. feature_highlights (id: string, title: string, description: string, icon_name: string, sort_order: int) — Astro content collection. pricing_tiers (id: string, name: string, price_monthly: int, features: string[], cta_label: string, is_featured: bool) — Astro content collection. lead_captures: forwarded to HubSpot only; zero rows stored in landing page infrastructure.
target_output.api_design: POST /api/subscribe {name: string, email: string, academy_name: string, _csrf: string} -> {success: true} | {error: string, field?: string}. GET /api/csrf-token -> {token: string, expires_at: string}. No other endpoints. CSRF token is HMAC-SHA256(CSRF_SECRET, timestamp, ip). HubSpot API call: POST https://api.hubapi.com/crm/v3/objects/contacts with Authorization: Bearer ${HUBSPOT_API_KEY}.
target_output.security_and_compliance: HTTPS enforced via Vercel TLS. CSP header in vercel.json: default-src 'self'; script-src 'self' plausible.io; img-src 'self' data:; connect-src 'self'. CSRF token validates HMAC-SHA256(secret, timestamp) with 30-minute window. HubSpot API key stored in HUBSPOT_API_KEY Vercel environment variable — never shipped to client. Honeypot field _hp in form; if non-empty, submission silently discarded. No reCAPTCHA or third-party ad scripts.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: GitHub repo connected to Vercel. Merges to main trigger automatic production deployment. PR branches generate preview deployments with Vercel URL posted to PR. GitHub Actions CI: astro check, ESLint, Lighthouse CI (performance >95 mobile and desktop, accessibility >90, best-practices >95) — Lighthouse failure blocks PR merge. Vercel environment variables: HUBSPOT_API_KEY, CSRF_SECRET (both set in Vercel dashboard, not in repo).
target_output.observability: Plausible Analytics dashboard tracks daily pageviews by source, cta_click events per CTA position (hero, pricing, footer), form_submit success and failure counts. Vercel deployment log notification to Slack on build success or failure. Lighthouse CI score history in GitHub Actions artifacts tracks performance regression over time.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Vercel Hobby tier ($0) sufficient for landing page traffic. Plausible Analytics $9/month. HubSpot free CRM tier covers lead volume. GitHub Actions free tier. Total: $9/month. Vercel Edge CDN handles traffic spikes during Product Hunt launch day without configuration changes.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (weeks 1-3): Hero, testimonials, pricing, free trial form with HubSpot integration, Plausible analytics, Vercel deployment, Lighthouse CI gate. Phase 2 Post-Launch: ML pipeline animated SVG explainer section, blog section for SEO content marketing, A/B test hero copy variants via Vercel Edge Config feature flags.
target_output.development_guidelines: Astro TypeScript strict mode. No client-side JS except Plausible script and form submission handler. All images in WebP or AVIF format with width and height attributes to prevent CLS. Lighthouse CI score >95 enforced in GitHub Actions on every PR. CSP header validated by a CI script checking vercel.json for required directives.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: HubSpot API outage causes form submission failures during Product Hunt launch traffic — mitigation: user-facing error message with prompt to email directly; Plausible error event fires for ops visibility. Risk: Astro upgrade breaks Tailwind CSS integration — pin both versions in package.json. Tradeoff: Plausible over Google Analytics — GDPR-compliant cookieless mode acceptable for marketing metrics at cost of less granular attribution data.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Plausible confirmed over Google Analytics for privacy compliance and Lighthouse best-practices score impact. Astro zero-JS output chosen over Next.js static export — Astro has smaller bundle size for a pure content page. CSRF via Edge Function chosen over reCAPTCHA to avoid third-party script violating CSP and performance constraints.

project.goal: Auto-score gymnastics vault from single-camera video using pose estimation
project.target_users: Sports science researchers and biomechanics coaches

frontend.approach: Single-page upload UI with real-time processing status and results display

backend.processing: MediaPipe Pose for keypoint extraction, custom phase segmentation logic

security.auth: None — local only

data.storage: Local filesystem for video files and Parquet outputs

reasoner_reviews.product: The core research hypothesis is sound: pose estimation provides sufficient keypoint fidelity for vault phase segmentation. The deviation scoring approach against an elite reference model is novel and publishable. Phase 3 should focus on annotation quality of the reference model rather than adding features.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: FastAPI async background tasks handle the video processing pipeline cleanly without blocking the Streamlit UI thread. DuckDB is appropriate for the analytical query patterns — joint angle time series across hundreds of frames benefit from columnar storage. The local MLflow server must be started before the FastAPI backend; document this dependency clearly.
reasoner_reviews.security: Local-only deployment substantially reduces the attack surface. The main risk is accidental data exfiltration if video files contain identifiable athletes. Recommend researchers use anonymized video filenames and document the data handling protocol for IRB compliance.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Sub-10s constraint for a 4-second vault at 60fps means 240 frames through MediaPipe Pose. On RTX 3090 with GPU acceleration, MediaPipe achieves ~80fps so this is achievable. Batch mode must use GPU-accelerated inference — CPU fallback will miss the latency target.
reasoner_reviews.critic: Two unresolved issues carried from round 2: the reference model serialization format has not been pinned (ISS-001) and the MLflow artifact storage path collides with DuckDB database files under concurrent runs (ISS-002). Both must be addressed before researcher handoff.

frontend.app_structure: Single Streamlit app (app.py) with sidebar controls for video upload, phase configuration parameters, and reference model selection
frontend.pages_and_flows: Video Upload page: drag-and-drop MP4/MOV, processing status spinner, phase timeline visualization, deviation score card, annotated video download button
frontend.performance_notes: Use st.session_state to cache pose keyframe sequences so re-scoring with different reference models does not re-run full MediaPipe inference
frontend.frontend_risks: Streamlit blocking I/O on large video files — use st.progress with polling against FastAPI status endpoint rather than synchronous call

backend.service_design: FastAPI with /upload endpoint accepting multipart video, /jobs/{job_id}/status for polling, /jobs/{job_id}/result for JSON output retrieval
backend.business_modules: VideoIngestion module (frame extraction via OpenCV), PoseEstimation module (MediaPipe Pose GPU), PhaseSegmenter (rule-based on flight apex keypoint velocity), DeviationScorer (cosine similarity against reference embedding)
backend.background_jobs: asyncio background task per uploaded video; job state tracked in in-memory dict keyed by UUID
backend.backend_risks: MediaPipe GPU context is not thread-safe; enforce single-worker Uvicorn to avoid concurrent inference collisions

security.auth_design: No authentication; bind FastAPI to 127.0.0.1 only
security.secrets_management: No secrets required; all processing local
security.security_risks: Video files may contain athlete faces; document anonymization guidance for lab IRB protocol
