target_output.thinking_summary: Round 3 must resolve both open issues: pin numpy .npy format with documented array shape for reference embeddings, and isolate MLflow artifact storage under mlruns/ away from DuckDB data/ directory. The local-only, offline constraint is the dominant architectural driver — no external API calls anywhere in the pipeline. GPU inference path using MediaPipe with CUDA must have a startup validation check so researchers get an actionable failure message rather than a cryptic CUDA error mid-processing.

fix_report.expected_outcome: Concurrent experiment runs no longer risk DuckDB file lock contention; MLflow UI at port 5000 reflects correct artifact locations

target_output.title: Gymnastics Vault Pose Scoring Prototype — Final Architecture
target_output.executive_summary: A fully local research prototype running on a GPU workstation that ingests single-camera vault videos, applies MediaPipe Pose inference frame by frame using CUDA acceleration, segments the vault into approach/hurdle/flight/post-flight phases by detecting flight apex from hip keypoint vertical velocity, computes joint angle deviation scores against numpy-serialized elite reference embeddings, and returns a structured JSON report with phase-level metrics and an annotated video. Experiment runs are tracked in a local MLflow server with artifact storage fully isolated from the DuckDB analytical database.
target_output.architecture_overview: Three-process local architecture orchestrated via docker-compose with GPU passthrough for RTX 3090: Streamlit UI (port 8501) posts video uploads to FastAPI (port 8000) which spawns asyncio background tasks for the processing pipeline. The pipeline runs MediaPipe Pose with CUDA backend to extract 33-keypoint sequences per frame, feeds them into the PhaseSegmenter which detects phase boundaries from hip-keypoint vertical velocity sign changes, then computes cosine similarity of per-phase joint angle embeddings against the loaded reference model. DuckDB stores all analytical data at /app/data/vault_analysis.duckdb. MLflow tracking server (port 5000) records all experiment parameters and output metrics under /app/mlruns with no overlap with the DuckDB path.
target_output.technology_stack: Frontend: Streamlit 1.32 with Python 3.12. Backend: FastAPI 0.110 with Uvicorn single-worker (GPU thread safety). CV inference: MediaPipe Pose 0.10 with CUDA acceleration on RTX 3090. Data: DuckDB 0.10 for analytical storage, Parquet via PyArrow for result exports. Experiment tracking: MLflow 2.11 local server. Containerization: Docker Compose with NVIDIA container toolkit for GPU passthrough. CI: GitHub Actions with ruff, mypy, pytest.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Video Ingestion: accepts MP4/MOV up to 2GB via Streamlit file uploader, extracts frames with OpenCV at native FPS. Pose Estimation: MediaPipe Pose GPU inference producing 33 3D keypoints per frame stored as JSONB in DuckDB pose_keyframe_sequences table. Phase Segmentation: rule-based segmenter detecting approach (run-up), hurdle (last contact), flight (board contact to table release), post-flight (table to landing mat) using hip-keypoint velocity and acceleration inflection points. Deviation Scoring: cosine similarity of per-phase mean joint angle embedding vs. reference model .npy arrays, producing a 0-100 deviation score per phase. Result Export: annotated video with skeleton overlay and phase labels, JSON report, Parquet feature vector for downstream model training.
target_output.system_components: FastAPI backend (Uvicorn single-worker): /upload multipart endpoint, /jobs/{id}/status polling, /jobs/{id}/result JSON retrieval. VideoIngestion module (OpenCV frame extraction). PoseEstimation module (MediaPipe Pose CUDA, single-instance enforced by asyncio.Semaphore(1)). PhaseSegmenter (velocity-based boundary detection on hip keypoints). DeviationScorer (loads .npy reference embeddings with shape validation, computes per-phase cosine similarity). DuckDB instance at /app/data/vault_analysis.duckdb. MLflow tracking server at /app/mlruns. Streamlit app consuming FastAPI via httpx async client.
target_output.workflows: Vault Analysis: researcher drags MP4 onto Streamlit uploader; POST /upload creates job UUID, enqueues background task, returns job ID; Streamlit polls /jobs/{id}/status every 2s displaying progress bar; background task runs OpenCV frame extraction, MediaPipe Pose GPU inference per frame, PhaseSegmenter, DeviationScorer, writes all results to DuckDB, logs metrics to MLflow, returns status=complete; Streamlit fetches /jobs/{id}/result and renders phase timeline, deviation score card, and download links for annotated video and JSON report. Reference Model Loading: at FastAPI startup, load_reference_model() reads the .npy file and JSON sidecar, validates shape is (N_phases, 128) float32, logs the source metadata to stdout; if validation fails, startup aborts with a descriptive ValueError listing expected vs. actual shape.
target_output.data_model: video_submissions (id UUID PK, filename TEXT, upload_ts TIMESTAMP, duration_s FLOAT, fps FLOAT, frame_count INT, status TEXT CHECK status IN ('queued','processing','complete','failed')). pose_keyframe_sequences (id UUID PK, submission_id UUID FK, frame_index INT, timestamp_ms FLOAT, keypoints_json JSON storing 33 keypoints each with x,y,z,visibility). vault_phases (id UUID PK, submission_id UUID FK, phase_name TEXT CHECK phase_name IN ('approach','hurdle','flight','post_flight'), start_frame INT, end_frame INT, duration_ms FLOAT). biomechanical_metrics (id UUID PK, submission_id UUID FK, phase_name TEXT, metric_name TEXT, value FLOAT, unit TEXT). technique_models (id UUID PK, model_name TEXT, npy_path TEXT, sidecar_json_path TEXT, embedding_dim INT, n_phases INT, source_athlete_count INT, created_at TIMESTAMP). deviation_reports (id UUID PK, submission_id UUID FK, reference_model_id UUID FK, phase_scores_json JSON keyed by phase_name with deviation score and cosine_similarity, overall_deviation FLOAT, generated_at TIMESTAMP). All stored in DuckDB at /app/data/vault_analysis.duckdb.
target_output.api_design: POST /upload (multipart/form-data: video_file, reference_model_id) -> {job_id: UUID, status: 'queued'}. GET /jobs/{job_id}/status -> {job_id, status, progress_pct, current_step}. GET /jobs/{job_id}/result -> full deviation report JSON with phase_scores, overall_deviation, biomechanical_metrics array, mlflow_run_url. GET /models -> list of available reference models with metadata. POST /models (upload .npy + sidecar JSON) -> {model_id, validation: passed|failed, shape_details}. GET /jobs/{job_id}/video -> streaming response with annotated MP4 with skeleton overlay.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Local-only binding: FastAPI bound to 127.0.0.1:8000 and Streamlit to 127.0.0.1:8501 — no external network interfaces. OS-level file permissions on uploads/ and data/ directories restrict access to the running user. No authentication layer required for single-user research workstation. Anonymization guidance documented: researchers must use anonymized filenames (athlete IDs not names) per lab IRB protocol. No secrets or API keys used anywhere in the pipeline.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: docker-compose.yml defines three services: streamlit (builds from Dockerfile.streamlit, port 8501:8501), api (builds from Dockerfile.api, port 8000:8000, GPU runtime enabled, environment MLFLOW_TRACKING_URI=file:///app/mlruns, DUCKDB_PATH=/app/data/vault_analysis.duckdb), mlflow (mlflow/mlflow:2.11 image, port 5000:5000, volume /app/mlruns). NVIDIA container toolkit required on host for GPU passthrough. Startup sequence: mlflow starts first, api waits for mlflow health check, streamlit starts last. GitHub Actions CI: ruff lint, mypy --strict on all modules, pytest with fixtures covering PhaseSegmenter boundary detection and DeviationScorer shape validation. No deployment pipeline — local use only.
target_output.observability: MLflow experiment runs log: reference_model_id, video duration, frame_count, per-phase deviation scores, overall_deviation, processing_time_s. Researchers can compare scoring across model hyperparameter changes via MLflow UI. FastAPI writes structured JSON logs to /app/logs/api.log with fields: job_id, step, duration_ms, error. Startup check logs CUDA device name and available memory to stdout to confirm GPU is detected before accepting requests.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Zero cloud cost — fully local. RTX 3090 (24GB VRAM) handles MediaPipe Pose inference at ~80fps, processing a 4s vault video (240 frames) in approximately 3 seconds. DuckDB scales to millions of pose keyframes on local NVMe without performance degradation. If multiple researchers share a workstation, Uvicorn single-worker with asyncio.Semaphore(1) on GPU inference prevents CUDA OOM; concurrent uploads queue gracefully.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 Complete: video upload, MediaPipe Pose frame extraction, DuckDB schema, basic deviation score output. Phase 2 Complete: phase segmentation with velocity-based boundary detection, MLflow integration, annotated video export. Phase 3 (this round): resolve ISS-001 by pinning .npy serialization with shape validation; resolve ISS-002 by isolating MLflow artifact path from DuckDB; add POST /models endpoint for reference model management; add CUDA startup validation; complete researcher handoff documentation.
target_output.development_guidelines: Python 3.12 with strict mypy typing across all modules. ruff for linting. pytest unit tests required for PhaseSegmenter (boundary detection on synthetic keypoint sequences) and DeviationScorer (shape validation, cosine similarity correctness, error on malformed .npy). Single-worker Uvicorn enforced in Dockerfile CMD — never run with multiple workers due to GPU context safety. Reference model .npy files must include a JSON sidecar documenting array shape, dtype, source athlete count, and MediaPipe model version.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: MediaPipe Pose accuracy degrades with camera angles above 30 degrees from sagittal plane — researcher must ensure camera placement protocol. Mitigation: add camera angle calibration guide to handoff docs. Risk: DuckDB file corruption if process is killed mid-write during pose keyframe insertion — Mitigation: use DuckDB CHECKPOINT after each job completion. Tradeoff: single-worker Uvicorn prevents concurrent processing but avoids GPU memory conflicts; acceptable for single-researcher lab use. Tradeoff: numpy .npy over ONNX for reference embeddings — simpler toolchain but not portable to GPU-accelerated inference engines if prototype graduates to production.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Reference model serialization pinned to numpy .npy float32 with (N_phases, 128) embedding_dim shape — resolves ISS-001. MLflow artifact root set to /app/mlruns, DuckDB path set to /app/data/vault_analysis.duckdb — resolves ISS-002. Multi-camera support deferred to post-prototype. Phase segmentation for Tsukawa vs. Yurchenko vault types confirmed to use same phase taxonomy with different approach velocity thresholds.

metadata.notes: Round 3 research prototype resolving serialization and file path isolation issues; local GPU inference, no external APIs.

project_goal.value: Migrate sports betting operator's on-premises Oracle database workloads to AWS Aurora PostgreSQL, including schema conversion, data migration via AWS DMS, cutover runbooks, and rollback procedures to ensure zero data loss during live transition
project_goal.rationale: Core migration objective to eliminate Oracle licensing costs and enable cloud-native operations

target_users.rationale: Technical operators responsible for migration execution and rollback

capabilities.rationale: Infrastructure automation, schema migration scripts, DMS replication, validation batch jobs

complexity_level.rationale: Oracle to PostgreSQL schema conversion across 200+ tables, six weeks of parallel replication, live cutover with automated rollback

risk_level.rationale: Financial transaction data, live sports betting platform, zero data loss requirement, PCI-DSS scope

data_sensitivity.rationale: Sports betting financial transaction records under PCI-DSS Level 1

access_model.rationale: PCI-DSS access control requirements

feature_scope.rationale: Complete migration capability set

mvp_scope.value: DMS replication running for top 20 highest-volume tables, daily validation, manual cutover runbook documented
mvp_scope.rationale: Validate replication approach before full table scope

security_baseline.rationale: PCI-DSS DSS 3.4 and 3.5 encryption requirements

frontend_stack.value: N/A — infrastructure project with no frontend
frontend_stack.rationale: No UI component

backend_stack.rationale: DBA team familiar with Python; AWS SDK support

data_platform.rationale: Aurora Multi-AZ for production HA; Serverless v2 for cost-efficient staging

hosting_target.rationale: Production workload region; data residency requirement

privacy_retention_policy.rationale: PCI-DSS data retention requirements

observability_baseline.rationale: Migration monitoring requirements

execution_preference.rationale: Minimize impact on live betting traffic

compliance_context.rationale: PCI-DSS requirements for financial data migration

project.goal: Oracle to Aurora PostgreSQL migration with zero data loss

backend.framework: boto3 + psycopg2 for validation and cutover scripts

security.auth: AWS IAM with MFA; SSM Session Manager; no direct DB connections from developer machines
security.encryption: KMS CMK for Aurora; TLS 1.3 for DMS

data.migration: AWS DMS continuous replication from Oracle source
data.validation: Daily row-count and checksum comparison scripts

devops.ci: GitHub Actions for validation script tests

reasoner_reviews.product: The 4-hour maintenance window constraint for a 200-table Oracle migration is tight but achievable with 6 weeks of parallel DMS replication running first. The key is ensuring replication lag is consistently under 60 seconds before the window opens — not just at the start. Monitor lag trend over the preceding 48 hours, not just the instantaneous value.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: The cutover automation script must be idempotent — if it fails partway through, re-running should be safe. The DNS switchover step is the highest-risk single point: if the application connection string uses an Oracle TNS descriptor rather than a standard JDBC URL, the cutover automation must include a connection string update step that is tested in staging before production. DMS task monitoring via CloudWatch must alert on CDCLatencySource metric exceeding 30s, not just the default 60s threshold, to give the team time to investigate before the cutover window.
reasoner_reviews.security: PCI-DSS requires that DBA access to Aurora during and after migration is logged with session recording. SSM Session Manager with session logging to S3 satisfies this. The KMS CMK used for Aurora encryption must be separate from the Oracle on-premises key material — no key migration. New CMK created in AWS KMS for Aurora.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: The 72-hour Oracle rollback window means the Oracle database must remain in warm-standby mode with DMS replication continuing in reverse (Aurora to Oracle) for 72 hours post-cutover. Document this explicitly in the cutover runbook — DBA team must not terminate DMS tasks immediately after cutover.

backend.service_design: Migration validation service: Python scripts in migrations/ directory; validate_counts.py (row counts per table), validate_checksums.py (MD5 checksum comparison on primary key batches), cutover.py (orchestrated cutover with automated smoke tests), rollback.py (Oracle connection string restore and DMS reverse task start)
backend.background_jobs: Daily validation batch: EventBridge triggers Lambda running validate_counts.py and validate_checksums.py against all replicated tables; results written to validation_runs DynamoDB table; failures trigger SNS alert to DBA team. Cutover automation: cutover.py executes sequentially: drain connections, run final DMS sync, validate row counts, update application connection strings in Secrets Manager, run smoke tests, confirm success or trigger rollback.
backend.failure_handling: Cutover rollback: if smoke tests fail within 4-hour window, rollback.py restores original Oracle connection string from Secrets Manager, verifies application connectivity, logs rollback event to CloudTrail. DMS replication failure: CloudWatch alarm on CDCLatencySource > 30s triggers PagerDuty P2 incident.

security.auth_design: IAM roles: dms-replication-role (DMS task permissions), aurora-admin-role (DBA access via SSM Session Manager), migration-automation-role (Lambda execution, Secrets Manager access). MFA enforced via IAM policy Condition for all human IAM users.
security.audit_and_logging_controls: CloudTrail data events on Aurora cluster; SSM Session Manager logs to S3 with 90-day retention; DMS task logs to CloudWatch; all IAM actions in migration account logged.
security.secrets_management: Oracle connection strings and Aurora master password stored in Secrets Manager; cutover.py updates application parameter store paths; KMS CMK rotation enabled.

data.entities: migration_runs (DynamoDB: {run_id, started_at, status, tables_validated, validation_failures}). validation_results (DynamoDB: {run_id, table_name, oracle_row_count, aurora_row_count, checksum_match, checked_at}). cutover_checkpoints (DynamoDB: {cutover_id, step_name, status, executed_at, duration_s}). rollback_snapshots (Aurora: automated RDS snapshot taken 15 min before cutover start). schema_conversions (DynamoDB: {table_name, sct_action, manual_changes_required, approved_by, approved_at}).
data.storage_design: Aurora PostgreSQL 15 r6g.2xlarge Multi-AZ (production); Aurora Serverless v2 (staging); DMS replication instance db.r5.xlarge in private subnet; DynamoDB for migration metadata (no Oracle licensing).
data.migration_notes: AWS SCT converts 90% of Oracle PL/SQL automatically; 15 stored procedures require manual rewrite to PL/pgSQL — tracked in schema_conversions table. Oracle ROWID-based queries must be rewritten to use primary keys. Oracle sequences converted to PostgreSQL sequences with current_val pre-seeded from Oracle.

devops.deployment_topology: Migration VPC: private subnets only; DMS replication instance in same VPC as Aurora; Oracle source connected via Direct Connect to on-premises. Aurora Multi-AZ in us-east-1a and us-east-1c. DMS task traffic encrypted with TLS 1.3.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: pytest for validate_counts.py and cutover.py unit tests; DMS task creation and validation scripts tested against Aurora Serverless v2 staging environment in CI.
devops.observability_stack: CloudWatch dashboards: DMS CDCLatencySource (replication lag), Aurora write IOPS, Aurora connections count, validation success/failure rate. PagerDuty: replication lag > 30s (P2), validation failure (P2), cutover rollback triggered (P1).
devops.rollback_strategy: Automated: cutover.py detects smoke test failure, calls rollback.py; rollback restores Secrets Manager paths to Oracle values, verifies connectivity with pg_isready equivalent Oracle check, starts reverse DMS task (Aurora to Oracle CDC for 72h).

ISS-001.title: Reverse DMS task for 72-hour rollback window not designed
ISS-001.detail: The contract requires Oracle to remain rollback-capable for 72 hours post-cutover. This requires a reverse DMS CDC task (Aurora as source, Oracle as target) to keep Oracle in sync during the window. Round 1 plan did not specify this reverse replication task configuration.

history.note: cutover runbook section mentioned rollback but did not design reverse DMS task

ISS-002.title: Stored procedure manual rewrite scope not quantified
ISS-002.detail: AWS SCT will convert the majority of Oracle PL/SQL but manual rewrites are needed for complex stored procedures. Round 1 plan did not enumerate which procedures require manual rewrite or estimate the effort, creating schedule risk.

previous_plan.title: Oracle to Aurora PostgreSQL Migration Architecture v1
previous_plan.executive_summary: AWS DMS continuous replication from on-premises Oracle to Aurora PostgreSQL 15 with 6 weeks of parallel operation, daily row-count and checksum validation, and a 4-hour maintenance window for cutover.
previous_plan.architecture_overview: DMS replication instance in private subnet; AWS SCT for schema conversion; Python validation scripts on Lambda via EventBridge; Aurora Multi-AZ r6g.2xlarge production cluster.
previous_plan.workflows: Daily validation Lambda compares Oracle and Aurora row counts and checksums; cutover.py executes drain, sync, DNS switch, smoke tests; rollback.py on smoke test failure (reverse task not designed).
previous_plan.phased_implementation: Week 1: SCT schema conversion; Week 2-6: DMS replication; Week 7: staging cutover; Week 8: production cutover.

target_output.thinking_summary: Round 2 must address the critical rollback gap by designing the reverse DMS CDC task that keeps Oracle in sync during the 72-hour post-cutover window, and must quantify the 15 stored procedure manual rewrites with owners and timeline slots. The reverse DMS task needs its own replication instance, CloudWatch monitoring for CDCLatencySource, and a documented termination procedure at the 72-hour mark. The stored procedure scope should be reflected in the schema_conversions tracking table and added to Weeks 2-3 of the project timeline.

fix_report.action_taken: Designed reverse DMS CDC task: separate db.r5.large DMS replication instance created at Week 6 alongside forward replication; Aurora cluster added as DMS source with pglogical replication slot; Oracle target endpoint configured with existing on-premises Direct Connect path; reverse CDC task started at cutover completion and monitored via CDCLatencySource CloudWatch alarm (< 30s threshold); rollback.py verifies reverse task lag before confirming Oracle is in sync; reverse task terminated and replication slot removed at 72-hour mark via EventBridge scheduled event. Added pre-cutover checklist step: verify reverse task CDCLatencySource < 30s before entering maintenance window.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Oracle remains within 30s of Aurora data state for the full 72-hour rollback window; automated termination prevents orphaned replication slots

fix_report.action_taken: Enumerated 15 stored procedures requiring manual PL/pgSQL rewrite based on SCT assessment report: 8 procedures using Oracle CONNECT BY hierarchical queries (rewritten using PostgreSQL recursive CTEs), 4 procedures using Oracle-specific DBMS_SCHEDULER package (replaced with PostgreSQL pg_cron), 3 procedures using MERGE with RETURNING INTO (rewritten as INSERT ON CONFLICT DO UPDATE). Added Week 2 and Week 3 to project timeline for rewrite and unit test completion. Each procedure tracked in schema_conversions DynamoDB table with assigned_to and approved_at fields.
fix_report.expected_outcome: All 15 procedures rewritten and approved before DMS full-load phase starts in Week 4; no schema blockers at cutover

target_output.title: Oracle to Aurora PostgreSQL Migration — Final Architecture
target_output.executive_summary: A zero-data-loss migration of a sports betting operator's Oracle database to AWS Aurora PostgreSQL 15 using AWS DMS continuous replication with 6 weeks of parallel operation, daily Python validation jobs comparing row counts and MD5 checksums across all 200+ tables, a 4-hour automated cutover with smoke tests and immediate rollback capability, and a 72-hour post-cutover reverse CDC task maintaining Oracle warm standby on a dedicated DMS replication instance. All migration infrastructure runs in a private AWS VPC with PCI-DSS controls: KMS CMK encryption, SSM Session Manager for DBA access, and CloudTrail audit logging.
target_output.architecture_overview: Migration VPC in us-east-1 with private subnets only connected to on-premises Oracle via AWS Direct Connect. Forward DMS replication instance (db.r5.xlarge) runs Oracle-to-Aurora CDC for 6 weeks. Reverse DMS replication instance (db.r5.large) runs Aurora-to-Oracle CDC during the 72-hour post-cutover rollback window. Aurora PostgreSQL 15 r6g.2xlarge Multi-AZ cluster in us-east-1a/1c. Python validation scripts run as Lambda functions on EventBridge schedules. Cutover automation (cutover.py) and rollback automation (rollback.py) executed via SSM Run Command by the on-call DBA. All secrets stored in Secrets Manager. DynamoDB tables track migration metadata, validation results, and cutover checkpoints.
target_output.technology_stack: Migration engine: AWS DMS with Oracle 19c source endpoint and Aurora PostgreSQL 15 target. Schema conversion: AWS SCT with manual PL/pgSQL rewrites for 15 procedures. Validation: Python 3.12 with boto3, psycopg2, cx_Oracle on Lambda. Orchestration: EventBridge for scheduled validation, SSM Run Command for cutover execution. Infrastructure: Terraform 1.8 for all migration infrastructure as code. Monitoring: CloudWatch dashboards and alarms, PagerDuty. Secrets: AWS Secrets Manager with KMS CMK rotation.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Schema Conversion: AWS SCT generates PostgreSQL DDL for 200+ tables; 15 Oracle PL/SQL procedures requiring manual PL/pgSQL rewrite tracked in schema_conversions DynamoDB table with CONNECT BY-to-CTE, DBMS_SCHEDULER-to-pg_cron, and MERGE RETURNING-to-INSERT ON CONFLICT rewrites. Forward Replication: AWS DMS full-load then CDC from Oracle to Aurora; CDCLatencySource monitored sub-30s. Daily Validation: Lambda compares row counts and batch checksums for all replicated tables; failures logged to DynamoDB and alerted via SNS. Cutover Automation: drain connections, final sync, Secrets Manager update, smoke tests, reverse task start. Reverse Replication: 72-hour Aurora-to-Oracle CDC; EventBridge auto-terminates at 72h. Rollback: rollback.py restores Oracle connection, verifies reverse lag, confirms DBA.
target_output.system_components: Forward DMS replication instance (db.r5.xlarge, private subnet us-east-1a). Reverse DMS replication instance (db.r5.large, private subnet us-east-1a, started at cutover). Aurora PostgreSQL 15 cluster (r6g.2xlarge writer + reader, Multi-AZ us-east-1a/1c). Oracle source endpoint via Direct Connect. DMS endpoints (Oracle source, Aurora target for forward; Aurora source with pglogical slot, Oracle target for reverse). Lambda validation functions (validate_counts, validate_checksums, validate_stored_procedures). EventBridge rules (daily validation, 72h reverse task termination). DynamoDB tables (migration_runs, validation_results, cutover_checkpoints, schema_conversions). Secrets Manager (Oracle credentials, Aurora master, application connection strings). CloudTrail with S3 archival.
target_output.workflows: Daily Validation: EventBridge triggers validate_counts Lambda; Python script queries Oracle and Aurora for row counts on all 200 tables; differences > 0.01% written to validation_results DynamoDB and SNS alert sent to DBA Slack channel. Validate_checksums runs on 10% random sample of rows using MD5 on concatenated primary key columns. Cutover Execution: DBA runs cutover.py via SSM Run Command; steps: (1) enable maintenance mode on betting application, (2) wait for Oracle transactions to drain (max 10 min), (3) run final validate_counts — abort if > 0 discrepancy, (4) update application connection strings in Secrets Manager to Aurora endpoint, (5) start reverse DMS task and verify CDCLatencySource < 30s, (6) run smoke tests (insert test bet, verify odds retrieval, check account balance), (7) disable maintenance mode. Rollback: on smoke test failure cutover.py calls rollback.py: restore Secrets Manager to Oracle values, disable maintenance mode, verify Oracle connectivity, alert PagerDuty P1.
target_output.data_model: migration_runs (DynamoDB: {run_id, type ENUM validation/cutover/rollback, started_at, completed_at, status ENUM running/passed/failed, failure_reason}). validation_results (DynamoDB: {run_id, table_name, oracle_row_count, aurora_row_count, delta_pct, checksum_match BOOL, checked_at}). cutover_checkpoints (DynamoDB: {cutover_id, step_name TEXT, status ENUM pending/complete/failed, executed_at, duration_s, notes}). schema_conversions (DynamoDB: {table_name, object_type ENUM table/procedure/view, sct_action ENUM auto_converted/manual_required, manual_change_type ENUM connect_by_cte/dbms_scheduler_pgcron/merge_returning, assigned_to, approved_at, approved_by}). rollback_snapshots (Aurora: automated snapshot taken at cutover T-15min, retained 30 days). The 15 manual procedures tracked: settle_parlay_bet (CONNECT BY), calculate_rolling_odds (CONNECT BY), archive_old_bets (DBMS_SCHEDULER), generate_risk_report (DBMS_SCHEDULER), process_withdrawal (MERGE RETURNING), and 10 additional procedures documented in schema_conversions table. Betting financial entities: player_accounts (id UUID PK, player_id VARCHAR UNIQUE, account_balance_cents BIGINT, currency CHAR(3), credit_limit_cents BIGINT, status ENUM active/suspended/closed, kyc_status ENUM pending/verified), bet_transactions (id UUID PK, player_id FK, bet_id VARCHAR UNIQUE, event_id VARCHAR, market_type VARCHAR, selection VARCHAR, stake_cents BIGINT, odds NUMERIC, potential_payout_cents BIGINT, currency CHAR(3), status ENUM open/settled/voided/cancelled, placed_at TIMESTAMPTZ, settled_at TIMESTAMPTZ), payment_transactions (id UUID PK, player_id FK, transaction_type ENUM deposit/withdrawal/bonus/settlement, amount_cents BIGINT, currency CHAR(3), method ENUM card/bank_transfer/crypto, status ENUM pending/completed/failed/reversed, processed_at TIMESTAMPTZ), ledger_entries (id UUID PK, account_id FK, transaction_id FK, entry_type ENUM debit/credit, amount_cents BIGINT, currency CHAR(3), balance_after_cents BIGINT, posted_at TIMESTAMPTZ).
target_output.api_design: No public API — infrastructure project. Operator interfaces: SSM Run Command documents for cutover.py and rollback.py execution. Lambda function invocations via EventBridge (validation) and manual trigger (ad hoc validation). DynamoDB console for migration status review. CloudWatch dashboards for DMS lag and Aurora metrics. Secrets Manager paths: /sports-betting/oracle/connection (pre-cutover), /sports-betting/aurora/connection (post-cutover), /sports-betting/active-db (application reads this path — cutover.py updates it atomically).
target_output.security_and_compliance: PCI-DSS controls: Aurora CMK encryption (separate key from Oracle on-premises key material, created in AWS KMS). TLS 1.3 for all DMS replication traffic. DBA access via SSM Session Manager only — no direct SSH, no public endpoints. SSM session logs written to encrypted S3 bucket with 90-day retention for PCI audit. CloudTrail data events enabled on Aurora cluster and Secrets Manager. IAM roles follow least-privilege: dms-replication-role (DMS task permissions only), aurora-admin-role (SSM-gated, MFA-required), migration-automation-role (Lambda execution + Secrets Manager read/write). Quarterly ASV scan scheduled on Aurora endpoint post-migration.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Terraform 1.8 manages all migration infrastructure: VPC, subnets, DMS replication instances and tasks, Aurora cluster, EventBridge rules, Lambda functions, DynamoDB tables, IAM roles, KMS keys. Terraform state in S3 with DynamoDB locking. GitHub Actions: terraform validate, terraform plan review gate, pytest for Python scripts. Pre-cutover checklist (automated gate in cutover.py): DMS CDCLatencySource < 30s for 4 consecutive hours, validation_results pass rate 100% for 3 consecutive days, reverse DMS task created and CDCLatencySource < 30s in staging, all 15 stored procedure approvals recorded in schema_conversions. Post-cutover: 72-hour monitoring with PagerDuty on-call rotation; EventBridge auto-terminates reverse DMS task and drops pglogical replication slot at T+72h.
target_output.observability: CloudWatch dashboards: DMS CDCLatencySource (forward and reverse tasks, alert > 30s), Aurora FreeableMemory, WriteIOPS, DBConnections, Aurora replica lag. Validation metrics: tables_passing_count / total_tables (must be 100% for 3 days before cutover). Cutover checkpoint duration per step tracked in DynamoDB cutover_checkpoints and displayed in pre-built CloudWatch dashboard. PagerDuty: forward DMS lag > 30s (P2), validation failure (P2), cutover rollback triggered (P1), reverse DMS lag > 60s post-cutover (P1).
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Migration infrastructure cost: DMS forward instance (db.r5.xlarge) $0.48/hr x 6 weeks = ~$483; DMS reverse instance (db.r5.large) $0.24/hr x 72h = ~$17; Aurora r6g.2xlarge Multi-AZ $0.78/hr ongoing. Total migration execution cost: ~$600 one-time. Oracle license elimination saves $180K/year. Aurora Serverless v2 for staging scales to zero outside business hours. DMS tasks stopped immediately after 72-hour rollback window closes.
target_output.phased_implementation: Week 1: Terraform infrastructure provisioning, AWS SCT schema conversion run, identify 15 manual procedure rewrites, assign owners. Week 2-3: Manual PL/pgSQL rewrite of 15 procedures (CONNECT BY-to-CTE, DBMS_SCHEDULER-to-pg_cron, MERGE RETURNING-to-INSERT ON CONFLICT); unit test each procedure against Aurora Serverless v2. Week 4: DMS full-load of all 200 tables; verify row counts post-load. Week 5-6: DMS CDC replication running; daily validation Lambda active; validate replication lag sub-30s. Week 6: Configure and test reverse DMS task against staging environment; verify Aurora-to-Oracle CDC works with sub-30s lag. Week 7: Staging cutover rehearsal with full cutover.py execution and rollback.py test. Week 8: Production cutover on Sunday 2am-6am; 72-hour Oracle warm standby with reverse DMS; EventBridge terminates reverse task at T+72h; Oracle decommission scheduled Week 12.
target_output.development_guidelines: Terraform modules for DMS task definitions — parameterized for forward vs. reverse task configuration. Python scripts use type hints and pytest fixtures with mock boto3 and psycopg2 clients. cutover.py is idempotent: each step checks DynamoDB cutover_checkpoints before executing to allow safe re-run on partial failure. Stored procedure rewrites must include an equivalence test: run same inputs against Oracle and Aurora and compare outputs before approval. No hardcoded connection strings — all read from Secrets Manager at runtime.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Oracle CONNECT BY queries have edge cases not handled by recursive CTE rewrite for self-referencing bet parlay chains — test with production-scale parlay depth data. Mitigation: equivalence tests with top 1000 most complex parlay records. Risk: pglogical replication slot for reverse DMS task blocks Aurora VACUUM if not terminated promptly — mitigated by EventBridge auto-termination at 72h. Tradeoff: Aurora Serverless v2 for staging vs. provisioned — ACU scaling adds 20-30ms connection overhead not present in production provisioned instance; smoke tests use provisioned staging clone for cutover rehearsal. Tradeoff: DynamoDB for migration metadata over RDS — avoids adding another PostgreSQL instance to migrate and provides serverless availability during the migration window.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Reverse DMS CDC task designed for 72-hour rollback window — resolves ISS-001. 15 stored procedures enumerated with rewrite strategy: 8 CONNECT BY-to-CTE, 4 DBMS_SCHEDULER-to-pg_cron, 3 MERGE RETURNING-to-INSERT ON CONFLICT — resolves ISS-002. Cutover window confirmed Sunday 2am-6am. Oracle decommission scheduled Week 12 after 72-hour rollback window closes and 2-week parallel observation period.

metadata.notes: Round 2 sports betting Oracle-to-Aurora migration; resolves critical reverse CDC task and stored procedure scope gaps.

project_goal.value: CLI tool for sports platform engineers to inspect and manage Kafka topic partitions across microservices powering a live sports event data platform, supporting lag monitoring per consumer group, topic configuration auditing, and automated partition rebalancing triggered by consumer lag thresholds
project_goal.rationale: Operational tooling to reduce manual Kafka administration burden

target_users.value: Platform engineers and SREs operating the live sports event data Kafka cluster
target_users.rationale: Internal operators with Kafka admin access

risk_level.rationale: Read-heavy tool; rebalancing commands require explicit --apply flag to prevent accidental changes

mvp_scope.rationale: Core operational commands for MVP

frontend_stack.rationale: No frontend component

backend_stack.rationale: Go compiles to single static binary; excellent Kafka client library support via franz-go

project.goal: Kafka partition management CLI for sports event platform engineers

backend.framework: cobra CLI framework, franz-go Kafka client

security.auth: Kafka mTLS via flags or environment variables
security.credentials: Never stored in CLI config or logs

data.storage: Stateless; audit snapshots to local JSON files
data.kafka: Admin API for consumer group offsets, topic configs, partition assignments

devops.distribution: GoReleaser to GitHub Releases + internal Homebrew tap

reasoner_reviews.product: The --apply flag safety requirement for rebalancing is the most critical UX decision. The dry-run output must be actionable — engineers need to see exactly which partition-to-broker assignments will change, not just 'rebalancing will be performed'. Round 1 did not show a concrete dry-run output format.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: franz-go is the right Kafka client choice over Sarama for Go 1.22 — better performance and maintained actively. The lag computation requires fetching both the consumer group committed offset and the partition high watermark, then computing lag = high_watermark - committed_offset per partition. The critical flaw from round 1 was the rebalance command: it called Kafka's AdminClient.AlterPartitionReassignments which is only available in Kafka 2.4+; the plan should use PreferredReplicaElection for simple leader rebalancing which is available in Kafka 3.x without full partition reassignment.
reasoner_reviews.security: mTLS certificate paths must not appear in debug log output at any verbosity level — cert paths could leak internal PKI structure. The --debug flag should log Kafka request/response metadata but never cert file content.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Binary size with franz-go and cobra should be approximately 15-18MB stripped — well under the 25MB limit. GoReleaser with -ldflags='-s -w' and optional UPX compression keeps binaries lean.

backend.service_design: CLI structured as cobra root command with three subcommands: 'lag' (consumer group lag inspection), 'audit' (topic configuration review), 'rebalance' (partition leader election). Global flags: --brokers (comma-separated bootstrap servers), --tls-cert, --tls-key, --tls-ca, --output (table|json), --debug.
backend.api_patterns: No HTTP API — Kafka admin API via franz-go client. lag command: ListConsumerGroupOffsets + DescribeTopicPartitions -> compute lag per partition. audit command: DescribeTopicConfigs + ListPartitionReassignments. rebalance command: dry-run by default shows reassignment plan in table/JSON; --apply triggers ElectLeaders (preferred replica election) not full partition reassignment.
backend.business_modules: KafkaClient (franz-go wrapper, mTLS config builder from flags/env), LagAnalyzer (offset delta computation, sorts by lag descending, applies --threshold filter), AuditReporter (topic config comparison against expected values, flags non-default retention and replication), RebalancePlanner (computes imbalanced partitions via leader distribution analysis, outputs election plan), OutputFormatter (colored table via lipgloss, JSON via encoding/json), PushGatewayExporter (Prometheus metrics push for lag snapshot history).
backend.backend_risks: PreferredReplicaElection is a best-effort operation — Kafka will not elect the preferred replica if it is out-of-sync (ISR). Must check ISR before issuing election and warn if preferred replica is not in ISR.

security.auth_design: TLS config built from --tls-cert, --tls-key, --tls-ca flags; fallback to KAFKA_TLS_CERT_PATH, KAFKA_TLS_KEY_PATH, KAFKA_TLS_CA_PATH env vars; if no TLS config provided, proceeds with plaintext and emits a warning
security.secrets_management: No credentials stored; never log TLS cert paths or cert file contents at any verbosity level; redact --tls-cert/--tls-key values in --debug output
security.security_risks: Engineer running audit command against a production Kafka cluster with an overly-broad Kafka ACL may inadvertently expose partition configurations to stdout in shared terminal sessions — document that audit output may contain sensitive topic names

data.entities: No persistent entities. In-memory structs: PartitionLag{Topic, Partition, ConsumerGroup, CommittedOffset, HighWatermark, Lag int64}, TopicAuditResult{TopicName, PartitionCount, ReplicationFactor, Retention, Configs map}, RebalancePlan{Topic, Partition, CurrentLeader, PreferredLeader, IsPreferredInISR bool}, AuditSnapshot{GeneratedAt, BrokerAddresses, ConsumerGroups, PartitionLags, TopicAudits}, PushGatewayMetrics{ConsumerGroup, Topic, Partition, LagValue float64}.
data.schema_notes: AuditSnapshot serializes to JSON via encoding/json for --output json mode and for snapshot file export (--snapshot-file flag). No external schema validation required.
data.data_risks: High partition counts (1000+ partitions per topic for large sports event topics) may cause ListConsumerGroupOffsets to return slowly — add --timeout flag with 30s default.

devops.deployment_topology: Single static binary; no server deployment. GitHub Releases for versioned binaries (linux-amd64, linux-arm64, darwin-amd64, darwin-arm64). Internal Homebrew tap formula auto-generated by GoReleaser.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: golangci-lint (revive, gosec, staticcheck), go test ./... with -race flag, goreleaser build (all platforms, --snapshot for non-tag runs), goreleaser release on semver tag push.
devops.observability_stack: No server-side observability. Optional --push-gateway flag pushes lag metrics to Prometheus pushgateway for historical trending via existing Grafana dashboards.

ISS-001.title: Rebalance command uses AlterPartitionReassignments instead of PreferredReplicaElection
ISS-001.detail: Round 1 plan described the rebalance command as calling AdminClient.AlterPartitionReassignments. This triggers full partition data movement which is disruptive. For leader imbalance correction, PreferredReplicaElection (ElectLeaders) is the correct and non-disruptive operation. Must be corrected.

previous_plan.title: Kafka Partition Management CLI v1
previous_plan.executive_summary: Go 1.22 CLI with cobra and franz-go providing lag inspection, topic configuration auditing, and partition rebalancing for sports platform Kafka clusters.
previous_plan.architecture_overview: Single static binary; three cobra subcommands: lag, audit, rebalance; franz-go Kafka client with mTLS support.
previous_plan.workflows: lag command fetches committed offsets and high watermarks, computes per-partition lag, renders colored table sorted by lag descending. rebalance command calls AlterPartitionReassignments (INCORRECT) by default in dry-run mode; --apply executes.
previous_plan.technology_stack: Go 1.22, cobra, franz-go, lipgloss for colored output, GoReleaser.

target_output.thinking_summary: Round 2 must correct the rebalancing API from AlterPartitionReassignments (triggers data movement) to ElectLeaders (preferred replica election, non-disruptive). The ElectLeaders API calls Kafka's preferred replica election which simply promotes the preferred replica as partition leader without moving any partition data — appropriate for leader skew correction. Also need to specify the dry-run output table format so engineers can review the election plan before using --apply. ISR check must precede any election to warn on out-of-sync preferred replicas.

fix_report.action_taken: Replaced AlterPartitionReassignments with ElectLeaders (preferred replica election) in the rebalance command. RebalancePlanner now: (1) calls DescribeTopicPartitions to get current leader and preferred replica per partition, (2) calls DescribeTopicPartitions replica ISR list to check if preferred replica is in-sync, (3) for partitions where current leader != preferred replica, adds to election plan with ISR-Safe=true if preferred is in ISR else ISR-Safe=false with WARNING flag, (4) with --apply calls franz-go AdminClient.ElectLeaders with ElectionTypePreferred for all ISR-safe partitions; skips ISR-unsafe partitions and logs warning.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Rebalance command corrects leader skew without triggering any partition data movement; ISR-unsafe partitions are skipped with warning

fix_report.action_taken: Defined dry-run output format: colored table with columns TOPIC | PARTITION | CURRENT LEADER | PREFERRED LEADER | ISR-SAFE | ACTION where ACTION is 'elect' for ISR-safe partitions and 'SKIP (not in ISR)' for unsafe. JSON output mode produces array of {topic, partition, current_leader_id, preferred_leader_id, is_preferred_in_isr, action} objects. Summary line at end of output: 'Dry run: X partitions eligible for election, Y skipped (not in ISR). Use --apply to execute.'
fix_report.expected_outcome: Engineers see exact partition election plan before applying; skipped partitions explained clearly

target_output.title: Kafka Partition Management CLI — Architecture v2
target_output.executive_summary: A single static Go 1.22 binary providing three cobra subcommands for sports platform Kafka cluster operations: 'lag' for per-partition consumer group lag inspection with colored table output, 'audit' for topic configuration review against expected defaults, and 'rebalance' for non-disruptive preferred leader election (dry-run by default, --apply to execute) with ISR safety checks that skip out-of-sync preferred replicas. Distributed via GitHub Releases and an internal Homebrew tap; mTLS credentials passed via flags or environment variables; binary size under 18MB.
target_output.architecture_overview: Single compiled Go 1.22 binary with zero runtime dependencies beyond the Kafka cluster. Three cobra subcommands share a global KafkaClient (franz-go with mTLS config built from flags/env). lag subcommand fetches ListConsumerGroupOffsets and DescribeTopicPartitions high watermarks from Kafka admin API, computes lag = high_watermark - committed_offset per partition, and renders sorted table via lipgloss. audit subcommand calls DescribeTopicConfigs and compares against configurable expected values. rebalance subcommand uses ElectLeaders (preferred replica election) — not AlterPartitionReassignments — checking ISR membership before any election. OutputFormatter renders colored tables or JSON. Optional PushGatewayExporter pushes lag metrics via --push-gateway flag.
target_output.technology_stack: Language: Go 1.22. CLI framework: cobra 1.8. Kafka client: franz-go 1.16 (Kafka 3.x compatible). Terminal UI: lipgloss 0.9 for colored table rendering. JSON output: encoding/json (standard library). Build: GoReleaser 1.24 with -ldflags='-s -w' for binary stripping. Lint: golangci-lint with revive, gosec, staticcheck. Test: go test with -race flag. Distribution: GitHub Releases (linux-amd64, linux-arm64, darwin-amd64, darwin-arm64) + internal Homebrew tap.
target_output.functional_feature_map: lag command: sporkctl lag --group live-scores-consumer --topic sports-events --threshold 1000 outputs table of Topic|Partition|Committed Offset|High Watermark|Lag|Status where Status is OK/WARNING/CRITICAL based on --threshold. audit command: sporkctl audit --topic sports-events shows current vs. expected retention.ms, replication.factor, min.insync.replicas; flags non-default values in red. rebalance command (dry-run): sporkctl rebalance --topic sports-events shows election plan table; --apply executes ElectLeaders for ISR-safe partitions. Snapshot export: sporkctl lag --snapshot-file lag-snapshot.json writes AuditSnapshot JSON. Prometheus push: sporkctl lag --push-gateway http://pushgateway:9091 pushes partition lag gauges.
target_output.system_components: cobra root command (sporkctl) with global flags: --brokers, --tls-cert, --tls-key, --tls-ca, --output, --debug, --timeout. KafkaClient: franz-go client with mTLS TLS config builder from flags with env fallback. LagAnalyzer: ListConsumerGroupOffsets + DescribeTopicPartitions, lag computation, threshold classification, sorts by lag descending. AuditReporter: DescribeTopicConfigs, configurable expected-values map loaded from --config file. RebalancePlanner: DescribeTopicPartitions replica+ISR analysis, ElectLeaders API call with ElectionTypePreferred, ISR safety gating. OutputFormatter: lipgloss table renderer and encoding/json encoder. PushGatewayExporter: prometheus/client_golang pushgateway client. GoReleaser config: four target platforms, binary name sporkctl.
target_output.workflows: Lag Check: engineer runs sporkctl lag --group live-scores-consumer --topic sports-events; KafkaClient connects with mTLS; LagAnalyzer calls ListConsumerGroupOffsets for the group and DescribeTopicPartitions to get high watermarks for each partition; computes lag = high_watermark - committed_offset; renders table sorted by lag descending with red rows for lag > --threshold; exits non-zero if any partition exceeds critical threshold (for CI pipeline integration). Rebalance Dry-Run: engineer runs sporkctl rebalance --topic sports-events; RebalancePlanner calls DescribeTopicPartitions; for each partition checks current_leader != preferred_replica; for those partitions checks preferred_replica in ISR list; renders table: TOPIC|PARTITION|CURRENT LEADER|PREFERRED LEADER|ISR-SAFE|ACTION; partitions not in ISR show 'SKIP (not in ISR)' in ACTION column; summary line: 'Dry run: 8 partitions eligible for election, 2 skipped. Use --apply to execute.' Rebalance Apply: engineer adds --apply flag; RebalancePlanner calls franz-go AdminClient.ElectLeaders with ElectionTypePreferred for ISR-safe partitions only; confirms with output 'Elected preferred leader for 8 partitions. 2 skipped (not in ISR).'
target_output.data_model: No persistent storage. In-memory structs: PartitionLag{TopicName string, Partition int32, ConsumerGroup string, CommittedOffset int64, HighWatermark int64, Lag int64, Status LagStatus}. TopicAuditResult{TopicName string, PartitionCount int, ReplicationFactor int, ConfigDiffs []ConfigDiff}. ConfigDiff{Key string, CurrentValue string, ExpectedValue string, Deviation bool}. ElectionCandidate{TopicName string, Partition int32, CurrentLeaderID int32, PreferredLeaderID int32, IsSPreferredInISR bool, Action ElectionAction}. AuditSnapshot{GeneratedAt time.Time, ClusterID string, BootstrapBrokers []string, PartitionLags []PartitionLag, TopicAudits []TopicAuditResult}.
target_output.api_design: No HTTP API. Kafka admin API interactions via franz-go: (1) ListConsumerGroupOffsets(ctx, groupID, topicPartitions) for committed offsets. (2) MetadataForTopics(ctx, topics) for partition high watermarks. (3) DescribeTopicConfigs(ctx, topicNames) for audit configs. (4) DescribeTopicPartitions(ctx, topicNames) for leader and ISR info. (5) ElectLeaders(ctx, ElectionTypePreferred, topicPartitions) for --apply rebalance. All calls respect --timeout flag (default 30s). Exit codes: 0 (success), 1 (lag threshold exceeded), 2 (kafka connection error), 3 (invalid arguments).
target_output.security_and_compliance: mTLS config built at startup from --tls-cert/--tls-key/--tls-ca flags with fallback to KAFKA_TLS_CERT_PATH/KAFKA_TLS_KEY_PATH/KAFKA_TLS_CA_PATH env vars. TLS cert file paths never logged at any verbosity level — --debug logs Kafka request metadata only, not cert content or paths. If no TLS config provided, emits warning 'Warning: connecting without TLS — ensure cluster is in secure network' and continues. Rebalance --apply writes action log to stderr with timestamp, topic, partition count for audit trail. No credentials persisted by CLI.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: GitHub Actions CI: golangci-lint (revive gosec staticcheck), go test ./... -race -cover -coverprofile=coverage.out (80% coverage gate), goreleaser build --snapshot --clean (PR builds). On semver tag: goreleaser release publishes to GitHub Releases and Homebrew tap formula auto-updated. Binary filenames: sporkctl-{version}-{OS}-{arch}. Internal Homebrew tap: engineers install via brew install sports-platform/tap/sporkctl. No server infrastructure to deploy or manage.
target_output.observability: No server metrics. CLI exit codes enable CI pipeline integration: exit 1 on lag threshold exceeded allows alerting pipelines to fail. --push-gateway flag pushes Prometheus Gauge metrics: kafka_consumer_lag{consumer_group, topic, partition} to Prometheus pushgateway for historical lag trending on existing Grafana dashboards. --debug flag logs franz-go request/response metadata to stderr for troubleshooting Kafka connectivity issues. --output json enables machine-readable parsing in monitoring scripts.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Zero infrastructure cost — stateless binary. franz-go admin API calls are O(partition_count) for lag check — at 1000 partitions per topic, ListConsumerGroupOffsets returns within 200ms on a healthy cluster. Binary size: 17.2MB stripped with -ldflags='-s -w'. Engineers install once from GitHub Releases; GoReleaser automates multi-platform builds in CI.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (complete): lag command, audit command, dry-run rebalance, mTLS flag support, lipgloss table output, JSON output mode, GoReleaser CI/CD, GitHub Releases distribution. Phase 2 (this round): resolve ISS-001 by replacing AlterPartitionReassignments with ElectLeaders and adding ISR safety check; resolve ISS-002 by defining and implementing dry-run output format; add Prometheus pushgateway integration; add internal Homebrew tap. Phase 3: interactive TUI mode with real-time lag auto-refresh using bubbletea, schema registry audit subcommand, Kafka ACL review subcommand.
target_output.development_guidelines: cobra command functions must not contain business logic — delegate to domain types (LagAnalyzer, RebalancePlanner). Table rendering via lipgloss only in OutputFormatter — no fmt.Printf table formatting in command handlers. go test -race on all packages in CI — franz-go Kafka client interactions mocked via interface for unit tests. ElectLeaders call must be preceded by ISR check — enforce via code review checklist item. --apply flag must require explicit user confirmation in interactive mode: print election plan and prompt 'Apply? [y/N]' unless --yes flag is also passed.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: ElectLeaders is asynchronous — Kafka acknowledges the request but leader election may not complete immediately; rebalance command exits after API call with note 'Election initiated — verify with sporkctl lag after 30s'. Risk: High partition counts (>5000 partitions) may cause franz-go DescribeTopicPartitions to time out at default 30s — increase --timeout for large clusters. Tradeoff: lipgloss over bubbletea for table rendering — simpler output with no interactive TUI complexity for MVP; bubbletea deferred to Phase 3. Tradeoff: no persistent audit snapshot storage — engineers must redirect JSON output to files manually; acceptable for CLI tool scope.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Rebalance command uses ElectLeaders (preferred replica election) not AlterPartitionReassignments — resolves ISS-001. Dry-run output format defined: TOPIC|PARTITION|CURRENT LEADER|PREFERRED LEADER|ISR-SAFE|ACTION table with summary line — resolves ISS-002. ISR-unsafe partitions skipped with warning, not elected. ElectLeaders is asynchronous — document verification step. Distribution confirmed via GitHub Releases + Homebrew tap.

metadata.notes: Round 2 Kafka partition management CLI; corrects Kafka rebalance API and defines dry-run output format.
