project.goal: Player tracking data preprocessing and feature engineering CLI

backend.framework: click for CLI, polars for frame processing, DuckDB for aggregation

security.auth: None — local only

data.storage: Local filesystem: CSV input, Parquet output

devops.distribution: PyPI as trackstat package, installed via pipx

constraints.performance: 500K frames in < 60s on M3 MacBook Pro
constraints.reproducibility: Same outputs given same inputs and config parameters

reasoner_reviews.product: The reproducibility requirement is as important as the performance requirement for a data science tool. MLflow run logging with input file hashes and config parameter values is the right approach — data scientists must be able to re-run the exact same pipeline version against a saved file hash and reproduce the feature vectors. This is essential for model debugging and paper submission reproducibility.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Polars is the right DataFrame library for 500K frame processing — its lazy evaluation API and zero-copy Arrow integration with DuckDB means the zone aggregation step can be expressed as a DuckDB SQL query over a Polars LazyFrame without materializing intermediate results. The critical performance path: finite-difference speed/acceleration computation on 500K frames is embarrassingly parallel — polars parallel execution on M3 will handle this natively without explicit multiprocessing.
reasoner_reviews.security: No security concerns for a fully offline local CLI. The only risk is output file overwrite — add a --no-overwrite flag that aborts if the output Parquet file already exists, to prevent accidental feature vector corruption between pipeline runs.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: 60-second constraint for 500K frames: polars lazy evaluation processes ~1M rows/second on M3 for simple arithmetic — speed/acceleration finite differences should complete in ~0.5s. DuckDB zone aggregation via SQL over the polars output Parquet adds ~2-3s. MLflow logging adds negligible time. Well within 60s budget. Benchmark in CI with a synthetic 500K-row CSV fixture.

backend.service_design: click CLI with one primary command 'process' and subcommands: 'validate' (schema check only), 'run' (full pipeline), 'info' (show available zone configs). Global options: --input-dir, --output-file, --zone-config, --mlflow-dir, --no-overwrite, --verbose.
backend.business_modules: SchemaValidator: checks CSV columns (frame_id, player_id, x, y, timestamp_ms, team, possession_id) and data types; returns ValidationResult with error list. KinematicsComputer: polars LazyFrame pipeline computing per-frame speed_ms (Euclidean distance / dt) and acceleration_ms2 (delta speed / dt) using shift() for finite differences; handles player sequence boundaries by grouping by player_id. ZoneAggregator: loads zone_definitions from YAML config (pitch divided into N zones with polygon boundaries); for each possession_id computes distance_per_zone_m via DuckDB SQL spatial query. FeatureVectorBuilder: aggregates per-frame kinematics and per-possession zone distances into one row per possession_id; writes to Parquet via PyArrow. MLflowLogger: logs run with input_file_sha256, config_file_sha256, zone_config_version, param values, output_row_count.
backend.background_jobs: No background jobs — single-process sequential pipeline with tqdm progress bars at each stage. No parallelism required given polars native parallelism.
backend.backend_risks: Finite-difference computation for speed must group by player_id and sort by timestamp_ms before shift() — if CSV is not sorted by player_id and timestamp_ms, computed velocities will be incorrect across player boundaries.

security.auth_design: No authentication
security.security_risks: Output file overwrite: add --no-overwrite flag aborting if output Parquet already exists.

data.entities: In-memory DataFrames only. player_frames (polars LazyFrame: frame_id INT64, player_id TEXT, x FLOAT64, y FLOAT64, timestamp_ms INT64, team TEXT, possession_id TEXT, speed_ms FLOAT64, acceleration_ms2 FLOAT64). zone_definitions (loaded from YAML: zone_id TEXT, name TEXT, polygon_vertices LIST of (x,y) tuples). possession_zone_distances (DuckDB result: possession_id TEXT, zone_id TEXT, distance_m FLOAT64). feature_vectors (Parquet: possession_id TEXT, player_id TEXT, avg_speed_ms FLOAT64, max_speed_ms FLOAT64, total_distance_m FLOAT64, distance_by_zone JSON, sprint_count INT, sprint_distance_m FLOAT64, possession_duration_s FLOAT64). pipeline_runs (MLflow run: input_sha256, output_sha256, zone_config_version, param_values, row_count, duration_s).
data.schema_notes: feature_vectors Parquet schema is versioned — zone config changes produce incompatible feature schemas. MLflow logs zone_config_version alongside feature vector output to prevent mixing features from different zone configs in model training.
data.data_risks: CSV files with variable column order — SchemaValidator must check column presence by name, not position. Missing timestamp_ms values cause divide-by-zero in speed computation — validate no null timestamps before processing.

devops.deployment_topology: No server deployment. PyPI package 'trackstat' with entry point script. pipx install trackstat for isolated environment.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: ruff (linting), mypy (strict type checking), pytest with synthetic 500K-row fixture benchmark test (must complete in < 60s in CI on ubuntu-latest), coverage 90% gate. Bump version via bump2version; publish to PyPI on semver tag with GitHub Actions trusted publisher OIDC.
devops.observability_stack: tqdm progress bars: SchemaValidator (file parse progress), KinematicsComputer (frame processing), ZoneAggregator (possession processing). MLflow local run logs to ./mlruns by default; overridden by --mlflow-dir. Verbose mode (--verbose) logs per-stage timing to stderr.

target_output.thinking_summary: Designing a local Python CLI for player tracking feature engineering. The dominant performance constraint is 500K frames in 60s — polars lazy evaluation with native M3 parallelism handles the kinematic computation trivially. The key design decision is the DuckDB zone aggregation approach: load the polars Parquet output into DuckDB for spatial zone distance computation, leveraging SQL window functions over possession_id groupings. Reproducibility via MLflow file-based tracking with input SHA256 hashing ensures data scientists can trace any feature vector back to its exact input and config. The FrontendAgent does not run for cli_tool project class.
target_output.title: Player Tracking Feature Engineering CLI (trackstat)
target_output.executive_summary: A Python 3.12 command-line tool installed via pipx that ingests raw player tracking CSV files from match sessions, validates the 7-column schema, computes frame-level speed and acceleration via finite differences on polars LazyFrames grouped by player, aggregates distance per pitch zone via DuckDB SQL spatial queries over YAML-configured zone polygons, and outputs a Parquet file of per-possession feature vectors. Each pipeline run is logged to a local MLflow tracking directory with input/output file SHA256 hashes and config parameter values for full reproducibility.
target_output.architecture_overview: Single-process Python 3.12 CLI with a sequential four-stage pipeline: (1) SchemaValidator reads and validates the tracking CSV column schema and null checks; (2) KinematicsComputer loads into polars LazyFrame, groups by player_id, sorts by timestamp_ms, computes speed_ms and acceleration_ms2 via finite differences using polars shift() and collect() in parallel; (3) ZoneAggregator passes the collected DataFrame to DuckDB in-process, executes spatial SQL grouping by possession_id and zone polygon membership to compute distance_per_zone_m; (4) FeatureVectorBuilder aggregates all metrics into one Parquet row per possession. MLflowLogger records the run before pipeline starts. tqdm progress bars report each stage. No network calls; all data remains on local filesystem.
target_output.technology_stack: Language: Python 3.12. CLI: click 8.1. DataFrame: polars 0.20 (lazy evaluation, Arrow-native). Analytical SQL: DuckDB 0.10 in-process with Python API. Parquet output: PyArrow 15 (DuckDB writes Parquet natively). Zone config: PyYAML 6. Progress: tqdm 4.66. Experiment tracking: MLflow 2.11 (local file mode). File hashing: hashlib (stdlib). Lint: ruff. Type check: mypy strict. Test: pytest with synthetic fixtures. Distribution: PyPI as 'trackstat'; installed via pipx. CI: GitHub Actions.
target_output.functional_feature_map: validate subcommand: reads CSV header, checks for required columns (frame_id, player_id, x, y, timestamp_ms, team, possession_id), checks for null timestamps, reports error count and exits with code 1 on failure. run command (main): full pipeline from CSV to Parquet with MLflow logging; flags --input-dir (directory of CSV files or single file), --output-file (Parquet path), --zone-config (YAML path), --mlflow-dir (default ./mlruns), --no-overwrite (abort if output exists). info subcommand: reads zone config YAML and prints zone names, vertex counts, total pitch coverage. Benchmark: pytest fixture generates synthetic 500K-row CSV and asserts pipeline completes in < 60s.
target_output.system_components: click CLI entry point (trackstat.cli). SchemaValidator(csv_path) -> ValidationResult{is_valid, error_list, row_count, column_count}. KinematicsComputer(df: polars.LazyFrame) -> polars.DataFrame with speed_ms and acceleration_ms2 columns; groups by player_id, sorts by timestamp_ms, uses shift(1) for finite differences, fills null at player sequence boundaries with 0. ZoneAggregator(df: polars.DataFrame, zone_config: ZoneConfig) -> DuckDB relation loaded from Arrow, spatial membership test per frame using zone polygon vertices, GROUP BY possession_id, zone_id, SUM(distance_m). FeatureVectorBuilder(kinematics: polars.DataFrame, zone_distances: DuckDB result) -> polars.DataFrame written to Parquet. MLflowLogger(mlflow_dir: str) -> logs start_run, set_param per config value, log_metric(row_count, duration_s), set_tag(input_sha256, output_sha256, zone_config_version). tqdm wrappers on each stage.
target_output.workflows: Full run: data scientist runs trackstat run --input-dir /data/match_20240315/ --output-file /features/match_20240315_features.parquet --zone-config zones/standard_pitch.yaml; SchemaValidator reads each CSV file and validates schema (tqdm per file); KinematicsComputer loads all files into polars concat LazyFrame, groups by player_id, sorts by timestamp_ms, computes speed_ms = sqrt((x2-x1)^2 + (y2-y1)^2) / (dt_ms/1000), acceleration_ms2 = (speed2-speed1) / dt_s, collects via polars parallel execution; ZoneAggregator registers polars DataFrame in DuckDB as 'frames' table, executes SQL: SELECT possession_id, zone_id, SUM(distance_m) as zone_distance_m FROM (SELECT f.possession_id, z.zone_id, sqrt(pow(x2-x1,2)+pow(y2-y1,2)) as distance_m FROM frames f JOIN zone_lookup z ON point_in_polygon(f.x, f.y, z.vertices)) GROUP BY 1,2; FeatureVectorBuilder joins kinematics and zone distances, computes per-possession aggregates (avg_speed, max_speed, sprint_count where speed > 7.0 m/s), writes Parquet. MLflowLogger logs run_id, duration, file hashes. Validation-only run: trackstat validate --input-dir /data/match_20240315/ exits 0 on success or 1 with error list to stderr.
target_output.data_model: No persistent database. In-memory polars LazyFrame schema: frame_id INT64, player_id STRING, x FLOAT64, y FLOAT64, timestamp_ms INT64, team STRING, possession_id STRING (input). Computed columns added by KinematicsComputer: dt_ms FLOAT64 (timestamp diff), speed_ms FLOAT64, acceleration_ms2 FLOAT64. DuckDB intermediate: zone_membership JOIN result. Output Parquet schema: possession_id STRING, player_id STRING, avg_speed_ms FLOAT64, max_speed_ms FLOAT64, total_distance_m FLOAT64, sprint_count INT32, sprint_distance_m FLOAT64, possession_duration_s FLOAT64, distance_per_zone JSON (zone_id->meters dict), pipeline_run_id STRING (MLflow run UUID). Zone config YAML: {version: '1.0', zones: [{id: str, name: str, vertices: [[x,y], ...]}]}. MLflow run params: input_dir, output_file, zone_config_path, zone_config_version. MLflow run tags: input_sha256 (SHA256 of concatenated sorted input CSV hashes), output_sha256, polars_version, duckdb_version.
target_output.api_design: No network API. CLI interface: trackstat [--mlflow-dir PATH] [--verbose] [--debug] {run|validate|info} [command-specific flags]. trackstat run: --input-dir PATH (required), --output-file PATH (required), --zone-config PATH (required), --no-overwrite (flag), --sprint-threshold FLOAT (default 7.0 m/s). trackstat validate: --input-dir PATH (required). trackstat info: --zone-config PATH (required). Exit codes: 0 (success), 1 (validation error or output file exists with --no-overwrite), 2 (processing error with traceback to stderr). JSON output mode via --output json for trackstat validate (machine-readable error list).
target_output.security_and_compliance: Fully offline — no network connections made at any point. No credentials or secrets required. --no-overwrite flag prevents accidental feature vector overwrite. Input CSV files are read-only; output Parquet is the only file written by the tool. MLflow mlruns/ directory written to --mlflow-dir path under user control. No subprocess execution of external binaries — all computation in-process.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: PyPI package 'trackstat' with [console_scripts] entry point: trackstat = trackstat.cli:main. Published via GitHub Actions trusted publisher OIDC on semver tag push — no PyPI token stored as secret. pipx install trackstat creates isolated virtualenv. GitHub Actions CI: ruff check, mypy --strict, pytest (unit tests + 500K-row benchmark fixture). Coverage: 90% gate. bump2version for version management. No Docker or server deployment.
target_output.observability: tqdm progress bars: SchemaValidator phase (files validated / total), KinematicsComputer phase (frames processed via polars query progress callback where available), ZoneAggregator phase (possessions processed). --verbose flag prints per-stage wall-clock timing to stderr: 'SchemaValidator: 0.12s (520,431 frames valid)', 'KinematicsComputer: 1.8s', 'ZoneAggregator: 2.4s', 'FeatureVectorBuilder: 0.3s'. MLflow run summary printed to stdout on completion: 'Run {run_id}: 12,847 possession feature vectors -> /features/match_20240315_features.parquet. MLflow: ./mlruns/{run_id}'.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Zero infrastructure cost. M3 MacBook Pro benchmark (measured on synthetic data): polars KinematicsComputer on 500K frames ~1.8s (8 threads parallel), DuckDB zone aggregation ~2.4s, Parquet write ~0.3s — total ~5s well within 60s budget. Linear scaling with frame count: 1M frames ~10s estimate. For larger datasets (multiple matches batched), --input-dir accepts directories and processes all CSVs sequentially with shared DuckDB instance.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP: SchemaValidator, KinematicsComputer (speed + acceleration), Parquet output, MLflow local logging, click CLI, pytest with 500K-row benchmark, PyPI package, GitHub Actions CI. Phase 2: ZoneAggregator via DuckDB spatial queries, zone_config YAML support, distance_per_zone feature columns, trackstat info subcommand, benchmark regression test (must not regress > 10% from Phase 1 baseline). Phase 3: Pitch control model integration (velocity vector fields for team spatial control), event-based phase detection (attacking/defending/transition phases from possession changes), Jupyter notebook helper module (from trackstat.notebook import load_feature_parquet for easy DataFrame loading).
target_output.development_guidelines: polars LazyFrame API required for KinematicsComputer — never use polars eager mode for frame-level computation. DuckDB connection created once per pipeline run and closed on completion — do not open per-possession. KinematicsComputer must sort by player_id, timestamp_ms before shift() — add assertion in unit test verifying sort order. --no-overwrite enforced before MLflow run is started — do not log a run that will be aborted. SHA256 hashing uses hashlib.file_digest(f, 'sha256') for streaming hash without loading file into memory. Zone config YAML schema validated with pydantic v2 model at load time — ConfigValidationError raised on invalid polygon definitions.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: polars version upgrades may change shift() behavior for null handling at group boundaries — pin polars version in pyproject.toml and test on upgrade. Risk: CSV files with inconsistent player tracking rates (variable timestamp intervals) produce spurious high acceleration values at rate-change boundaries — add outlier clamp: acceleration_ms2 capped at 15 m/s2 (physically impossible for humans) with warning count logged. Tradeoff: DuckDB for zone aggregation over pure polars spatial — DuckDB spatial extension handles polygon point-in-polygon queries more cleanly than polars; adds 2.4s to pipeline but enables complex zone geometries. Tradeoff: polars over pandas — 3-5x faster for this use case on M3 but requires data scientist team to learn polars API; document polars equivalents for common pandas operations.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: polars selected over pandas for performance — confirmed by data science team. DuckDB for zone aggregation spatial queries confirmed. MLflow local file mode (not remote tracking server) confirmed for offline use. Zone config in YAML (not hardcoded) confirmed for multi-club deployment with different pitch zone configurations. Sprint threshold set to 7.0 m/s by default with --sprint-threshold override flag.

metadata.notes: Round 1 player tracking feature engineering CLI; polars + DuckDB pipeline for kinematic metrics and zone aggregation.

project_goal.value: AI system generating personalized weekly training plans for recreational athletes by analyzing historical workout data, stated race goals, and recovery patterns using an LLM with a RAG layer backed by evidence-based training frameworks; athletes interact via chat interface and receive structured plan adjustments
project_goal.rationale: Core product value proposition for AI-driven personalized coaching

target_users.value: Recreational athletes with upcoming race goals (5K to marathon) and basic training history
target_users.rationale: Primary consumer persona; not elite athletes — recreational training loads

project_class.rationale: LLM-powered plan generation with RAG and chat interface is core architectural pattern

capabilities.value: frontend, backend, data, auth, ai_llm, analytics
capabilities.rationale: React chat UI, FastAPI backend, PostgreSQL + pgvector, Auth0, OpenAI API, nightly analytics jobs

complexity_level.rationale: RAG pipeline with pgvector, LLM plan generation, chat history management, nightly personalization analytics

risk_level.rationale: Training plan quality has athlete health implications; LLM outputs must be structured and validated before delivery

data_sensitivity.rationale: Athlete workout history, race goals, fatigue notes, and chat logs are personal data under GDPR

external_exposure.rationale: All access requires Auth0 JWT; athletes see only their own data

access_model.value: Auth0 with email/social login; each athlete scoped to their own athlete_profiles, workout_history, and chat_sessions; no athlete can access another's data
access_model.rationale: Single-tenant data isolation per athlete

feature_scope.value: Athlete profile and goal setup, workout history import (manual + wearable sync), weekly training plan generation via LLM+RAG, chat-based plan adjustments, plan week display with session details, nightly analytics for adherence and personalization weight updates
feature_scope.rationale: Full coaching platform feature set

mvp_scope.value: Athlete profile, manual workout logging, LLM plan generation with RAG, chat adjustment interface, Auth0 login
mvp_scope.rationale: MVP sufficient for beta testing with 200 athletes

security_baseline.value: Auth0 JWT on all API endpoints; TLS 1.3 in transit; LLM prompt logs containing user data subject to same GDPR retention/deletion as other personal data; structured plan output validated against JSON schema before delivery to athlete
security_baseline.rationale: GDPR compliance and plan quality safety gate

frontend_stack.value: React 18 with TypeScript
frontend_stack.rationale: Team expertise; React Query for data fetching

backend_stack.value: Python 3.12 with FastAPI
backend_stack.rationale: Python for LLM and data science integrations; FastAPI for async throughput

data_platform.value: PostgreSQL 16 with pgvector extension for RAG document embeddings; Redis 7 for LLM response caching
data_platform.rationale: pgvector enables cosine similarity search in same database; Redis caches plan generation for identical athlete state

hosting_target.value: AWS ECS Fargate in us-east-1
hosting_target.rationale: Managed containers; scales to zero between plan generation requests

privacy_retention_policy.value: Workout data and chat logs retained for duration of account plus 12 months; LLM prompt logs retained 30 days (minimal retention given user data content); athletes can request deletion of all personal data
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: GDPR Article 5 data minimization for LLM prompt logs; Article 17 right to erasure

future_scope.value: Wearable integration for automatic workout import, voice coaching interface, social sharing of race achievement milestones
future_scope.rationale: Post-MVP growth features

constraints.value: LLM plan generation must complete within 15 seconds; structured JSON plan output must pass schema validation before delivery; no LLM outputs delivered to athletes without structured validation
constraints.rationale: User experience and safety requirements

observability_baseline.value: Datadog APM with LLM observability traces; structured JSON logging with athlete_id (hashed); custom metrics for plan generation latency, RAG retrieval precision, chat response time
observability_baseline.rationale: LLM system requires specialized observability

execution_preference.value: Agile 2-week sprints; GitHub Actions CI/CD; trunk-based development
execution_preference.rationale: Team development workflow

compliance_context.value: GDPR — athlete workout history, goals, and chat logs are personal data; DPIA required; LLM prompt logs containing personal data must follow same retention and deletion policies; right-to-erasure must cascade across workout_history, chat_sessions, and llm_prompt_logs tables
compliance_context.rationale: GDPR Article 5, 17 compliance for personal data in AI systems

llm_integration.value: OpenAI GPT-4o for training plan generation and chat responses; text-embedding-3-small for RAG document embeddings; pgvector cosine similarity search for top-5 training framework document retrieval; Redis caching of plan generation responses keyed by athlete_state_hash
llm_integration.rationale: GPT-4o for reasoning quality on training science; embedding model for cost-efficient vector search

project.goal: AI-powered personalized training plan generator with chat adjustments

frontend.approach: Chat interface with plan display; React Query for server state

security.auth: Auth0 JWT

data.rag: Training framework documents embedded with text-embedding-3-small

reasoner_reviews.product: The chat-based plan adjustment flow is the core differentiator — athletes must feel the plan responds intelligently to their fatigue and schedule changes, not just regenerate a new plan from scratch. The LLM revision response should include a brief 'what changed and why' rationale so athletes understand the adjustment. Round 1 did not specify the structured plan schema passed to and from the LLM.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: The RAG pipeline needs the training framework documents pre-chunked and embedded at system startup — not on each request. pgvector cosine_distance(<embedding>, query_vector) ORDER BY LIMIT 5 is the retrieval query; add an ivfflat index on the rag_documents.embedding column for sub-10ms retrieval at 10K document scale. The critical gap from round 1: the JSON schema validation step for LLM plan output was mentioned but the schema itself was not defined. Must specify the plan JSON schema with required fields.
reasoner_reviews.security: LLM prompt logs are the highest GDPR risk: they contain athlete personal data (workout descriptions, fatigue notes) in plain text. The 30-day retention policy must be enforced by a nightly purge job that hard-deletes prompt log rows older than 30 days. This must be tested in CI with a unit test verifying the purge query.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: 15-second latency constraint: RAG retrieval ~50ms (pgvector), GPT-4o streaming response 8-12s typical for plan-length output (use streaming API to begin rendering before full response). Redis cache hit for identical athlete state hash eliminates LLM call for athletes re-requesting the same week's plan without workout updates.

frontend.app_structure: React SPA: features/onboarding, features/dashboard, features/plan, features/chat, features/profile. React Query for API calls with optimistic updates on workout logging.
frontend.pages_and_flows: Onboarding: athlete profile setup (name, current fitness, race goal, race date, weekly availability). Dashboard: this week's training plan with session cards (type, distance, pace target). Chat: conversation thread with coach AI showing plan rationale; message input. Plan Detail: expanded view of a session with prescribed workout structure. Profile: account settings, data deletion request.
frontend.state_management: React Query for server state (plan, workout history, chat messages); Zustand for UI state (chat draft, selected plan week).

backend.service_design: FastAPI with routers: /plan (generate, retrieve, update), /chat (send message, list messages), /workouts (log, list), /athletes (profile CRUD), /admin (prompt log purge status).
backend.api_patterns: POST /plan/generate triggers RAG + LLM pipeline; returns structured TrainingPlan JSON. POST /chat/message sends athlete message to LLM with chat history + current plan context; returns PlanRevision or CoachResponse. GET /plan/{week_id} returns cached plan. POST /workouts/log adds workout to history.
backend.llm_integration_design: PlanGenerator: 1) hash athlete_state (last 4 weeks workouts + race_goal + week_number) -> Redis cache key; 2) cache miss: fetch top-5 RAG docs via pgvector cosine search; 3) build prompt with athlete context + RAG docs + structured output instructions; 4) call GPT-4o with response_format=json_object; 5) validate output against TrainingPlanSchema (pydantic model); 6) store in training_plans; 7) log prompt to llm_prompt_logs. ChatAdjuster: sends chat message + current plan + last 5 chat messages to GPT-4o; parses response for plan_update field; if present validates updated plan week against schema and persists.
backend.background_jobs: Nightly analytics job: compute planned vs. completed workout volumes per athlete, update adherence_score in athlete_profiles. Nightly prompt log purge: DELETE FROM llm_prompt_logs WHERE created_at < NOW() - INTERVAL '30 days'. Weekly plan generation pre-warm: for athletes with weekly_plan_enabled=true, pre-generate plans Sunday night to warm Redis cache.

security.auth_design: Auth0 JWT validated via fastapi-auth0 middleware; sub claim used as athlete_id foreign key
security.authorization_model: All database queries include WHERE athlete_id = current_user.sub — no cross-athlete data access possible. pgvector RAG queries are global (training framework docs are not athlete-specific).
security.privacy_controls: GDPR erasure endpoint: DELETE /athletes/me/data hard-deletes athlete_profiles, workout_history, training_plans, chat_sessions, llm_prompt_logs rows for the requesting athlete.

data.entities: athlete_profiles (id UUID PK, auth0_sub TEXT UNIQUE, display_name, race_goal_type ENUM 5k/10k/half/marathon, race_date DATE, weekly_hours_available INT, current_weekly_km FLOAT, adherence_score FLOAT, created_at). workout_history (id UUID PK, athlete_id FK, activity_type TEXT, distance_km FLOAT, duration_min INT, avg_hr INT, perceived_exertion INT 1-10, logged_date DATE, source ENUM manual/wearable). training_plans (id UUID PK, athlete_id FK, week_start DATE, plan_json JSONB, status ENUM active/completed/revised, generated_at, athlete_state_hash TEXT). plan_weeks (id UUID PK, plan_id FK, week_number INT, sessions JSONB array). chat_sessions (id UUID PK, athlete_id FK, created_at). chat_messages (id UUID PK, session_id FK, role ENUM user/assistant, content TEXT, plan_revision_json JSONB nullable, created_at). rag_documents (id UUID PK, title TEXT, source TEXT, chunk_text TEXT, embedding VECTOR(1536), created_at). llm_prompt_logs (id UUID PK, athlete_id FK, prompt_type ENUM plan_generation/chat_adjustment, prompt_hash TEXT, response_preview TEXT 200chars, tokens_used INT, created_at).
data.storage_design: PostgreSQL 16 with pgvector on Aurora Serverless v2 for plan data. ivfflat index on rag_documents.embedding with lists=100 for cosine similarity search. Redis 7 ElastiCache for plan generation cache keyed by athlete_state_hash.
data.retention_and_deletion: llm_prompt_logs TTL enforced by nightly purge job (30 days). Workout data and chat logs follow account lifetime + 12 months. GDPR erasure cascades across all athlete_id-keyed tables.

devops.deployment_topology: ECS Fargate: api-service (FastAPI), nightly-jobs (scheduled ECS tasks for analytics and prompt purge). Aurora Serverless v2 PostgreSQL. ElastiCache Redis. ALB with WAF. CloudFront for React SPA. Route53. ACM.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: ruff, mypy, pytest 85% coverage, build Docker, push ECR, deploy ECS rolling update. PR requires 1 approval. Feature flags via AWS AppConfig.
devops.observability_stack: Datadog APM with LLM trace spans: plan_generation_latency_ms, rag_retrieval_latency_ms, llm_tokens_used, cache_hit_rate. Custom Datadog metrics: plan_generation_success_rate, schema_validation_failure_rate, chat_adjustment_plan_update_rate.

ISS-001.title: TrainingPlan JSON schema not defined for LLM output validation
ISS-001.detail: Round 1 plan mentioned LLM output validation against a schema but did not define the schema fields. Without a concrete schema, the validation step is unimplemented and LLM plan outputs cannot be reliably structured.

history.note: security_baseline and api_design mention schema validation but schema is not defined

ISS-002.title: LLM prompt log purge job not specified with GDPR retention enforcement
ISS-002.detail: The 30-day retention policy for llm_prompt_logs containing personal data was stated in the contract but round 1 did not design the nightly purge job, its schedule, or how purge completion is audited.

history.note: privacy_retention_policy mentions 30 days but no purge job designed

previous_plan.title: AI Training Plan Coach Architecture v1
previous_plan.executive_summary: FastAPI backend with GPT-4o plan generation, pgvector RAG over training framework documents, and React chat interface for recreational athletes.
previous_plan.architecture_overview: ECS Fargate API service with PostgreSQL+pgvector on Aurora Serverless v2; Redis caches plan generation on athlete_state_hash; Auth0 JWT authentication.
previous_plan.workflows: POST /plan/generate: hash athlete state, check Redis, RAG retrieval from pgvector, GPT-4o generation, schema validation (schema undefined), store plan. POST /chat/message: send to GPT-4o with context, parse plan_revision, validate and store.
previous_plan.data_model: athlete_profiles, workout_history, training_plans, chat_messages, rag_documents, llm_prompt_logs in PostgreSQL.

target_output.thinking_summary: Round 2 must define the concrete TrainingPlan JSON schema that GPT-4o outputs are validated against, and design the nightly prompt log purge job with GDPR audit trail. The schema needs to be specific enough for pydantic validation but flexible enough for different race distances (5K sessions differ from marathon long-run sessions). The purge job must be an ECS scheduled task with CloudWatch logging of deleted row count for GDPR audit evidence.

fix_report.action_taken: Defined TrainingPlanSchema as a pydantic v2 model: TrainingPlanSchema{week_start: date, week_number: int (1-based from plan start), total_planned_km: float, sessions: list[TrainingSession], weekly_rationale: str (2-3 sentences explaining load decision)}. TrainingSession{session_date: date, session_type: Literal['easy_run','tempo','interval','long_run','rest','cross_train'], distance_km: float|None, duration_min: int|None, pace_target_min_km: str|None (e.g. '5:30-6:00'), description: str (1-2 sentences), perceived_effort_target: int 1-10}. GPT-4o called with response_format={'type':'json_object'} and system prompt containing schema definition. PlanGenerator validates response with TrainingPlanSchema.model_validate(json.loads(llm_response)); on ValidationError retries once with error context appended to prompt; on second failure raises PlanGenerationError logged to Datadog.
fix_report.expected_outcome: All LLM plan outputs pass pydantic schema validation before storage or delivery; athletes never see malformed plan responses

fix_report.action_taken: Added prompt_log_purge ECS scheduled task triggered by EventBridge rule every night at 2am UTC. Task runs a Python script that executes: result = db.execute('DELETE FROM llm_prompt_logs WHERE created_at < NOW() - INTERVAL 30 days RETURNING id'); publishes custom CloudWatch metric prompt_logs_purged_count with value len(result.fetchall()); exits 0. CloudWatch alarm triggers PagerDuty if purge task fails (ECS task exit code != 0). Pytest unit test: insert 3 rows (2 older than 30 days, 1 recent), run purge query, assert 2 rows deleted. GDPR audit: CloudWatch metric retention 90 days enables demonstrating purge compliance.
fix_report.expected_outcome: llm_prompt_logs table never contains rows older than 31 days (1 day tolerance); purge count logged to CloudWatch as GDPR audit evidence

target_output.title: AI Training Plan Coach — Architecture v2
target_output.executive_summary: A React 18 + FastAPI AI coaching system on AWS ECS Fargate that generates personalized weekly training plans for recreational athletes using GPT-4o with a pgvector RAG layer over evidence-based training frameworks. Athletes interact via a chat interface to adjust plans based on fatigue or schedule changes; all LLM outputs are validated against a pydantic TrainingPlanSchema before delivery. Athlete data is protected under GDPR with 30-day retention for LLM prompt logs enforced by a nightly ECS purge task with CloudWatch audit metrics.
target_output.architecture_overview: Three-tier AWS architecture: React SPA on CloudFront, FastAPI on ECS Fargate behind ALB, Aurora Serverless v2 PostgreSQL (pgvector) + ElastiCache Redis. Auth0 JWT validated on all API endpoints. PlanGenerator service: hash athlete state (last 4 weeks workouts + race goal) -> Redis cache check -> on miss: pgvector cosine similarity retrieval of top-5 training framework documents -> GPT-4o structured output call -> pydantic TrainingPlanSchema validation -> Redis write -> PostgreSQL store. ChatAdjuster: message + current plan + last 5 messages -> GPT-4o -> parse plan_revision -> validate -> persist. Nightly ECS scheduled tasks: analytics adherence update, prompt_log_purge (30-day GDPR enforcement).
target_output.technology_stack: Frontend: React 18, TypeScript, React Query 5, Zustand, Tailwind CSS. Backend: Python 3.12, FastAPI 0.110, pydantic v2, openai 1.x (GPT-4o + text-embedding-3-small), pgvector 0.2 Python client. Database: Aurora Serverless v2 PostgreSQL 16 with pgvector, ivfflat index. Cache: ElastiCache Redis 7. Auth: Auth0 via fastapi-auth0. Infrastructure: ECS Fargate, ALB + WAF, CloudFront, EventBridge for scheduled tasks. Monitoring: Datadog APM with LLM tracing.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Athlete Onboarding: profile setup with race goal type (5K/10K/half/marathon), race date, current weekly km, available hours; stored in athlete_profiles. Plan Generation: POST /plan/generate triggers PlanGenerator; returns TrainingPlan with 5-7 TrainingSession objects covering the week; sessions include easy_run, tempo, long_run, rest days. Chat Adjustment: athlete types 'I'm feeling tired this week, can we reduce the long run?'; ChatAdjuster sends to GPT-4o with plan context; response includes revised plan_week with reduced long_run distance and weekly_rationale explaining the change. Workout Logging: athlete logs completed workouts; nightly job updates adherence_score. GDPR: DELETE /athletes/me/data cascades across all personal data tables including llm_prompt_logs.
target_output.system_components: PlanGenerator (Python class: athlete_state_hash computation, Redis cache check, pgvector RAG retrieval, GPT-4o call, TrainingPlanSchema validation, plan storage). ChatAdjuster (Python class: chat history retrieval, GPT-4o call with plan context, plan_revision parsing, schema validation). RAGService (pgvector cosine_distance query with ivfflat index, returns top-5 rag_documents by similarity). TrainingPlanSchema (pydantic v2 model with nested TrainingSession; used for both generation validation and chat revision validation). FastAPI routers: /plan, /chat, /workouts, /athletes. Nightly ECS tasks: adherence_analytics, prompt_log_purge. ElastiCache Redis for plan generation cache. Aurora Serverless v2 with pgvector.
target_output.workflows: Plan Generation: POST /plan/generate -> PlanGenerator computes athlete_state_hash (SHA256 of sorted workout_history last 28 days + race_goal + week_number); Redis GET {hash} -> cache hit returns cached plan JSON; cache miss: RAGService executes SELECT chunk_text FROM rag_documents ORDER BY embedding <=> query_embedding LIMIT 5 (cosine distance, ivfflat index); builds GPT-4o prompt with athlete context (current km/week, race date, recent workouts) + RAG docs + TrainingPlanSchema JSON definition; calls openai.chat.completions.create(model='gpt-4o', response_format={'type':'json_object'}, stream=True); collects stream, validates with TrainingPlanSchema.model_validate(); on ValidationError appends error to prompt and retries once; stores in training_plans, sets Redis cache with 7-day TTL; returns plan to client. Chat Adjustment: POST /chat/message -> fetch current week's plan and last 5 chat_messages; build GPT-4o prompt with full plan JSON + athlete message + instruction to return {response: str, plan_revision: TrainingPlanSchema|null}; validate plan_revision if present; persist chat_message and updated plan_week.
target_output.data_model: athlete_profiles (id UUID PK, auth0_sub TEXT UNIQUE, display_name TEXT, race_goal_type TEXT CHECK('5k','10k','half','marathon'), race_date DATE, weekly_hours_available INT, current_weekly_km FLOAT, adherence_score FLOAT DEFAULT 0, created_at TIMESTAMP). workout_history (id UUID PK, athlete_id UUID FK, activity_type TEXT, distance_km FLOAT, duration_min INT, avg_hr INT, perceived_exertion INT CHECK(1-10), logged_date DATE, source TEXT CHECK('manual','wearable'), synced_at TIMESTAMP). training_plans (id UUID PK, athlete_id UUID FK, week_start DATE, plan_json JSONB conforming to TrainingPlanSchema, status TEXT CHECK('active','completed','revised'), athlete_state_hash TEXT, generated_at TIMESTAMP). chat_sessions (id UUID PK, athlete_id UUID FK, created_at TIMESTAMP). chat_messages (id UUID PK, session_id UUID FK, role TEXT CHECK('user','assistant'), content TEXT, plan_revision_json JSONB nullable, created_at TIMESTAMP). rag_documents (id UUID PK, title TEXT, source TEXT, chunk_text TEXT, embedding VECTOR(1536), created_at TIMESTAMP). llm_prompt_logs (id UUID PK, athlete_id UUID FK, prompt_type TEXT CHECK('plan_generation','chat_adjustment'), prompt_hash TEXT, response_preview TEXT CHECK(LENGTH <= 200), tokens_used INT, created_at TIMESTAMP). TrainingPlanSchema (pydantic): {week_start: date, week_number: int, total_planned_km: float, sessions: list[TrainingSession], weekly_rationale: str}. TrainingSession: {session_date: date, session_type: Literal['easy_run','tempo','interval','long_run','rest','cross_train'], distance_km: float|None, duration_min: int|None, pace_target_min_km: str|None, description: str, perceived_effort_target: int}.
target_output.api_design: POST /plan/generate (body: {week_start: date}) -> TrainingPlan JSON (validated). GET /plan/{week_id} -> cached plan. PATCH /plan/{week_id}/sessions/{session_id} (manual override). GET /plan/history -> list of past plan week summaries. POST /chat/message (body: {session_id, message: str}) -> {response: str, plan_revision?: TrainingPlanSchema}. GET /chat/{session_id}/messages -> message history. POST /workouts/log -> workout_history entry. GET /workouts/me?since&until -> workout list. PUT /athletes/me -> profile update. DELETE /athletes/me/data -> GDPR erasure (cascades all tables). GET /admin/prompt-purge/status -> last purge timestamp and count (admin role only).
target_output.security_and_compliance: Auth0 JWT validation via fastapi-auth0 dependency injector; all queries include WHERE athlete_id = current_athlete.sub. pgvector RAG queries are global (rag_documents have no athlete_id). LLM outputs validated with pydantic TrainingPlanSchema.model_validate() before any storage or response — ValidationError triggers retry then PlanGenerationError; no unvalidated LLM output delivered to athletes. llm_prompt_logs store only response_preview (first 200 chars) and prompt_hash — full prompts not stored. 30-day purge enforced by nightly ECS task; CloudWatch metric for audit evidence. GDPR erasure: DELETE /athletes/me/data hard-deletes all athlete_id-keyed rows in a single transaction across all tables. openai API key stored in AWS Secrets Manager, injected via ECS task role.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: GitHub Actions: ruff, mypy, pytest (85% coverage gate including prompt_log_purge unit test), Docker build, push ECR, ECS rolling deploy. EventBridge rules: nightly adherence_analytics (2am UTC), nightly prompt_log_purge (2am UTC, 5min after analytics). ECS scheduled tasks use same Docker image as API service with CMD override. Aurora Serverless v2 auto-scales to 0.5 ACU during off-peak. CloudFront caches React SPA assets. Environments: dev (RDS t3.micro, no Redis), staging (Aurora Serverless v2 + Redis), production.
target_output.observability: Datadog APM LLM traces: plan_generation_latency_ms, rag_retrieval_latency_ms, llm_tokens_used (input + output), cache_hit (boolean), schema_validation_success. Custom Datadog metrics: plan_generation_success_rate (target >98%), schema_validation_failure_rate (alert >2%), chat_adjustment_plan_update_rate, prompt_logs_purged_count (from purge task CloudWatch metric, synced to Datadog). PagerDuty: plan generation error rate >5% (P2), prompt log purge task failure (P2 — GDPR compliance risk), API p99 latency > 20s (P2).
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Estimated $350/month for 200 active athletes. GPT-4o plan generation: ~2000 tokens/plan x $0.005/1K = $0.01/plan; 200 athletes x 52 plans/year = $104/year. Redis cache hit rate target 60% for repeat weekly plan requests without workout updates saves ~$62/year in GPT-4o costs. Aurora Serverless v2 scales to 0.5 ACU off-peak. ElastiCache t4g.micro: $14/month.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (complete): athlete profile, manual workout logging, LLM plan generation, RAG with pgvector, Auth0, React chat UI, basic plan display. Phase 2 (this round): resolve ISS-001 with TrainingPlanSchema pydantic model and GPT-4o validation; resolve ISS-002 with prompt_log_purge ECS scheduled task and CloudWatch audit metric; add streaming plan generation for faster perceived response; add wearable workout import (Apple Health via Health Export CSV). Phase 3: voice coaching interface, social achievement sharing, coach review mode (optional human coach review of AI-generated plans for premium tier).
target_output.development_guidelines: TrainingPlanSchema must be imported from a shared schema module used by both PlanGenerator and ChatAdjuster — never duplicate schema definition. GPT-4o calls must specify response_format={'type':'json_object'} — never rely on text parsing for structured plan output. prompt_log_purge script must have a pytest test asserting rows older than 30 days are deleted and rows within 30 days are not. All LLM prompt construction must exclude personally identifying fields (athlete name) — use athlete_id (UUID) in prompts, not display_name. Redis cache key: f'plan:{athlete_state_hash}' — state hash must include zone_config_version equivalent (week_number + race_date).
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: GPT-4o API unavailability blocks plan generation — add fallback to pre-computed template plan based on race distance and weeks to race, served from training_plans_templates table with disclaimer. Risk: pydantic ValidationError on malformed LLM response — one retry with error context in prompt usually resolves; log schema_validation_failures metric and alert if > 2% of requests. Tradeoff: pgvector over dedicated vector DB (Pinecone) — avoids additional infrastructure cost and data synchronization complexity; ivfflat index sufficient at 10K document scale. Tradeoff: 30-day prompt log retention over no logging — minimal retention required for debugging LLM quality issues and safety review; GDPR-justified by legitimate interest in service quality.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: TrainingPlanSchema defined with pydantic v2 including TrainingSession array — resolves ISS-001. Prompt log purge ECS task with EventBridge schedule and CloudWatch audit metric designed — resolves ISS-002. Cache key strategy: athlete_state_hash includes last 28 days workout hashes + race_goal + week_number. RAG document count: 127 training framework chunks from 8 sources (Daniels Running Formula, Pfitzinger, Hal Higdon plans, sports science literature) embedded at system startup.

metadata.notes: Round 2 AI training coach; resolves LLM output schema and GDPR prompt log purge gaps.

project_goal.value: Automation tool for sports venue operators that continuously monitors HVAC, lighting, and digital scoreboard systems across a multi-venue estate, auto-tunes energy settings based on occupancy schedules and live attendance data, and triggers maintenance work orders when anomalous sensor readings are detected
project_goal.rationale: Core product goal: reduce energy costs and preventive maintenance response time across venue estate

target_users.value: Venue operations managers and facilities technicians across a multi-venue sports estate
target_users.rationale: Primary operators who monitor dashboards and act on maintenance alerts

project_class.rationale: Backend automation service with IoT integrations and batch optimization jobs; no customer-facing UI

capabilities.value: backend, data, integrations, realtime, batch_jobs, analytics
capabilities.rationale: Sensor polling, anomaly detection, BMS API integration, pre-event optimization jobs, post-event analytics

complexity_level.rationale: Real-time sensor ingestion at 30s cadence, ML-trained anomaly thresholds, multi-venue BMS API integrations

risk_level.rationale: Incorrect HVAC setpoints could create uncomfortable conditions for fans; anomaly false positives waste technician time

data_sensitivity.rationale: Sensor readings and energy setpoints are proprietary operational data; not personal data

external_exposure.rationale: Services accessible only to venue operations team via internal network; no public endpoints

access_model.value: Custom JWT for internal service-to-service calls; operations staff access via Grafana dashboards protected by Azure AD SSO
access_model.rationale: Internal tooling with Azure AD for operator authentication

feature_scope.value: 30-second sensor polling from venue BMS APIs, ML-trained anomaly threshold evaluation, automated HVAC/lighting setpoint adjustments via BMS write APIs, maintenance work order creation via ServiceNow integration, pre-event optimization job based on ticketing attendance data, post-event energy analytics, Grafana dashboards per venue
feature_scope.rationale: Full automation platform capability set

mvp_scope.value: Sensor polling for 2 pilot venues, rule-based anomaly detection, maintenance work order creation via ServiceNow, pre-event HVAC setpoint adjustment
mvp_scope.rationale: MVP validates core automation loop with pilot venues

security_baseline.value: BMS API credentials stored in Azure Key Vault; no hardcoded credentials in application code; all BMS write operations logged with operator_id and timestamp; internal-only network exposure; TLS 1.3 for all API calls
security_baseline.rationale: Prevents unauthorized HVAC/lighting changes

frontend_stack.value: N/A — automation tool; operator interface is Grafana dashboards
frontend_stack.rationale: No custom frontend; Grafana used for dashboards

backend_stack.value: Python 3.12 with FastAPI
backend_stack.rationale: Python for ML anomaly model integration and BMS API clients; FastAPI for internal service endpoints

data_platform.value: InfluxDB for time-series sensor readings; Redis 7 for current setpoint state and anomaly alert deduplication
data_platform.rationale: InfluxDB optimal for high-frequency sensor time-series; Redis for low-latency current state lookups

hosting_target.value: Azure Container Apps
hosting_target.rationale: Managed serverless containers on Azure; scales to zero between polling cycles

privacy_retention_policy.value: Sensor readings retained 2 years in InfluxDB; work order records retained 7 years; aggregated energy analytics retained indefinitely
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Equipment maintenance records retention requirement; energy benchmarking data

future_scope.value: Carbon footprint tracking integration with energy grid APIs, predictive equipment failure modeling, operator mobile app for work order management
future_scope.rationale: Post-MVP sustainability and mobile roadmap

constraints.value: HVAC setpoint changes must not exceed +/-2°C from current value in a single automated adjustment; work order creation must not duplicate if an open work order exists for the same device; anomaly detection false positive rate target < 5%
constraints.rationale: Safety constraints for fan comfort and technician workload management

observability_baseline.value: Grafana + Prometheus for service metrics; InfluxDB dashboards for sensor data trends; Azure Monitor alerts for service failures; Grafana alerting for venue anomaly events
observability_baseline.rationale: Standard Azure-native and Grafana observability stack

execution_preference.value: GitHub Actions CI/CD; Azure Container Registry for images; staged rollout starting with pilot venues
execution_preference.rationale: Azure-native deployment toolchain

project.goal: Multi-venue sports estate HVAC/lighting automation with anomaly detection and work order creation
project.target_users: Venue operations managers and facilities technicians

backend.framework: FastAPI with asyncio sensor polling workers

security.auth: Azure Key Vault for BMS credentials; custom JWT for service-to-service
security.bms_writes: All BMS write operations logged with operator_id and device_id

data.cache: Redis 7 for setpoint state and dedup

constraints.dedup: No duplicate work orders for same open device fault

reasoner_reviews.product: Round 1 core loop is validated: sensor polling at 30s cadence, pre-event HVAC optimization, and ServiceNow integration are all correct. Round 2 focus must resolve the deduplication gap (ISS-001) and per-device-type threshold inadequacy (ISS-002) before production rollout to 12 venues.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: The ServiceNow open-incident check for deduplication (ISS-001) should call GET /incident?caller_id=venue_automation&cmdb_ci={device_id}&state!=closed before creating a new work order. For ISS-002, per-device-type z-score thresholds stored in InfluxDB rolling aggregates (30-day mean and stddev per measurement series) allow device-class calibration without a separate model service.
reasoner_reviews.security: BMS API credentials must remain exclusively in Azure Key Vault; setpoint write audit log must capture operator context (automated vs manual) and the before/after setpoint values for every write. ServiceNow integration credentials also require Key Vault rotation policy.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: The +/-2°C clamping constraint is enforced in PreEventOptimizer but must also be enforced in the AnomalyRemediation path where HVAC corrections are applied. Duplicate detection via ServiceNow must use device CMDB CI identifier, not free-text asset name.
reasoner_reviews.critic: ISS-001 is the most operationally damaging issue: technicians at the 2 pilot venues already reported duplicate work orders. ISS-002 produces unacceptable false positive rates for server-room-adjacent HVAC zones. Both must be resolved in round 2 before multi-venue rollout.

backend.service_design: Four Python services in Azure Container Apps: SensorPoller (asyncio tasks per venue, 30s interval), AnomalyEvaluator (consumes InfluxDB latest readings, applies per-device-type z-score thresholds loaded from Blob Storage), WorkOrderCreator (calls ServiceNow GET then POST with device CMDB CI dedup), PreEventOptimizer (nightly batch job 4h before event start reading Ticketmaster attendance data).
backend.api_patterns: FastAPI internal endpoints: POST /control/setpoint (BMS write with +/-2°C clamp), GET /anomalies/active (current anomaly feed for Grafana data source), POST /workorders (internal trigger with ServiceNow dedup check). All endpoints validate custom JWT signed by shared HMAC secret stored in Key Vault.
backend.business_modules: AnomalyEvaluator.evaluate(device_type, reading): load per-device-type threshold (mean, stddev) from Azure Blob Storage model file; compute z-score = (reading - mean) / stddev; flag anomaly if abs(z-score) > 3.0. WorkOrderCreator.create_if_not_duplicate(venue_id, device_cmdb_ci, description): GET ServiceNow /incident?cmdb_ci={device_cmdb_ci}&state=1,2,3 (states 1/2/3 = new/in-progress/on-hold); skip creation if any open incident found; POST /incident with priority and assignment group.
backend.background_jobs: ModelRefreshJob: daily at 03:00 UTC, queries InfluxDB for 30-day rolling mean and stddev per measurement series (hvac_temp by zone_type, lighting_lux by fixture_class, scoreboard_power by display_type), serializes threshold file to Azure Blob Storage for AnomalyEvaluator hot-reload. PreEventOptimizer: 4h before event_start per venue, reads Ticketmaster API for attendance forecast, computes zone-by-zone HVAC setpoints with +/-2°C clamp, writes to BMS via control API.
backend.failure_handling: SensorPoller: per-venue retry with exponential backoff (3 retries, max 90s delay) before marking venue as degraded in Redis. AnomalyEvaluator: if Blob Storage threshold file fetch fails, falls back to static interquartile range thresholds from config. WorkOrderCreator: ServiceNow HTTP timeout (10s) triggers retry; after 3 failures, anomaly is logged to anomaly_events table with status 'workorder_failed' for manual follow-up.
backend.backend_risks: BMS vendor API rate limits may reject bulk setpoint writes during pre-event optimization for large venues; implement write queue with 200ms spacing between BMS write calls.

security.auth_design: Azure Key Vault stores BMS API credentials per venue (secret name: bms-cred-{venue_slug}), ServiceNow integration credentials (servicenow-api-key), and HMAC signing secret for internal JWT. Container Apps use managed identity for Key Vault access — no credential files in container images.
security.audit_and_logging_controls: Every BMS write emits a structured log entry to Azure Log Analytics: {timestamp, service, action:'setpoint_write', device_id, venue_id, before_value, after_value, trigger_type:'automated'|'manual', operator_jwt_sub}. ServiceNow POST calls log: {timestamp, device_cmdb_ci, incident_number, dedup_check_result:'new'|'skipped', open_incident_id}.
security.secrets_management: Azure Key Vault with 90-day rotation policy for BMS and ServiceNow credentials. ModelRefreshJob uses Azure Managed Identity — never downloads raw credentials. Key Vault audit logs retained 1 year in Log Analytics.
security.security_risks: BMS write API endpoints on vendor-managed systems may not enforce TLS 1.3; document minimum TLS version requirement in vendor onboarding checklist and reject connections to non-TLS endpoints.

data.entities: InfluxDB measurements: venue_sensor_readings (tags: venue_id, device_id, device_type, zone; fields: reading_value FLOAT, unit STRING; 30s cadence). anomaly_events table (PostgreSQL): id UUID, venue_id, device_id, device_cmdb_ci, anomaly_type, z_score FLOAT, threshold_mean FLOAT, threshold_stddev FLOAT, detected_at TIMESTAMP, workorder_status ENUM new/created/skipped/failed, servicenow_incident_id TEXT, resolved_at TIMESTAMP. energy_setpoint_log: id UUID, venue_id, device_id, before_value FLOAT, after_value FLOAT, trigger_type TEXT, written_at TIMESTAMP.
data.storage_design: InfluxDB retention policy: 2 years for raw sensor readings; 90-day rollup for 5-minute aggregates. Azure Blob Storage: per-device-type threshold model files (JSON: {mean, stddev, computed_at}), refreshed daily by ModelRefreshJob. Redis 7: key=anomaly:{device_id}:active, value=incident_number from ServiceNow (set on workorder creation, cleared on resolved status webhook).
data.analytics_events: PostEventAnalyticsJob (runs 2h after event_end): aggregates energy_setpoint_log by venue and event_id, computes total kWh delta vs baseline, writes to energy_analytics table for Grafana energy savings dashboard.

ISS-001.title: Anomaly deduplication relies on Redis TTL only, not ServiceNow open work order status
ISS-001.detail: Round 1 used a Redis key with 24h TTL for deduplication. If a ServiceNow work order remains open for >24h, the TTL expires and a duplicate work order is created for the same device. Deduplication must integrate with ServiceNow to check for open work orders.

history.note: deduplication section used Redis TTL without ServiceNow open work order check

ISS-002.title: Static global anomaly thresholds produce high false positive rate
ISS-002.detail: Round 1 used a single temperature threshold (>35°C = anomaly) applied globally. Different device types (HVAC units near server rooms vs. concourse zones) have very different normal operating ranges. Per-device-type thresholds are required to meet the <5% false positive target.

history.note: anomaly_engine section used static global threshold without per-device calibration

focus_issues.title: Anomaly dedup must check ServiceNow open work orders not just Redis TTL
focus_issues.detail: Integrate ServiceNow GET /incident?device_id=X&state=open check before creating duplicate work orders.

focus_issues.title: Per-device-type anomaly thresholds required
focus_issues.detail: Train and apply z-score thresholds per device_type (hvac_temp, lighting_lux, scoreboard_power) using 30-day rolling baseline.

latest_recommendations: Add ServiceNow GET /incident?caller_id=venue_ops&device_id={device_id}&active=true check before WorkOrderCreator POST; only create if no open incidents found
Replace static threshold with per-device-type z-score model: load 30-day rolling mean and stddev from InfluxDB, flag if |reading - mean| > 3*stddev
Daily model refresh job recomputes per-device-type thresholds and serializes to Azure Blob Storage for AnomalyEvaluator to load at startup

convergence_state.reason: Deduplication and threshold design insufficient for production quality

previous_audits.summary: Solid automation architecture with correct Azure Container Apps topology. Two quality issues: anomaly deduplication will generate duplicate work orders for long-running faults, and static global thresholds will not achieve <5% false positive rate for diverse device types.
strengths: BMS vendor-specific client abstraction is correct
InfluxDB time-series design is appropriate
+/-2°C setpoint cap is enforced in PreEventOptimizer
concerns: Redis TTL dedup will expire before work orders close
Static threshold produces high false positives for mixed device types
blocking_issues: ISS-001: Redis-only dedup creates duplicate work orders for faults lasting > 24h
ISS-002: Static threshold will not meet <5% false positive SLA
recommendations: Query ServiceNow for open incidents by device CMDB CI before creating new work order
Implement per-device-type z-score thresholds with 30-day rolling baseline from InfluxDB

issue_updates.title: Redis-only dedup
issue_updates.detail: anomaly_engine section describes Redis TTL dedup without ServiceNow check

issue_updates.title: Static global thresholds
issue_updates.detail: anomaly_engine uses static >35°C threshold for all HVAC readings

previous_plan.title: Venue Energy Automation Architecture v1
previous_plan.executive_summary: Python 3.12 FastAPI automation backend on Azure Container Apps polling BMS sensor data across 12 sports venues at 30s cadence. Anomaly detection flags readings exceeding static thresholds and creates ServiceNow work orders. Pre-event HVAC optimization job runs 4h before event start.
previous_plan.architecture_overview: SensorPoller asyncio tasks per venue push readings to InfluxDB. AnomalyEvaluator reads latest readings, applies static thresholds, writes anomaly events. WorkOrderCreator integrates ServiceNow. Redis used for 24h TTL deduplication. PreEventOptimizer runs as nightly batch on Azure Container Apps job.
previous_plan.system_components: SensorPoller, AnomalyEvaluator, WorkOrderCreator, PreEventOptimizer; InfluxDB for sensor time-series; Redis for dedup TTL; Azure Container Apps; Grafana dashboards.

target_output.thinking_summary: Round 2 must resolve ISS-001 (Redis-only deduplication creates duplicate ServiceNow work orders for faults lasting >24h) and ISS-002 (static global thresholds yield unacceptable false positive rate). For ISS-001: WorkOrderCreator must call ServiceNow GET /incident?cmdb_ci={device_cmdb_ci}&state=1,2,3 before any POST; Redis key stores the confirmed ServiceNow incident number rather than just a TTL flag, so dedup survives TTL expiry. For ISS-002: replace static thresholds with per-device-type z-score model — AnomalyEvaluator loads {mean, stddev} from Azure Blob Storage threshold files refreshed daily by ModelRefreshJob from 30-day InfluxDB aggregates. Both fixes are performance-critical and directly improve multi-venue rollout confidence.

fix_report.action_taken: WorkOrderCreator now calls ServiceNow GET /incident?cmdb_ci={device_cmdb_ci}&state=1,2,3 before every POST. Open incident number is stored in Redis as key anomaly:{device_id}:active for fast subsequent dedup checks without repeat ServiceNow API calls. Redis key is cleared only when ServiceNow resolved-status webhook fires.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Zero duplicate work orders for faults of any duration; Redis acts as cache of known-open incidents, not sole dedup authority.

fix_report.action_taken: AnomalyEvaluator replaced static threshold with per-device-type z-score evaluation: z = (reading - mean) / stddev where mean and stddev are loaded from Azure Blob Storage threshold files. ModelRefreshJob runs daily at 03:00 UTC, queries InfluxDB for 30-day rolling aggregates per measurement series (hvac_temp by zone_type, lighting_lux by fixture_class, scoreboard_power by display_type), and writes updated threshold JSON files to Blob Storage.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Per-device-type z-score thresholds achieve <5% false positive target by calibrating to each zone's actual operating range rather than global worst-case values.

target_output.title: Venue Energy Automation Architecture v2
target_output.executive_summary: Python 3.12 FastAPI automation platform on Azure Container Apps for a 12-venue sports estate, polling HVAC, lighting, and scoreboard BMS data at 30s cadence. Round 2 resolves both open quality issues: anomaly deduplication now integrates live ServiceNow open-incident checks (eliminating duplicate work orders for sustained faults), and per-device-type z-score thresholds replace static global limits (targeting <5% false positive rate). Pre-event HVAC optimization adjusts zone setpoints based on Ticketmaster attendance forecasts with enforced +/-2°C single-step clamps.
target_output.architecture_overview: Five Python services deployed as Azure Container App revisions: SensorPoller (asyncio concurrent tasks per venue at 30s interval, pushes to InfluxDB), AnomalyEvaluator (reads InfluxDB latest, loads per-device-type threshold files from Azure Blob Storage, computes z-scores, writes anomaly_events to PostgreSQL), WorkOrderCreator (queries ServiceNow for open incidents by CMDB CI before creating new work orders, caches incident numbers in Redis), ModelRefreshJob (daily 03:00 UTC InfluxDB aggregation job writing threshold files to Blob Storage), and PreEventOptimizer (attendance-driven setpoint adjustment with +/-2°C clamp). Grafana reads from InfluxDB and Prometheus metrics endpoint for all operational dashboards. All services authenticate to Azure resources via managed identity; BMS credentials per venue stored in Azure Key Vault.
target_output.technology_stack: Backend: Python 3.12, FastAPI 0.110, asyncio, httpx 0.27 for BMS and ServiceNow API calls, influxdb-client 1.40, redis-py 5.0. Database: InfluxDB 2.7 (sensor time-series), PostgreSQL 15 (anomaly_events, energy_setpoint_log), Redis 7 (active incident cache). Storage: Azure Blob Storage (threshold model JSON files). Hosting: Azure Container Apps with Azure Container Registry. Secrets: Azure Key Vault with managed identity. Observability: Prometheus + Grafana, Azure Monitor, Azure Log Analytics.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Sensor Ingestion: SensorPoller spawns per-venue asyncio tasks polling BMS REST APIs every 30s; readings written to InfluxDB measurement venue_sensor_readings with tags venue_id, device_id, device_type, zone. Anomaly Detection: AnomalyEvaluator reads latest readings per device, loads threshold file for device_type from Blob Storage, computes z-score; flags anomaly if abs(z-score) > 3.0. Work Order Deduplication: WorkOrderCreator calls ServiceNow GET /incident?cmdb_ci={device_cmdb_ci}&state=1,2,3; skips POST if open incident found; stores incident number in Redis anomaly:{device_id}:active. Threshold Refresh: ModelRefreshJob queries 30-day InfluxDB aggregates per measurement series, computes mean and stddev, writes threshold JSON to Blob Storage. Pre-Event Optimization: PreEventOptimizer reads Ticketmaster attendance forecast 4h before event, computes zone HVAC setpoints, clamps each adjustment to +/-2°C, writes to BMS via control API.
target_output.system_components: SensorPoller: asyncio task-per-venue BMS polling worker with per-venue exponential backoff retry. AnomalyEvaluator: reads InfluxDB latest-value queries, applies z-score threshold from Blob Storage model file, writes anomaly_events rows. WorkOrderCreator: ServiceNow API client with open-incident GET check (cmdb_ci filter, state 1/2/3) before POST; Redis incident number cache with webhook-cleared TTL. ModelRefreshJob: InfluxDB aggregate query job producing per-device-type threshold JSON files to Azure Blob Storage. PreEventOptimizer: Ticketmaster attendance API client, zone-to-HVAC-device mapping, setpoint clamp enforcer, BMS write queue (200ms inter-write spacing). PostgreSQL: anomaly_events and energy_setpoint_log tables. InfluxDB 2.7: venue_sensor_readings measurement (2-year retention, 5-min rollup). Redis 7: active incident cache keys. Azure Key Vault: BMS and ServiceNow credentials per venue.
target_output.workflows: Anomaly-to-Work-Order Workflow: AnomalyEvaluator detects z-score > 3.0 for device hvac-zone-b3 at venue StadiumNorth -> inserts anomaly_events row with status 'new' -> WorkOrderCreator called with device_cmdb_ci='CI-HVAC-B3-SN' -> GET ServiceNow /incident?cmdb_ci=CI-HVAC-B3-SN&state=1,2,3 -> if open incident found (e.g., INC0045123), log 'dedup_skipped' and store incident number in Redis key anomaly:hvac-zone-b3:active -> no duplicate POST. If no open incident: POST /incident with priority 2, assignment_group 'Venue Facilities', store returned INC number in Redis. Pre-Event Optimization Workflow: 4h before StadiumNorth match start (Ticketmaster forecast 18,500 attendees) -> PreEventOptimizer reads zone_capacity mapping -> computes target HVAC setpoints for concourse, seating bowl, VIP zones -> clamps each adjustment: if current setpoint 22°C and target 19°C, apply 20°C (max -2°C step) -> writes to BMS control API with structured audit log -> schedules next adjustment check 2h pre-match.
target_output.data_model: venue_sensor_readings (InfluxDB measurement): tags: venue_id STRING, device_id STRING, device_type ENUM hvac_temp/lighting_lux/scoreboard_power, zone_type ENUM concourse/seating/vip/server_room; fields: reading_value FLOAT, unit STRING; timestamp 30s cadence. anomaly_events (PostgreSQL): id UUID PK, venue_id TEXT, device_id TEXT, device_cmdb_ci TEXT, anomaly_type TEXT, z_score FLOAT, threshold_mean FLOAT, threshold_stddev FLOAT, detected_at TIMESTAMP, workorder_status ENUM new/created/skipped/failed, servicenow_incident_id TEXT, resolved_at TIMESTAMP. energy_setpoint_log (PostgreSQL): id UUID PK, venue_id TEXT, device_id TEXT, before_value FLOAT, after_value FLOAT, delta FLOAT, trigger_type ENUM automated/manual, event_id TEXT, written_at TIMESTAMP. threshold_model (Azure Blob Storage JSON): {device_type: STRING, measurement_series: STRING, mean: FLOAT, stddev: FLOAT, sample_count: INT, computed_at: TIMESTAMP}. Redis keys: anomaly:{device_id}:active -> servicenow_incident_id STRING (cleared by webhook).
target_output.api_design: Internal FastAPI endpoints (JWT-authenticated, internal_only network): POST /control/setpoint (body: {venue_id, device_id, target_value, trigger_type}) -> clamps to +/-2°C, writes to BMS, logs to energy_setpoint_log. GET /anomalies/active?venue_id={id} -> returns anomaly_events WHERE resolved_at IS NULL for Grafana data source. POST /anomalies/{anomaly_id}/workorder -> internal trigger calling WorkOrderCreator (ServiceNow GET check then conditional POST). POST /webhooks/servicenow (ServiceNow resolved-status callback) -> clears Redis anomaly:{device_id}:active key, updates anomaly_events.resolved_at. GET /metrics -> Prometheus metrics endpoint for Grafana scraping. GET /health -> liveness probe for Azure Container Apps.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Azure Key Vault managed identity access: BMS credentials keyed as bms-cred-{venue_slug}, ServiceNow API key as servicenow-api-key; 90-day rotation policy enforced. Every BMS setpoint write emits structured audit log to Azure Log Analytics: {timestamp, device_id, venue_id, before_value, after_value, trigger_type, operator_jwt_sub}. ServiceNow dedup check logged: {device_cmdb_ci, dedup_result new/skipped, open_incident_id}. Custom JWT for service-to-service calls uses HMAC-SHA256 secret from Key Vault; 15-minute token expiry. Azure Container Apps VNet integration: services only reachable on internal subnet; no public ingress. TLS 1.3 enforced for all outbound BMS and ServiceNow API calls.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: GitHub Actions pipeline: ruff lint, mypy type check, pytest (unit tests for z-score evaluation, ServiceNow dedup logic, +/-2°C clamp enforcer), Docker build, push to Azure Container Registry, deploy to Azure Container Apps via rolling update. Environments: dev (single revision, 1 pilot venue), staging (multi-revision traffic split), production (min 2 replicas, scale-to-zero for ModelRefreshJob and PreEventOptimizer). Container Apps Jobs (scheduled): ModelRefreshJob (CRON 0 3 * * *), PostEventAnalyticsJob (CRON triggered 2h after event_end via EventGrid). Secrets injected at runtime via Key Vault references in Container Apps environment configuration — never in container image. Rollback: Container Apps traffic revision switch within 2 minutes for SensorPoller and WorkOrderCreator.
target_output.observability: Prometheus metrics endpoint scraped by Grafana: sensor_polling_latency_ms per venue, anomaly_detection_z_score (histogram), workorder_creation_total (counter by result: created/skipped/failed), threshold_model_age_hours (staleness gauge). Grafana dashboards: per-venue sensor heatmap (InfluxDB source), anomaly event feed with z-score annotations, work order creation rate. Azure Monitor alerts: SensorPoller container restart >2 times in 1h, ModelRefreshJob failure, BMS write error rate >5%. Grafana alert: any venue with 0 sensor readings for >2 polling cycles (BMS connectivity loss).
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Azure Container Apps pay-per-use: SensorPoller estimated 0.5 vCPU continuous per 4 venues; scales to 3 vCPU at 12-venue full rollout. ModelRefreshJob and PostEventAnalyticsJob scale to zero between runs — no idle cost. InfluxDB Cloud Serverless billed per write and query volume: 12 venues x 50 devices x 2 writes/min = ~17,000 writes/min; estimated $180/month. PostgreSQL (Azure Database for PostgreSQL Flexible Server): Standard_D2s v3 ($110/month). Redis Cache for Azure: C1 tier ($55/month). Total estimated $420/month at 12-venue scale.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 (complete — pilot 2 venues): SensorPoller, static threshold AnomalyEvaluator, Redis TTL dedup, ServiceNow work order creation, PreEventOptimizer, Grafana dashboards. Phase 2 (this round — production readiness): ISS-001 resolved via ServiceNow open-incident GET check in WorkOrderCreator; ISS-002 resolved via per-device-type z-score thresholds with ModelRefreshJob; +/-2°C clamp enforced in AnomalyRemediation path as well as PreEventOptimizer; ServiceNow webhook receiver for Redis cache invalidation on incident resolution. Phase 3 (post-launch): Ticketmaster API integration for dynamic pre-event planning (replaces manual attendance input), carbon footprint tracking via energy grid API, operator mobile work order management app.
target_output.development_guidelines: AnomalyEvaluator must load threshold files from Blob Storage on startup and refresh every 10 minutes via background asyncio task — never on per-evaluation hot path. WorkOrderCreator ServiceNow GET check must use cmdb_ci field (not asset_name) for reliable device matching across vendor systems. +/-2°C clamp logic must be a shared utility function used by both PreEventOptimizer and AnomalyRemediation — never duplicated. All BMS write calls must go through the single POST /control/setpoint internal endpoint for centralized audit logging. pytest fixtures must mock ServiceNow API responses for both open-incident-found and no-open-incident paths.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: ServiceNow API rate limiting during mass anomaly events (e.g., HVAC system failure across 5 venues simultaneously) could throttle work order creation checks. Mitigation: implement ServiceNow client with circuit breaker (fail-open: log anomaly as workorder_failed and alert operations team for manual follow-up). Risk: Azure Blob Storage threshold file unavailability forces fallback to interquartile range static thresholds, increasing false positive rate temporarily. Mitigation: AnomalyEvaluator caches last-known-good threshold in memory to survive transient Blob Storage outages. Tradeoff: per-device-type z-score model requires 30 days of sensor history per device before thresholds are meaningful; newly onboarded venues use conservative static IQR thresholds for first 30 days.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: ISS-001 resolved: WorkOrderCreator uses ServiceNow GET /incident?cmdb_ci={device_cmdb_ci}&state=1,2,3 as primary dedup check; Redis stores confirmed incident numbers for fast subsequent lookups; webhook clears Redis on incident resolution. ISS-002 resolved: per-device-type z-score thresholds with 30-day InfluxDB rolling baseline; ModelRefreshJob refreshes daily at 03:00 UTC; AnomalyEvaluator loads from Blob Storage with in-memory cache fallback.

metadata.notes: Sports venue IoT automation: round 2 resolves ServiceNow dedup and per-device-type z-score threshold issues.

project_goal.value: Mobile app for gig-economy sports officials (referees, umpires, linespeople) to manage availability, accept assignment requests from leagues, track officiating fees owed, and submit post-match incident reports; backend services for scheduling, payments, and reporting are independently deployed
project_goal.rationale: Core platform for connecting sports officials with leagues as a marketplace

target_users.value: Freelance sports officials and league scheduling administrators
target_users.rationale: Two-sided marketplace: officials as supply, leagues as demand

project_class.rationale: React Native cross-platform mobile app as primary official-facing surface

capabilities.value: frontend, backend, data, auth, integrations, payments, analytics
capabilities.rationale: Mobile frontend, three microservices backend, PostgreSQL data, Cognito auth, Stripe Connect payments, analytics

complexity_level.rationale: Three independent microservices with event coordination; Stripe Connect payout flow; push notifications

risk_level.rationale: Financial disbursement to officials; incident reports with legal implications; correct assignment matching required

data_sensitivity.rationale: Payment disbursement to officials requires compliant handling of banking details; Stripe Connect reduces scope

external_exposure.rationale: All API access requires Cognito JWT; officials see their own data; league admins see their own league data

access_model.value: Cognito with email/phone login; RBAC: official role and league_admin role; officials see own assignments and fees; league_admins manage their own league's matches
access_model.rationale: Two-sided marketplace role separation

feature_scope.value: Official availability calendar, assignment request push notification, accept/decline with deadline, post-match incident report submission, officiating fee tracking, Stripe Connect bank transfer disbursement, league admin match scheduling dashboard
feature_scope.rationale: Full marketplace platform features

mvp_scope.value: Official availability, assignment accept/decline, basic fee display, incident report form, Cognito auth; no Stripe Connect disbursement in MVP
mvp_scope.rationale: MVP validates core workflow before payment integration

security_baseline.value: Cognito JWT on all API calls; TLS 1.3; banking details handled exclusively via Stripe Connect (never stored in app database); Stripe Connect webhook events validated via Stripe signature; officials cannot see each other's fees or banking details
security_baseline.rationale: PCI-DSS scope reduction via Stripe Connect; data isolation between officials

frontend_stack.value: React Native with TypeScript (Expo managed workflow)
frontend_stack.rationale: Cross-platform iOS and Android; Expo for OTA updates without App Store submission for minor fixes

backend_stack.value: Node.js 22 with Fastify (three microservices: scheduling-service, payments-service, reporting-service)
backend_stack.rationale: Independent deployment of scheduling, payment disbursement, and incident reporting concerns

data_platform.value: PostgreSQL 16 (shared cluster, schema-per-service); Redis 7 for assignment request expiry timers
data_platform.rationale: Schema-per-service logical isolation while sharing infrastructure; Redis for TTL-based assignment response deadlines
