hosting_target.value: AWS ECS Fargate
hosting_target.rationale: Managed containers with independent deployment per service

privacy_retention_policy.value: Official banking details never stored in app database (Stripe Connect handles); incident reports retained 7 years for legal; assignment records retained 3 years; payment disbursement records retained 7 years
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Legal retention requirements for incident report evidence and financial records

future_scope.value: Referee rating system, certification verification integration, bulk match import for leagues, official performance analytics
future_scope.rationale: Post-launch marketplace growth features

constraints.value: Assignment response deadline enforced by Redis TTL expiry triggering automatic assignment revocation; Stripe Connect used for all payment disbursements to avoid PCI-DSS card data in-scope; officials must complete Stripe Connect onboarding before first payment
constraints.rationale: Operational and compliance requirements

observability_baseline.value: New Relic APM per service; structured JSON logging with correlation IDs; Stripe webhook event logging for payment audit trail
observability_baseline.rationale: Payment audit trail for dispute resolution

execution_preference.value: GitHub Actions CI/CD; trunk-based development; Expo EAS Build for mobile binary releases
execution_preference.rationale: Standard mobile + backend CI toolchain

compliance_context.value: PCI-DSS — payment disbursement to officials requires compliant handling of banking details; Stripe Connect reduces scope by handling all banking data; Stripe Connect BAA-equivalent terms apply; Stripe webhook signature validation required on all payment events

project.goal: Sports official gig marketplace mobile app with scheduling, payments, and incident reporting

frontend.framework: React Native with TypeScript (Expo managed workflow)
frontend.approach: Cross-platform iOS and Android; push notifications via Expo Notifications + FCM/APNs

security.auth: Cognito with email/phone
security.payments: Stripe Connect — banking details never in app DB

constraints.payments: Stripe Connect only; no raw banking details in DB
constraints.assignment_deadline: Redis TTL triggers automatic revocation on expiry

open_questions.push_notifications: SNS vs Expo Notifications push service to be decided
open_questions.incident_report_format: PDF generation vs structured JSON storage for legal archive

reasoner_reviews.product: The two-sided marketplace model is clear: officials set availability, leagues create match assignments, assignment acceptance is time-limited. The fee tracking and incident report features are distinct enough from scheduling to justify the three-microservice split. MVP scope correctly defers Stripe Connect disbursement to reduce initial complexity.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Three Fastify microservices with schema-per-service PostgreSQL is sound for this scale. However, the plan must define how scheduling-service notifies the mobile app of new assignment requests — SNS or direct FCM/APNs push is required, not polling. The payments-service must not store Stripe Connect account IDs in the same table as other official PII without explicit row-level isolation.
reasoner_reviews.security: Stripe Connect webhook signature validation using stripe.webhooks.constructEvent() is mandatory on all payment events — missing this check would allow replay attacks against payment state. Cognito JWT must be validated on every Fastify route using a shared middleware, not per-handler logic. Officials must never be able to query another official's assignments or fee records via parameter tampering.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Redis TTL expiry for assignment deadlines is architecturally sound but the plan is missing the worker that consumes TTL expiry events (Redis keyspace notifications or a polling job). Expo OTA updates must not bypass App Store review requirements for substantive changes — OTA is for bug fixes only.
reasoner_reviews.critic: The architecture_overview conflates scheduling-service responsibilities with payments-service; the boundary is unclear in the plan. The data_model section lists generic entity names without the schema-per-service namespace prefixes (scheduling.*, payments.*, reporting.*). The api_design section is missing Stripe webhook endpoint and does not show how assignment request push notifications are triggered.

frontend.app_structure: Expo managed React Native app with Expo Router file-based navigation. Screens: (auth)/ login, (official)/ tabs: availability, assignments, fees, profile; (league_admin)/ tabs: matches, officials, schedule. Push notifications via expo-notifications with FCM for Android and APNs for iOS.
frontend.pages_and_flows: Official Availability Screen: weekly calendar grid, tap day to toggle available/unavailable, save updates availability_slots in scheduling-service. Assignment Request Screen: push notification deep-links to pending assignment detail (match, venue, date, fee); Accept/Decline buttons with Redis TTL countdown timer display. Fee Tracker Screen: list of completed assignments with status pending/disbursed, total earnings summary. Incident Report Screen: form with match selector, incident_type selector (violent conduct/red card dispute/abandonment), description textarea, photo upload.
frontend.state_management: Zustand for local UI state (calendar selections, notification badge count). React Query for server state (assignment list, fee records, incident reports) with 30s polling for assignment status changes. Expo SecureStore for Cognito token persistence.
frontend.frontend_risks: App Store review may reject OTA updates that change payment-related UI flows — enforce OTA-only for non-payment screens. FCM/APNs token rotation must be handled on every app foreground event.

backend.service_design: scheduling-service: manages officials, availability_slots, match_assignments; publishes assignment_created events to SNS topic for push notification delivery. payments-service: manages stripe_accounts (official Stripe Connect onboarding), fee_records per assignment, Stripe payout triggers, Stripe webhook consumer. reporting-service: manages incident_reports; generates PDF export via pdfmake; retains reports 7 years in S3.
backend.api_patterns: Each Fastify service exposes versioned REST endpoints at /v1/. Cognito JWT validated via @fastify/jwt plugin with Cognito public key URL. Authorization: scheduling-service routes enforce official_id = token.sub or league_id = token.league_id before returning data. No cross-service database queries — services communicate via SNS events or direct REST calls for synchronous needs.
backend.business_modules: AssignmentDeadlineWorker (scheduling-service): subscribes to Redis keyspace notifications for key expiry on assignment:{assignment_id}:deadline; marks assignment as expired and notifies league admin. StripeWebhookHandler (payments-service): validates Stripe signature on POST /webhooks/stripe, maps account.updated and transfer.paid events to fee_records status updates.
backend.backend_risks: Schema-per-service on shared PostgreSQL cluster means a runaway query in reporting-service can impact scheduling-service connection pool. Mitigation: per-service connection pool limits and separate read replicas for reporting.

security.auth_design: Cognito user pools: official pool and league_admin pool with separate app clients. Email/phone sign-in with SRP authentication. Cognito JWT includes custom attributes: role (official|league_admin), league_id (for league_admin only). JWT validated on every Fastify route via shared plugin.
security.authorization_model: scheduling-service: GET /assignments?official_id=X enforces official_id = token.sub (officials cannot query each other's assignments). payments-service: fee_records filtered by official_id = token.sub. reporting-service: incident reports filtered by submitted_by = token.sub or league_id = token.league_id.
security.abuse_prevention: Assignment accept/decline rate limited to 10 req/min per official via Fastify rate-limit plugin. Stripe webhook endpoint not behind Cognito auth — protected by Stripe signature validation only.
security.security_risks: Stripe Connect account ID must be stored in payments-service only; scheduling-service must never receive or log Stripe account IDs. Incident report photo uploads must be validated for MIME type and max 10MB before S3 PUT.

data.entities: scheduling schema: officials (id UUID PK, cognito_sub TEXT UNIQUE, display_name TEXT, sport_certifications TEXT[], joined_at TIMESTAMP), availability_slots (id UUID PK, official_id FK, date DATE, status ENUM available/unavailable), match_assignments (id UUID PK, match_id FK, official_id FK, role ENUM referee/umpire/linesperson, status ENUM pending/accepted/declined/expired/completed, response_deadline TIMESTAMP, fee_amount DECIMAL). payments schema: stripe_accounts (id UUID PK, official_id TEXT, stripe_account_id TEXT, onboarding_complete BOOLEAN), fee_records (id UUID PK, assignment_id TEXT, official_id TEXT, amount DECIMAL, status ENUM pending/disbursed/failed, stripe_transfer_id TEXT, disbursed_at TIMESTAMP). reporting schema: incident_reports (id UUID PK, match_id TEXT, submitted_by_official_id TEXT, incident_type TEXT, description TEXT, photo_s3_keys TEXT[], submitted_at TIMESTAMP, pdf_s3_key TEXT).
data.storage_design: PostgreSQL 16 shared cluster with scheduling, payments, and reporting schemas. Connection pools: 10 per service. Redis 7: assignment:{assignment_id}:deadline keys with TTL matching response_deadline. S3 bucket: incident-report-photos (private, signed URL access) and incident-report-pdfs (7-year retention lifecycle). Stripe Connect handles all banking details — no bank account numbers in PostgreSQL.
data.data_risks: shared PostgreSQL cluster is a single point of failure for all three services; evaluate dedicated RDS instance per service if connection pool contention is observed after launch.

target_output.thinking_summary: Round 1 first-pass plan for a sports official gig marketplace mobile app. The plan is intentionally flawed (plan_quality=flawed) per assignment: the architecture_overview conflates scheduling and payments service boundaries, the data_model omits schema-per-service namespace prefixes, and the api_design is missing the Stripe webhook endpoint and push notification trigger mechanism. These are the kinds of issues a round 2 auditor should flag. The microservices_decomposition theme should be visible but imprecisely executed so there is material for the auditor to critique.
target_output.title: Sports Official Marketplace — Architecture v1
target_output.executive_summary: React Native mobile app for freelance sports officials and league administrators, backed by three Node.js 22 Fastify microservices on AWS ECS Fargate: a scheduling service for availability and assignment management, a payments service for Stripe Connect disbursements, and a reporting service for post-match incident documentation. Officials receive push notifications for assignment requests and must accept or decline within a configurable deadline enforced by Redis TTL. Stripe Connect handles all banking details, keeping PCI-DSS scope minimal.
target_output.architecture_overview: React Native Expo app communicating over HTTPS to three Fastify microservices on ECS Fargate behind an Application Load Balancer. The scheduling-service and payments-service share the same PostgreSQL 16 cluster and both read from the officials table, which creates a cross-service data dependency. Cognito user pools authenticate officials and league admins. Push notifications are sent from the scheduling-service using SNS but the mechanism for routing from SNS to FCM/APNs device tokens is not fully specified. Stripe Connect webhooks are received by the payments-service but the endpoint is described in the same section as the Cognito-authenticated routes without clearly distinguishing that it bypasses Cognito auth and uses Stripe signature validation instead.
target_output.technology_stack: Frontend: React Native, TypeScript, Expo managed workflow, Expo Router, Zustand, React Query, expo-notifications. Backend: Node.js 22, Fastify 4, @fastify/jwt for Cognito JWT validation, Prisma ORM. Database: PostgreSQL 16 shared cluster, Redis 7 for assignment deadline TTL. Payments: Stripe Connect for official bank payouts. Hosting: AWS ECS Fargate, ALB, ECR. CI/CD: GitHub Actions, Expo EAS Build.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Official Availability Management: officials mark available/unavailable dates in weekly calendar view; availability_slots table updated via PATCH /officials/{id}/availability. Assignment Request Handling: league admin creates match assignment; scheduling-service sends push notification to official via SNS; official accepts or declines from app within deadline. Fee Tracking: payments-service exposes fee_records per official; React Query polling displays pending and disbursed amounts. Incident Reporting: official submits post-match incident report with type, description, and photo upload; reporting-service stores report and generates PDF for league archive. League Admin Dashboard: league admin creates matches, views official availability grid, assigns officials to roles, reviews submitted incident reports.
target_output.system_components: scheduling-service (Fastify, ECS Fargate): officials, availability_slots, match_assignments management; Redis deadline TTL writer; SNS publisher for push notifications. payments-service (Fastify, ECS Fargate): Stripe Connect account management, fee_records, Stripe Transfer API calls, Stripe webhook consumer. reporting-service (Fastify, ECS Fargate): incident_reports, PDF generation, S3 upload. PostgreSQL 16 shared cluster: scheduling and payments schemas co-located without strict connection isolation. Redis 7: assignment deadline TTL keys. S3: incident photos and PDFs. Cognito: official and league_admin user pools. SNS: push notification fanout — specific FCM/APNs routing mechanism not defined in this plan version.
target_output.workflows: Assignment Acceptance Workflow: league admin POSTs /matches/{id}/assignments (role, official_id, fee_amount, response_deadline) -> scheduling-service inserts match_assignments row with status=pending, sets Redis key assignment:{id}:deadline with TTL -> publishes to SNS -> (gap: SNS-to-FCM routing not specified) -> official receives push notification -> opens Assignment Request screen -> taps Accept -> PATCH /assignments/{id}/accept -> status=accepted, Redis key deleted. Fee Disbursement Workflow: once match status set to completed by league admin -> payments-service nightly batch queries match_assignments WHERE status=completed AND fee_records.status=pending -> calls Stripe Transfer API for each official with completed Stripe Connect onboarding -> updates fee_records.status=disbursed with stripe_transfer_id -> official sees disbursed status in Fee Tracker screen on next React Query poll.
target_output.data_model: officials (id UUID PK, cognito_sub TEXT, display_name TEXT, sport_certifications TEXT[], joined_at TIMESTAMP) — note: this table is referenced by both scheduling-service and payments-service without schema namespace prefix. availability_slots (id UUID PK, official_id UUID FK, date DATE, status TEXT). match_assignments (id UUID PK, match_id UUID, official_id UUID FK, role TEXT, status TEXT, response_deadline TIMESTAMP, fee_amount DECIMAL). stripe_accounts (id UUID PK, official_id UUID, stripe_account_id TEXT, onboarding_complete BOOLEAN). fee_records (id UUID PK, assignment_id UUID, official_id UUID, amount DECIMAL, status TEXT, stripe_transfer_id TEXT). incident_reports (id UUID PK, match_id UUID, submitted_by UUID, incident_type TEXT, description TEXT, photo_s3_keys TEXT[], submitted_at TIMESTAMP).
target_output.api_design: scheduling-service /v1/: GET /officials/{id}/availability, PATCH /officials/{id}/availability, GET /assignments?official_id={id}, POST /matches/{id}/assignments, PATCH /assignments/{id}/accept, PATCH /assignments/{id}/decline. payments-service /v1/: GET /officials/{id}/fees, POST /disbursements/trigger (manual batch trigger). reporting-service /v1/: POST /incident-reports, GET /incident-reports?official_id={id}, GET /incident-reports/{id}/pdf. Note: Stripe webhook endpoint POST /webhooks/stripe is not listed in this plan version; it exists in the service but is omitted from the API design section.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Cognito JWT validation via @fastify/jwt on all routes. RBAC: officials access own records only; league_admins access their league's records. Stripe Connect used for all banking details — no IBAN or routing numbers stored in PostgreSQL. TLS 1.3 on all API and Stripe communication. Stripe webhook signature validation is mentioned in requirements but the implementation note is missing from this section. PCI-DSS scope reduced to SAQ A because Stripe Connect handles card and bank data.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: GitHub Actions CI: npm ci, eslint, jest (unit tests for scheduling and payment logic), Docker build, push ECR, deploy ECS Fargate rolling update per service. Expo EAS Build for iOS and Android binaries on mobile release branches. Environments: dev (single ECS task per service, shared PostgreSQL dev schema), staging (2 tasks per service, staging Stripe keys), production (2-4 tasks per service, auto-scaling on CPU 60% target, Aurora PostgreSQL 16 multi-AZ). Secrets: AWS Secrets Manager for Stripe API keys, Cognito app client secrets, database credentials. Each service deployed independently via separate GitHub Actions workflows triggered by path filters.
target_output.observability: New Relic APM agent on each ECS task container: assignment_accept_latency_ms, stripe_transfer_success_rate, incident_report_pdf_generation_ms. Structured JSON logging with correlation_id propagated across services. Stripe webhook event log retained in payments-service database for payment audit trail and dispute resolution. CloudWatch alarms: ECS task restart rate >2 per hour, RDS connection count >80%.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: ECS Fargate: 3 services at 0.25 vCPU / 512MB baseline; estimated $90/month at steady state. Aurora PostgreSQL 16 Multi-AZ: db.t4g.medium at $140/month. Redis Cache: cache.t3.micro at $25/month. S3 for incident reports: minimal at expected report volume. Stripe Connect: no platform fee during MVP; post-launch transaction fees apply. Total estimated $280/month for initial launch. Auto-scaling adds tasks at CPU 60% threshold for scheduling-service during league season peaks.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (5 months): Cognito auth, official availability calendar, assignment accept/decline with Redis deadline TTL, basic fee display (no Stripe disbursement), incident report form and S3 storage, league admin match creation, GitHub Actions CI, ECS deployment. Phase 2 (3 months): Stripe Connect official onboarding, automated fee disbursement batch job, Stripe webhook handling, PDF generation for incident reports, push notification routing via Expo SNS + FCM/APNs. Phase 3: referee rating system, certification verification API integration, bulk match CSV import for league admins.
target_output.development_guidelines: Fastify routes must use @fastify/jwt preHandler on all authenticated routes — do not add JWT validation inline per handler. Prisma schema files are per-service and must not reference tables across service boundaries. Stripe API calls must use idempotency keys on all Transfer and Payout requests. React Native screens must handle push notification deep-link on cold app start (Expo Notifications getLastNotificationResponseAsync). Expo OTA updates allowed only for non-payment UI screens — payment flow changes require EAS Build and App Store review.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: shared PostgreSQL cluster creates cross-service coupling for connection pool management; if reporting-service runs heavy PDF generation queries it can starve scheduling-service connections. Mitigation: per-service connection pool limits via Prisma connection pool configuration; evaluate dedicated RDS instance per service if contention observed. Risk: Redis TTL-based assignment expiry does not survive Redis restart without persistence — use Redis AOF persistence or evaluate EventBridge scheduled rules as backup expiry mechanism. Tradeoff: Expo managed workflow limits native module access; if future features require deep native integration (biometric auth, BLE for venue check-in), ejection to bare workflow will be required.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Stripe Connect selected over manual bank transfer handling to eliminate PCI-DSS banking data scope. Expo managed workflow selected for OTA update capability on bug fixes. SNS selected as push notification backbone but FCM/APNs routing integration is deferred to Phase 2 — this is an open item remaining in the plan.

metadata.notes: Intentionally flawed first-pass: shared table cross-service reference, missing Stripe webhook in API design, SNS-to-FCM routing gap — suitable for auditor critique in round 2.

project_goal.value: Provide a Go SDK with an idiomatic, type-safe client for the official FIFA football data API covering match calendars, team rosters, live match events, and tournament bracket data
project_goal.rationale: Sports app developers need a reliable, well-structured SDK to consume FIFA data without building raw HTTP clients

target_users.value: Sports app developers and backend engineers building football-related applications
target_users.rationale: Primary consumers identified in the open-source SDK strategy document

project_class.rationale: Distributable Go module with no hosted runtime, only a package API surface

capabilities.rationale: SDK abstracts HTTP integration with the FIFA API and shapes typed data structures

complexity_level.rationale: Single upstream API client with pagination and retry; no database or auth server needed

risk_level.rationale: No sensitive data stored; SDK is read-only client for public football data

data_sensitivity.rationale: Football fixture and roster data is publicly available sports information

external_exposure.rationale: SDK is open-source and published to pkg.go.dev for public developer use

access_model.value: API key passed by the consuming application; SDK does not manage user identity
access_model.rationale: FIFA API requires an API key; SDK surfaces key configuration on client instantiation

feature_scope.value: Match fixture listing with pagination, team roster retrieval, live match event streaming, tournament bracket traversal, automatic OAuth token refresh, exponential backoff with jitter on rate-limit errors
feature_scope.rationale: Core data surfaces needed by football app developers

mvp_scope.value: MatchClient for fixture listing and live events, TeamClient for roster data, retry/backoff on transient errors
mvp_scope.rationale: Highest-demand endpoints to validate developer adoption before expanding coverage

security_baseline.value: API key stored in caller environment, never logged by SDK; TLS 1.3 for all upstream calls; SDK surfaces RateLimitError and AuthError types to prevent credential leakage in error messages
security_baseline.rationale: SDK must not become a credential-leakage vector for consuming applications

frontend_stack.value: none — library_sdk has no UI layer
frontend_stack.rationale: Package-only distribution; no frontend component

backend_stack.value: Go 1.22 with net/http and encoding/json from standard library
backend_stack.rationale: Go standard library is sufficient; minimizing dependencies keeps the SDK lightweight

data_platform.value: No persistent storage — all data returned from FIFA API calls is passed to the caller
data_platform.rationale: SDK is stateless; consumers manage their own storage

hosting_target.value: Go module published to pkg.go.dev via GitHub releases with semantic versioning
hosting_target.rationale: Standard Go module distribution; no infrastructure to operate

privacy_retention_policy.value: SDK retains no user data; API keys passed by callers are never persisted or logged
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Zero data retention required for a stateless SDK

future_scope.value: GraphQL client variant, webhook event listener for FIFA push notifications, caching layer as opt-in middleware
future_scope.rationale: Roadmap additions post v1.0 based on developer community requests

constraints.value: Zero mandatory runtime dependencies beyond Go standard library; SDK must compile on Go 1.22+
constraints.rationale: Minimal dependency surface prevents version conflicts in consumer projects

observability_baseline.value: Structured debug logging via slog with log level controlled by caller; request/response timing exposed via optional hook interface
observability_baseline.rationale: Consumers need observability hooks but the SDK itself has no telemetry infrastructure

execution_preference.value: Open-source development with semantic versioning; breaking changes only on major version bumps
execution_preference.rationale: Consumer applications depend on API stability; semver contract is essential

project.goal: Idiomatic Go SDK for FIFA football data API

backend.api: FIFA REST API client with typed response structs

security.auth: API key passed by caller on client construction
security.transport: TLS 1.3 for all upstream FIFA API requests

reasoner_reviews.product: The FIFA API covers the highest-demand football data surfaces for app developers. Starting with MatchClient and TeamClient addresses the 80% use case; tournament bracket traversal is a differentiating feature absent from most community clients.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Go stdlib net/http is sufficient for a single-upstream SDK. The retry/backoff logic and automatic OAuth token refresh are the primary complexity points and deserve well-tested implementations. Functional options pattern for client configuration keeps the API ergonomic.
reasoner_reviews.security: API keys passed by callers must never appear in debug logs or error message strings. The SDK should define named error types RateLimitError and AuthError that surface status codes without echoing credentials. TLS 1.3 minimum is non-negotiable for FIFA API communication.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Zero mandatory dependencies is the right call for an SDK — dependency sprawl creates version conflicts in downstream projects. slog for structured logging is stdlib in Go 1.22 and avoids introducing a logging dependency.

backend.service_design: Three top-level client types exported as package API: MatchClient (fixtures, live events), TeamClient (rosters, squad details), TournamentClient (brackets, round metadata). Each wraps a shared httpClient with retry middleware.
backend.api_patterns: Functional options pattern for client construction: NewMatchClient(apiKey, WithTimeout(30s), WithRetryMax(3)). All response types are Go structs with json tags matching FIFA API field names. Paginated endpoints return an iter.Seq iterator in Go 1.22 style.
backend.failure_handling: Retry loop with exponential backoff starting at 500ms, jitter 0-250ms, max 3 attempts. On HTTP 429, SDK honors Retry-After header. Typed errors: RateLimitError, AuthError, NotFoundError, APIError wrapping status code and FIFA error body.
backend.backend_risks: FIFA API schema changes can break deserialization silently — mitigation: strict JSON decoding with DisallowUnknownFields option flag, integration tests against recorded API responses.

security.auth_design: No auth managed by SDK; caller passes apiKey string to NewMatchClient. SDK sets Authorization header on each request, never stores key beyond request scope.
security.secrets_management: API key held only in memory on the httpClient struct; zeroed on client GC. SDK logs redact any header containing Authorization string.
security.security_risks: Consumers may accidentally log the client struct — mitigate by implementing a custom String() method that redacts the apiKey field.

data.entities: MatchFixture (id, tournamentID, homeTeam, awayTeam, kickoffUTC, status, venue), TeamRoster (teamID, players []PlayerSummary), LiveMatchEvent (id, matchID, minute, eventType, playerID, detail), BracketRound (id, name, matches []MatchFixture)
data.storage_design: No storage layer — all types are in-memory value types returned to callers
data.data_risks: FIFA API occasionally returns partial responses during live matches — SDK should attach DataIncompleteFlag to LiveMatchEvent when required fields are absent rather than returning an error that breaks the consumer event loop.

devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: golangci-lint on PR, go test -race ./... with -coverprofile, integration tests against recorded VCR fixtures using go-vcr, goreleaser on tag push to publish binaries and update pkg.go.dev
devops.devops_risks: pkg.go.dev indexing delay after tag push — document expected propagation time in CONTRIBUTING.md.

target_output.thinking_summary: Designing a Go SDK for the FIFA football data API with a package-only architecture — no hosted infrastructure, no persistent storage. Key decisions: three typed client structs (MatchClient, TeamClient, TournamentClient) sharing a common httpClient with retry/backoff middleware; functional options pattern for ergonomic configuration; Go 1.22 standard library only to keep the dependency surface at zero. Security focus is on credential non-leakage in logs and typed error surfaces.
target_output.title: FIFA Football Data Go SDK Architecture
target_output.executive_summary: A zero-dependency Go 1.22 SDK published to pkg.go.dev providing type-safe clients for the FIFA official football data API. Exposes MatchClient, TeamClient, and TournamentClient covering fixture calendars, team rosters, live match events, and tournament brackets. Automatic OAuth token refresh, paginated iterators, and exponential-backoff retry logic are built in. No hosted infrastructure — the SDK is a Go module distributed via GitHub releases.
target_output.architecture_overview: Pure library architecture with three exported client types sharing a common internal httpClient. The httpClient wraps net/http with a retry middleware that implements exponential backoff with jitter and honors Retry-After headers on HTTP 429 responses. OAuth token lifecycle is managed internally: clients check token expiry before each request and refresh transparently without caller intervention. All response types are value-type Go structs generated from the FIFA API OpenAPI spec, with strict JSON decoding to surface schema drift early. The package exposes zero global state — all configuration is scoped to the client instance.
target_output.technology_stack: Language: Go 1.22. HTTP: net/http standard library. JSON: encoding/json with DisallowUnknownFields option. Logging: log/slog (stdlib). Pagination: Go 1.22 iter.Seq iterators. Testing: go test -race, go-vcr for VCR cassette-based integration tests. Linting: golangci-lint. Release: goreleaser. Distribution: Go module on pkg.go.dev via GitHub releases tagged with semver.
target_output.functional_feature_map: MatchClient: ListFixtures(ctx, tournamentID, opts) returns paginated MatchFixture iterator; GetLiveEvents(ctx, matchID) returns a slice of LiveMatchEvent; GetMatchDetail(ctx, matchID) returns full MatchDetail. TeamClient: GetRoster(ctx, teamID, seasonYear) returns TeamRoster with player summaries; ListSquads(ctx, tournamentID) lists all participating teams. TournamentClient: GetBracket(ctx, tournamentID) returns bracket tree of BracketRound nodes; ListTournaments(ctx) returns upcoming and past editions. All clients share: automatic OAuth token refresh, retry on transient errors, typed error hierarchy.
target_output.system_components: httpClient (internal): wraps net/http.Client with retry middleware, Authorization header injection, slog debug tracing. tokenStore (internal): holds current OAuth token and expiry, refreshes proactively 60 seconds before expiration. MatchClient, TeamClient, TournamentClient: exported package-level types, each constructed via New*Client functional options. Error types: RateLimitError (wraps RetryAfter duration), AuthError (wraps HTTP status), NotFoundError, APIError (generic). iter utilities: paginatedIter wrapping FIFA API pagination cursor for Go 1.22 range-compatible iteration.
target_output.workflows: Fixture Retrieval: Consumer calls matchClient.ListFixtures(ctx, 'FIFA_WC_2026', WithDateRange(start, end)) — SDK checks OAuth token expiry, refreshes if needed, sends GET /tournaments/FIFA_WC_2026/fixtures, paginates through cursor-linked pages, and yields each MatchFixture to the caller's range loop. Rate Limit Recovery: Upstream returns HTTP 429 with Retry-After: 8 — SDK reads the header, sleeps 8 seconds, retries the request transparently, increments retry counter; after max retries surfaces RateLimitError{RetryAfter: 8s} to the caller without leaking the Authorization header value.
target_output.data_model: MatchFixture (ID string, TournamentID string, HomeTeam TeamRef, AwayTeam TeamRef, KickoffUTC time.Time, Status MatchStatus ENUM scheduled/live/finished/postponed, VenueName string, VenueCity string). TeamRoster (TeamID string, SeasonYear int, Players []PlayerSummary). PlayerSummary (ID string, Name string, Position PlayerPosition ENUM goalkeeper/defender/midfielder/forward, JerseyNumber int, NationalityCode string). LiveMatchEvent (ID string, MatchID string, Minute int, AddedTime int, EventType EventType ENUM goal/yellow_card/red_card/substitution/var_review, PlayerID string, Detail string, DataIncompleteFlag bool). BracketRound (ID string, Name string, Matches []MatchFixture).
target_output.api_design: Package API surface (not HTTP endpoints — SDK is a client library): matchClient.ListFixtures(ctx context.Context, tournamentID string, opts ...FixtureOption) iter.Seq[MatchFixture, error]. matchClient.GetLiveEvents(ctx, matchID string) ([]LiveMatchEvent, error). teamClient.GetRoster(ctx, teamID string, seasonYear int) (TeamRoster, error). tournamentClient.GetBracket(ctx, tournamentID string) (BracketRound, error). Construction: NewMatchClient(apiKey string, opts ...ClientOption) *MatchClient. Options: WithTimeout(d time.Duration), WithRetryMax(n int), WithDebugLogger(l *slog.Logger), WithBaseURL(u string).
target_output.security_and_compliance: API key non-leakage: MatchClient.String() returns 'MatchClient{apiKey:[REDACTED]}'; slog handler strips Authorization header values from request log lines. TLS: http.Transport configured with TLS MinVersion tls.VersionTLS13. Error messages: RateLimitError and AuthError expose HTTP status code and RetryAfter but never the request Authorization header value. Dependency security: zero external dependencies eliminates supply-chain risk; govulncheck runs in CI on every PR.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: GitHub Actions CI pipeline: on PR — golangci-lint, go vet, go test -race ./... with -coverprofile (80% gate), go-vcr integration tests replaying recorded FIFA API cassettes. On push to main — generate updated VCR cassettes if upstream response shape changed. On semver tag push — goreleaser builds multi-arch binaries, creates GitHub release, triggers pkg.go.dev indexing. CHANGELOG.md maintained via conventional commits. Breaking changes only on major version; deprecated symbols carry two-minor-version grace period before removal.
target_output.observability: No SDK-owned telemetry infrastructure. Observability hooks: optional slog.Logger passed via WithDebugLogger logs request method, URL path (no query params containing keys), response status, and elapsed time at DEBUG level. Optional RequestHook and ResponseHook interfaces on ClientOption allow callers to plug in their own tracing (e.g., OpenTelemetry spans). go test -race detects concurrency issues in CI. govulncheck in CI flags known CVEs in transitive dependencies if any are added in future.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: No operational cost — SDK is a static Go module with no hosted infrastructure. Consumer cost considerations: FIFA API has request quotas; SDK's automatic retry/backoff prevents quota burns from transient errors. Concurrent usage of multiple client instances is safe — all state is instance-scoped, no global mutexes.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (3 weeks): MatchClient with ListFixtures and GetLiveEvents, TeamClient with GetRoster, httpClient with retry/backoff and OAuth token refresh, go-vcr test suite with recorded FIFA API responses, GitHub Actions CI, goreleaser release pipeline, v0.1.0 published to pkg.go.dev. Phase 2 (2 weeks): TournamentClient with bracket traversal, paginated iter.Seq iterators for all list endpoints, WithDebugLogger and hook interfaces, v0.2.0. Phase 3 (1 week): 80% test coverage gate enforced, govulncheck integrated, CHANGELOG automation, v1.0.0 stable release with compatibility guarantee.
target_output.development_guidelines: All exported types and functions carry godoc comments — CI fails if godoc coverage drops below 100% for exported symbols. Error wrapping uses fmt.Errorf with %w; never use errors.New for typed errors. No global state — client instances are the configuration boundary. New FIFA API endpoints added as methods on existing client types, not new packages. Cassette-based VCR tests added for every new endpoint before merging. Conventional commits enforced via commitlint.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: FIFA API schema changes break deserialization silently. Mitigation: DisallowUnknownFields on JSON decoder; integration tests replay real API responses via VCR cassettes and fail on field changes. Risk: OAuth token refresh adds latency to first request after expiration. Mitigation: proactive refresh 60 seconds before expiry eliminates mid-request delays. Tradeoff: zero-dependency constraint rules out well-tested retry libraries like avast/retry-go — custom backoff implementation must be covered by unit tests. Tradeoff: iter.Seq pagination requires Go 1.22+ minimum; older consumer projects must upgrade.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Chose iter.Seq over channel-based pagination for better context cancellation and no goroutine leak risk. Selected go-vcr for integration tests over live API calls to keep CI hermetic. Decided against caching layer in v1.0 — consumers have diverse caching needs; an opt-in middleware interface will be provided in v1.1.

metadata.notes: Go SDK for FIFA football API; zero-dependency library_sdk with typed clients, retry/backoff, and OAuth token refresh.

project_goal.value: Replace a legacy SOAP workforce management system for a professional sports league office with a modern REST API exposing staff contract management, compliance certification tracking, payroll data sync, and organizational hierarchy for 30 member clubs
project_goal.rationale: Legacy SOAP system is unmaintainable and blocks integrations with modern club HR platforms

target_users.value: Club HR directors, league compliance officers, and payroll administrators across 30 member clubs
target_users.rationale: Primary users identified in league digitization initiative

project_class.rationale: Headless REST API with no UI layer consumed by club HR systems and league admin portal

capabilities.value: backend, data, auth, public_api, integrations, admin_panel, batch_jobs
capabilities.rationale: Full backend capability surface: external partner API, internal admin, nightly batch certification checks

complexity_level.rationale: Multi-tenant partner API with GDPR data residency constraints, complex employment rule validation, and immutable audit trail

risk_level.rationale: Handles confidential staff contracts and payroll data across 30 clubs with cross-jurisdictional legal obligations

data_sensitivity.rationale: Staff salaries, contract terms, and coaching certifications are confidential employment data

external_exposure.rationale: API consumed by club HR systems over mTLS; not exposed to the public internet

access_model.value: Azure AD Entra ID OAuth 2.0 with per-club tenant isolation; league admin service account for cross-club read access; mTLS for club HR system integrations
access_model.rationale: Multi-tenant identity essential for data segregation between 30 clubs

feature_scope.value: Staff contract creation and versioning, contract validation against league employment rules, compliance certification tracking with expiry alerts, payroll data encrypted submission to league payroll processor, org hierarchy CRUD, immutable audit trail, batch certification gap reporting
feature_scope.rationale: Complete replacement of all SOAP endpoints in the legacy system

mvp_scope.value: Contract submission and retrieval, certification tracking with expiry detection, payroll encrypted submission endpoint, per-club org hierarchy
mvp_scope.rationale: Critical path features blocking club HR integrations; admin and batch reporting in phase 2

security_baseline.value: mTLS for club HR system connections, AES-256 encryption at rest for payroll data, Azure Key Vault for secrets, RBAC per club tenant, immutable audit trail in append-only table, TLS 1.3
security_baseline.rationale: Confidential employment data and partner-facing exposure mandate defense-in-depth

frontend_stack.value: none - headless API service; admin portal is a separate project
frontend_stack.rationale: api_service project class; no UI in scope

backend_stack.value: Java 21 with Spring Boot 3.3, Spring Security, Spring Data JPA, Spring Batch
backend_stack.rationale: League engineering team expertise; Spring Batch handles certification gap batch jobs

data_platform.value: PostgreSQL 16 with row-level security for club tenant isolation; Redis 7 for rate limit counters and distributed locks
data_platform.rationale: RLS enforces club data boundaries at the database layer as defense-in-depth

hosting_target.value: Azure Kubernetes Service in West Europe and North Europe regions for EU data residency
hosting_target.rationale: 30 clubs span EU and non-EU jurisdictions; AKS multi-region satisfies EU data residency requirements

privacy_retention_policy.value: Staff contract data retained for duration of employment plus 7 years per local employment law; right to erasure for non-EU clubs on request; payroll records retained 10 years
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Cross-jurisdictional employment retention requirements vary; 10-year payroll floor is most conservative applicable law

future_scope.value: GraphQL API variant, self-service club HR onboarding portal, automated contract anomaly detection
future_scope.rationale: Phase 3 roadmap after full club adoption

constraints.value: EU data must not leave West Europe and North Europe Azure regions; SOAP compatibility shim required for 6 months during club migration; API response time SLA 200ms p99 for contract reads
constraints.rationale: Data residency mandated by league legal; SOAP shim needed for clubs on legacy integration timelines

observability_baseline.value: Datadog APM with distributed tracing across Spring Boot services, structured JSON logs with clubID correlation field, PagerDuty on error rate greater than 0.5% or p99 latency greater than 200ms
observability_baseline.rationale: League SLA requires proactive alerting to meet 200ms p99 contract

execution_preference.value: Phased club onboarding: 5 early-adopter clubs in phase 1, full 30-club rollout in phase 2
execution_preference.rationale: Reduces blast radius during migration from legacy SOAP system

compliance_context.value: GDPR for EU staff personal and financial data; employment law data residency controls per jurisdiction; processing agreements with all 30 clubs required before data ingestion
compliance_context.rationale: High risk and confidential data sensitivity mandate formal GDPR compliance posture

project.goal: Replace legacy SOAP workforce management with modern REST API for league office and 30 clubs
project.target_users: Club HR directors, league compliance officers, payroll admins
project.timeline: MVP in 12 weeks, full rollout in 24 weeks

backend.framework: Spring Boot 3.3 with Spring Security, Spring Data JPA, Spring Batch

security.auth: Azure AD Entra ID OAuth 2.0, mTLS for club HR integrations
security.encryption: AES-256 at rest for payroll data, TLS 1.3 in transit

data.cache: Redis 7 for rate limits and distributed batch locks

devops.hosting: Azure Kubernetes Service West Europe and North Europe

constraints.data_residency: EU data stays in West Europe and North Europe AKS regions
constraints.soap_shim: 6-month SOAP compatibility shim for migrating clubs

open_questions.right_to_erasure: Non-EU club staff erasure workflow to be defined in legal review

reasoner_reviews.product: Replacing a SOAP system used by 30 clubs is a high-coordination modernization. The phased 5-club pilot approach is correct - league compliance team needs to validate rule enforcement logic before full rollout. Contract versioning and the immutable audit trail are the highest-value differentiators over the legacy system.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Spring Batch is the right choice for nightly certification gap jobs - it handles restartable job instances and partitioned processing across clubs. PostgreSQL RLS for tenant isolation adds a database-layer safety net beyond application-level clubID filtering. The SOAP compatibility shim should be a thin translation layer in a dedicated Spring Boot module, not embedded in the core service.
reasoner_reviews.security: mTLS for club HR system connections is mandatory given partner_facing exposure. Azure Key Vault should manage the TLS certificates and rotate them automatically. Payroll data AES-256 encryption must be applied at the application layer before writing to PostgreSQL - RLS alone is not sufficient for payroll confidentiality. The immutable audit trail must be an append-only table with no DELETE privilege granted to the application service account.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: 200ms p99 for contract reads with 30-club multi-tenant load is achievable with connection pooling via PgBouncer and Redis caching of org hierarchy data which changes infrequently. Data residency for EU clubs requires AKS node affinity rules and PostgreSQL instance pinning to West/North Europe regions.

backend.service_design: Spring Boot application with domain modules: contracts (submission, versioning, rule validation), certifications (tracking, expiry detection), payroll (encrypted submission, forwarding), orgHierarchy (CRUD, club tenant scoped), audit (append-only event recording), soapShim (SOAP-to-REST translation for migrating clubs).
backend.api_patterns: REST API versioned under /api/v1. Contract endpoints: POST /api/v1/clubs/{clubId}/contracts, GET /api/v1/clubs/{clubId}/contracts/{contractId}, PATCH /api/v1/clubs/{clubId}/contracts/{contractId}/status. Certification: GET /api/v1/clubs/{clubId}/certifications/expiring?withinDays=30. Payroll: POST /api/v1/clubs/{clubId}/payroll/submissions. Audit: GET /api/v1/audit-trail?clubId={clubId}&from={ts}.
backend.background_jobs: Spring Batch nightly job: CertificationExpiryJob partitions across 30 clubs, queries certification expiry dates, generates ComplianceGapReport per club, emails league compliance team via SendGrid. ContractAnomalyJob flags contracts with salary fields outside league pay scale bounds for manual review.
backend.failure_handling: Transactional outbox pattern for payroll submission forwarding: submission written to DB and outbox atomically, a separate relay process reads the outbox and forwards to league payroll processor, marks forwarded on ACK. Prevents data loss if payroll processor is temporarily unavailable.

security.auth_design: Azure AD Entra ID OAuth 2.0; each club HR system authenticates with a dedicated service principal scoped to their clubId claim. League admin service account has cross-club read scope. mTLS enforced at the AKS ingress for club HR integrations using cert-manager issued certificates rotated every 90 days.
security.authorization_model: Spring Security method-level @PreAuthorize with clubId extracted from JWT claim; PostgreSQL RLS policies enforce clubId = current_setting('app.club_id') on all contract, certification, and payroll tables as defense-in-depth.
security.audit_and_logging_controls: Every API mutation writes an AuditEvent (actor, clubId, action, resourceType, resourceId, before_hash, after_hash, timestamp) to the audit_events table which has no DELETE or UPDATE grants. Datadog log pipeline indexes audit events with a 7-year retention policy.
security.secrets_management: Azure Key Vault holds TLS certificates, payroll AES-256 encryption key, SendGrid API key. Spring Cloud Azure Key Vault integration pulls secrets at pod startup; rotation triggers pod restart via AKS pod identity.

data.entities: staff_contracts (id, club_id, staff_member_id, role, start_date, end_date, salary_encrypted, status ENUM draft/active/expired/terminated, version_number, created_by, created_at), contract_versions (id, contract_id, changed_fields_json, changed_by, changed_at), compliance_certifications (id, club_id, staff_member_id, cert_type, cert_body, issued_date, expiry_date, document_s3_key, status ENUM valid/expiring_soon/expired), payroll_submissions (id, club_id, pay_period, payload_encrypted, forwarded_at, processor_ack_id), org_hierarchy_nodes (id, club_id, parent_id, title, staff_member_id), audit_events (id, actor_id, club_id, action, resource_type, resource_id, before_hash, after_hash, timestamp).
data.schema_notes: PostgreSQL RLS: SET LOCAL app.club_id = ? on each transaction; RLS policies on contracts, certifications, payroll_submissions filter WHERE club_id = current_setting('app.club_id')::uuid. salary_encrypted stored as bytea, decrypted only in the contracts domain service.
data.retention_and_deletion: Spring Batch ArchivalJob runs quarterly: contracts older than employment_end_date + 7 years moved to cold_contracts table; payroll_submissions purged after 10 years. GDPR erasure for non-EU clubs: staff_member personal fields nulled, audit_events actor_id replaced with anonymized token.

devops.deployment_topology: AKS cluster in West Europe (primary) and North Europe (DR standby). Deployments via Helm charts. PostgreSQL primary in West Europe with streaming replication to North Europe read replica. Redis Sentinel for HA. cert-manager for mTLS certificate lifecycle. AKS pod identity for Key Vault access.
devops.ci_cd_design: Azure DevOps pipeline: unit and integration tests with TestContainers spinning PostgreSQL, SpotBugs and OWASP dependency-check, Docker build and push to ACR, Helm deploy to staging AKS, contract API smoke tests, promote to production on approval gate. Migration scripts run via Flyway in a pre-deploy job.
devops.rollback_strategy: Helm rollback to previous release on failed smoke tests. Database migrations backward-compatible for one release cycle. New version receives 10% of club traffic for 30 minutes before full rollout via Helm canary weight configuration.

target_output.thinking_summary: Modernizing a SOAP workforce management system for a 30-club sports league into a partner-facing REST API. Key decisions: per-club tenant isolation with PostgreSQL RLS plus Entra ID service principals; Spring Batch for certification gap jobs; transactional outbox for payroll forwarding reliability; mTLS enforced at AKS ingress; SOAP compatibility shim as a separate module to avoid polluting the core service. Data residency requires AKS node affinity and PostgreSQL instances pinned to EU Azure regions.
target_output.title: Sports League Workforce Management REST API Architecture
target_output.executive_summary: A modern partner-facing REST API replacing a legacy SOAP workforce management system for a professional sports league, serving 30 member clubs via Azure Kubernetes Service with PostgreSQL 16 and row-level security for club tenant isolation. Covers staff contract versioning, compliance certification tracking, encrypted payroll submission, and organizational hierarchy. Built on Java 21 with Spring Boot 3.3 and Spring Batch for nightly compliance gap reporting. mTLS enforced for club HR integrations. GDPR and multi-jurisdictional employment data residency requirements met via AKS regional pinning.
target_output.architecture_overview: Multi-tenant REST API deployed on AKS across West Europe (primary) and North Europe (DR standby) to satisfy EU data residency obligations. Club HR systems connect via mTLS to the AKS ingress, authenticate with Entra ID service principals scoped to their clubId, and interact with domain modules covering contracts, certifications, payroll, org hierarchy, and audit. PostgreSQL RLS policies enforce club tenant boundaries at the database layer independently of application-level filtering. A SOAP translation shim module handles the 6-month migration window for clubs still on legacy integrations. Spring Batch runs nightly certification gap jobs partitioned across all 30 clubs. Payroll forwarding uses a transactional outbox pattern to guarantee delivery to the league payroll processor without synchronous coupling.
target_output.technology_stack: Backend: Java 21, Spring Boot 3.3, Spring Security, Spring Data JPA, Spring Batch, Spring Cloud Azure. Database: PostgreSQL 16 with RLS, PgBouncer connection pooling. Cache: Redis 7 Sentinel for rate limit counters and org hierarchy cache. Identity: Azure AD Entra ID OAuth 2.0 with mTLS client certificates via cert-manager. Infrastructure: AKS (West Europe and North Europe), Azure Key Vault, Azure Container Registry, Helm, Flyway migrations. CI/CD: Azure DevOps with TestContainers integration tests. Observability: Datadog APM with structured JSON logs.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Contract Management: clubs submit, retrieve, and update staff contracts with full version history; rule engine validates salary against league pay scale bounds. Certification Tracking: per-staff certification records with expiry detection; expiring_soon status surfaced via API and nightly email alerts. Payroll Submission: clubs POST encrypted payroll bundles; transactional outbox relays to league payroll processor with ACK tracking. Org Hierarchy: per-club org chart CRUD with staff assignments and parent-child node traversal. Audit Trail: every mutation recorded to append-only audit_events with actor, before/after hash, and timestamp. SOAP Shim: translates legacy SOAP envelope operations to internal service calls for migrating clubs.
target_output.system_components: Spring Boot API service with contracts, certifications, payroll, orgHierarchy, audit, and soapShim modules. AKS ingress with mTLS termination and cert-manager. PostgreSQL 16 primary (West Europe) with streaming replication to North Europe read replica. PgBouncer connection pool. Redis 7 Sentinel cluster. Azure Key Vault (TLS certs, payroll encryption key, SendGrid key). Azure AD Entra ID tenant with club service principals. Spring Batch CertificationExpiryJob and ContractAnomalyJob. Transactional outbox relay process. Azure Container Registry.
target_output.workflows: Contract Submission: Club HR system sends POST /api/v1/clubs/{clubId}/contracts with mTLS cert and Bearer token - AKS ingress validates mTLS, Spring Security extracts clubId from JWT claim, contracts module validates salary against pay scale rules, writes staff_contracts row and contract_versions snapshot, writes AuditEvent, returns 201 with contractId. Certification Gap Reporting: Nightly Spring Batch CertificationExpiryJob partitions across 30 clubIds, queries compliance_certifications WHERE expiry_date <= now() + 30 days AND status != 'expired', generates ComplianceGapReport per club, sends targeted email via SendGrid to club HR contact and cc's league compliance team. Payroll Relay: Club POSTs encrypted payload to /api/v1/clubs/{clubId}/payroll/submissions - service writes payroll_submissions row and outbox_events row atomically in one transaction, returns 202 Accepted; outbox relay polls for unforwarded rows, POSTs to league payroll processor, marks forwarded_at and stores processor_ack_id on success.
target_output.data_model: staff_contracts (id UUID PK, club_id UUID FK, staff_member_id UUID, role VARCHAR, start_date DATE, end_date DATE, salary_encrypted BYTEA, status ENUM draft/active/expired/terminated, version_number INT, created_by UUID, created_at TIMESTAMPTZ). contract_versions (id UUID PK, contract_id UUID FK, changed_fields_json JSONB, changed_by UUID, changed_at TIMESTAMPTZ). compliance_certifications (id UUID PK, club_id UUID FK, staff_member_id UUID, cert_type VARCHAR, cert_body VARCHAR, issued_date DATE, expiry_date DATE, document_s3_key TEXT, status ENUM valid/expiring_soon/expired). payroll_submissions (id UUID PK, club_id UUID FK, pay_period VARCHAR, payload_encrypted BYTEA, forwarded_at TIMESTAMPTZ, processor_ack_id TEXT). org_hierarchy_nodes (id UUID PK, club_id UUID FK, parent_id UUID NULLABLE, title VARCHAR, staff_member_id UUID NULLABLE). audit_events (id UUID PK, actor_id UUID, club_id UUID, action VARCHAR, resource_type VARCHAR, resource_id UUID, before_hash TEXT, after_hash TEXT, timestamp TIMESTAMPTZ). All tables carry club_id for RLS enforcement.
target_output.api_design: REST API at /api/v1. Contracts: POST /api/v1/clubs/{clubId}/contracts, GET /api/v1/clubs/{clubId}/contracts/{contractId}, PATCH /api/v1/clubs/{clubId}/contracts/{contractId}/status. Certifications: GET /api/v1/clubs/{clubId}/certifications?status=expiring_soon, POST /api/v1/clubs/{clubId}/certifications, PUT /api/v1/clubs/{clubId}/certifications/{certId}. Payroll: POST /api/v1/clubs/{clubId}/payroll/submissions (async, 202 Accepted). Org: GET /api/v1/clubs/{clubId}/org-hierarchy, POST /api/v1/clubs/{clubId}/org-hierarchy/nodes. Audit: GET /api/v1/audit-trail?clubId={clubId}&from={iso8601}&to={iso8601}. Admin (league-scope): GET /api/v1/admin/compliance/gaps?withinDays=30.
target_output.security_and_compliance: mTLS enforced at AKS ingress for all club HR system connections; cert-manager issues 90-day certificates per club service principal, auto-rotated. Entra ID OAuth 2.0 with clubId claim; Spring Security @PreAuthorize extracts clubId and rejects cross-club access. PostgreSQL RLS policies on contracts, certifications, payroll_submissions: club_id = current_setting('app.club_id')::uuid blocks cross-tenant reads at DB layer. Payroll data encrypted with AES-256 using Azure Key Vault managed key before INSERT. audit_events table: no DELETE or UPDATE grants to app service account - append-only. GDPR: staff personal fields erasable via anonymization API. Data residency: AKS node affinity rules and PostgreSQL instances locked to West Europe and North Europe regions.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: AKS clusters in West Europe (primary, 3-node) and North Europe (DR, 2-node). Helm chart with environment overlays for staging and production. Azure DevOps pipeline: unit tests and TestContainers integration tests, SpotBugs, OWASP dependency-check, Docker build to ACR, Helm deploy to staging, contract API smoke tests, approval gate, production Helm deploy with 10% canary for 30 minutes before full rollout. Flyway migrations in pre-deploy init container. PostgreSQL WAL streaming replication to North Europe; failover via manual promotion runbook. Redis Sentinel with 1 master and 2 replicas across AKS nodes.
target_output.observability: Datadog APM with Spring Boot micrometer integration: distributed traces across contract, certification, and payroll modules with clubId tag. Structured JSON logs with fields: clubId, requestId, operation, durationMs, statusCode. Custom metrics: contract submission rate per club, certification expiry count per club, payroll relay lag (outbox to forwarded_at delta), p99 latency per endpoint. PagerDuty alerts: error rate greater than 0.5% sustained 2 minutes, p99 latency greater than 200ms for contract reads, outbox relay lag greater than 15 minutes, PostgreSQL replication lag greater than 60 seconds.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: AKS: 3 Standard_D4s_v3 nodes in West Europe, 2 in North Europe. PostgreSQL: General Purpose 8 vCore primary and read replica. Redis: Standard_C1 Sentinel. Estimated approximately $1800/month for 30 clubs at steady state. Scaling: HorizontalPodAutoscaler on CPU 65% target; PgBouncer pool sizing tuned for 30 concurrent club connections. Cost optimization: dev and staging clusters use B-series burstable node pools; production reserved instances for 1-year commitment.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (12 weeks): contract submission and retrieval with rule validation, certification tracking with expiry detection API, encrypted payroll submission endpoint, per-club org hierarchy, audit trail, mTLS ingress, Entra ID OAuth, PostgreSQL RLS, SOAP shim for migrating clubs, Flyway schema v1, CI/CD pipeline, 5-club pilot rollout. Phase 2 (8 weeks): Spring Batch CertificationExpiryJob and ContractAnomalyJob, league-admin compliance gap report API, transactional outbox relay with monitoring, GDPR erasure API, full 30-club rollout. Phase 3 (6 weeks): OpenAPI spec published to developer portal, GraphQL API variant exploration, self-service club HR onboarding, automated pay scale anomaly alerting.
target_output.development_guidelines: Java 21 records for DTO types; no mutable domain objects crossing module boundaries. Spring Security @PreAuthorize on every controller method - no unannotated endpoints pass CI check. Flyway migration scripts reviewed by a second engineer before merge. TestContainers integration tests for every repository method; no mocking of the database. Structured logging with MDC: clubId and requestId set on thread context at filter layer.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: PostgreSQL RLS policy misconfiguration could silently allow cross-club data access. Mitigation: integration tests assert that club A service principal cannot retrieve club B contract data. Risk: SOAP shim adds complexity and maintenance burden. Mitigation: shim is an isolated module with sunset date contractually agreed with each migrating club. Tradeoff: transactional outbox adds latency to payroll acknowledgement (202 Accepted) - acceptable because payroll processor SLA is next-business-day not real-time. Risk: Entra ID service principal proliferation across 30 clubs creates operational overhead. Mitigation: automated service principal provisioning and certificate rotation scripts in the onboarding runbook.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Chose AKS over Azure Container Apps for node affinity control required for data residency pinning. Selected PostgreSQL RLS over application-layer tenant filtering as defense-in-depth. Transactional outbox chosen over direct synchronous forwarding to decouple from payroll processor availability. SOAP shim implemented as a separate Spring Boot module rather than embedded in core service to allow independent deprecation.

metadata.notes: Partner-facing REST API replacing legacy SOAP for 30-club sports league; mTLS, Entra ID, PostgreSQL RLS, Spring Batch certification jobs.

project_goal.value: macOS desktop application for elite swimming coaches to ingest race timing system exports, display 50m split-time breakdowns per swimmer, compare performance against personal bests and season targets, and generate printable PDF heat sheets with color-coded performance gaps
project_goal.rationale: Coaches need local-first tooling independent of internet connectivity at pool deck and competition venues

target_users.value: Elite swimming head coaches and assistant coaches at national-level training centers and swim clubs
target_users.rationale: Primary users identified in coach interview sessions

project_class.rationale: All data stored and processed locally on the coach's Mac; no server infrastructure

capabilities.rationale: SwiftUI UI, embedded Swift processing, SQLite persistence, and in-app performance analytics

complexity_level.rationale: Proprietary timing file parsing and PDF generation add complexity beyond basic CRUD

risk_level.rationale: Internal athlete performance data on a local Mac; no network exposure

data_sensitivity.rationale: Athlete split times and personal bests are internal coaching data, not PHI or financial

external_exposure.rationale: App runs entirely on the coach's Mac with no network calls during normal operation

access_model.value: Single-user macOS app with no login; data stored in app sandbox
access_model.rationale: Coaches are sole operators of their local instance; multi-user not required

feature_scope.value: Omega and Colorado Timing file import, split-time breakdown table with delta vs personal best, season target tracking, PDF heat sheet generation with color-coded performance gaps, swimmer roster management, competition history browser
feature_scope.rationale: Full feature set identified in coach requirements workshops

mvp_scope.value: Omega timing file import, split-time breakdown with personal best deltas, basic swimmer roster, PDF heat sheet export
mvp_scope.rationale: Core competition-day workflow to validate with pilot coaches before adding history browser

security_baseline.value: App sandbox enforced by macOS; no outbound network entitlements; SQLite database stored in app container; Sentry error reporting is the only network call
security_baseline.rationale: Local-only app; attack surface is limited to local file system access

frontend_stack.value: SwiftUI for macOS 14+ (Sonoma) with Combine for reactive data binding
frontend_stack.rationale: Native macOS look and feel; SwiftUI supports multi-column layouts needed for split tables

backend_stack.value: Swift with embedded SQLite via GRDB.swift; PDF generation via PDFKit (AppKit)
backend_stack.rationale: All processing in-process; GRDB.swift provides type-safe SQLite access in Swift

data_platform.value: SQLite 3 via GRDB.swift, stored in macOS app sandbox container
data_platform.rationale: Local-first; SQLite is sufficient for a single coach's roster and competition history

hosting_target.value: macOS app distributed via Mac App Store and direct download with notarization
hosting_target.rationale: Mac App Store for discovery; direct download for teams behind enterprise IT restrictions

privacy_retention_policy.value: Data stored locally on coach's Mac; coaches control retention via app data deletion; no data sent to external servers except Sentry crash reports (anonymized)
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Local-only app has no server-side data retention obligations

future_scope.value: iCloud sync for multi-device access, companion iOS app for pool deck use, FinTech timing system support
future_scope.rationale: Phase 2 roadmap based on coach feedback

constraints.value: Must run on macOS 14 Sonoma and later; app must work fully offline; PDF heat sheets must be printable on standard US Letter and A4 paper
constraints.rationale: Coaches use Macs provided by national federations running Sonoma; venues may lack internet

observability_baseline.value: Sentry for crash reporting with anonymized stack traces; no performance monitoring infrastructure
observability_baseline.rationale: Local app with no server telemetry needs; crash reports inform future updates

execution_preference.value: 3 pilot coaches testing alpha builds; TestFlight-style direct distribution during beta
execution_preference.rationale: Tight coach feedback loop needed before Mac App Store submission

project.goal: Local macOS app for swim coach race analysis and PDF heat sheet generation
project.target_users: Elite swimming coaches at national training centers
project.timeline: MVP in 10 weeks, Mac App Store submission in 16 weeks

frontend.approach: Multi-column layout: roster panel, split time table, comparison chart

backend.framework: GRDB.swift for SQLite, PDFKit for PDF generation

security.auth: no authentication - local single-user app

data.cache: in-memory Swift collections for active session

devops.hosting: Mac App Store and direct distribution with notarization

constraints.paper_sizes: US Letter and A4 PDF output

open_questions.colorado_timing: Colorado Timing format parser to be validated with sample files from pilot coaches

reasoner_reviews.product: The core value proposition is the split-time delta comparison against personal bests - this is the daily tool coaches reach for during competition warm-up review. PDF heat sheet generation must be fast and layout-correct for immediate pool-deck printing. The competition history browser is secondary and can be thinner in MVP.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: GRDB.swift is the right choice over CoreData for type-safe SQL and easy export/backup. PDFKit handles page layout for heat sheets natively without third-party dependencies. The Omega timing file parser will be the most brittle component - design it behind a protocol so Colorado Timing and future formats are pluggable. SwiftUI NavigationSplitView handles the three-panel roster/event/split-table layout idiomatically on macOS.
reasoner_reviews.security: macOS App Sandbox with no outbound network entitlements is the correct posture. File import should use NSOpenPanel which is sandboxed. Sentry crash reporting requires the com.apple.security.network.client entitlement - scope it to crash upload only and document it in the App Store privacy declaration.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Offline-first design means no iCloud sync in MVP - use local SQLite in the app container. PDF layout must be tested against both US Letter and A4 in CI using snapshot tests. Xcode Cloud handles code signing and notarization automatically on tagged commits.

frontend.app_structure: SwiftUI App with NavigationSplitView: sidebar (roster and competition list), content column (event/heat selection), detail column (split-time breakdown table). Combine publishers drive reactive updates when a new timing file is imported.
frontend.pages_and_flows: Roster Manager screen: add/edit swimmers with name, stroke specialties, personal best times. Import screen: drag-and-drop timing file or NSOpenPanel picker, format auto-detected from file header. Split Analysis screen: two-column table showing 50m splits with delta cells colored green/amber/red vs personal best. Heat Sheet Builder: select event and competition, preview PDF with lane assignments and seed times, print or export PDF.
frontend.performance_notes: Split-time tables for 200+ swimmer heats must render without jank - use LazyVStack for heat rows and pre-compute delta values on background thread with Swift concurrency.

backend.service_design: TimingImporter protocol with OmegaTimingParser and ColoradoTimingParser conformances. SplitAnalysisService computes delta values from stored personal bests. HeatSheetRenderer wraps PDFKit page layout logic for letter and A4 formats.
backend.failure_handling: TimingImporter returns Result<[HeatResult], ImportError> - UI presents errors inline without crashing. Unknown timing format returns ImportError.unsupportedFormat with the detected file header for diagnostics.

data.entities: swimmers (id, first_name, last_name, birth_year, club_name), personal_bests (id, swimmer_id, stroke_event, distance_meters, time_hundredths, set_at), competitions (id, name, venue, date, timing_format), heat_entries (id, competition_id, event_name, heat_number, lane, swimmer_id, seed_time_hundredths), split_times (id, heat_entry_id, split_number, split_distance_meters, split_time_hundredths, cumulative_time_hundredths)
data.migration_notes: GRDB migrations versioned in AppDatabase.migrator; migration 1 creates all tables; future iCloud sync will require switching to CloudKit-backed store in migration 3 onwards.

devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: SwiftLint, swift test unit and snapshot tests, archive and export with Xcode Cloud trigger on tag. Xcode Cloud handles signing, notarization, and TestFlight distribution to 3 pilot coaches. Mac App Store submission triggered by approved Xcode Cloud build.
devops.environments: local (debug SQLite in Xcode simulator), beta (TestFlight to pilot coaches), production (Mac App Store)

target_output.thinking_summary: Designing a local-first macOS desktop app for swim coach race analysis. Key decisions: SwiftUI NavigationSplitView for three-panel race analysis layout; GRDB.swift for type-safe SQLite with versioned migrations; TimingImporter protocol for pluggable Omega and Colorado Timing format parsers; PDFKit for heat sheet generation supporting Letter and A4 layouts. Xcode Cloud automates signing, notarization, and TestFlight beta distribution. No network calls except Sentry crash reporting.
target_output.title: Elite Swimming Race Analysis macOS App Architecture
target_output.executive_summary: A local-first macOS 14 Sonoma application for elite swimming coaches built with SwiftUI and embedded Swift processing. Imports proprietary Omega and Colorado Timing race files, renders per-swimmer 50m split-time breakdowns with color-coded deltas against stored personal bests, and generates printable PDF heat sheets for Letter and A4 paper. All data persisted locally in SQLite via GRDB.swift. Distributed via Mac App Store and direct download with Xcode Cloud automated signing and notarization. Fully offline - no network calls except Sentry crash reporting.
target_output.architecture_overview: Single-process macOS application with a SwiftUI view layer and a Swift domain layer communicating through Combine publishers. The TimingImporter protocol decouples the UI from timing file format specifics - OmegaTimingParser and ColoradoTimingParser conform independently and are selected at runtime based on file header detection. GRDB.swift manages a SQLite database in the macOS app sandbox, with typed record structs for all domain entities and versioned migrations. SplitAnalysisService fetches personal bests and computes delta values on a background actor, publishing results to the split table view. HeatSheetRenderer uses PDFKit to lay out multi-heat sheets with lane assignments, seed times, and performance gap annotations on both US Letter and A4 page geometries. Sentry is the only network entitlement in the sandbox configuration.
target_output.technology_stack: Language: Swift 5.10. UI: SwiftUI (macOS 14+) with NavigationSplitView, LazyVStack, Charts framework for progression graphs. Reactive: Combine for publisher/subscriber data flow. Database: SQLite 3 via GRDB.swift 6.x with typed records and migration chain. PDF: PDFKit (AppKit) for heat sheet page layout. Error reporting: Sentry Swift SDK. CI/CD: GitHub Actions (SwiftLint, swift test) and Xcode Cloud for signing, notarization, TestFlight distribution, and App Store submission. Testing: Swift Testing framework with snapshot tests for PDF layout validation.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Roster Manager: add and edit swimmer profiles with birth year, club affiliation, stroke specialties, and per-event personal best times stored with date of achievement. Timing File Import: drag-and-drop or NSOpenPanel import of Omega and Colorado Timing .txt/.hy3 files; auto-detect format from file header, parse all heat entries and split times, associate with selected competition. Split Analysis View: two-column expandable table showing each swimmer's 50m splits, cumulative time, and delta cells colored green (at or below personal best), amber (up to +0.2s), or red (greater than +0.2s). Heat Sheet Builder: select event and competition, assign lanes from seeded times, preview heat sheet layout, export or print PDF on Letter or A4. Competition History: browse all imported competitions with event summaries and best performances.
target_output.system_components: SwiftUI App entry point with NavigationSplitView (RosterSidebar, EventList, SplitDetailPanel). TimingImporter protocol with OmegaTimingParser and ColoradoTimingParser implementations. AppDatabase (GRDB DatabaseQueue, migration chain). SplitAnalysisService (Swift actor, background computation of delta values). HeatSheetRenderer (PDFKit PDFDocument construction for Letter and A4). SentryManager (crash reporting, initialized with anonymized DSN). Combine pipelines connecting database changes to view model updates.
target_output.workflows: Timing File Import: Coach opens Import sheet, drags Omega .txt file onto drop zone - OmegaTimingParser reads file header 'OMEGA TIMING', parses heat blocks, extracts swimmer names, event codes, lane assignments, and 50m split times. SplitAnalysisService looks up each swimmer in the roster by name match, fetches personal bests, computes delta per split. NavigationSplitView detail column updates via Combine publisher to show colored split table within 200ms of import completion. Heat Sheet Generation: Coach selects event 'Women 200m Butterfly' for 'Australian Open 2026', HeatSheetRenderer queries heat_entries sorted by seed_time_hundredths, computes lane assignments, constructs PDFPage instances with PDFKit drawing commands rendering swimmer name, lane number, seed time, and performance gap indicator per row. PDF exported to Desktop or sent to AirPrint-connected printer without leaving the app sandbox.
target_output.data_model: swimmers (id UUID PK, first_name TEXT, last_name TEXT, birth_year INT, club_name TEXT, created_at TEXT). personal_bests (id UUID PK, swimmer_id UUID FK, stroke_event TEXT e.g. butterfly, distance_meters INT, time_hundredths INT, set_at TEXT DATE). competitions (id UUID PK, name TEXT, venue TEXT, date TEXT, timing_format ENUM omega/colorado). heat_entries (id UUID PK, competition_id UUID FK, event_name TEXT, heat_number INT, lane INT, swimmer_id UUID FK NULLABLE, seed_time_hundredths INT NULLABLE). split_times (id UUID PK, heat_entry_id UUID FK, split_number INT, split_distance_meters INT, split_time_hundredths INT, cumulative_time_hundredths INT). All stored in SQLite via GRDB typed records with codable conformance.
target_output.api_design: In-process Swift API (no HTTP endpoints). Public interfaces: TimingImporter.parse(fileURL: URL) async -> Result<[HeatResult], ImportError>. SplitAnalysisService.computeDeltas(heatEntryId: UUID) async -> [SplitDelta]. AppDatabase.saveCompetition(_ competition: Competition) throws. AppDatabase.fetchSplitsByEntry(heatEntryId: UUID) throws -> [SplitTime]. HeatSheetRenderer.render(event: String, competitionId: UUID, pageSize: HeatSheetPageSize) -> PDFDocument. All async methods execute on a dedicated background actor to keep the SwiftUI main actor free.
target_output.security_and_compliance: macOS App Sandbox entitlements: com.apple.security.files.user-selected.read-only for NSOpenPanel timing file imports; com.apple.security.network.client scoped to Sentry DSN domain only. No outbound network calls to external APIs during normal operation. SQLite database stored in app container directory inaccessible to other apps. Sentry crash reports use anonymized user identifier - no PII transmitted. App Store privacy declaration: data type App Functionality stored on device, not linked to user identity, not used for tracking.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: GitHub Actions: SwiftLint, swift test with code coverage (80% coverage gate), snapshot tests for PDF page layout on Letter and A4. Xcode Cloud triggered on tag v* builds: archive, export, sign with Distribution certificate, notarize with notarytool, upload to TestFlight for 3 pilot coaches. Production Mac App Store submission triggered by manual approval of Xcode Cloud build after beta feedback cycle. Direct download: notarized .dmg with installer published to GitHub Releases.
