frontend.frontend_risks: Tryout registration form may collect minor's information; ensure COPPA compliance notice and parental consent flow if collecting data for under-13s

target_output.thinking_summary: Simple static Next.js site for a youth soccer academy with Contentful CMS and Vercel Edge hosting. Key decisions: generateStaticParams for all tryout event pages, webhook-triggered ISR rebuilds, Plausible cookieless mode for COPPA compliance. No forms on the static site — registration deferred to a future backend service. Weak quality means some sections will be thin.
target_output.title: Youth Soccer Academy Static Website
target_output.executive_summary: A Next.js 14 static export website for a youth soccer academy delivering program listings, coach bios, tryout event schedules, and photo galleries to prospective families. Content is managed in Contentful and published to Vercel Edge via webhook-triggered rebuilds with no server-side runtime and no personal data collection, ensuring COPPA compliance by design.
target_output.architecture_overview: Fully static Next.js 14 site with Contentful Content Delivery API as the data source at build time. All program, coach, tryout event, and gallery pages are pre-rendered HTML deployed to Vercel Edge CDN. A single serverless function handles Contentful webhook events to trigger incremental static regeneration. Plausible Analytics runs in cookieless mode. No database, no sessions, no server processes running post-deploy.
target_output.technology_stack: Frontend: Next.js 14 static export, TypeScript, Tailwind CSS, next-contentful SDK. CMS: Contentful with GraphQL Content Delivery API. Hosting: Vercel Edge Network with automatic HTTPS. Analytics: Plausible Analytics cookieless. CI: GitHub Actions with Lighthouse CI. next-sitemap for automatic XML sitemap generation on build. react-schema-dts for JSON-LD structured data (LocalBusiness and SportsActivityLocation schemas) improving Google rich result eligibility for program listings.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Programs pages: pre-rendered per age group from Contentful program entries via generateStaticParams. Coaching staff directory: individual coach bio pages with photo and certifications. Tryout event listing: age-group-filtered pages with date and registration deadline. Announcement banner: Contentful-managed global notice. Photo gallery: album-tagged grid from Contentful media assets.
target_output.system_components: Contentful CMS, Contentful CDN for media, Next.js static export build, Vercel Edge Network, Vercel Serverless Function for webhook ISR, GitHub Actions CI, Plausible Analytics. next-sitemap sitemap generator (build-time), next-schema-dts JSON-LD injector (per-page component), Contentful Webhook listener (Vercel Deploy Hook URL), Vercel Analytics real-user performance metrics (Core Web Vitals per route).
target_output.workflows: Content Update: Academy staff edits coach bio in Contentful and publishes; Contentful fires webhook to Vercel Deploy Hook; serverless function validates HMAC and calls revalidatePath for affected routes; updated pages served from CDN within 60 seconds. New Season Launch: Engineering adds program entries in Contentful, updates generateStaticParams queries, merges to main; GitHub Actions lint and Lighthouse CI pass; Vercel deploys updated static export.
target_output.data_model: programs (id: string, name: string, age_group: string, season: string, description: string, hero_image: ContentfulAsset). coaches (id: string, name: string, role: string, bio: string, photo: ContentfulAsset, certifications: string[]). tryout_events (id: string, age_group: string, event_date: string, location_name: string, registration_deadline: string). announcements (id: string, title: string, body: string, published_at: string, expires_at: string|null). gallery_items (id: string, image: ContentfulAsset, caption: string, album_tag: string). All TypeScript interfaces generated from Contentful schema at build time. No runtime database.
target_output.api_design: No public-facing API. Build-time fetching via Contentful GraphQL. Webhook receiver: POST /api/revalidate — validates x-contentful-signature HMAC-SHA256, calls revalidatePath for affected content type route patterns. No runtime endpoints exposed to visitors.
target_output.security_and_compliance: HTTPS via Vercel TLS. CSP in next.config.js: default-src 'self'; script-src 'self' plausible.io; img-src 'self' images.ctfassets.net. Webhook receiver validates x-contentful-signature HMAC-SHA256. Plausible in cookieless mode — COPPA compliant, no consent banner needed. Zero data collection forms on static pages.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: Vercel connected to GitHub repository. Preview deployments on PRs. Production on merge to main. Contentful webhook with Deploy Hook URL in Vercel env var. GitHub Actions: ESLint, tsc --noEmit, Lighthouse CI on PR — score regressions block merge. Vercel Analytics enabled in project settings for real-user Core Web Vitals monitoring without a cookie banner. Image optimisation uses next/image with Contentful image transformation URL params (w=, fm=webp) to eliminate cumulative layout shift on staff bio photos.
target_output.observability: Plausible Analytics tracks pageviews by path, referral sources, and tryout event page engagement. Vercel deployment notifications to Slack. Lighthouse CI score history tracked in GitHub Actions artifacts. Vercel Analytics tracks Web Vitals (LCP, FID, CLS) per route to catch regressions after Contentful content updates that alter hero image dimensions. Uptime Robot monitors the production URL on a 5-minute interval with Slack webhook notification.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Vercel Hobby tier sufficient for estimated traffic under 5000 monthly visits. Contentful free tier. Plausible Analytics $9/month. Total approximately $9/month. Upgrade path: Vercel Pro at $20/month adds concurrent builds and Vercel Analytics expanded history if traffic exceeds Hobby tier. Contentful CDN handles global image delivery at no additional cost within the free tier bandwidth allocation.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (weeks 1-3): Contentful schema, Next.js page templates, Tailwind components, GitHub Actions CI, Vercel setup with webhook. Phase 2 Content (weeks 4-5): Marketing populates programs, coaches, spring tryout events. Phase 3 Launch (week 6): Lighthouse audit pass, CSP validation, Plausible cookieless verification.
target_output.development_guidelines: TypeScript strict mode. ESLint with next/recommended. Contentful types generated via contentful-typescript-codegen. Lighthouse accessibility score >95 enforced in CI. No inline event handlers or scripts. next/image required for all editorial photography — raw img tags blocked via ESLint rule. Contentful field names snake_case to align with TypeScript codegen output; camelCase conversion handled by SDK.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Contentful outage prevents content updates. Mitigation: existing pages remain served from Vercel CDN; manual Deploy Hook trigger available for urgent updates. Tradeoff: static export over SSR eliminates real-time personalization — accepted for fully public content.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Plausible over Google Analytics for COPPA compliance — cookieless mode eliminates consent banner for under-13 visitors. Static export chosen over SSR to eliminate server infrastructure. Registration forms excluded from static site scope.

metadata.notes: Simple static Next.js site for youth soccer academy with Contentful CMS; COPPA-compliant no-data-collection design; weak quality.

project_goal.value: Publicly accessible static website publishing 20 years of marathon race results with pre-built finisher listing pages, split-time breakdowns, and age-group leaderboards generated at build time from CSV exports
project_goal.rationale: Replace slow dynamic result-lookup system with fast static CDN delivery

target_users.value: Marathon finishers looking up personal results, coaches analyzing athlete splits, and race statisticians researching historical leaderboard data
target_users.rationale: Three distinct audiences identified in user research

project_class.rationale: All data pre-rendered at build time from CSV pipeline — no server-side query engine needed

capabilities.rationale: Static delivery with CloudWatch analytics; no backend required

complexity_level.rationale: 20 years of data across hundreds of editions requires complex build-time processing and search index generation

risk_level.rationale: Public archival data with no user accounts or financial transactions

data_sensitivity.rationale: Race result data publicly distributed by timing officials; no private data stored by the site

external_exposure.rationale: Global public archive accessible to all

access_model.value: Fully public read access — no authentication; S3 bucket policy allows public GET via CloudFront OAC only
access_model.rationale: Historical results intended for open public access

feature_scope.value: Race edition index pages, per-edition finisher listing with bib lookup, split-time breakdown per 5km segment, age-group leaderboards, overall and gender top-10 leaderboards, build-time search index for name and bib, course record hall of fame
feature_scope.rationale: Full feature set for a 20-year results archive

mvp_scope.value: Last 5 years of race editions with finisher listings and split times; name search by bib number
mvp_scope.rationale: Covers the most-accessed historical window first

security_baseline.value: HTTPS via CloudFront ACM certificate, S3 bucket not publicly accessible directly (CloudFront OAC only), no user input forms
security_baseline.rationale: Defense-in-depth for read-only static content delivery

frontend_stack.value: Astro with TypeScript
frontend_stack.rationale: Astro build-time data integration and island architecture ideal for large static page generation from CSV data

backend_stack.value: No backend runtime; Python 3.12 build scripts for CSV ingestion; Pagefind for client-side search indexing
backend_stack.rationale: All data transformation happens at build time in CI pipeline

data_platform.value: No database; CSV source files in S3 ingested at build time via Python scripts; Pagefind search index built as static JSON bundles
data_platform.rationale: Immutable historical data suits static file storage

hosting_target.value: AWS CloudFront + S3
hosting_target.rationale: High-throughput CDN for global race result traffic spikes on race day

privacy_retention_policy.value: GDPR takedown process: finishers may request removal of their named result via email; affected pages regenerated with record redacted within 5 business days
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: GDPR Article 17 right to erasure applies to named personal result data in the EU

future_scope.value: Live race-day result streaming via SSE to a dynamic result page, mobile app integration for personal result sharing
future_scope.rationale: Real-time race day display requires a separate dynamic architecture

constraints.value: Build time must complete within 30 minutes for 20 years of data; CloudFront in us-east-1 for ACM certificate compatibility
constraints.rationale: CI runner time limits and AWS ACM regional requirement

observability_baseline.value: AWS CloudWatch for CloudFront request metrics (4xx/5xx rates, cache hit ratio); GitHub Actions build time tracking
observability_baseline.rationale: CDN performance visibility sufficient for static content monitoring

execution_preference.value: Annual full rebuild; ad-hoc partial rebuilds after each race event
execution_preference.rationale: Race results added once per event; annual full rebuild for historical corrections

project.goal: Publish 20 years of marathon results as a fast globally accessible static archive

security.cdn: CloudFront with OAC to S3; public direct S3 access blocked

data.source: CSV exports from race timing system in S3
data.search: Pagefind static index built at compile time

devops.trigger: Manual workflow dispatch or CSV commit to data branch

reasoner_reviews.product: The 20-year archive scope is the primary user value proposition. The GDPR takedown process is correctly scoped as a documented procedure rather than automated tooling — request volume will be low enough for manual handling. Build-time name search via Pagefind eliminates the need for a backend query service.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Astro is the right choice for large-scale static generation. The 30-minute build constraint requires parallelized page generation. Pagefind is purpose-built for this pattern: it scans built HTML and generates compact JSON search indexes without a separate backend. Recommend sharding finisher pages by race edition to avoid single large page payloads.
reasoner_reviews.security: CloudFront OAC replacing the deprecated OAI pattern is correct for restricting S3 direct access. ACM certificate must be in us-east-1 for CloudFront. No user input means no XSS surface beyond the search widget which reads static JSON bundles.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: 30-minute build constraint achievable with Astro parallelized generation and incremental CSV processing that skips unchanged editions. Recommend caching Python CSV output in GitHub Actions cache keyed by CSV file hash.

backend.service_design: Build pipeline: Python 3.12 pandas and polars for CSV normalization, split time computation, leaderboard ranking, and Pagefind-compatible HTML annotation. Astro content collections load transformed JSON from build script output. No runtime backend.
backend.api_patterns: No HTTP API. Data contract: Python scripts output race_edition_{year}_{city}.json, finishers_{edition_id}.json, leaderboards_{edition_id}.json consumed by Astro getCollection(). Pagefind indexes generated post-build via pagefind --source dist.
backend.backend_risks: Build time growth as archive expands — mitigate with incremental processing tracking processed_editions.json manifest and skipping editions with unchanged CSV checksums.

security.auth_design: No authentication. CloudFront OAC policy restricts S3 GetObject to CloudFront service principal. S3 bucket policy denies all public GetObject.
security.abuse_prevention: CloudFront WAF basic rate rule 1000 requests per 5 minutes per IP to prevent scraping of finisher name data.
security.privacy_controls: GDPR takedown: maintain redact_list.json in repo with bib numbers of erasure-requested finishers; Python build script excludes matching records from all generated pages and search index.

data.entities: race_editions (edition_id, year, city, course_name, date, total_finishers, course_record_time, course_record_holder). finisher_records (finisher_id, edition_id, bib_number, athlete_name, finish_time_seconds, age_group, gender, nationality). split_times (finisher_id, checkpoint_km: 5|10|15|20|25|30|35|40|42.2, split_time_seconds, cumulative_time_seconds). age_group_categories (category_id, label, min_age, max_age, gender). course_records (edition_id, category, holder_name, time_seconds, set_date). gender_leaderboards (edition_id, gender, rank, finisher_id).
data.schema_notes: All entities materialized as static JSON files during Python build step. Finisher records sharded at 500 records per JSON file to keep page payload sizes manageable.
data.data_risks: Finisher name typos in source CSV propagate to live pages — CSV validation script in CI flags suspicious characters before build proceeds.

devops.deployment_topology: GitHub Actions runner builds Astro site and syncs dist/ to S3 via aws s3 sync. CloudFront invalidation on each deployment. S3 versioning enabled for rollback. CloudFront with PriceClass_All for global audience.
devops.ci_cd_design: Workflow triggers: push to data/ branch or manual workflow_dispatch. Steps: checkout, Python CSV validation, pandas build scripts, astro build, Pagefind index, s3 sync, CloudFront invalidation, build time assertion under 30 min.
devops.observability_stack: CloudWatch metrics: 4xxErrorRate, 5xxErrorRate, CacheHitRate (target >95%). Alarm on 5xxErrorRate >0.1% for 5 minutes triggers SNS email to ops team.

frontend.app_structure: Astro static site with TypeScript; race edition pages and finisher listings pre-rendered from structured JSON/CSV data; bib lookup as client island
frontend.pages_and_flows: Home, Race Edition Index (20 years), Per-Edition Finisher Listing, Bib Number Lookup, Split-Time Detail, Records Page
frontend.ui_modules: RaceEditionCard, FinisherListingTable with pagination, BibLookupSearchIsland (client), SplitTimeBreakdownTable, RecordsLeaderboard
frontend.performance_notes: Large finisher listings (thousands of rows) use Astro's static pagination to generate per-page HTML; bib lookup uses Pagefind or prebuilt lookup index
frontend.accessibility_notes: Results tables need caption, thead with scope; bib lookup search input must have visible label and announce result count via aria-live

target_output.thinking_summary: Large-scale static archive for marathon results — 20 years, hundreds of editions, potentially millions of finisher records. Key decisions: Astro content collections for typed static data ingestion, Python pandas pipeline for CSV normalization and leaderboard computation, Pagefind for client-side search without a backend, S3 and CloudFront OAC for secure global delivery. GDPR takedown via redact_list.json in the build pipeline.
target_output.title: Marathon Results Archive Static Website
target_output.executive_summary: An Astro-based static website publishing 20 years of marathon race results to a global audience via AWS CloudFront and S3. All finisher listings, split-time breakdowns, age-group leaderboards, and course records are pre-rendered at build time from a CSV export pipeline. A Pagefind client-side search index enables name and bib-number lookups without any backend runtime. A documented GDPR takedown process redacts personal results from future builds within five business days of request.
target_output.architecture_overview: Fully static architecture with Astro 4 generating pre-rendered HTML pages for every race edition, finisher result, and leaderboard from JSON data produced by a Python 3.12 build pipeline. The Python pipeline reads CSV timing exports from S3, normalizes split times, computes age-group rankings, and emits sharded JSON bundles consumed by Astro content collections. After the Astro build, Pagefind scans generated HTML and produces compact JSON search indexes. The dist/ directory is synced to an S3 bucket served exclusively through CloudFront with Origin Access Control. No runtime servers, no databases, no user sessions.
target_output.technology_stack: Frontend: Astro 4 with TypeScript, Tailwind CSS, Astro content collections for typed result data. Search: Pagefind static index via pagefind --source dist. Data pipeline: Python 3.12 with pandas and polars for CSV ingestion and leaderboard ranking. Hosting: AWS S3 (static origin) with CloudFront OAC and ACM TLS certificate in us-east-1. CI: GitHub Actions with aws-actions/configure-aws-credentials and CloudFront invalidation.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Race Edition Index: list of all marathon editions by year with total finishers and course record. Per-Edition Finisher Pages: paginated HTML tables with bib, name, finish time, and split link; sharded at 500 finishers per page. Split Time Detail: per-finisher page with 5km checkpoint splits and pace-per-km. Age-Group Leaderboards: per-category top-25 per edition. Overall and Gender Top-10 per edition. Course Record Hall of Fame across all editions. Name/Bib Search via Pagefind client-side widget.
target_output.system_components: Python CSV Validation Script, Python Pandas Build Pipeline, Astro Content Collections, Astro Page Generator, Pagefind Indexer, GitHub Actions CI, S3 Origin Bucket (versioned), CloudFront Distribution (OAC and WAF rate rule).
target_output.workflows: Post-Race CSV Import: timing official commits finisher CSV to data/ branch; GitHub Actions CI triggers; Python validation script checks schema and redact_list bib numbers; pandas pipeline normalizes splits and ranks age groups; Astro generates all affected edition pages; Pagefind re-indexes; aws s3 sync uploads changed files; CloudFront wildcard invalidation. GDPR Takedown: finisher emails ops team; staff adds bib to redact_list.json and merges; next build omits finisher from all pages; CloudFront invalidation within 15 minutes of deploy.
target_output.data_model: race_editions (edition_id: string, year: int, city: string, course_name: string, date: string, total_finishers: int, course_record_time_seconds: int, course_record_holder: string). finisher_records (finisher_id: string, edition_id: string, bib_number: string, athlete_name: string, finish_time_seconds: int, age_group: string, gender: string, nationality: string). split_times (finisher_id: string, checkpoint_km: 5|10|15|20|25|30|35|40|42.2, split_time_seconds: int, cumulative_time_seconds: int). age_group_categories (category_id: string, label: string, min_age: int, max_age: int, gender: string). course_records (edition_id: string, category: string, holder_name: string, time_seconds: int, set_date: string). gender_leaderboards (edition_id: string, gender: string, rank: int, finisher_id: string). All materialized as static JSON files at build time via Python pipeline.
target_output.api_design: No HTTP API. Build-time data interface: Python outputs to src/content/editions/*.json and src/content/finishers/{edition_id}/*.json consumed by Astro getCollection(). Pagefind client API: window.pagefind.search(query) returns result objects with metadata. CloudFront serves index HTML with Cache-Control: public, max-age=3600 and hashed assets with max-age=31536000.
target_output.security_and_compliance: HTTPS via CloudFront ACM certificate in us-east-1. S3 bucket policy denies all GetObject except from CloudFront OAC service principal. CloudFront WAF rate rule: 1000 requests per 5 minutes per IP. No cookies, no user input forms, no third-party script origins. GDPR: redact_list.json mechanism removes named personal data from all future builds; documented 5 business-day SLA for takedown completion.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: GitHub Actions workflow: trigger on push to data/ branch or manual workflow_dispatch with rebuild_type input (incremental or full). Steps: configure-aws-credentials, Python 3.12, pip install pandas polars, validate_csvs.py, build_results.py (skips editions matching processed_editions.json checksums), astro build, pagefind --source dist, aws s3 sync with cache-control settings, CloudFront create-invalidation --paths '/*'. Build time gate: CI step timeout assertion at 30 minutes. S3 versioning for rollback.
target_output.observability: CloudWatch dashboard: 4xxErrorRate, 5xxErrorRate, CacheHitRate (target >95%), BytesDownloaded. Alarm: 5xxErrorRate >0.1% over 5 minutes triggers SNS email. GitHub Actions build duration tracked in workflow summary; alert on build exceeding 28 minutes.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: S3 storage approximately $5/month for 50GB of pre-rendered HTML and JSON. CloudFront data transfer $20-50/month depending on traffic; race-day spikes handled by CDN elasticity. WAF basic rule $5/month. Total approximately $30-60/month.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (months 1-2): Python CSV pipeline for last 5 race editions, Astro templates, Pagefind search integration, CloudFront and S3 deployment, GitHub Actions CI. Phase 2 Full Archive (month 3): Process all 20 years of CSV data, age-group leaderboards, course record hall of fame, incremental rebuild optimization. Phase 3 Post-Launch: GDPR tooling improvements, CloudWatch alarm tuning.
target_output.development_guidelines: Astro TypeScript strict mode. Content collection schemas defined with Zod for build-time JSON validation. Python scripts must pass ruff linting with unit tests for split-time normalization. Build time asserted in CI. Pagefind bundle size must not exceed 5MB total for fast search loading on mobile.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: CSV data quality issues silently corrupt leaderboard rankings. Mitigation: Python validation with schema checks and duplicate detection exits CI on error. Risk: GDPR takedown spanning multiple editions requires full rebuild. Mitigation: redact_list.json applied globally across all editions in every build. Tradeoff: static search over Elasticsearch means no fuzzy matching — accepted for archive use case where exact bib or name is typically known.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Pagefind selected over Lunr.js — smaller indexes and native Astro post-build integration. CloudFront OAC confirmed over deprecated OAI pattern. Finisher JSON sharded at 500 records per file to bound Astro memory during large edition processing.

metadata.notes: Strong first-pass for 20-year marathon results archive; Astro+Pagefind+CloudFront OAC; GDPR takedown via build-time redact_list.json.

project_goal.value: TypeScript SDK standardizing ingestion and normalization of workout data from Apple Health, Garmin Connect, Polar Flow, and Wahoo into a unified activity schema with consistent units, timezone handling, and data-quality flags
project_goal.rationale: Core SDK value proposition for fitness app developers

backend.api: Programmatic SDK API — no HTTP endpoints

security.auth: Consumer-supplied OAuth tokens; SDK validates token presence and scope
security.token_safety: Never log, serialize, or persist OAuth tokens

data.normalization: Unit conversion tables, timezone normalization, data-quality flag logic

devops.ci: GitHub Actions: lint, type-check, test, build dual CJS/ESM

constraints.dependencies: axios only — no other third-party deps

reasoner_reviews.product: The non-throwing error strategy — attaching DataQualityFlags rather than throwing on malformed provider data — is the right call for fitness app consumers who must display partial data gracefully. The flawed aspect in round 1 was the dependency list: three third-party packages were added (got, date-fns, zod) that violate the zero-extra-deps constraint. This must be fixed.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Dual CJS/ESM build via tsup is the correct approach for Node.js 22 + React Native compatibility. The provider adapter interface pattern is well-structured. The critical flaw from round 1: the data_model section described a persistent SQLite database for caching activity records, which directly contradicts the stateless SDK design principle in the contract. Remove all persistence from the SDK design.
reasoner_reviews.security: OAuth token handling must be explicit: tokens passed as function arguments, never stored in module-level variables, never included in error stack traces or Sentry payloads. The round 1 plan included a token cache to avoid redundant OAuth refresh calls — this is a security anti-pattern for a library and must be removed.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: The 50KB bundle constraint is achievable without date-fns (adds ~13KB) or zod (adds ~8KB). Implement timezone normalization using Intl.DateTimeFormat (built-in) and validation using hand-rolled TypeScript type guards. This keeps the bundle under budget.

backend.service_design: Provider adapter interface: ProviderAdapter<T> generic with fetchActivities(token: string, since: Date, until: Date): Promise<RawActivity<T>[]>; four concrete adapters: GarminAdapter, AppleHealthAdapter, PolarAdapter, WahooAdapter
backend.api_patterns: Programmatic API: normalizeActivity(raw: RawActivity, provider: ProviderType): UnifiedActivity; fetchAndNormalize(token: string, provider: ProviderType, options: FetchOptions): Promise<UnifiedActivity[]>; each returns UnifiedActivity or attaches DataQualityFlags for degraded data
backend.business_modules: NormalizationPipeline: UnitConverter (pace/speed, energy, distance), TimezoneNormalizer (Intl.DateTimeFormat-based UTC conversion), DataQualityAnalyzer (flag MISSING_HEART_RATE, TRUNCATED_GPS, ZERO_DISTANCE, TIMEZONE_AMBIGUOUS), SchemaMapper per provider
backend.backend_risks: Apple Health data is exported as XML dumps (HealthKit export), not REST API — adapter must handle XML parsing in browser-safe manner; use built-in DOMParser in browser, fast-xml-parser as optional peer dep for Node.js

security.auth_design: Token passed as function parameter to each adapter call; never stored at module scope; Sentry breadcrumbs explicitly scrub token fields via beforeBreadcrumb hook
security.secrets_management: No secrets stored in SDK; consumer app manages OAuth refresh cycles
security.security_risks: Console.log statements in adapter code may inadvertently print token substrings — enforce lint rule no-console and token redaction in error serialization

data.entities: UnifiedActivity {id: string, provider: ProviderType, start_time: Date (UTC-normalized), end_time: Date, duration_s: number, distance_m: number, avg_heart_rate_bpm: number|null, max_heart_rate_bpm: number|null, calories_kcal: number|null, activity_type: ActivityType ENUM run/cycle/swim/strength/other, gps_track: GpsPoint[]|null, data_quality_flags: DataQualityFlag[]}. DataQualityFlag {code: FlagCode, severity: 'warning'|'error', detail: string, affected_field: string}. RawActivity<T> provider-typed raw response wrapper.
data.schema_notes: UnifiedActivity uses number|null (not undefined) for optional fields — allows JSON serialization without surprises in consumer apps. ActivityType enum is open-ended via 'other' to handle future provider-specific types without breaking changes.
data.data_risks: Garmin Connect returns pace in min/km as a string '5:32' rather than a float — NormalizationPipeline must parse string pace values and handle divide-by-zero for zero-distance activities

devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions on push: eslint + prettier check, tsc --noEmit type check, jest unit tests with 90% coverage gate, tsup build (CJS + ESM), bundle size check (< 50KB). On tag push: npm publish with provenance attestation.
devops.observability_stack: Sentry SDK integration as optional peer dependency; if Sentry is initialized by consumer app, SDK captures provider API errors with scrubbed context. Structured error classes: ProviderAuthError, ProviderRateLimitError, NormalizationError extend base SdkError.
devops.devops_risks: npm provenance attestation requires npm token with publish permissions scoped to the package — document in repository secrets setup guide

previous_plan.title: Wearable Data Normalization SDK v1
previous_plan.executive_summary: TypeScript SDK for normalizing workout data from Apple Health, Garmin Connect, Polar Flow, and Wahoo into a unified activity schema.
previous_plan.architecture_overview: Provider adapters fetch raw data via OAuth-authenticated HTTP calls; normalization pipeline converts units and attaches quality flags; outputs typed UnifiedActivity objects. (Flawed: also described SQLite caching layer.)
