previous_plan.technology_stack: TypeScript, Node.js 22, axios, got (BANNED), date-fns (BANNED), zod (BANNED), tsup for dual build.

target_output.thinking_summary: Round 2 must fix three high-severity contract violations from round 1. Remove all persistence — the SDK is stateless by design, consumers own their storage. Replace banned dependencies (got, date-fns, zod) with built-in equivalents and axios. Remove module-level token cache entirely — tokens are per-call parameters. The Apple Health XML adapter is the trickiest piece: use DOMParser in browser contexts and document fast-xml-parser as an optional peer dep for Node.js server environments only.

fix_report.action_taken: Removed all database references from data_model, system_components, and technology_stack. The data_model section now describes only the TypeScript interface definitions (UnifiedActivity, DataQualityFlag, RawActivity) — no storage schema. The SDK returns typed objects to consumers and stores nothing.
fix_report.expected_outcome: SDK has zero persistence footprint; consumers receive UnifiedActivity objects and decide how to store them

fix_report.action_taken: Removed got, date-fns, and zod from dependencies. Timezone normalization implemented using Intl.DateTimeFormat with IANA timezone IDs — zero bundle cost. Schema validation uses TypeScript type guards (isValidActivityType, isNumericOrNull) — no runtime schema library. HTTP client is axios 1.x only. Bundle size verified at 34KB.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Bundle stays under 50KB; React Native compatibility maintained; zero banned dependencies

fix_report.action_taken: Removed module-level tokenCache from all adapter descriptions. Each adapter method accepts token as a required string parameter. Added ESLint rule @typescript-eslint/no-module-level-variable for token-type variables. Sentry breadcrumb hook added to scrub fields named 'token', 'access_token', 'refresh_token' before capture.
fix_report.expected_outcome: No token values stored at module scope; Sentry payloads safe to transmit without token leakage risk

target_output.title: Wearable Data Normalization SDK — Architecture v2
target_output.executive_summary: A stateless TypeScript SDK published to npm providing provider adapters for Apple Health, Garmin Connect, Polar Flow, and Wahoo that normalize raw wearable workout data into a typed UnifiedActivity schema with consistent SI units, UTC-normalized timestamps, and structured DataQualityFlags for degraded or missing fields. The SDK has zero persistence, a single HTTP dependency (axios), and a dual CJS/ESM build under 50KB compatible with Node.js 18+ and React Native.
target_output.architecture_overview: Pure library architecture: no server, no database, no persistent state. Consumer application calls fetchAndNormalize(token, provider, options), which routes to the appropriate ProviderAdapter, fetches raw data via provider REST API (or parses Apple Health XML export), applies the NormalizationPipeline (UnitConverter, TimezoneNormalizer, DataQualityAnalyzer), and returns a typed UnifiedActivity[]. All OAuth token management is the consumer's responsibility; the SDK accepts tokens as parameters and never stores them. Package published as dual CJS/ESM build via tsup targeting Node.js 18+ and React Native.
target_output.technology_stack: Language: TypeScript 5.4 with strict mode. Runtime: Node.js 22 (primary), React Native (compatible). Dependencies: axios 1.6 (only third-party dep). Build: tsup for dual CJS/ESM output. Test: Vitest with mock provider responses. Lint: ESLint with @typescript-eslint rules including no-console and custom token-variable rule. CI/CD: GitHub Actions with npm provenance attestation on release tags. Error tracking: Sentry as optional peer dep with token-scrubbing beforeBreadcrumb hook.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Provider Adapters: GarminAdapter (Garmin Connect REST API v2, OAuth 1.0a), AppleHealthAdapter (HealthKit XML export parsing via DOMParser/optional fast-xml-parser peer dep), PolarAdapter (Polar Flow REST API v3, OAuth 2.0), WahooAdapter (Wahoo Cloud API v1, OAuth 2.0). NormalizationPipeline: pace string-to-float parser for Garmin, kJ-to-kcal conversion, distance m normalization, Intl.DateTimeFormat UTC conversion. DataQualityAnalyzer: flags MISSING_HEART_RATE (null avg_heart_rate_bpm), TRUNCATED_GPS (gps_track < 10 points for >10min activity), ZERO_DISTANCE (distance_m <= 0), TIMEZONE_AMBIGUOUS (provider returned no timezone info). Public API: fetchAndNormalize, normalizeActivity, listSupportedProviders.
target_output.system_components: ProviderAdapter interface (fetchActivities method signature). GarminAdapter (Garmin Connect /v2/activities endpoint, pace string normalization). AppleHealthAdapter (XML workout parsing, HKWorkout element mapping). PolarAdapter (Polar Flow /v3/exercises endpoint). WahooAdapter (Wahoo /v1/workouts endpoint). NormalizationPipeline (UnitConverter, TimezoneNormalizer, DataQualityAnalyzer). Public API exports (fetchAndNormalize, normalizeActivity). Error classes (ProviderAuthError, ProviderRateLimitError, NormalizationError).
target_output.workflows: Primary fetch flow: consumer calls fetchAndNormalize(garminToken, 'garmin', {since, until}); GarminAdapter calls Garmin REST API with OAuth 1.0a header; response mapped to RawActivity<GarminActivity>[]; NormalizationPipeline maps each: UnitConverter parses pace string '5:32' to 5.533 m/s, converts kJ to kcal; TimezoneNormalizer converts startTimeLocal + timezone to UTC Date; DataQualityAnalyzer checks each field and attaches DataQualityFlags; returns UnifiedActivity[]. Degraded data flow: Garmin returns activity with missing heart rate data; DataQualityAnalyzer attaches DataQualityFlag{code:'MISSING_HEART_RATE', severity:'warning', affected_field:'avg_heart_rate_bpm'}; function returns UnifiedActivity with null avg_heart_rate_bpm and the flag — consumer app decides whether to display or discard.
target_output.data_model: TypeScript interface definitions only — no database schema. UnifiedActivity: {id: string, provider: ProviderType ('garmin'|'apple_health'|'polar'|'wahoo'), start_time: Date, end_time: Date, duration_s: number, distance_m: number, avg_heart_rate_bpm: number|null, max_heart_rate_bpm: number|null, calories_kcal: number|null, activity_type: ActivityType ('run'|'cycle'|'swim'|'strength'|'other'), gps_track: GpsPoint[]|null, data_quality_flags: DataQualityFlag[]}. DataQualityFlag: {code: 'MISSING_HEART_RATE'|'TRUNCATED_GPS'|'ZERO_DISTANCE'|'TIMEZONE_AMBIGUOUS', severity: 'warning'|'error', detail: string, affected_field: string}. RawActivity<T>: {provider: ProviderType, raw: T, fetched_at: Date}. All types exported from the package root index.ts.
target_output.api_design: fetchAndNormalize(token: string, provider: ProviderType, options: {since: Date, until: Date, includeGpsTrack?: boolean}): Promise<UnifiedActivity[]>. normalizeActivity(raw: RawActivity<unknown>, provider: ProviderType): UnifiedActivity. listSupportedProviders(): ProviderType[]. validateToken(token: string, provider: ProviderType): Promise<TokenValidationResult>. Error types: ProviderAuthError (401/403 from provider), ProviderRateLimitError (429, includes retryAfterMs), NormalizationError (schema mapping failure with raw value context).
target_output.security_and_compliance: Token safety: OAuth tokens accepted as function parameters, never assigned to module-level variables, never included in error messages or Sentry payloads. ESLint rule enforces no-console throughout adapter code. Sentry beforeBreadcrumb hook strips fields matching /token|auth|secret/i before breadcrumb is sent. No token refresh logic in SDK — consumer app owns OAuth lifecycle. HIPAA advisory: SDK documentation states that consumer apps combining UnifiedActivity with user identity may be processing PHI; developers are advised to evaluate BAA requirements with wearable providers. SDK itself is a data processor with no retention.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: GitHub Actions workflows: on push to main — eslint check, tsc --noEmit, vitest with 90% branch coverage gate, tsup build, bundle-size-action verification under 50KB. On semver tag push — npm publish with --provenance flag for supply chain transparency. Package.json exports field maps CJS (dist/index.cjs) and ESM (dist/index.js) with TypeScript types (dist/index.d.ts). Changesets for version management and CHANGELOG generation.
target_output.observability: Structured error classes carry provider context: ProviderAuthError includes provider name and HTTP status. ProviderRateLimitError includes retryAfterMs from Retry-After header. NormalizationError includes affected_field and raw_value (truncated to 100 chars) for debugging. Optional Sentry integration: if consumer has initialized Sentry, SDK captures errors with scrubbed breadcrumbs. Consumer apps can enable debug logging via WEARABLE_SDK_LOG_LEVEL=debug env var which writes structured JSON to stderr.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Zero infrastructure cost — npm package. Bundle size 34KB (CJS) / 31KB (ESM) after removing banned dependencies. Vitest test suite runs in under 15 seconds. Provider API rate limits are surfaced via ProviderRateLimitError for consumer apps to implement backoff. SDK has no concurrency constraints — stateless functions are inherently parallelizable.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (delivered): GarminAdapter and AppleHealthAdapter, core NormalizationPipeline, UnifiedActivity schema, DataQualityFlag framework, GitHub Actions CI. Phase 2 (this round): resolve all three issues — remove persistence, remove banned deps, remove token cache; add PolarAdapter; publish v1.0.0 to npm with provenance. Phase 3: WahooAdapter, batch historical sync option, webhook adapter pattern for streaming updates, Sentry integration as documented optional peer dep.
target_output.development_guidelines: TypeScript strict mode with no-any ESLint rule. All public functions have JSDoc with @param token notes explicitly stating 'never stored by SDK'. no-console ESLint rule enforced — use structured SdkLogger instead. 90% branch coverage required in Vitest. Each provider adapter has a dedicated mock response fixture in tests/fixtures/ covering happy path, missing fields, and rate limit response. Semver via changesets — breaking changes require major bump.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Apple Health XML export format changes with new iOS versions — adapter must be versioned with HealthKit export format version detection. Mitigation: schema version header parsed at XML root; unsupported versions throw ProviderSchemaVersionError. Risk: Garmin Connect API v2 deprecation — track Garmin developer changelog. Tradeoff: axios over fetch — fetch is available in Node.js 18+ but React Native fetch has inconsistencies; axios provides consistent interface across targets. Tradeoff: Intl.DateTimeFormat over date-fns — no bundle cost but less precise for edge-case DST transitions; acceptable for workout start/end time precision.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Stateless SDK design confirmed — no persistence layer. Dependency constraint: axios only. Token-per-call pattern confirmed — no module-level caching. Apple Health XML via DOMParser in browser with fast-xml-parser as optional peer dep for Node.js confirmed. Polar Flow OAuth 2.0 vs. OAuth 1.0a confirmed: Polar uses OAuth 2.0, Garmin uses OAuth 1.0a — both handled by provider-specific adapter implementations.

metadata.notes: Round 2 fixing three high-severity contract violations: SQLite persistence, banned dependencies, and module-level token caching.

project_goal.value: Cross-platform desktop application replacing Excel periodization templates with a structured program builder for strength and conditioning coaches; syncs completed workout logs from companion mobile app over local network
project_goal.rationale: Coaches need structured plan building and session tracking beyond spreadsheet capabilities

target_users.value: Strength and conditioning coaches at high-performance sport academies managing training programs for individual athletes and groups
target_users.rationale: Primary users identified in coach interviews

project_class.rationale: Packaged Electron application running locally with embedded FastAPI backend — no cloud dependency

capabilities.rationale: Local UI, embedded API, SQLite persistence, local-network mobile sync

complexity_level.rationale: Progressive overload calculation logic and local-network device sync add meaningful complexity

risk_level.rationale: Local-only deployment with no external network exposure

data_sensitivity.rationale: Athlete training data is internal coaching records not exposed externally

external_exposure.rationale: Desktop app communicates only on local Wi-Fi network — no internet-facing endpoints

access_model.value: Single-coach desktop login via local username and password; no multi-tenancy
access_model.rationale: One coach per installation; athlete data scoped to that coach's roster

feature_scope.value: Multi-week periodization plan builder with mesocycle and session templates, progressive overload parameter configuration, athlete and group roster management, workout log sync from mobile companion app over local Wi-Fi, performance deviation alerts, PDF plan export
feature_scope.rationale: All features needed to replace Excel coaching workflow

mvp_scope.value: Plan builder with exercise templates and progressive overload, athlete roster, manual workout log entry, basic performance deviation flag
mvp_scope.rationale: Mobile sync and PDF export are phase 2; core planning and logging ships first

security_baseline.value: bcrypt-hashed local password for coach login; SQLite database file encrypted with SQLCipher; local API bound to 127.0.0.1 only for MVP; companion sync over local Wi-Fi mDNS with pre-shared key
security_baseline.rationale: Protects athlete data if device is lost or shared

frontend_stack.value: Electron with React 18 and TypeScript
frontend_stack.rationale: Cross-platform desktop with familiar React component model

backend_stack.value: Python 3.12 with FastAPI embedded in Electron main process via PyInstaller bundle
backend_stack.rationale: FastAPI for clean REST API within the desktop process; Python enables scientific library access for load calculations

data_platform.value: SQLite with SQLCipher encryption
data_platform.rationale: Embedded relational database with no external server dependency

hosting_target.value: Electron packaged app distributed via direct download on Windows, macOS, and Linux
hosting_target.rationale: Coaches install once; no ongoing hosting cost

privacy_retention_policy.value: All data stored locally on coach device; no telemetry transmitted externally; coach responsible for device backup
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: Local-only architecture means no cloud retention policy required

future_scope.value: Cloud sync for multi-device coach access, mobile app companion build, team calendar export to Google Calendar
future_scope.rationale: Year 2 roadmap after core desktop product validates

constraints.value: Must run offline; installer size under 200MB; Windows 10+ and macOS 13+ targets
constraints.rationale: Coaches in facility environments may lack reliable internet access

observability_baseline.value: Sentry error reporting for uncaught Electron and Python exceptions; local SQLite log table for sync events
observability_baseline.rationale: Crash reporting without external data transmission for athlete data

execution_preference.value: Feature-flag driven sprints; beta distribution to 3 partner coaching staff before public release
execution_preference.rationale: Iterative validation with real coaching workflows before broad distribution

project.goal: Desktop periodization builder and workout log tracker for S&C coaches
project.timeline: MVP in 12 weeks; mobile sync in week 20

frontend.state: Zustand for local state, React Query for embedded FastAPI calls

security.auth: bcrypt local password hash
security.network: Local API bound to 127.0.0.1; companion sync over LAN mDNS

data.sync: mDNS device discovery with pre-shared key sync protocol

reasoner_reviews.product: Plan builder replacing Excel is the highest-value feature for coaches. Progressive overload automation — automatically computing next session load from previous performance — is what drives adoption over spreadsheets. Mobile sync over local Wi-Fi is the right architecture for a local-first tool.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Embedding FastAPI via PyInstaller inside Electron is a proven pattern for desktop apps needing a Python backend. The embedded API should listen on a random available port communicated to the Electron renderer via IPC. SQLCipher adds transparent encryption to SQLite with minimal overhead. mDNS service discovery avoids IP address configuration by coaches.
reasoner_reviews.security: SQLCipher encryption key should be derived from the coach's password via PBKDF2 rather than hardcoded. Local API must reject requests from origins other than the Electron renderer. Companion sync pre-shared key should be displayed as a QR code for convenient pairing.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Offline-first is well-served by SQLite. PyInstaller bundle will approach the 200MB limit — recommend UPX compression and exclusion of unused hooks.

backend.service_design: Embedded FastAPI application spawned as subprocess by Electron main process via PyInstaller-bundled executable. Listens on random available port; port communicated to renderer via IPC contextBridge. Modules: plans, athletes, exercises, sessions, sync.
backend.api_patterns: REST API accessible only from localhost. GET/POST/PATCH/DELETE /api/plans, /api/athletes, /api/exercises, /api/sessions. POST /api/sync/receive for companion app log uploads. All responses return typed Pydantic models.
backend.background_jobs: Progressive overload calculator runs after each session log import — reads prescribed vs actual loads and computes recommended next session targets.
backend.backend_risks: FastAPI subprocess crash leaves Electron renderer unresponsive — Electron main process watchdog restarts embedded FastAPI on unhealthy health check.

security.auth_design: Coach login: bcrypt.checkpw(password, stored_hash). SQLCipher key derived via PBKDF2(password, salt=device_id, iterations=260000). Session token as short-lived JWT signed with process-lifetime secret stored in Electron safeStorage.
security.secrets_management: SQLCipher key never stored in plaintext — derived fresh from password on each app launch. mDNS pre-shared key generated as UUID on first launch, stored in SQLite encrypted table, displayed as QR code for mobile pairing.
security.audit_and_logging_controls: Local sync_events table records event_type, device_name, timestamp, records_received, status. Sentry captures uncaught exceptions — no athlete data in Sentry payloads.

data.entities: athletes (id UUID PK, name, sport, position, date_of_birth, notes, created_at), training_blocks (id UUID PK, athlete_id FK, name, start_date, duration_weeks, goal, status ENUM draft/active/archived), mesocycles (id UUID PK, training_block_id FK, week_number, focus, volume_modifier_pct), sessions (id UUID PK, mesocycle_id FK, day_of_week, session_type, notes), exercises (id UUID PK, session_id FK, template_id FK, sets, reps, load_kg, load_pct_1rm, rest_seconds, overload_increment_pct), workout_logs (id UUID PK, session_id FK, athlete_id FK, logged_date, completed_sets_json, actual_load_kg, rpe, deviation_flag), performance_benchmarks (id UUID PK, athlete_id FK, exercise_template_id FK, one_rep_max_kg, tested_date)
data.schema_notes: SQLite WAL mode. SQLCipher PRAGMA key set on connection open derived from coach password via PBKDF2.
data.data_risks: SQLite WAL file left unencrypted on crash recovery — mitigate with PRAGMA journal_mode=DELETE on sync completion.

frontend.app_structure: Electron main process manages FastAPI subprocess, SQLCipher connection, and IPC bridge. Renderer is React 18 SPA with React Router for plan/athlete/session views.
frontend.pages_and_flows: Dashboard (upcoming sessions, deviation alerts), Plan Builder (drag-and-drop mesocycle calendar), Athlete Roster (profile and benchmark history), Session Logger (manual or synced entry), Settings (pairing QR code, backup).
frontend.state_management: Zustand stores: appStore (current plan, selected athlete), uiStore (modal state, notifications). React Query for all FastAPI calls with optimistic updates.

devops.deployment_topology: Electron Builder: Windows NSIS installer, macOS DMG with code signing and notarization, Linux AppImage. GitHub Actions matrix build across ubuntu-latest, windows-latest, macos-latest runners.
devops.ci_cd_design: GitHub Actions: ESLint, tsc, pytest for FastAPI routes, Electron Builder build, installer size gate (<200MB), artifact upload. Release on tag push triggers signed build and GitHub Release.
devops.observability_stack: Sentry Electron SDK with beforeSend hook filtering athlete PII fields. Local app_log table for sync events and overload calculation results.

target_output.thinking_summary: Electron desktop app with embedded FastAPI and SQLite/SQLCipher for S&C coaches. Key decisions: FastAPI spawned on random port with IPC handoff, SQLCipher key via PBKDF2 from coach password, mDNS for companion sync, PyInstaller with UPX compression for 200MB installer limit. Moderate quality — solid architecture with a couple of thinner sections.
target_output.title: Strength and Conditioning Coach Desktop Application
target_output.executive_summary: A cross-platform Electron desktop application for strength and conditioning coaches that replaces Excel-based periodization with a structured training block builder, progressive overload automation, and local-network workout log sync from a companion mobile app. The embedded Python FastAPI backend persists all athlete data in an SQLCipher-encrypted SQLite database with no cloud dependency. Coaches on Windows or macOS build multi-week periodization plans, track completed sessions, and receive deviation alerts when actual training loads differ from prescribed targets.
target_output.architecture_overview: Three-layer local architecture: Electron main process manages the application lifecycle, spawns the embedded FastAPI subprocess on a random port, and exposes an IPC contextBridge. The React 18 renderer communicates with FastAPI via localhost REST calls using React Query. FastAPI persists to SQLCipher-encrypted SQLite with WAL mode. Companion mobile sync uses mDNS service advertisement on the local Wi-Fi; paired mobile devices post workout logs to the FastAPI sync endpoint authenticated with a pre-shared key shown as a QR code. No external network calls except optional Sentry crash reporting.
target_output.technology_stack: Frontend: Electron 31, React 18, TypeScript, Tailwind CSS, shadcn/ui, Zustand, React Query. Backend: Python 3.12, FastAPI, SQLAlchemy with aiosqlite, Pydantic v2. Database: SQLite with SQLCipher. Packaging: PyInstaller for Python bundle, Electron Builder for platform installers (NSIS/DMG/AppImage). Testing: pytest for FastAPI, Playwright for Electron E2E. CI: GitHub Actions matrix.
target_output.functional_feature_map: Training Block Builder: named multi-week blocks with drag-and-drop mesocycle calendar. Exercise Template Library: lifts with sets, reps, load prescription method (% 1RM or kg), and overload increment. Progressive Overload Engine: after each session, calculates next-session load targets using linear periodization applied to actual vs prescribed loads. Athlete Roster: profiles with sport, position, DOB, and 1RM benchmark history. Workout Log: manual entry or companion-synced completion with RPE. Deviation Alert: flags sessions >15% from prescribed, shown on dashboard.
target_output.system_components: Electron Main Process (subprocess lifecycle, IPC bridge, safeStorage), FastAPI Subprocess (REST endpoints, SQLCipher connection), SQLCipher SQLite Database, React 18 Renderer (plan builder UI), Zustand State Stores, React Query Cache, mDNS Sync Advertiser (Zeroconf), Sentry Electron SDK.
target_output.workflows: Periodization Plan Build: Coach creates 12-week block for athlete; assigns Squat, Bench, Deadlift templates to Mon/Wed/Fri with 75% 1RM and 2.5% weekly overload; publishes plan. Workout Log Sync: Athlete opens companion app on same Wi-Fi; discovers desktop via mDNS; enters pairing QR code; authenticates to FastAPI /api/sync/receive with pre-shared key; completed sets and RPE posted; FastAPI saves to workout_logs; progressive overload engine recalculates next session targets; deviation flag set if actual_load_kg differs >15% from prescribed.
target_output.data_model: athletes (id UUID PK, name, sport, position, date_of_birth, notes, created_at). training_blocks (id UUID PK, athlete_id FK, name, start_date DATE, duration_weeks INT, goal TEXT, status ENUM draft/active/archived). mesocycles (id UUID PK, training_block_id FK, week_number INT, focus TEXT, volume_modifier_pct REAL). sessions (id UUID PK, mesocycle_id FK, day_of_week ENUM, session_type ENUM strength/power/hypertrophy/deload). exercises (id UUID PK, session_id FK, template_id FK, sets INT, reps INT, load_kg REAL, load_pct_1rm REAL, rest_seconds INT, overload_increment_pct REAL). workout_logs (id UUID PK, session_id FK, athlete_id FK, logged_date DATE, completed_sets_json TEXT, actual_load_kg REAL, rpe INT, deviation_flag BOOLEAN). performance_benchmarks (id UUID PK, athlete_id FK, exercise_template_id FK, one_rep_max_kg REAL, tested_date DATE).
target_output.api_design: Local REST API on random port communicated via IPC. GET /api/athletes, POST /api/athletes, GET /api/athletes/:id/benchmarks. GET /api/plans, POST /api/plans. POST /api/sessions/:id/log. POST /api/sync/receive (requires X-Sync-Key header). GET /api/health (Electron watchdog). All responses are Pydantic JSON; 422 on validation error.
target_output.security_and_compliance: SQLCipher key derived via PBKDF2(password, salt=device_uuid, iterations=260000, hash=SHA256) — never stored in plaintext. Electron safeStorage encrypts JWT signing secret. Local FastAPI rejects requests with Host header other than localhost:<port>. Companion sync endpoint validates X-Sync-Key against PBKDF2-hashed stored key. Sentry beforeSend strips athlete_name, date_of_birth, and load fields. SQLite WAL; PRAGMA journal_mode=DELETE on sync completion to avoid unencrypted WAL artifact.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: GitHub Actions matrix: ubuntu-latest (AppImage), windows-latest (NSIS), macos-latest (DMG with Apple code signing and notarization). PyInstaller: pyinstaller --onefile --upx-dir api/main.py. Electron Builder packages PyInstaller output as extraResource. Installer size gate in CI. Release: git tag triggers signed build and GitHub Release asset upload. Electron autoUpdater polls GitHub Releases on launch.
target_output.observability: Sentry Electron SDK with beforeSend filtering athlete_name, date_of_birth, completed_sets_json, actual_load_kg. Local sync_events table: event_type, device_name, records_received, status, timestamp. FastAPI /api/health polled by Electron watchdog every 30 seconds; 3 consecutive failures trigger subprocess restart.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Zero ongoing infrastructure cost for local-only deployment. Sentry free tier. GitHub Releases hosting free. Electron autoUpdater bandwidth via GitHub CDN. Future cloud sync would add approximately $20/month.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (weeks 1-12): SQLite schema, FastAPI plan/athlete/exercise/session endpoints, Electron IPC bridge, React Plan Builder and Athlete Roster views, SQLCipher encryption, bcrypt login, Sentry, GitHub Actions CI with installer size gate. Phase 2 Mobile Sync (weeks 13-20): mDNS advertisement, companion sync endpoint, QR pairing, progressive overload engine, deviation alert dashboard, PDF export via Weasyprint. Phase 3: Cloud sync, Google Calendar export, exercise video library.
target_output.development_guidelines: TypeScript strict mode in renderer. Pydantic v2 for all FastAPI schemas. pytest coverage >80% for overload calculation and sync endpoint. Playwright E2E for plan creation and session log flows. Sentry beforeSend data scrubbing enforced via unit test. Installer size CI gate on every release branch build.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: PyInstaller bundle antivirus false positives on Windows — mitigate with code signing EV cert. Risk: mDNS blocked by facility VLAN — fallback manual IP entry for companion pairing. Risk: SQLCipher key loss if coach forgets password — encrypted backup export on plan publish. Tradeoff: embedded FastAPI subprocess over Node.js native backend — Python enables numpy-based load calculation at cost of installer size and startup latency; acceptable for offline-first requirement.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: SQLCipher chosen over plain SQLite for encryption at rest — coach devices shared in academy environments. PyInstaller confirmed over cx_Freeze for better cross-platform compatibility. Linear percentage-based progressive overload model chosen over block periodization for MVP simplicity.

metadata.notes: Moderate first-pass for S&C coach desktop app; Electron+FastAPI+SQLCipher; offline-first with mDNS companion sync.

project_goal.value: CLI tool for sports data engineers to benchmark and stress-test live sports data feed ingestion pipelines by simulating burst traffic at configurable events-per-second rates and reporting latency percentiles, throughput, and dropped message counts against Kafka and Redis Streams targets
project_goal.rationale: Engineering teams lack a domain-specific benchmarking tool for sports feed burst patterns

target_users.value: Sports data platform engineers testing ingestion pipeline performance before major sporting event traffic
target_users.rationale: Primary users are backend engineers on data infrastructure teams

project_class.rationale: Distributed as a local Go binary — no server runtime or browser UI

capabilities.rationale: CLI with Kafka/Redis Streams integration and DuckDB result storage

complexity_level.rationale: Concurrent producer goroutines, histogram statistics, and Prometheus export add meaningful complexity

risk_level.rationale: Local tooling with no external exposure; test data does not include production PII

data_sensitivity.rationale: Benchmark configurations and result files are internal engineering data

external_exposure.rationale: CLI runs on engineer workstation or CI runner; Kafka/Redis targets are internal infrastructure

access_model.value: No authentication — CLI run by engineers with existing Kafka/Redis cluster access via environment variables
access_model.rationale: Access control handled by Kafka ACLs and Redis AUTH at the cluster level

feature_scope.value: Configurable burst traffic simulation, concurrent producer goroutines, p50/p95/p99 latency histogram reporting, throughput and dropped-message counters, Kafka and Redis Streams targets, Prometheus pushgateway export, YAML feed configuration files
feature_scope.rationale: Full feature set for engineering benchmark workflows

mvp_scope.value: Kafka target, configurable EPS rate and duration, p50/p95/p99 report, JSON result output
mvp_scope.rationale: Kafka is the primary target; Redis Streams and Prometheus export are phase 2

security_baseline.value: Kafka connection via TLS with broker certificates; Redis AUTH via environment variable; no credentials in YAML config files
security_baseline.rationale: Cluster credentials must not appear in version-controlled configuration files

frontend_stack.value: N/A — CLI tool with stdout/stderr output only
frontend_stack.rationale: No browser or UI component

backend_stack.value: Go 1.22 with confluent-kafka-go and go-redis client libraries
backend_stack.rationale: Go goroutine model ideal for concurrent producer workloads

data_platform.value: DuckDB for local benchmark result storage and historical comparison queries; JSON file output per run
data_platform.rationale: DuckDB enables SQL queries over benchmark history without a running database server

hosting_target.value: Local binary distributed via GitHub Releases and go install
hosting_target.rationale: CLI tool installed on engineer workstation or CI runner

privacy_retention_policy.value: Benchmark result files stored locally by engineer; no retention policy required — internal engineering data
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: No personal data in benchmark payloads

future_scope.value: Redis Streams target adapter, HTML report generation from DuckDB history, CI integration mode outputting GitHub Actions step summary
future_scope.rationale: Roadmap after Kafka benchmark validates with engineering teams

constraints.value: Single binary distribution with no external runtime dependencies; Go 1.22+ required; YAML feed config for reproducible benchmark specs
constraints.rationale: Engineers should run the tool with a single download

observability_baseline.value: Structured JSON run logs to stdout with --verbose flag; Prometheus pushgateway export via --push-gateway flag
observability_baseline.rationale: Engineers integrate benchmark results into their Grafana dashboards

execution_preference.value: Trunk-based development with semantic versioning; binary releases on every tagged version
execution_preference.rationale: Engineering tool release cadence should match pipeline changes

project.goal: Benchmark Kafka/Redis Streams sports feed ingestion pipelines with configurable burst patterns

security.credentials: KAFKA_BROKERS, KAFKA_TLS_CERT, REDIS_AUTH via environment variables only

reasoner_reviews.product: Benchmark scope is well-suited for sports feed engineering — burst traffic simulation at configurable EPS directly models traffic patterns during match-start events. YAML feed configuration enables reproducible benchmark specs committable alongside pipeline code.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Go goroutines are the natural fit for concurrent producer simulation. Round 1 used a shared time.Ticker; this revision should use per-goroutine golang.org/x/time/rate.Limiter for accurate EPS. DuckDB Go driver enables SQL aggregation over historical runs without a server process.
reasoner_reviews.security: No credentials in YAML is the critical control. TLS cert path should reference a file path not inline PEM. KAFKA_BROKERS env var pattern is correct. Add YAML startup scan for credential patterns.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Single binary requirement is met by Go static compilation. DuckDB CGo dependency requires CGo-enabled builds — confirm matrix build per platform.

backend.service_design: cobra root command with subcommands: run (execute benchmark), compare (compare run IDs from DuckDB), config validate (validate YAML). Run command spawns N producer goroutines using a semaphore pool, each rate-limited via golang.org/x/time/rate.Limiter. HDRHistogram records per-message latency.
backend.api_patterns: CLI: sports-bench run --config feed.yaml --target kafka --duration 60 --eps 5000 --workers 20. sports-bench compare --run-id <id1> --run-id <id2>. sports-bench config validate --config feed.yaml. Flags parsed by cobra; YAML config loaded with viper.
backend.failure_handling: Kafka produce errors counted in dropped_messages counter. Producer goroutine panic recovery logs stack trace and continues. Graceful shutdown on SIGINT drains in-flight messages with 5-second timeout.
backend.backend_risks: confluent-kafka-go CGo dependency complicates cross-compilation — pre-build matrix for linux/amd64, darwin/arm64, darwin/amd64 in GitHub Actions.

security.auth_design: Kafka credentials via KAFKA_BROKERS, KAFKA_TLS_CERT_PATH, KAFKA_SASL_USERNAME, KAFKA_SASL_PASSWORD environment variables. Redis AUTH via REDIS_AUTH. Tool validates at startup that no credential values appear in YAML config file via regex scan.
security.abuse_prevention: YAML config validation enforces max_eps <= 100000 and max_duration_seconds <= 3600 to prevent accidental cluster saturation.
security.security_risks: Prometheus pushgateway endpoint may be accessible on internal network — validate pushgateway URL requires http://localhost or private RFC1918 range.

data.entities: benchmark_runs (run_id UUID, config_file_hash, target_type, target_endpoint, eps_target, worker_count, duration_seconds, started_at, completed_at, total_messages_sent, dropped_messages, p50_latency_us, p95_latency_us, p99_latency_us, max_latency_us, throughput_msg_per_sec). feed_configurations (config_hash, config_yaml, parsed_at). throughput_samples (run_id, sample_second, messages_in_window, bytes_in_window). latency_histograms (run_id, percentile_bucket, count).
data.schema_notes: DuckDB file at ~/.sports-bench/results.duckdb. JSON run summary also written to ./sports-bench-results/{run_id}.json for portability.
data.data_risks: DuckDB file corruption on process kill mid-write — mitigate by writing to temp file and atomically renaming on run completion.

devops.deployment_topology: goreleaser builds for linux/amd64, darwin/amd64, darwin/arm64, windows/amd64. CGo enabled with pre-installed librdkafka in GitHub Actions matrix. Binary artifacts on GitHub Release assets on tag push.
devops.ci_cd_design: PR CI: go vet, staticcheck, golangci-lint, go test ./... with -race. Release: goreleaser with ldflags version injection, binary checksums, GitHub Release creation.
devops.observability_stack: zerolog JSON to stdout on --verbose. Prometheus metrics to pushgateway: sports_bench_messages_total, sports_bench_dropped_total, sports_bench_p99_latency_us, sports_bench_throughput_mps labeled by run_id and target_type.

ISS-001.title: Rate limiter inaccuracy at high EPS under goroutine contention
ISS-001.detail: Round 1 used a shared time.Ticker channel for rate limiting across all producer goroutines. At >2000 EPS with 20 workers the shared channel created false dropped-message counts due to goroutine scheduling latency attributed to pipeline drops rather than tool-side rate limiter contention.

history.note: Identified in benchmark validation against a known-good Kafka cluster

ISS-002.title: Missing credential leak detection in YAML config validation
ISS-002.detail: Round 1 plan described env-var-only credential pattern but did not implement startup validation that YAML config files do not contain credential values.

history.note: Security review finding

focus_issues.title: Rate limiter inaccuracy at high EPS
focus_issues.detail: Shared time.Ticker causes false dropped-message attribution at >2000 EPS. Per-goroutine golang.org/x/time/rate.Limiter required.

focus_issues.title: No credential leak detection in YAML
focus_issues.detail: YAML config startup scan for PEM headers and password-like patterns not implemented.

latest_recommendations: Replace shared Ticker with per-goroutine golang.org/x/time/rate.Limiter
Implement YAML startup scan for PEM headers and SASL password patterns with fatal error on detection
Add --dry-run flag that validates YAML without connecting to Kafka/Redis
revision_memory.latest_plan_title: Sports Feed Pipeline Benchmark CLI v1

convergence_state.reason: Rate limiter accuracy issue causes incorrect benchmark results blocking engineering team adoption

previous_audits.summary: Solid CLI architecture with correct Go concurrency patterns, but rate limiter inaccuracy undermines the tool's core utility and the missing YAML credential scan is a security gap.
strengths: cobra+viper CLI structure is clean and extensible
DuckDB result storage with SQL comparison queries is a differentiating feature
HDRHistogram for latency recording is the correct high-performance choice
concerns: Shared Ticker rate limiter causes EPS inaccuracy above 2000 EPS
YAML startup credential scan not implemented
blocking_issues: ISS-001: Rate limiter cannot produce accurate benchmark results — teams cannot trust output
ISS-002: Credential leak risk in YAML configs without detection
recommendations: Use golang.org/x/time/rate.NewLimiter(rate.Every(time.Second/N), burst) per goroutine
Run regex scan on raw YAML bytes at startup before parsing
Add --dry-run flag for YAML validation without cluster connection

issue_updates.title: Shared Ticker rate limiter
issue_updates.detail: BenchmarkRunner.runProducers() uses a single time.Ticker divided by numWorkers

issue_updates.title: Missing YAML credential scan
issue_updates.detail: config.Validate() checks schema but not credential value patterns

previous_plan.title: Sports Feed Pipeline Benchmark CLI v1
previous_plan.executive_summary: Go 1.22 CLI tool for benchmarking Kafka sports feed ingestion pipelines. Concurrent producer goroutines, shared Ticker rate limiting, HDRHistogram latency recording, DuckDB result storage.
previous_plan.architecture_overview: cobra CLI with run/compare subcommands. N goroutines produce messages at shared Ticker rate. Results stored in DuckDB, JSON output per run.
previous_plan.workflows: Engineer runs sports-bench run --config match_feed.yaml --eps 5000 --duration 60; shared Ticker sends ticks to goroutine pool; each goroutine produces on tick; HDRHistogram records round-trip latency; report printed to stdout.
previous_plan.data_model: benchmark_runs, feed_configurations, throughput_samples, latency_histograms in DuckDB.

target_output.thinking_summary: Round 2 fixes the shared Ticker rate limiter (ISS-001) with per-goroutine golang.org/x/time/rate.Limiter and adds YAML startup credential scan (ISS-002). The flawed plan_quality introduces an intentional inconsistency: the workflows section describes a web dashboard contradicting the cli_tool project class.

fix_report.action_taken: Replaced shared time.Ticker with per-goroutine rate.NewLimiter(rate.Every(time.Second/workerEPS), 1) where workerEPS=totalEPS/numWorkers. Each goroutine calls limiter.Wait(ctx) before producing, yielding accurate per-goroutine rate control. Dropped-message counter now only increments on Kafka produce error or context deadline.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Accurate EPS delivery at target rates up to 50000 events/second; dropped-message count reflects actual Kafka failures not rate limiter artifacts

fix_report.action_taken: Added validateConfigCredentials(rawYAML []byte) function called at startup before viper parsing. Scans raw YAML bytes for '-----BEGIN', 'password:', 'sasl_password:', 'auth_token:'. Any match triggers log.Fatal with matched line number.
fix_report.expected_outcome: Startup scan prevents engineers running benchmarks with credential-containing YAML configs

target_output.title: Sports Feed Pipeline Benchmark CLI v2
target_output.executive_summary: A Go 1.22 CLI tool enabling sports data platform engineers to benchmark Kafka ingestion pipelines by simulating burst traffic at configurable events-per-second rates. Per-goroutine rate limiters using golang.org/x/time/rate deliver accurate EPS targeting, while an HDRHistogram records p50/p95/p99 latency. Results persist to a local DuckDB database for historical comparison and optionally push to Prometheus pushgateway. Startup YAML validation scans for credential patterns and aborts with a fatal error on detection.
target_output.architecture_overview: cobra CLI with run, compare, and config validate subcommands. BenchmarkRunner spawns N producer goroutines using a semaphore-bounded pool; each goroutine holds an independent golang.org/x/time/rate.Limiter initialized at totalEPS/numWorkers, calling limiter.Wait(ctx) before each produce call. An HDRHistogram recorder goroutine receives latency samples via unbuffered channel. DuckDB via CGo stores run results. No network server; pure CLI binary.
target_output.technology_stack: Language: Go 1.22. CLI: cobra and viper. Rate limiting: golang.org/x/time/rate per-goroutine Limiter. Kafka: confluent-kafka-go with librdkafka. Histograms: hdrhistogram-go. Result storage: DuckDB via marcboeker/go-duckdb. Logging: zerolog JSON. CI: GitHub Actions with goreleaser for multi-platform releases. pprof profiling endpoint exposed via net/http on --pprof-port flag for heap and goroutine dump capture during high-throughput benchmark runs. go test -benchmem tracks allocations per message to detect GC pressure regressions in hot-path serialisation code.
target_output.functional_feature_map: sports-bench run: load and validate YAML config, scan for credential patterns, spawn N rate-limited producer goroutines, collect HDR latency samples, print p50/p95/p99 report, write JSON result, append to DuckDB. sports-bench compare: SQL query DuckDB for two run IDs, render side-by-side diff table. sports-bench config validate: parse YAML, apply schema and credential scan, print resolved config or error. --dry-run: validate config without connecting to cluster.
target_output.system_components: BenchmarkRunner (goroutine pool orchestrator), ProducerGoroutine (per-worker Limiter and confluent-kafka-go producer), LatencyRecorder (HDRHistogram via channel), DropCounter (atomic counter), ResultWriter (DuckDB append and JSON file), ConfigLoader (viper YAML and validateConfigCredentials scan), PrometheusExporter (push to pushgateway on completion).
target_output.workflows: Benchmark Execution: Engineer runs sports-bench run --config match_day_feed.yaml --eps 5000 --duration 60 --workers 20; ConfigLoader reads YAML and scans for credential patterns (fatal on match); BenchmarkRunner initializes 20 ProducerGoroutines each with rate.NewLimiter(rate.Every(time.Second/250), 1); goroutines call limiter.Wait(ctx) then produce to Kafka; LatencyRecorder collects percentiles; after 60 seconds SIGALRM triggers graceful drain; HDRHistogram printed and JSON written. INTENTIONAL FLAW: A web dashboard is available at http://localhost:8080 after running sports-bench serve to view benchmark history — this contradicts the cli_tool project class and local_only exposure.
target_output.data_model: benchmark_runs (run_id VARCHAR PK, config_file_hash, target_type, eps_target INT, worker_count INT, duration_seconds INT, started_at TIMESTAMP, total_messages_sent BIGINT, dropped_messages BIGINT, p50_latency_us DOUBLE, p95_latency_us DOUBLE, p99_latency_us DOUBLE, max_latency_us DOUBLE, throughput_msg_per_sec DOUBLE). feed_configurations (config_hash VARCHAR PK, config_yaml TEXT, parsed_at TIMESTAMP). throughput_samples (run_id VARCHAR, sample_second INT, messages_in_window BIGINT). latency_histograms (run_id VARCHAR, percentile_bucket DOUBLE, count BIGINT). All in ~/.sports-bench/results.duckdb.
target_output.api_design: CLI interface only. sports-bench run [flags]: --config, --target kafka/redis-streams, --eps, --duration, --workers, --dry-run, --push-gateway, --verbose. sports-bench compare --run-id (repeatable). sports-bench config validate --config. Exit codes: 0 success, 1 config error, 2 cluster connection error, 3 credential detection error.
target_output.security_and_compliance: Kafka credentials via KAFKA_BROKERS, KAFKA_TLS_CERT_PATH, KAFKA_SASL_USERNAME, KAFKA_SASL_PASSWORD env vars only. Redis AUTH via REDIS_AUTH. validateConfigCredentials scans for '-----BEGIN', 'password:', 'sasl_password:', 'auth_token:' — fatal on match with line number. Kafka TLS via security.protocol SASL_SSL. Prometheus pushgateway URL validated for localhost or RFC1918 range.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: goreleaser: linux/amd64, darwin/amd64, darwin/arm64, windows/amd64. CGo enabled; librdkafka pre-installed in GitHub Actions matrix. PR CI: go vet, staticcheck, golangci-lint, go test -race. Release on git tag: goreleaser release --clean, GitHub Release with checksums. go install supported via module proxy. GitHub Actions matrix builds across linux/amd64, darwin/amd64, darwin/arm64, windows/amd64 with CGO_ENABLED=1 and pre-installed librdkafka via apt/brew/choco. Release checksums verified against SLSA provenance attestation generated by goreleaser's --provenance flag for supply-chain integrity.
target_output.observability: zerolog JSON to stderr on --verbose with run_id, worker_id, eps_target, total_sent, dropped, p50_us, p95_us, p99_us. Prometheus metrics on --push-gateway completion: sports_bench_messages_total, sports_bench_dropped_total, sports_bench_p99_latency_us, sports_bench_throughput_mps labeled by run_id. DuckDB result file includes a run_metadata table (run_id, kafka_broker, topic, target_eps, duration_s, cli_version) for longitudinal regression analysis across benchmark versions using a simple SELECT with JOIN.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: Zero infrastructure cost — local binary. GitHub Actions free tier. goreleaser OSS tier. DuckDB file grows at ~1KB per benchmark run. DuckDB file stored on local NVMe for maximum write throughput during high-frequency benchmark runs; results exported to CSV for sharing. No cloud egress costs; self-hosted Kafka required — tool assumes engineer provides their own broker.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 (v1, complete): Kafka target, shared Ticker (known-flawed), DuckDB, JSON output. Phase 2 (v2, current): Per-goroutine rate.Limiter, validateConfigCredentials, --dry-run, goreleaser multi-platform release. Phase 3: Redis Streams adapter, HTML report from DuckDB, GitHub Actions step summary mode.
target_output.development_guidelines: Go modules, no global state. gosec in CI with zero high-severity findings. Rate limiter accuracy regression test: assert p99 EPS error <5% at 10000 EPS with 50 workers. Credential scan unit test: assert validateConfigCredentials returns error on YAML containing 'password: secret123'.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: confluent-kafka-go CGo complicates cross-compilation — mitigated by platform-native GitHub Actions runners. Risk: HDRHistogram memory grows with very long runs — max_duration_seconds: 3600 cap. Tradeoff: DuckDB CGo over pure-Go SQLite — DuckDB chosen for SQL aggregation power on histogram data.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: golang.org/x/time/rate chosen over custom token bucket — standard library, well-tested. DuckDB confirmed over pure-Go SQLite for compare subcommand aggregation. HDRHistogram channel-based recorder avoids contention on hot produce path.

project.goal: Aggregate and normalize real-time sports statistics into a unified partner API
project.target_users: Betting platforms and fantasy sports app engineering teams
project.timeline: MVP in 14 weeks; multi-region in week 24

backend.api: REST and WebSocket; OpenAPI 3.0 published to partner portal

security.auth: HMAC-SHA256 signed API keys per partner; IP allowlist at ALB

data.db: ClickHouse for event storage and analytics
data.cache: Redis 7 for live state and pub/sub

reasoner_reviews.product: Multi-tier partner API with live_scores, play_by_play, box_scores, and analytics entitlements maps to the existing partner licensing model. WebSocket fanout via Redis pub/sub is the correct pattern for 10000 concurrent subscribers without stateful connection tracking.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Go with Fiber is an excellent choice for this latency profile. Ingest workers connecting to upstream feeds should be decoupled from the API serving process: separate ECS tasks for ingestion writing to ClickHouse and Redis, with the API service reading from Redis for live data. ClickHouse MergeTree on event_time enables sub-second rolling average queries.
reasoner_reviews.security: SOC 2 CC6 requires HMAC-SHA256 API key signing in Fiber middleware. IP allowlisting must be at ALB security group level not application code. Data-tier entitlement enforcement must be in middleware before route handlers.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: 10000 concurrent WebSocket connections per region is achievable with Go goroutines but requires kernel TCP tuning. ECS Fargate task size should be at least 2 vCPU and 8GB RAM for WebSocket-heavy tasks.

backend.service_design: Two ECS service types: IngestWorker (connects to Sportradar and Stats Perform WebSocket feeds, normalizes events, writes to ClickHouse and Redis) and APIService (Fiber HTTP/WebSocket server reading from Redis, enforcing entitlements). Separate deployments to isolate failure domains.
backend.api_patterns: REST: GET /v1/events/:event_id/score, GET /v1/events/:event_id/boxscore, GET /v1/teams/:team_id/roster, GET /v1/analytics/events. WebSocket: WS /v1/stream/:event_id/plays. OpenAPI 3.0 auto-generated from Fiber annotations.
backend.scaling_notes: API service auto-scaling on ALB RequestCountPerTarget. WebSocket task auto-scaling on custom CloudWatch metric active_ws_connections. IngestWorker scales on upstream_event_lag_seconds.
backend.backend_risks: Upstream feed reconnection during high-traffic events causes event gap — IngestWorker reconnect backoff and gap-detection alert.

security.auth_design: API key issued as base64(partner_id + random_bytes_32). HMAC-SHA256 signature required: X-Signature header with timestamp within 300s window. Validated in Fiber middleware before route dispatch.
security.authorization_model: Entitlement middleware checks partner tier from Redis hash against requested endpoint. live_scores tier allows /score only; play_by_play adds /stream; box_scores adds /boxscore; analytics adds /analytics. Tier loaded from Redis keyed by partner_id.
security.audit_and_logging_controls: Datadog APM spans for every API request with partner_id, endpoint, tier, latency_ms. ClickHouse api_request_log for SOC 2 access audit.
security.security_risks: Redis entitlement cache stale if partner tier downgraded — cache TTL 60s; downgrade triggers Redis key invalidation.

data.entities: sports_events (event_id, sport, league, home_team_id, away_team_id, status, started_at, completed_at). play_by_play_records (record_id, event_id, sequence_num, play_type, description, clock_seconds_remaining, score_home, score_away, team_id, player_id, occurred_at) ENGINE=MergeTree PARTITION BY toYYYYMM(occurred_at). box_scores (event_id, team_id, player_id, minutes_played, points, assists, rebounds, field_goals_made, field_goals_attempted, generated_at). team_rosters (team_id, player_id, jersey_number, position, status). data_provider_feeds (feed_id, provider_name, sport, status, last_event_at, event_lag_seconds). api_consumers (partner_id, api_key_hash, tier, ip_ranges[], created_at). subscription_tiers (tier_name, allowed_endpoints[], rate_limit_rpm, burst_allowance).
data.storage_design: ClickHouse: play_by_play_records MergeTree partitioned by toYYYYMM(occurred_at). Redis: HSET live:event:{event_id} score_home score_away status; pub/sub channel events:{event_id}:plays.
data.consistency_notes: IngestWorker uses sequence_num to detect and discard duplicate or out-of-order events from provider reconnections.

devops.deployment_topology: Two ECS services per region in private subnets. ALB in public subnet with TLS. ElastiCache Redis Multi-AZ. ClickHouse on EC2 r6i.2xlarge with EBS GP3. Cross-region ClickHouse replication to eu-west-1.
devops.ci_cd_design: Buildkite: go vet, golangci-lint, go test -race, Docker build, ECR push, ECS blue/green with Datadog canary monitor gate (error rate <0.1% and p99 <50ms for 5 minutes). Rollback: manual unblock or automatic on canary failure.
devops.observability_stack: Datadog APM Go tracer. Custom metrics: upstream_event_lag_seconds, api_requests_total per partner_tier, ws_connections_active, clickhouse_query_duration_p99. PagerDuty: upstream_event_lag >30s (P1 during live events), p99 >45ms (P2), ws_connections >9000 (P2).

target_output.thinking_summary: High-scale partner-facing API for real-time sports stats. Key decisions: separate IngestWorker and APIService ECS services to isolate upstream failures from API availability; Redis pub/sub for WebSocket fanout; ClickHouse MergeTree partitioned by month; HMAC-SHA256 per-request signing in Fiber middleware for SOC 2 CC6; blue/green with Datadog canary gates for zero-downtime.
target_output.title: Real-Time Sports Statistics Aggregation API
target_output.executive_summary: A high-throughput Go 1.22 and Fiber API service aggregating live sports statistics from Sportradar and Stats Perform into a unified normalized schema, serving live scores via REST, play-by-play events via WebSocket with Redis pub/sub fanout, and rolling-window analytics via ClickHouse to authenticated partner organizations. Deployed as two independent ECS Fargate service types per region (IngestWorker and APIService) in us-east-1 and eu-west-1, with HMAC-SHA256 signed API keys, per-partner data-tier entitlement enforcement, and blue/green deployments gated by Datadog canary monitors.
target_output.architecture_overview: Two-service architecture deployed per region. IngestWorkers maintain persistent WebSocket connections to Sportradar and Stats Perform feeds, normalize events to a canonical schema, write play-by-play records to ClickHouse via batch inserts, and publish live score updates to Redis pub/sub channels keyed by event_id. APIService instances read live score state from Redis for sub-millisecond REST responses, subscribe to Redis pub/sub and fan out to downstream WebSocket clients, and execute ClickHouse rolling-window queries for analytics. ALB enforces TLS 1.3 and IP allowlisting via security group rules. HMAC-SHA256 signature validation and data-tier entitlement enforcement run in Fiber middleware before any route handler.
target_output.technology_stack: Backend: Go 1.22, Fiber v2, fasthttp. Pub/sub: Redis pub/sub for in-region event fanout. Analytics DB: ClickHouse (self-managed on EC2 r6i.2xlarge GP3, MergeTree). Cache: Redis 7 ElastiCache Multi-AZ. Upstream: custom Go WebSocket clients. Auth: HMAC-SHA256 Fiber middleware. CI: Buildkite. Monitoring: Datadog APM. Infra: ECS Fargate, ALB, ECR, ElastiCache, Route 53 latency-based routing.
target_output.functional_feature_map: GET /v1/events/:event_id/score — Redis hash read, <5ms response. WS /v1/stream/:event_id/plays — Redis pub/sub fanout to subscribers as normalized PlayByPlayEvent JSON. GET /v1/events/:event_id/boxscore — ClickHouse aggregation query. GET /v1/teams/:team_id/roster — Redis hash read. GET /v1/analytics/events — ClickHouse rolling average query. POST /v1/admin/api-keys — partner key provisioning. GET /v1/admin/api-keys/:partner_id/usage — ClickHouse api_request_log aggregation.
target_output.system_components: IngestWorker ECS tasks (Sportradar client, Stats Perform client, event normalizer, ClickHouse batch writer, Redis publisher), APIService ECS tasks (Fiber HTTP server, HMAC auth middleware, entitlement middleware, REST handlers, WebSocket upgrade handler, Redis subscriber pool), ElastiCache Redis Multi-AZ, ClickHouse cluster, ALB with WAF, Datadog APM, Buildkite CI.
target_output.workflows: Live Score REST: Partner calls GET /v1/events/nba-2025-gsw-bos/score with X-Api-Key and X-Signature; HMAC auth middleware validates signature and timestamp; entitlement middleware confirms live_scores tier; Fiber handler calls HGET from Redis; returns LiveScoreResponse in <10ms. Play-by-Play WebSocket: Partner upgrades to WS /v1/stream/nba-2025-gsw-bos/plays; auth and entitlement check play_by_play tier; goroutine subscribes to Redis SUBSCRIBE events:nba-2025-gsw-bos:plays; each IngestWorker-published play forwarded as normalized JSON frame. Upstream Ingest: IngestWorker maintains WebSocket to Sportradar; on each event, normalizer converts to canonical PlayEvent; ClickHouse batch writer buffers 100 events or 500ms then INSERT; Redis HSET updates live score; Redis PUBLISH fans out to subscriber channels.
target_output.data_model: sports_events (event_id VARCHAR, sport VARCHAR, league VARCHAR, home_team_id VARCHAR, away_team_id VARCHAR, status ENUM, started_at DateTime, completed_at DateTime nullable). play_by_play_records (record_id UUID, event_id VARCHAR, sequence_num BIGINT, play_type VARCHAR, description VARCHAR, clock_seconds_remaining INT, score_home INT, score_away INT, team_id VARCHAR, player_id VARCHAR, occurred_at DateTime) ENGINE=MergeTree PARTITION BY toYYYYMM(occurred_at) ORDER BY (event_id, sequence_num). api_consumers (partner_id VARCHAR PK, api_key_hash VARCHAR, tier VARCHAR, ip_ranges Array(VARCHAR), created_at DateTime). subscription_tiers (tier_name VARCHAR PK, allowed_endpoints Array(VARCHAR), rate_limit_rpm INT, burst_allowance INT).
target_output.api_design: All endpoints require X-Api-Key and X-Signature (hmac-sha256=hex(HMAC(secret, METHOD+path+timestamp+sha256(body)))) with X-Timestamp within 300s. GET /v1/events/:event_id/score -> LiveScoreResponse. WS /v1/stream/:event_id/plays -> stream of PlayByPlayEvent. GET /v1/events/:event_id/boxscore -> BoxScoreResponse. GET /v1/analytics/events?window_minutes=15 -> AnalyticsResponse. Rate limits: live_scores 300 RPM, play_by_play 100 RPM, analytics 60 RPM.
target_output.security_and_compliance: SOC 2 CC6: HMAC-SHA256 per-request signing validates X-Signature against recomputed HMAC; timestamp window 300s prevents replay. Entitlement middleware loads partner tier from Redis (TTL 60s); downgrade sends Redis DEL for immediate cache invalidation. IP allowlisting at ALB security group. TLS 1.3 on ALB; mTLS with Sportradar using provider-issued client certificate. Partner data redistribution logged to ClickHouse api_request_log for contractual compliance reporting.
target_output.deployment_and_operations: ECS Fargate: IngestWorker 2 tasks per region (1 vCPU/4GB, no ALB), APIService auto-scaling 4-20 tasks (2 vCPU/8GB, ALB). Blue/green: new task definition registered to target group B, 10% canary, Datadog monitor watches error_rate <0.1% and p99 <50ms for 5 minutes, then 100% shift. Route 53 latency-based routing between regions. ClickHouse replication from us-east-1 to eu-west-1 replica.
target_output.observability: Datadog APM Go tracer on all ECS tasks. Custom metrics: upstream_event_lag_seconds per provider, api_requests_total per partner tier and endpoint, ws_connections_active per region, clickhouse_query_duration_p99. Dashboards: Live API health, Ingest health. PagerDuty: event lag >30s P1, p99 >45ms for >2 min P2, ws_connections >9000 P2.
target_output.cost_and_scaling: ECS APIService ~$800/month base (4 tasks) scaling to ~$2400 at 20 tasks. ElastiCache Redis r7g.large Multi-AZ ~$400/month per region. ClickHouse EC2 r6i.2xlarge ~$600/month. Datadog ~$300/month. ALB and data transfer ~$200/month. Total $2300-4000/month per region. Reserved baseline reduces cost ~30%.
target_output.phased_implementation: Phase 1 MVP (weeks 1-14): IngestWorker for Sportradar basketball, APIService with live score and WebSocket play-by-play, HMAC auth middleware, Redis pub/sub, single-region us-east-1, Buildkite with blue/green gate, Datadog APM. Phase 2 (weeks 15-24): Stats Perform ingest, box score and analytics ClickHouse endpoints, eu-west-1 region, ClickHouse replication, usage dashboard. Phase 3: GraphQL subscriptions, multi-sport schema, partner self-service key provisioning.
target_output.development_guidelines: Go modules, no global mutable state. golangci-lint with errcheck and gosec. Integration tests against local Redis and ClickHouse in Docker Compose. go test -race in CI. OpenAPI 3.0 spec auto-generated and published to partner developer portal on each release.
target_output.risks_and_tradeoffs: Risk: Sportradar WebSocket disconnect during live championship — IngestWorker reconnects with backoff; gap-detection flags sequence_num discontinuity and emits metric. Risk: Redis pub/sub overflow during burst — Redis client buffer limits and slow-client disconnect. Tradeoff: self-managed ClickHouse over ClickHouse Cloud — lower cost but requires operator expertise. Risk: partner HMAC key compromise — 5-minute timestamp window limits replay; revocation propagates via Redis pub/sub within 1 second.
target_output.open_questions_resolved: Redis pub/sub over Kafka for WebSocket fanout — sub-millisecond publish latency avoids Kafka consumer lag in real-time stream. ClickHouse MergeTree over TimescaleDB for play-by-play storage — superior INSERT throughput and OLAP query performance. Per-request HMAC signing over OAuth 2.0 — stateless, avoids token refresh latency in hot path.

metadata.notes: Strong first-pass for high-scale partner-facing sports stats API; Go+Fiber+ClickHouse+Redis; SOC 2 CC6 HMAC auth; blue/green Datadog canary gates.

project_goal.value: Mobile app for corporate wellness programs enabling employees to log daily movement minutes, participate in team step-count challenges, and earn reward points redeemable for benefits; backend decomposed into activity tracking, challenge management, rewards, and push notification microservices
project_goal.rationale: Core product scope for corporate wellness platform

target_users.value: Corporate employees (wellness program participants) and HR administrators configuring programs
target_users.rationale: Dual user personas: employees as primary mobile users, HR admins via web admin panel

project_class.rationale: Flutter cross-platform mobile app as primary consumer surface

capabilities.value: frontend, backend, data, auth, integrations, analytics, realtime
capabilities.rationale: Full-stack mobile with microservices backend, wearable integrations, real-time leaderboards

complexity_level.rationale: Microservices decomposition with event-driven inter-service communication and real-time leaderboard updates

risk_level.rationale: Health-adjacent employee data requires consent management and employment law compliance

data_sensitivity.rationale: Health-adjacent activity data tied to employee identity; GDPR and employment law require careful consent and data minimization

external_exposure.rationale: All access requires Firebase Auth JWT; employees access their own data, HR admins access aggregate program data

access_model.value: Firebase Auth with email/SSO; RBAC with employee and hr_admin roles; employees see only their own activity and team aggregates, HR admins see program-level analytics
access_model.rationale: Role separation required to prevent employees from accessing colleagues' individual data

feature_scope.value: Manual activity logging, wearable sync (Apple Health/Google Fit), team step-count challenges with live leaderboards, reward points accrual and redemption catalog, push notifications for milestones and challenge updates, HR admin dashboard for program configuration and reporting
feature_scope.rationale: Full platform feature set

mvp_scope.value: Manual activity logging, basic team challenge (step count), reward points accrual, push notifications, Firebase Auth; HR admin program creation
mvp_scope.rationale: MVP sufficient for initial corporate pilot

security_baseline.value: Firebase Auth JWT required on all API calls; TLS 1.3 in transit; employee activity data access scoped to own data only; HR admin cannot view individual employee health data, only aggregates
security_baseline.rationale: Employment law in most jurisdictions restricts employer access to individual employee health data

frontend_stack.value: Flutter with Dart (cross-platform iOS and Android)
frontend_stack.rationale: Single codebase for iOS and Android, strong performance for real-time UI updates

backend_stack.value: Node.js 22 with Fastify (four microservices: activity, challenge, rewards, notifications)
backend_stack.rationale: Consistent JS/TS stack across microservices, Fastify for high throughput

data_platform.value: MongoDB 7 with Atlas; Redis 7 for leaderboard sorted sets and session cache
data_platform.rationale: Document model fits variable activity schema; Redis sorted sets for O(log N) leaderboard operations

hosting_target.value: GCP Cloud Run per microservice
hosting_target.rationale: Serverless containers with independent scaling per service

privacy_retention_policy.value: Employee activity data retained for duration of employment plus 12 months; employees can request deletion of personal data; aggregate challenge data retained indefinitely
privacy_retention_policy.rationale: GDPR Article 17 right to erasure; employment records retention law

future_scope.value: Manager coaching insights, nutrition tracking module, biometric screening integration, native wearable SDK for Garmin and Fitbit
future_scope.rationale: Post-launch roadmap

constraints.value: Employee individual data must not be visible to HR admins or managers; GDPR right-to-erasure within 30 days; app must function with offline activity logging when cellular coverage is poor
constraints.rationale: Legal compliance and usability in gym environments with poor connectivity

observability_baseline.value: Grafana + Prometheus per service; structured logging with correlation IDs; Firebase Crashlytics for mobile crashes
observability_baseline.rationale: Standard observability stack for microservices

execution_preference.value: CI/CD via CircleCI; trunk-based development with feature flags for gradual rollout to corporate clients
execution_preference.rationale: Feature flags allow per-client feature enablement for enterprise sales

compliance_context.value: GDPR — employee activity data is personal data; DPIA required before launch; consent must be freely given (not coerced by employer); applicable employment law in EU and US states requires data minimization and right-to-erasure
compliance_context.rationale: Corporate wellness data tied to employment relationship requires heightened consent standards

project.goal: Corporate wellness mobile app with team challenges and rewards

frontend.approach: Cross-platform iOS and Android; offline-first activity logging with local SQLite sync queue

security.auth: Firebase Auth with email + SSO (Google Workspace, Azure AD)
security.authorization: RBAC: employee role scoped to own data; hr_admin role scoped to aggregate program data only

constraints.privacy: HR admins cannot access individual employee health data
constraints.offline: Local SQLite queue for offline activity logs

reasoner_reviews.product: The offline-first activity logging requirement is well-motivated for gym environments. The consent model must make clear that participation is voluntary and reward point accumulation is not visible to managers — this should be explicit in the privacy notice shown at onboarding. HR admin analytics must be aggregated to a minimum group size (e.g., 5 employees) before displaying to prevent indirect identification.
reasoner_reviews.architect_reasoner: Microservices decomposition makes sense here because activity, challenge, and rewards have genuinely different scaling profiles — challenge leaderboard updates are bursty during work hours while rewards processing is batch-oriented. GCP Pub/Sub as the inter-service event bus is the right choice over direct HTTP calls to avoid tight coupling. The Redis sorted set per challenge for leaderboard is correct and must be updated by the challenge-service consuming ActivityLogged events, not by the activity-service directly.
reasoner_reviews.security: The HR admin data access boundary is the highest-risk element: the authorization layer must enforce aggregate-only access at the API gateway level, not just in UI. Individual employee_id fields must be stripped from all HR admin API responses server-side. GDPR erasure must cascade across all four services — document the erasure choreography.
reasoner_reviews.constraints: Offline activity logging via local SQLite sync queue is achievable in Flutter using drift (SQLite ORM). The sync queue must include a retry mechanism and conflict resolution strategy for cases where the same activity is submitted multiple times during a reconnection burst.

frontend.app_structure: Flutter app with feature-based directory structure: features/activity, features/challenges, features/rewards, features/profile, shared/auth, shared/sync_queue
frontend.pages_and_flows: Home Dashboard (today's activity ring, active challenge progress), Activity Log (manual entry form + wearable sync), Challenge Detail (live leaderboard, team members, countdown), Rewards Catalog (available rewards, points balance, redemption history), Profile (consent settings, data deletion request)
frontend.state_management: Riverpod for global state; Hive for local cache; drift (SQLite) for offline sync queue
frontend.accessibility_notes: Support dynamic font scaling for corporate device accessibility policies; minimum tap target 44x44pt per WCAG 2.1

backend.service_design: activity-service: logs employee_profiles activities, publishes ActivityLogged event to Pub/Sub. challenge-service: subscribes to ActivityLogged, updates Redis sorted set for active challenges, evaluates goal completion. rewards-service: subscribes to ChallengeGoalMet, credits reward_points, publishes PointsAwarded. notification-service: subscribes to PointsAwarded and MilestoneReached, sends FCM push notifications.
backend.api_patterns: REST APIs per service behind API Gateway (Cloud Endpoints); activity-service POST /activities, GET /activities/me; challenge-service GET /challenges, GET /challenges/{id}/leaderboard (aggregate, no individual identification for hr_admin role); rewards-service GET /rewards/balance, POST /rewards/redeem
backend.background_jobs: Month-end batch job in rewards-service aggregates final challenge standings, distributes bonus points, triggers payout notifications. Nightly analytics job computes program-level participation rates for HR admin reports.

security.auth_design: Firebase Auth JWT validated at Cloud Endpoints API gateway; role claim 'employee' or 'hr_admin' in custom claims
security.authorization_model: employee role: activity-service scoped to own employee_id; challenge-service leaderboard endpoint returns team aggregate only. hr_admin role: no access to individual activity endpoints; program analytics endpoint enforces minimum group size of 5 before returning data
security.privacy_controls: GDPR erasure endpoint: DELETE /employees/{id}/data triggers saga across all four services via Pub/Sub EmployeeDataDeletionRequested event; each service hard-deletes records and publishes DeletionConfirmed; saga coordinator confirms all four within 30-day SLA

data.entities: employee_profiles (MongoDB: {_id, firebase_uid, corporate_email, company_id, created_at, consent_given_at, consent_version}). activity_logs (MongoDB: {_id, employee_id, activity_type, duration_minutes, step_count, source ENUM manual/apple_health/google_fit, logged_at, synced_at}). team_challenges (MongoDB: {_id, company_id, name, goal_type ENUM steps/minutes/sessions, goal_value, start_date, end_date, eligible_employee_ids[], status}). challenge_participants (MongoDB: {_id, challenge_id, employee_id, current_progress, goal_met_at}). reward_points (MongoDB: {_id, employee_id, balance, last_updated}). redemption_events (MongoDB: {_id, employee_id, reward_catalog_id, points_cost, redeemed_at, status}). notification_subscriptions (MongoDB: {_id, employee_id, fcm_token, preferences}).
data.storage_design: MongoDB Atlas M30 cluster per environment; Redis 7 sorted sets keyed challenge:{challenge_id}:leaderboard for O(log N) ZADD/ZRANK operations. Offline sync: Flutter drift SQLite queue on device with pending_activity_logs table.
data.retention_and_deletion: Employment-duration + 12 months retention enforced via MongoDB TTL index on activity_logs.logged_at post-employment date. GDPR erasure within 30 days via EmployeeDataDeletionRequested Pub/Sub saga.

devops.deployment_topology: Four Cloud Run services (activity, challenge, rewards, notification) behind Cloud Endpoints API gateway. GCP Pub/Sub topics: ActivityLogged, ChallengeGoalMet, PointsAwarded, MilestoneReached, EmployeeDataDeletionRequested. MongoDB Atlas M30 in GCP us-central1. Redis 7 on GCP Memorystore.
devops.ci_cd_design: CircleCI pipelines per service: lint (ESLint), type check (tsc), unit tests (Jest 90% coverage), integration tests against MongoDB testcontainer. Docker build and push to GCP Artifact Registry. Cloud Run deploy with traffic split for canary rollouts.
devops.observability_stack: Prometheus metrics sidecar per Cloud Run service; Grafana dashboards: activity ingestion rate, challenge leaderboard update latency, rewards saga completion time, FCM delivery rate. Firebase Crashlytics for Flutter crash reports.
